
--- PAGE 2 ---
The company
Our purpose: To explore and discover the infinite possibilities of energy so that everyone can move forward.

Our values help us to make our purpose a reality – “Courage: we question with meaning and ambition”, “Curiosity: we 
are  open  to  learning”,  “Achievement:  we  make  things  happen”  and  “Support:  we  unlock  people’s  potential”.  Further 
information can be found at www.repsol.com.

The Management Report
Repsol1,  as  a  further  show  of  its  commitment  to  transparency,  has  drawn  up  this  consolidated  Management 
Report,  which  integrates  both  financial  and  sustainability  information,  serving  as  the  cornerstone  of  the  Group's 
annual public reporting. 

This  Management  Report  faithfully  represents  the  Repsol  Group's  business,  results,  and  financial  position,  and 
provides a description of the main risks and uncertainties it faces, along with its strategic lines of action and  their 
foreseeable trend moving forward. It also provides information on sustainability, including Environmental, Social and 
Governance (ESG) criteria.
The report not only complies with the applicable2 legal requirements but is also aligned with best practices and, in 
particular,  with  the  recommendations  of  the Conceptual  Framework  for  the  Preparation  of  Integrated  Reports  of 
the International Integrated Reporting Council (IIRC) and the Guide for the Preparation of the Management Report 
of Listed Entities of the CNMV, Spain’s securities market regulator.

This report should be read in conjunction with the 2025 consolidated Financial Statements, which together with 
this Report have been filed with the CNMV (www.cnmv.es) and are also available at www.repsol.com.

Report information
The  financial  information  included  in  this  document,  unless  expressly  stated  otherwise,  has  been  prepared  in 
accordance with Repsol’s segment information reporting model (revised in 2025), which is described in Appendix II 
and in the Note 4 “Segment information” of the 2025 consolidated Financial Statements. This reporting model uses 
Alternative  Performance  Measures  (AMS)  in  accordance  with  the  Guidelines  of  the  European  Securities  Markets 
Authority  (ESMA),3 i.e. "adjusted" magnitudes with respect to those presented in accordance with EU-IFRS. They are 
updated quarterly on the Repsol  website(www.repsol.com). 

The information on sustainability is presented in accordance with the requirements of the Corporate Sustainability 
Reporting  Directive  (CSRD)  and  the  European  Sustainability  Reporting  Standards  (ESRS),  as  adopted  through 
Commission Delegated Act of July 31, 2023. It also includes the information required by Spanish Law 11/2018, as 
well  as  the  disclosures  on  environmentally  sustainable  activities  according  to  the  Sustainable  Finance  Taxonomy, 
and  addresses  the  requirements  of  Royal  Decree  214/2025  of  March  18,  which  establishes  the  obligation  to 
calculate  the  carbon  footprint  and  to  prepare  and  publish  greenhouse  gas  emission  reduction  plans.  This  report 
also  includes  voluntary  indicators  in  accordance  with  the  Guidelines  of  the  Global  Reporting  Initiative  (GRI). 
Together,  this  information  makes  up  the  Statement  Non-Financial  Information  and  Sustainability  Information 
included in Appendix IV.

In the realm of corporate governance, Repsol publishes an Annual Corporate Governance Report (Appendix V)  and 
an Annual Report on Directors’ Remuneration (Appendix VI), drawn up in accordance with Articles 540 and 541 of the 
Spanish Companies Act, following the instructions of the CNMV4.
The  forward-looking  information  contained  in  the  different  sections  of  this  document  reflects  the  plans, 
objectives,  commitments,  aspirations,  forecasts  or  estimates  of  the  Group’s  directors  at  the  date  of  their 
authorization for issue. Such forward-looking information is based on assumptions that are considered reasonable 
as of the date of their preparation, and cannot be interpreted as a guarantee of the entity’s future performance, in 
the sense that such plans, forecasts or estimates are subject to risks and uncertainties, meaning that the Group’s 
future performance may not necessarily coincide with what was initially envisaged or planned.

--- PAGE 3 ---
Message 
from the Chairman

The EU has a real 
opportunity to regain 
relevance if it embraces 
reindustrialization 
and turns the energy 
transition into a driver 
of growth

Dear Shareholders,

The world is going through a period of profound 
transformation, marked by major geopolitical shifts, 
trade tensions, and growing global competition for 
leadership in energy, industry, and technology. The 
current scenario is uncertain, and the European Union 
faces a historic challenge: redefining its role and moving 
toward a more resilient model that will allow it to 
successfully address these global challenges.

For decades, growth across the globe was underpinned 
by trade openness and multilateral cooperation. Today, 
those foundations are being challenged by an 
unrelenting drive to secure a more strategic position in 
the value chains of the future. In this new context, access 
to reliable and competitively priced energy, critical raw 
materials, and homegrown industrial and technological 
capabilities is essential to ensure that Europe continues 
to have a voice in the world and is heard. 

Europe facing the challenge of rebuilding 
its industrial base

The European Union approaches this moment 
from a challenging position. Its industrial fabric has 
steadily weakened over recent decades, and 
a significant technological gap persists compared 
with the United States and China. This is compounded 
by structurally higher energy costs, which undermine 
the competitiveness of European companies and 
accelerate offshoring.

This context marks a decisive turning point. The EU has 
a real opportunity to regain relevance if it embraces 
reindustrialization and turns the energy transition into 
a driver of growth — rather than a drag — on its 
economy. Achieving this goal will also require the EU 
institutions to focus on promoting investment rather 
than generating additional regulation. 

Recognizing the strategic nature of the refining sector 
in terms of fuel supply security, industrial development, 
and strategic autonomy will be critical for the 
continent’s future. Refineries supply 97% of the energy 
used in the European transport sector and provide 
approximately 50% of the essential raw materials for 
the chemical industry, supporting critical value chains 
ranging from automotive manufacturing to aviation 
and defense.

However, since 2009, one in four European refineries 
has closed or been converted, reducing total refining 
capacity by nearly 20%, creating growing vulnerabilities 
in Europe’s security of supply, and undermining the 
region’s industrial competitiveness. This erosion has 
turned the European Union into a net importer of diesel 
and jet fuel, increasing its dependence on third 
countries in an increasingly unstable geopolitical context.

This situation contrasts with the more pragmatic energy 
policies of the United States, which has reduced 
greenhouse gas emissions as much as, if not more than, 
the European Union, while pursuing all technologies 
that can incrementally improve sustainability and meet 
the world’s growing energy demand. As a result of 
these policies, the United States has achieved 
significantly lower energy costs than Europe and has 
become a major exporter of natural gas and crude oil, 
helping Europe reduce its dependence on Russia.

2025  Consolidated Management Report

1

Repsol Group

--- PAGE 4 ---
The role of hydrocarbons in ensuring supply

Consolidating a strong industrial refining base in 
transition is essential if we are to safeguard Europe’s 
strategic autonomy and ensure highly skilled industrial 
jobs across the continent. In this transformation process, 
it is crucial to acknowledge the role that hydrocarbons 
will continue to play over the coming decades in 
guaranteeing supply and ensuring the functioning of 
society, industry, and transport.

At Repsol, we clearly understand the strategic role of oil 
and natural gas in the global economy. That is why we 
have been investing for years in efficient exploration 
and production of these energy resources. Our activity 
focuses on developing new projects in key geographic 
areas where we have competitive advantages, including 
Spain, Portugal, the United States, Brazil, the United 
Kingdom, and Peru, among others.

Business leadership to secure Europe’s future

A robust industrial base is the foundation for fostering 
sustainable economic growth capable of withstanding 
different economic cycles and contributing to societal 
progress. The European Union must create space for 
genuinely robust, innovative, and forward-looking 
companies to continue investing within its borders.

The roadmap we defined at Repsol years ago is moving 
in this direction. We are busy transforming our industrial 
complexes into multi-energy hubs capable of 
processing a wide range of feedstocks and producing 
low-carbon solutions that are essential for a more 
strategically autonomous Europe.

At our Cartagena industrial complex, we are already 
operating a plant dedicated to the large-scale 
production of 100% renewable fuels. A second facility 
will follow in Puertollano in 2026. Moreover, in 2025 
we took the decision to invest in the Ecoplanta in 
Tarragona, a ground-breaking project in Europe that will 
convert municipal solid waste into renewable methanol 
for transport. These initiatives not only strengthen our 
industrial position; they also place Spain and Europe in 
a strategic position to lead the energy transition and 
become more competitive.

Industry needs far more decisive support from the 
European Union to make meaningful progress in 
projects of this kind under truly competitive conditions. 
This requires regulatory certainty and stable, 
predictable frameworks that allow companies to plan 
long-term investments and facilitate access to the 
necessary financing. Clear and coherent investment 
signals are also needed to align climate objectives
with industrial realities and to reduce the current 
administrative complexity that delays project execution.

The European Union must move toward a common 
energy policy that enables it to compete with the United 
States, along the lines highlighted by former European 
Central Bank President Mario Draghi in his report on the 
competitiveness of the European economy. This means 
strengthening interconnections, investing in strategic 
infrastructure, coordinating energy and industrial 
policies, and not losing sight of the importance of 
defense. Only a joint strategy involving both institutions 
and companies will allow Europe to maintain its stability 
and global influence in the decades ahead.

All of us in Europe need to be more ambitious and aspire 
to build a more cohesive and pragmatic European Union 
— one that is capable of responding more quickly and 
decisively to the challenges posed by the new world 
order. The challenge is immense, but so is the 
opportunity. Europe can shake off the risk of decline to 
achieve a genuine industrial revitalization if it chooses
to act resolutely, ambitiously, and with a long-term vision.

Repsol will continue working to help make this happen.

Thank you all for your trust.

Antonio Brufau Niubó
Chairman

2025  Consolidated Management Report

2

Repsol Group

--- PAGE 5 ---
Message 
from the CEO

We are convinced 
that our strategy 
and path to becoming 
a truly multi-energy 
company will enable 
us to successfully 
navigate all the changes 
going on around us

Dear Shareholders,

The global environment in which companies such as 
Repsol operate has become increasingly uncertain 
lately. Over the past year, the geopolitical world order 
has shifted abruptly, and the priority for most countries 
is now to secure reliable access to the energy and raw 
materials needed to fuel their economies.

While the rapid transformation of our industry creates 
uncertainty around market trends, it also presents 
business opportunities for the companies that adapt 
most quickly to the new rules. In this new phase, the 
ability to anticipate change will be essential to ensuring 
long-term business viability.

To grasp these emerging opportunities, energy 
companies must build sufficient flexibility into their 
operations to respond swiftly to increasingly frequent 
changes. The sector must also focus on further 
diversifying supply chains and reducing critical 
dependencies, with the aim of ensuring clients have a 
reliable supply of all forms of energy..

At Repsol, we are ready to meet the challenges of the 
new global economic order. In recent years, our strategy 
has focused on concentrating our operations in 
countries where we hold competitive advantages, such 
as Spain, Portugal, the United States, Brazil, and the 
United Kingdom. This approach reduces our exposure
to geopolitical volatility and guarantees access to the 
resources we need.

We also happen to operate one of the most efficient 
and competitive refining systems in the European Union, 
comprising five industrial complexes in Spain. These 
facilities ensure the production of fuels used by most 
transport—particularly road transport—and are fully 
capable of adapting output to market developments
and even to potential energy crises.

Another of our strengths is our business model built on 
diversification. For instance, in a matter of years we 
have become a significant player in low-carbon power 
generation, primarily in Spain, with a portfolio that 
includes wind and solar assets, as well as hydroelectric 
and combined cycle plants. These facilities are 
strategically important in providing added stability 
to the Spanish energy system 

The diversification of our activities has enabled us to 
evolve into a multi-energy company capable of 
producing and providing customers with the full range 
of energy they need, whether for mobility, homes, or 
businesses. We are convinced that this new corporate 
profile positions us to capitalize on the opportunities 
emerging from this new phase of the global economy.

Significant progress across all businesses

In such a demanding environment, our company 
achieved significant progress in all business areas 
during the past year, reaching milestones that have 
strengthened our competitiveness and consolidated 
our position as a leading multi-energy company.

At Repsol, we remain committed to responsible 
exploration and production of oil and gas, two energy 
sources that will continue to be necessary for society 
over the coming decades. Our strategy is underpinned 
by active portfolio management, prioritizing operations 
in key markets where we have top-tier assets
and future growth projects. In 2025, average roduction 
stood at 548,000 barrels of oil equivalent per day, 
in line with the strategic plan.

The United States is a key country for our Upstream 
business, where we have invested $21 billion over 
the past 15 years. In 2025, we began oil production 
at Leon-Castile, offshore Louisiana, and in the first half 
of 2026 we will start production at Pikka in Alaska. These 
two projects will allow us to increase energy output
 in a country that is critical to the company’s growth 
moving forward.

In 2025, we strengthened Repsol’s position in the 
United Kingdom by integrating our North Sea assets 
with NEO Energy Group and subsequently reaching 
an agreement to merge them with those of 
TotalEnergies. The resulting joint company will be called 
NEO NEXT+ and will become the largest oil and gas 
producer in the region, with expected production of 
250,000 barrels of oil equivalent per day this year.

2025  Consolidated Management Report

3

Repsol Group

--- PAGE 6 ---
The international context has not slowed the 
transformation of our industrial complexes, which we are 
converting into multi-energy hubs capable of processing 
different types of feedstock to manufacture low-carbon 
products, such as 100% renewable fuels. These fuels can 
be used without modifying internal combustion engines 
and will be key to reducing CO2 emissions from transport.

Repsol currently leads the production of 100% 
renewable diesel in Spain, thanks to the plant 
inaugurated in Cartagena in 2024, which will be joined 
this year by a second plant in Puertollano with a 
capacity to produce 200,000 tons annually. Moreover, 
our Tarragona complex achieved a technological 
milestone by producing 100% renewable gasoline 
at industrial scale for the first time, a clear example
of the company’s innovation capabilities. 

We have also approved ground-breaking projects that 
confirm our commitment to industrial development, such 
as the construction of the Tarragona Ecoplant, the first 
facility of its kind in Europe to convert urban waste into 
renewable methanol. We have further announced the 
construction of our first two large scale electrolyzers 
in Cartagena and at Petronor, which will have a capacity 
of 100 MW each and be able to produce 15,000 tons
of renewable hydrogen every year for use as feedstock
at both industrial centers.

In the Customer business, we continue to expand 
our multi-energy offering and strengthen key business 
attributes, such as improving customer experience and 
product quality. A key highlight in the period was the 
rollout of Nexa Diesel of 100% renewable origin—our 
premium-grade diesel—which is now available at more 
than 1,500 service stations across Spain and Portugal, 
making ours one of the largest 100% renewable fuel 
distribution networks in Europe.

In 2025, our electricity and gas retail business continued 
to grow, reaching three million customers. This allowed 
us to increase the volumes sold to 8,568 GWh of 
electricity and 2,239 GWh of gas. We now also have 
10.8 million digital customers, most of them using 
Waylet, our loyalty and payment app, which is helping 
to drive the company’s digital strategy.

In renewable energy, our business model is based 
on developing projects from their earliest stages 
and subsequently bringing in strategic partners 
to secure higher returns. In 2025, two examples 
of this asset rotation strategy were the entry 
of Schroders Greencoat into a 400 MW portfolio 
of wind and solar assets in Spain, and Stonepeak 
into several solar parks in the United States.

Our renewable generation capacity increased by more 
than 2,000 MW, reaching a total of 5,813 MW, mainly 
due to the commissioning of two large-scale solar 
facilities in the United States: Outpost and, partially, 
Pinnington, which is set to become our largest 
photovoltaic plant. The start of commercial operations 
at these projects, along with others in Spain, was key 
to increasing our wind and solar output by 34%.

Technology and digitalization play a central role in 
the management of our businesses and have become 
cross-cutting pillars of Repsol’s strategy. In recent years, 
we have deployed artificial intelligence across all areas, 
from optimizing well drilling times to personalizing 
commercial offers and managing legal matters.

We are transforming our businesses on the back 
of technology and digitalization, but above all through 
the capability, dedication, and talent of the more 
than 24,000 professionals who work at this company—
a diverse and multicultural team whose commitment
is enabling us to deliver on our projects and make 
progress toward the objectives set out in our 2024–2027 
strategic plan. 

Improved shareholder remuneration

In 2025, Repsol once again increased its shareholder 
remuneration, one of the most attractive in the market. 
During the year, dividends totaling €0.975 per share were 
paid out, representing an 8.3% increase over the previous 
year. Moreover, share capital was reduced through the 
cancellation of 52 million treasury shares, improving 
shareholder returns by increasing earnings per share.

Last year we achieved results that allow us to continue 
pursuing our strategy and maintaining our remuneration 
commitments. Net income amounted to €1.899 billion, 
up 8% on the previous year, despite operating in a 
context marked by geopolitical uncertainty, market 
volatility, and declining oil prices.

All of these figures confirm the soundness of the 
strategy we have designed for the 2024–2027 period.
In March, we will present an update of our projections, 
enabling us to adapt to developments in the 
macroeconomic, regulatory, and business environment 
of the energy sector. This update will include minor 
adjustments to business strategy guidelines, without 
altering the core principles of our plan: competitive 
shareholder remuneration, financial strength, and 
disciplined investment.

At Repsol, we look to the future with resolve, convinced 
that our strategy and our path to becoming a truly 
multi-energy company will enable us to successfully 
navigate all the changes going on around us. In 2026, 
we will continue to strive to become more competitive 
and, above all, to capitalize on the opportunities
in the energy sector.

To all our shareholders, I extend my deepest 
appreciation and gratitude

Josu Jon Imaz 
Chief Executive Officer

2025  Consolidated Management Report

Repsol Group

Appendices

1. 2025 overview

The economic environment during 2025 was marked by 
persistent market volatility, geopolitical uncertainty and 
concerns about the oversupply of oil.

The energy markets witnessed a decline in crude oil 
prices (Brent averaged $69/bbl, down 14% versus the 
2024 price) and higher gas prices (the average Henry 
Hub price was up 51% compared with 2024 to reach 
$3.4/MBtu) and electricity prices in Spain (up 4% to €66/
MWh). 

For more information, see section 3. Environment.

Results and financial 
position5 

Despite the prevailing volatility and uncertainty within 
the energy markets, Repsol’s 2025 results improved on 
those of the previous year by 8%, which included 
negative impacts arising from value adjustments at 
Upstream assets and the Temporary Energy Levy in 
Spain.

Adjusted income, which measures the ordinary 

management of the businesses, amounted to €2,568 

million, down 15% on 2024. 

At Upstream, earnings (€957 million) were down 7% as a 
result of falling crude oil prices, divestments and 
earnings at investees (Brazil, etc.), mitigated by the rise 
in gas realization prices and lower depreciation.

At Industrial, earnings totaled €963 million, 33% down 
on the previous year, impacted by the poorer 
performance of Refining in Spain, Chemicals and the 
crude oil and gas trading businesses.

At Customer (€754 million), earnings were up 17%, 
driven by Mobility (higher sales of gasoline and diesel, 
impacted in 2024 by fraud in the Spanish market), 
Aviation (higher margins and volumes) and LPG (higher 
margins from bottled gas).

Improved net income and shareholder 
return amid high uncertainty and 
volatility in the energy markets

• Results impacted by the worse performance of 

industrial businesses.

• Earnings at Customer continue to improve, 

thanks to its multi-energy offer.

• Financial strength and high liquidity.

• Increasing shareholder return.

Last but not least, Low Carbon Generation (LCG) saw an 
improvement in net income (€53 million) on the back of 
higher captured prices and increased production (due to 
the contribution of both new renewable projects and 
combined cycles).

The inventory effect was very unfavorable (-633 
million), as was the case in 2024, due to the decline in 
the prices of crude oil and products in the period.

Special items (-36 million) primarily include provisions 

for credit risk in Venezuela and expenses recognized in 

connection with the agreement to end the dispute with 

Hecate Holdings LLC, along with reversal of impairment 

at Upstream and provisions for other risks and expenses.

In short, net income, which reflects the profit to which 
the shareholders of Repsol, S.A. are entitled, stood at 
€1,899 million, up 8% on 2024. This figure includes a tax 
expense of €1,983 million (effective tax rate of 50%), 
with a total of €13,265 million in taxes paid in 2025, of 
which €9,640 in Spain. 

Operating cash flow amounted to €5,365 million in 

2025, up €400 million on the 2024 figure, at year in 

which €986 million was paid under the agreement to 

settle the arbitration proceedings with Sinopec in the 

UK (Upstream). Significant investments (albeit 33% 

lower than in the previous year) and divestments, mainly 

in non-strategic Upstream assets, contributed to free 

cash flow of €2,208 million in 2025 (-391 million in 

2024). After taking into account net collections from 

non-controlling interests and other items, cash 

generated by the businesses amounted to €2,939 

million (€-188 million in 2024).

Net debt stood at €4,487 million, up €472 million on 

2024, resulting in a leverage ratio of 14.0% (€1,616 

million excluding leases; leverage ratio of 5.5%). The 

company’s high liquidity (€10,271 million) and robust 

financial position were recognized by the rating 

agencies, which  maintained Repsol's investment grade 

status.

The shareholder return in 2025 totaled €0.975 gross  
per share, materialized through the payment of two 
cash dividends, up 8.3% on 2024. Moreover, share 
capital was reduced through the retirement of 52 
million treasury shares. 	

5 In the fourth quarter of 2025, the Group revised the financial and performance measures of its business segments that are reviewed internally for 

decision-making purposes. Specifically, the results and financial metrics of joint ventures are now integrated using the equity method in the 
segment financial performance presentation model, and the segment profit measure has been revised to reflect profit attributable to the parent 
company (adjusted net income). In addition, a new metric has been defined to monitor segment cash performance (cash generated by the 
businesses). The comparative segment information reported has been restated to allow for appropriate comparability between periods. For 
further information, see Note 4, “Business segments”, to the consolidated financial statements.

Pursuing transformation                                                                   
and a profitable energy transition

• The industrial transformation continues with the 

decision to invest in the Ecoplanta in Tarragona and in 
the first large-scale electrolyzers in Cartagena and 
Bilbao, together with the progress made in 
constructing the 100% renewable fuel production 
plant in Puertollano.

• New partners at Upstream (new joint venture in the 
United Kingdom) and renewables (asset rotations in 
Spain and the United States).

• Development of the multi-energy offer (3.0 million 

electricity and gas customers and more than 1,500 service 
stations supplying fuels of 100% renewable origin across 
the Iberian Peninsula) supported by digitalization (more 
than 10 million digital customers, mostly through the 
Waylet app).

• Commissioning of new LCG wind and solar projects in 

Spain, the United States, and Chile, reaching 5,120 MW of 
wind and solar capacity in operation.

Performance and transformation

Repsol has continued to drive the transformation of its 
businesses, focusing on a profitable energy transition.

At Upstream, the company continued to deploy the 
strategic lines through active portfolio management, 
prioritizing those areas with the greatest strategic fit in 
terms of value and competitive advantages, such as the 
integration of assets in the North Sea in the United 
Kingdom alongside NEO Energy Group and the 
agreement to merge them shortly with those of 
TotalEnergies UK, or divestments in certain countries 
(Colombia and Indonesia) and in non-strategic assets. 
Production (548 Kboe/d) was 4% lower than in 2024, 
largely due to divestments, partially mitigated by the 
alliance with NEO Energy (see section 5.1) and the 
absence of force majeure outages in Libya. 

At Industrial, comprising the company’s industrial 
complexes in Spain, the use of facilities was affected by 
various stoppages and incidents, including those caused 
by the electricity blackout that occurred on April 28. 
Meanwhile, the Chemical businesses had to adapt their 
production, logistical, and commercial schemes to 
respond to an environment of weak demand and low 
product prices.

Repsol pressed ahead with the transformation of large 
industrial complexes during the period, focusing on to 
the future sustainability of the industry by approving the 
investment at the Ecoplanta in Tarragona, which will 
become the first plant in Europe to manufacture 
renewable and circular methanol from waste, and two 
large-scale electrolyzers (100 MW each), one in 
Cartagena and the other at the Petronor refinery. The 
company is also moving forward with the construction of 
its second renewable fuels plant in Puertollano, as well as 
the synthetic fuels plant (demo e-fuels) and the 10 MW 
electrolyzer at the Port of Bilbao. Moreover, a major 
technological milestone was achieved with the first 
industrial-scale production of 100% renewable gasoline 
(Nexa 95 gasoline) in Tarragona.

Further highlights included the acquisition of 40% of 
three industrial complexes through our stake in Bunge 
Ibérica, thus allowing Repsol to access a broad portfolio 
of low-carbon intensity raw materials to produce 
renewable fuels.

At Customer, notable efforts were made in the period to 
develop Repsol’s multi-energy offer, focusing on the 
customer and supported by digitalization. Repsol 
managed to increase the number of electricity and gas 
customers (3.0 million of customers, up 21% on the 
previous year) and digital customers (more than 10 
million), most of whom now use the Waylet app. At the 
end of the year, Repsol already had upward of 1,500 
service stations supplying 100% renewable fuel across 
Spain and Portugal, under the Diesel Nexa brand and 
100% renewable Nexa gasoline. Moreover, the number 
of public charging points up and running increased to 
more than 3,600.

At Low Carbon Generation, a total installed capacity in 
operation of 8,030 MW (5,120 MW in renewable power) 
was reached, with the start-up of new projects in Spain 
(wind: Delta 2, Pi and Cerrato; and solar: Trillo), the United 
States (solar: Outpost and Pinnington), and Chile (wind: 
Antofagasta), all of which helped to increase the 
generation of renewable wind and solar electricity by 
43%. Further progress was also made in developing 
operations in the United States, with the start of 
construction of Pecan Prairie (a 595 MW solar plant in 
Texas). Other highlights included the fifth asset rotation 
in Spain (sale of 49% of a 400 MW wind and solar 
portfolio) and the first rotation in the United States (with 
the incorporation of a partner in a 777 MW solar and 
storage portfolio and the agreement to incorporate it in 
another 629 MW solar plant), which also included 
financing obtained through the monetization of tax 
credits (“tax equity”).

	For more information, see section 5. Business 
performance. 

Sustainability

Repsol remains aligned with the global objectives of 
limiting warming to 1.5°C by the end of the century and 
achieving net-zero greenhouse gas emissions by 2050. As 
part of its strategy to manage climate-related risks and 
opportunities, the Company has established targets to 
reduce absolute greenhouse gas emissions across Scopes 
1, 2, and 3: a 20% reduction by 2030 compared with the 
2018 base year (224 Mt CO₂e) and the objective of net-zero 
greenhouse gas emissions by 2050. In 2025, Repsol’s 
emissions (Scopes 1, 2, and 3) amounted to 184.7 Mt CO₂e, 
representing a reduction of 17.4% compared with the base 
year.
Furthermore, Repsol has defined a Carbon Intensity 
Indicator (CII), measured as emissions per unit of primary 
energy, with a target of greenhouse gas emissions 
neutrality by 2050. In 2025, this indicator reached its 
interim target, with a reduction of 15.1% compared with 
2016.

Also in 2025, Repsol defined new Company-wide targets 
related to air pollution and biodiversity.

For more information, see section 6. “Sustainability”.

Brand refresh: “With all the energy”

En 20In 2025, Repsol launched its revamped brand to adapt it 
to the Company’s strategy; a strategy which, through its 
businesses, responds to the energy needs of society, with a 
focus on all energies to be able to offer solutions that best suit 
each person.

Update outlook for 26-28

On March 10, 2026, Repsol will present a market update of its 
forecasts for the 2026–2028 period, with the aim achieving of 
the objectives set, without losing sight of its current targets 
and orientation.

2. Our company

2.1 Business model 

Exploration

After acquiring new acreage, 
we carry out geological and 
geophysical work, conduct 
environmental impact 
studies, and perform 
exploratory drilling to 
evaluate its potential, using 
cutting-edge digital 
technologies to analyze the 
information.

Retail and 
wholesale 
supply of gas  

We market and trade 
natural gas in North 
America, where we also 
have an LNG 
regasification plant in 
Canada. We also market 
and trade LNG globally 
and natural gas to 
wholesale customers in 
Spain and Portugal.

Refining

We transform crude oil and 
various alternative raw 
materials into value-added 
products such as fuels, 
advanced biofuels and carbon-
neutral materials. Our priorities 
also include the production of 
renewable hydrogen, which will 
be key to decarbonizing 
various sectors.

Development

We drill wells, build 
collection systems and 
processing plants and 
evacuation and transport 
systems, always under 
sustainability, safety and 
transparency policies that 
ensure the proper 
development of the project.

Production

We extract hydrocarbons 
from the reservoir to 
market and sell them. In 
addition, we carry out 
maintenance, control and 
transport work leveraged 
on artificial intelligence 
technologies and following 
the same sustainability and 
safety policies as in 
previous phases.

Trading

We transport the 
hydrocarbons produced to 
supply raw material to our 
refineries or we sell them on 
international markets. We 
also export the surplus 
production of our industrial 
complexes that are not 
consumed in the domestic 
market.

Lubricants, 
Asphalts, Aviation 
and Specialized 
products

We develop, produce and market 
lubricants, aviation fuels, asphalt 
bitumen and petroleum-based 
specialties in more than 90 
countries worldwide.

Our businesses

Repsol is a global energy group that, with the purpose of exploring and discovering the infinite possibilities of energy, 
It carries out activities in the hydrocarbons sector along its entire value chain (exploration, development and 
production of crude oil and natural gas, refining, production, transport and marketing of a wide range of petroleum 
products, petrochemicals and derivatives, natural gas and biofuels), as well as electricity generation and marketing 
activities:

• Upstream: exploration and production of crude oil 

• Low-Carbon Generation (LCG): generation of low-

and natural gas reserves.

• Customer: mobility (service stations) and marketing 
of fuels (gasoline, diesel, aviation kerosene, liquefied 
petroleum gases, biofuels, etc.), electricity and gas 
and lubricants, asphalts and other specialties.

•

emissions electricity (combined cycle natural gas and 
pumped storage) and renewable sources.

Industrial: oil refining, petrochemicals and trading, 
transportation and marketing of crude oil, natural gas 
and fuels, including the development of new growth 
platforms such as hydrogen, sustainable biofuels and 
synthetic fuels.

For more information on business segments, see section 5 Performance of our businesses.

Chemicals

We produce and market a wide 
variety of petrochemical products, 
which are used to manufacture 
everyday objects that improve 
people’s quality of life, well-being 
and safety and are found in almost 
all industrial segments.

LPG

We distribute and market 
liquefied petroleum gas in 
Spain, Portugal and 
France. We do this in 
various formats: Bottled, 
bulk and AutoGas.

Low-emission 
electricity 
generation

Repsol has low-emission 
electricity generation projects 
(hydroelectric plants, 
combined cycles, 
cogeneration plants, wind 
and photovoltaic farms). It 
builds new renewable assets 
to increase their capacity.

Mobility

We have the largest network of service stations in Spain, 
where we offer customers multi-energy solutions linked 
to the Waylet app. We are pioneers when it comes to 
introducing renewable fuels and we will soon be offering 
renewable hydrogen for mobility. We have one of the 
most important networks of electric charging points in 
Spain and we are a leader in AutoGas.

Retail supply of 
electricity and gas

We supply low-emission 
gas and electricity in 
Spain and Portugal by 
offering our customers 
cutting-edge digital 
solutions and energy 
management initiatives 
such as self-consumption 
and distributed 
generation.

applicable legal and tax framework, and avoids the use 
of artificial and opaque structures.

2.4 Corporate governance

Repsol’s corporate governance system guides the 
structure, organization, and operation of its corporate 
bodies in the best interests of the company and its 
shareholders. It is based on the principles of transparency, 
independence, and accountability and respects prevailing 
national and international standards. 

The governance structure adequately differentiates 
governance and management functions from those of 
supervision, control and strategic definition.

There are no controlling or significant shareholders 
represented on the Board. Repsol’s shareholder structure is 
shown below.7 

For more information on corporate governance, see the 
Annual Corporate Governance Report (Appendix V).

6 It is considered that Repsol has a presence in the country of its domicile and in all other countries where it has registered branches or permanent  

establishments. 

7 For example, in shareholder engagement, 33% of investors engage with us on ESG-related topics.

Board remuneration

Directors receive fixed remuneration for fulfilling their supervisory and decision-making duties. Aside from the 
remuneration payable to the Chairman of the Board of Directors, remuneration is calculated by assigning points 
for seats held on the Board or its various committees, or for holding specific positions on those bodies. Each 
point has a remuneration equivalence, meaning there is no difference in remuneration by gender. 

Further information on the application of the Directors’ Remuneration Policy can be found in the Annual Report 
on Directors’ Remuneration, included in Appendix VI and available at www.repsol.com.

For further information on the remuneration of the Board and Senior Management, see Note 29 to the 2025 
consolidated Financial Statements.

3.1 Macroeconomic 
environment

Recent economic trends

Global activity remained relatively stable in 2025, 
following a prolonged and challenging period marked 
by several significant shocks.

According to International Monetary Fund (WEO, 
October 2025), global economic growth was around 
3.2% in 2025 (3.3% in 2024), with global output close to 
the potential level, that is, sustainably achievable 
without generating serious imbalances. More 
importantly, the  disinflation process continued, albeit 
more gradually, with global inflation projected to be 
around 4.2% in 2025.

Since early 2025, the global environment has become 
substantially more uncertain as a result of the shift in 
trade and geopolitical policy promoted by the U.S. 
administration. On April 2, the U.S. government imposed 
new tariffs on a broad group of countries, including the 
European Union and China, setting a universal minimum 
tariff of 10% and additional tariffs for partners with 
larger bilateral surpluses. Although the initial market 
reaction was adverse, it was subsequently tempered by 
the exemptions announced and the efforts made by 
most countries to negotiate bilateral agreements to 
avoid an escalation of trade protectionism. The only 
exception was China, which has taken a more aggressive 
stance by imposing restrictions on the export of rare 
earths. In recent months, both countries have reached a 
partial truce that reduces part of the tariffs and puts 
back certain restrictions for a year, although without 
resolving the underlying structural tensions.

The level of protectionism remains the highest since the 
mid-20th century. In this context, the impact on 
inflation is manifested asymmetrically. In the United 
States, tariffs are already exerting upward pressure on 
goods prices, partially offset by the fall in energy and 
services, while in Europe and other regions the 
appreciation of the euro and lower external demand are 
generating disinflationary effects. 

Trend in interest rates

Source: Bloomberg and Repsol's Research Department.

In this context, a divergence in monetary trajectories 
emerged between the European Central Bank (ECB) and 
the U.S. Federal Reserve (Fed), although this has become 
less noticeable in recent months. The ECB began a cycle 
of cuts in June 2024, bringing its deposit rate close to 
2.0% by mid-2025. The Fed, on the other hand, remained 
more cautious (given the relative buoyancy still shown by 
the US economy and the persistence of inflationary risks 
linked to tariffs) and put back its rate cuts until 
September 2025, from which time, and until the end of 
the year, it implemented three cuts, leaving the federal 
funds rate in the 3.50–3.75% range. This change in tone is 
explained by a slowdown in the labor market that is 
beginning to outweigh the risk of inflation.

As for the exchange rate, and following a 2024 
dominated by the strength of the dollar, aided by the 
rate differential and the greater relative buoyancy of the 
U.S. economy, we witnessed a gradual depreciation of 
the U.S. currency in the early stages of 2025. This trend 
reflected the change in tone of the Fed's monetary 
policy, the lower attractiveness of dollar assets and a 
certain perception of legal and macroeconomic 
uncertainty. The depreciation was especially visible 
against the euro, which has acted as a strong reserve 
currency. However, after this initial phase of appreciation, 
the movement has stabilized, and in recent months the 
euro/dollar exchange rate has remained around  1.16–
1.17. More precisely, at the end of December 2025, the 
euro/dollar exchange rate stood at 1.18.

2025  Consolidated Management Report

EUR/USD exchange rate performance (monthly 
averages)

In this context, towards the end of 2025, global 
geopolitical tensions eased, which further contributed to 
the moderation of crude oil prices. However, uncertainty 
remains, especially in relation to developments in major 
producing countries such as Russia, Iran, and Venezuela, 
where further developments could reintroduce volatility 
into the market.

Brent price performance ($/bbl)

Source: Bloomberg and Repsol Research Unit.

For more information, see section 7.1 Outlook for the 
sector.

3.2 Energy landscape

Crude oil – Brent

In 2025, the average price of Brent stood at $69.1/bl, 
after a year marked by high volatility that led crude oil 
to move in a range of more than $20. The year started 
with strong upticks, peaking at $82.03/bl on January 15 
due to low temperatures in both the United States and 
Europe, which boosted demand for diesel. 
Subsequently, the market experienced a drop to near 
$60/bl on May 1, reflecting a broader context of lower 
economic strength and trade tensions. The trend in the 
price was largely determined by geopolitical 
uncertainty, with alternating episodes of increases and 
corrections, closing the year at around $61/bbl, as 
geopolitical uncertainty eased.

On the demand side, the initial impact of trade tensions 
between various economies moderated as the year 
progressed. The IMF's improved growth projections for 
2025 and 2026 offered a more favorable environment for 
global oil consumption. However, significant doubts 
persisted over Chinese demand, driven by structural 
factors such as prolonged weakness in the real estate 
sector and the transition to cleaner energy, especially 
LNG and electric vehicles. As a result, despite a somewhat 
better macroeconomic climate than that observed after 
the April tariff announcement, the pace of demand 
growth showed signs of slowing compared to 2024.

The factor that exerted the greatest downward pressure 
on prices was supply, especially due to OPEC+'s 
production strategy. Between April and September, the 
group reintroduced the 2.2 million barrels a day it had 
cut in November 2023 to the market, accelerating a 
process that had originally been planned for an 18-
month horizon. Moreover, the gradual reversal of the 
1.65 million barrels a day withdrawn in April 2023 got 
underway in October 2025. These movements 
unbalanced the market and weakened the fundamentals 
supporting prices, causing its potential for recovery to 
be limited. OPEC+’s announcement to suspend barrel 
returns during the first quarter of 2026 reflects its 
intention to halt a further decline in a period 
characterized by lower seasonal demand.

Source: Bloomberg and Repsol Research Unit.

Natural Gas - Henry Hub (HH)

In 2025, the price of U.S. HH natural gas averaged $3.4 
MBtu, above the 2024 average ($2.3/MBtu). During 
January and February of 2025, the intense cold caused 
production freeze-offs and high heating demand, which 
increased the price during the first quarter and 
eliminated the surplus of gas in U.S. storage compared 
with the average for the last five years, which had kept 
prices moderate. 

In the second and third quarters, the existing deficit in 
storage was reduced, bringing it above the average for 
the last five years. Production remained at historically 
high values of 109.1 bcf/d in August and is expected to 
remain close to these levels for the rest of the year, 
driven mainly by demand from exports, due to the 
increase in LNG feed gas (new LNG export facilities and 
additional trains at existing terminals that have come 
online) and increased pipeline exports to Mexico. 
Additionally, HH prices increased in the fourth quarter 
due to higher winter demand for heating in the United 
States.

Henry Hub price performance ($/MBtu)

Source: Bloomberg and Repsol Research Unit.

Electricity prices

In 2025, the average wholesale price of electricity in 
Spain stood at €65.5/MWh, slightly higher than in 2024 
(62.9 €/MWh). Despite the significant change brought 
about by the increase in self-consumption and new 
photovoltaic generation facilities, the system remains in 
a context of growth in demand and an increase in gas 
prices compared with 2024. This, together with lower 
hydroelectric generation, despite the maximum levels in 
hydroelectric dams due to the rains in the first half of 
the year, led to an increase in prices compared with 
2024.

Although the annual average has hardly changed, there 
is significant volatility in hourly prices. While last year 
(the first in which negative prices were recorded), these 
only reached -2 €/MWh, this year the minimum has 
reached -15 €/MWh (largely as a result of the start of 
operations in “enhanced mode” following the blackout 
that affected the entire Iberian Peninsula and the south 
of France). Maximum prices have also increased, 
reaching 240 €/MWh, a figure that had only been 
reached in 2021 and 2022, in the midst of the gas crisis. 

The trend of steadily declining prices in the central 
hours of the day continues, causing the price captured 
by solar installations to be approximately half of the 
average daily price (just five years ago it was higher than 
the daily price). In addition, in 2025 there were almost as 
many hours with prices close to zero as there had been 
in 2024, although they were spread out more evenly 
during the year. In total, they amounted to more than 
1,500 hours (the years with the most hours at low prices
—2014 and 2023—they barely reached 500 hours). All 
of this has been causing difficulties among the 
promoters of photovoltaic installations, especially those 
who sell their production to the market and those who 
are considering the construction of new plants. 
Conversely, it is conducive to energy storage projects, 
either at pumping stations or with batteries, given their 
greater need and profitability.

On the demand side, demand increased by almost 3% 
compared with 2024 (this figure is substantially lower 
after adjusting for the effects of temperature and 
working-day effects). This growth, the second 
consecutive increase, appears to be related to the 
demographic and economic growth of the country. 
Notably, the preliminary (provisional) data indicate that, 
despite the fact that the blackout in April may have 
sparked greater interest in the installation of residential 
self-consumption systems (with and without batteries), 
this was not able to offset the lower level of 
installations in the industrial sector, causing a decline in 
new self-consumption installations for the third year 
running.

The generation mix was once again characterized by new 
highs in photovoltaic generation, driven by increased 
installed capacity, although the greatest growth was 
seen in gas-fired generation. Since the blackout, the 
operation of the system has prioritized stability of 
supply, which led to greater use of combined cycles 
(increasing almost 30% compared with 2024), reaching 
levels similar to those recorded in 2020, when the price 
of gas was less than half of what it reached in 2025.

OMIE electricity pool – price performance (€/
MWh)

Source: Bloomberg and Repsol Research Unit.

CO2 emission allowances

The average price of CO2 emission allowances in 2025  
increased to almost 74.9 €/t, up 10 €/t on the average 
for 2024. During the first part of the year, the market 
continued to trade in parallel to European gas. However, 
in the second quarter, while the price of gas remained 
stable, CO2 prices rose from 65 €/t to over 80 €/t, even 
with lower demand for emission allowances.

This loss of correlation is due to the expectation that in 
2026 there will be a deficit of allowances for various 
reasons, including the entry into force of the Carbon 
Border Adjustment Mechanism (CBAM); the possible 
tightening of the Market Stability Reserve (MSR) and 
delays in the cancellation of surplus transport rights. In 
addition, this tension in the market is happening in a 
context of the gradual phase-out of free allocations, 
which is constraining supply in the medium term.

Trend in CO2 prices (€/t)

Source: Bloomberg and Repsol Research Unit.

The results for 2025 were achieved amid high volatility 
within the energy markets, with lower crude oil prices 
and higher gas and electricity prices, depretiation of the 
dollar, coupled with growing geopolitical uncertainty. 

Adjusted net income, which measures the ordinary 

management of the businesses, amounted to €2,568

(455) 

. Trend in adjusted income at Upstream

Adjusted income for the segment stood at €957 million 

in the year, down €71 million on 2024, for the following 

reasons:

•

Increase in gas realization prices (+34%), with a 
significant impact on the results obtained at 
Marcellus. Conversely, lower realized crude oil prices 
(-14%) negatively affected the results achieved in 
Libya, the Gulf of Mexico (GoM), and Norway.

• Higher sales volumes, driven in particular by a 

significant increase in Libya and GoM, partially offset 
by lower volumes in Eagle Ford, Norway, Algeria, Peru, 
and Marcellus.

• Higher taxes on production and hydrocarbon 

royalties. Mainly in Alaska, Eagle Ford, and Libya.

• Lower amortization due to the impairment recognized 

in December 2024 and lower production.

• Higher production costs, mainly due to First Oil in the 

GoM.

• Other; chiefly the divestment of all productive assets 
in Indonesia and Colombia and the results of UK until 
its incorporation to Neo Next  (July 2025).

• Higher income tax (effective rate of -51%), in line with 

higher operating results.

• Lower results from equity-accounted companies 
(associates), due to weaker performance in Brazil 
(lower prices and lower production) and Venezuela 
(higher production costs, lower prices, and higher 
taxes, despite the increase in production), partially 
offset by improved performance in the United 
Kingdom (associate since July 2025) and in Trinidad 
and Tobago (higher gas prices and lower production 
costs)

(1) These figures do not include information on investments accounted for using the equity method (nor those relating to the United Kingdom, a 
controlled entity until July 2025), nor those relating to countries in which Repsol has divested all its assets (Indonesia and Colombia).

8  In  the  fourth  quarter  of  2025,  the  Group  revised  the  financial  and  performance  measures  reviewed  internally  for  decision-making  purposes. 
Specifically, the results and financial metrics of joint ventures are now incorporated using the equity method in the segment financial performance 
presentation model, and the segment profit measure has been revised to reflect profit attributable to the parent company (adjusted net income); 
in  addition,  a  new  metric  is  defined  to  monitor  segment  cash  flows  (cash  generated  by  the  businesses).  Comparative  segment  information  has 
been restated to allow for appropriate comparability between periods. For further information, Note 4. "Business segments" of the consolidated 
Financial Statements.

(11) 

Adjusted income amounted to €963 million in 2025, 
compared with €1,447 million in 2024.

Adjusted income in 2025 amounted to €754 million, 
compared with €644 million in 2024. The change is 
mainly due to:

• At Mobility, results in Spain improved following an 
increase in volumes sold of automotive diesel and 
gasoline (lower in 2024 as a result of market fraud). 
Meanwhile, supply procurement costs fell in Spain and 
Portugal.

• Results were up at Lubricants, Aviation, Asphalts and 

Specialized Products, driven by improved margins and 
also by higher volumes at Aviation and Lubricants.

•

Improved results at LPG, mainly due to better margins 
(predominantly in relation to bottled gas).

• Earnings at Electricity & Gas sales were up, due to 

higher sales arising in turn from an increase in 
customers, who reached 3.0 million (including Spain 
and Portugal).

For more information on the activities of the segment, 
see section 5.3 Customer.

The main reasons for this change are as follows:

• The Refining business in Spain underperformed 

compared with 2024. Although margin indicators 
were, on average, higher than those in 2024 (7.9 $/bbl 
compared with 6.6 $/bbl in 2024), the results were 
lower as a result of lower distillation and conversion 
utilization ratios (impacted by an increase in 
maintenance shutdowns and incidents, such as the 
presence of water in the Maya crude processed or the 
power outages that occurred in the second quarter—
including the one on April 28—), higher costs 
(maintenance, personnel, depreciation, etc.), and the 
effect of the depreciation of the dollar.

• At Repsol Peru, results increased compared with the 
previous year, due to better refining margins, and 
higher earnings at service stations.

• At Chemicals, weak demand and strong international 

competition persisted, leading the European business 
to incur losses, despite efforts to optimize operations 
and costs. The activity of the plants was also impacted 
by the electricity blackout that took place on April 28.

• At the Trading businesses, although a good level of 

results was maintained, they fared worse than in 2024 
due to a lower contribution from the gas businesses in 
the United States (where margins captured in the 
winter season of 2024 were extraordinarily high), and 
also the interruption since March of operations with 
Venezuela.

For further information on the segment's activities, see 
section 5.2 Industrial.

Adjusted income in 2025 amounted to €53 million, 
compared with -€24 million in 2024.

• At Renewables, earnings were higher than in 2024, 
mainly due to higher captured prices and increased 
production (new projects commissioned in 2025 that 
increased operating capacity by 73% vs. December 31, 
2024), as well as the application of tax incentives in 
the United States. These impacts were partially 
mitigated by higher fixed costs.

• At Combined Cycle Plants, results were up due to

Iberian Peninsula on April 28) and prices, partially 
offset by higher costs (gas prices and depreciation).

Earnings amounted to €-159 million euros in 2025 
(compared with €-70 million in 2024). 

The segment has continued to streamline corporate 
costs, despite the efforts also being made to promote 
and implement digital initiatives.

The financial result improved compared with the 
previous year due to the better result of treasury shares 
positions and lower updating of provisions, albeit 
partially offset by higher net interest (lower investments 
income).

Consolidation adjustments (which eliminate earnings 
from operations between business segments not been 
made with third parties) amounted to €-54 million.

Net income

The adjusted income figure must be adjusted to reflect the 
following effects:

• The inventory effect, which reflects the impact on 

inventories (mainly of the Industrial and, to a lesser 
extent, Customer businesses) of the downward trend 
in crude oil and product prices during the period.

•

 Special items in 2025, net of taxes and non-
controlling interests, amounted to €-36 million. They 
primarily include workforce restructuring costs, the 
posting of provisions for credit risk in Venezuela, the 
environmental expenses incurred to settle the dispute 
with Hecate Holdings LLC in connection with the 
investment in Hecate Energy Group LLC, and the 
reversals of asset impairment at the Upstream 
business, mainly in the United States, and provisions 
for other risks and expenses.

Adjusted EBITDA amounted to €5,312 million, down 
12% on the previous year. The largest fall was seen at 
Industrial, due to lower earnings captured from refining 
and the underperformance of the trading businesses, and 
also at Upstream, due to the divestments made 
(Indonesia and Colombia) and the loss of control in the 
United Kingdom. The Customer and LCG businesses 
increased their EBITDA in line with the improvement in 
their earnings.

Financial position

• At Customer, investments amounted to €391 million 
euros, down 3% on the 2024 figure. The investments 
were mainly aimed at the growth in Spain of the 
Electricity and Gas marketing businesses (customer 
acquisition and inorganic growth with the acquisition of 
70% of On Demand Facilities -ODF Energía-) and the 
growth of the international Lubricants businesses, with 
the constitution of joint ventures in the Philippines and 
Romania.

• At LCG, investments in 2025 amounted to €987 million 
(-60% vs. 2024), due to lower inorganic investments. 
They were mainly used to bring new energy projects 
into production in the United States (Pinnington, 
Connect Gen and Outpost solar parks) and in Spain 
(Delta II, Pi and Trillo).

Capital employed at the end of the year amounted to 
€32,105 million, down 3%, mainly due to asset 
depreciation and also the depreciation of the dollar 
against the euro, despite the investments made.

Despite the lower results achieved in 2025, Repsol 
pressed ahead with various measures within the 
framework of its financial prudence policy, which have 
helped to maintain a high degree of liquidity. Liquid 
assets at the end of the year (in the form of cash and 
available credit facilities) were sufficient to cover debt 
maturities until the second quarter of 2034, without the 
need for refinancing.

Main financing transactions

On March 25, Repsol International Finance B.V. (RIF) 
redeemed the remaining balance of the issuance of the 
subordinated bonds placed in March 2015 for a nominal 
amount of €726 million. 

On June 26, REF issued subordinated perpetual bonds 
(guaranteed by Repsol, S.A.), admitted to trading on the 
Luxembourg Stock Exchange, for a total amount of 
€750 million. The bonds pay a coupon of 4.50% until 
June 26, 2031, the date on which Repsol's first option to 
redeem them expires.

On June 17, RIF launched a repurchase offer on the 
subordinated perpetual bonds issued in 2020 with a 
coupon of 3.75% and a nominal value of €750 million. 

The buyback offer was settled on June 27, had an 
81.13% uptake, and led to a disbursement of €614 
million (repurchased nominal amount of €608 million). 
On October 27, the remaining balance was redeemed in 
the amount of €142 million.

On November 19, REF issued subordinated perpetual 
bonds (with the guarantee of Repsol, S.A.), admitted to 
trading on the Luxembourg Stock Exchange, for a total 
amount of €750 million. The bonds pay a coupon of 
4.197% until February 19, 2032, the date on which 
Repsol's first option to redeem them expires.

Additionally, on September 16, 2025, Repsol E&P 
Capital Markets US LLC. carried out a bond issue backed 
by Repsol E&P S.à r.l. for an amount of $2,500 million, 
structured in three series, for 3, 5, and 10 years, and 
paying coupons of 4.805%, 5.204%, and 5.976%, 
respectively.

On December 15, 2025, the bond issued by RIF in April 
2020, with a nominal amount of €750 million and paying 
a 2.0% coupon, was redeemed at maturity.

For further information, see Note 11.4 “Other equity 
instruments” to the 2025 consolidated Financial 
Statements.

Repsol Europe Finance, S.à.r.l. (REF) has a Euro 
Commercial Paper (ECP) Program, secured by Repsol, S.A., 
with a limit of up to €3,000 million. Under this program, 
various issues and retirements were made throughout the 
period, with the outstanding balance as of December 31, 
2025 amounting to €195 million (€743 million as of 
December 31, 2024).

For further information, see Note 12 “Financial 
resources” to the 2025 consolidated Financial 
Statements.

4.4 Taxation

€13,265 M Taxes paid  50%

Percentage of own taxes accrued over profit (before these taxes).

Tax contribution

Taxes and other similar public charges paid during the 
period 2025 amounted to €13,265 million. In Spain, they 
exceeded €9,800 million.

This significant tax contribution—generated by our 
activities and paid by our companies—has been borne 
both by the company (own taxes) and by its customers, 
employees and investors (taxes collected).

Tax payments are detailed in section 5.6 of Appendix IV 
and in the Tax Contribution Report available at 
www.repsol.com.

(1) Includes, among other items, taxes on profits, production, local taxes on property and other non-tax public property benefits, social security

Tax payable on profits

Responsible tax policy

Repsol's own taxes accrued in 2025 represent 50% net 
income (before those taxes).

Repsol's businesses were subject to a high tax burden in 
2025 as a result of the generally high taxation on the 
profits of Upstream.

Repsol has defined a fiscal policy based on the 
responsible and efficient payment of its taxes, 
the application of good tax practices, transparency 
in its actions and the promotion of cooperative 
relations with tax administrations.

Repsol undertakes not to have a presence in tax havens, 
unless it is necessary for legitimate business reasons. 
In such a case, it shall be transparent and shall not use 
opaque or contrived structures. Repsol's presence in 
those jurisdictions is irrelevant. 

Repsol discloses information about its tax contribution 
and approach (www.repsol.com) following the best 
external reporting standards in tax matters. This effort 
for transparency has earned several public recognitions.

In Appendix V, section 4.2. Responsible taxation 
contains structured information on this matter, in 
accordance with the requirements established 
in the CSRD.

4.5 Remuneration to our 
shareholders

Shareholder remuneration depends on a number of 
factors, including business performance and operating 
results.

Shareholder return in 2025

During 2025 dividends totaling €0.975 gross per share 
were paid out, as follows:

•

•

In January, a total of €0.475 gross per share was paid, 
corresponding to: (i) a gross dividend of €0.45 per share 
charged to voluntary reserves; and (ii) an interim dividend 
of €0.025 gross per share charged against 2024 earnings. 
The amount paid out amounted to €5509 million. 

In July, a total of €0.50 gross per share was paid out 
against voluntary reserves, for a total amount of 
€56810 million. 

In addition, in July and December, two capital reductions 
were carried out, through the cancellation of 52 million 
treasury shares (29 and 23 million shares in the first and 
second reductions, respectively), which contributes to 
the shareholder return by increasing earnings per share.

As of December 31, 2025, Repsol held a total of 
274,966 treasury shares11, representing 0.02% of share 
capital at that date.

Shareholder return in 2026

In January 2026, shareholders were remunerated with the 
payment of €0.5 gross per share charged to voluntary 
reserves. The total amount paid out amounted to €55212 
million.

Additionally, the Company’s Board of Directors has 
agreed to propose to the next Annual General Meeting 
the payment in 2026 of a supplementary dividend (in 
addition to that paid in January 2026), charged to 2025 
profit, of €0.551 gross per share, which is expected to 
take place on July 8, 2026.

It has also agreed to propose to the next Annual General 
Shareholders’ Meeting a capital reduction through the 
redemption of the shares acquired under a share buyback 
program, with a maximum net investment of €350 million.

For more information, see Note 11 “Equity” to the 2025 
consolidated Financial Statements.

Share price performance (€/share) 

Source: Bloomberg and Repsol's Research Department.

During 2025, Repsol's share rose by 36.2%, above the 
average of its peers in the European Oil & Gas sector, 
which rose by 8.1% during the year, despite the 
geopolitical complexity and trade tensions experienced 
during the year.

5. Performance of our businesses

5.1 Upstream

Our activities

• New areas: identification and entry into new projects 

(organic or inorganic growth).

• Exploration: geology, geophysics and exploratory 
drilling activities in the search for hydrocarbon 
resources.

• Evaluation: drilling of appraisal wells, definition of the 

volumes discovered and determination of their 
commercial viability.

• Development: drilling of production wells, 

construction of collection systems, processing plants, 
and evacuation and transport systems to produce 
reserves. 

• Production: commercial operation of hydrocarbons.

• Decommissioning: abandonment and refurbishment of 
all facilities to leave the area in the same environmental 
condition as prior to the commencement of operations.

(1) Includes controlled assets and direct interests in assets and joint operations (commonly referred to as Joint Operating Agreements, or “JOAs”), 
which are not structured through jointly controlled entities (Joint Ventures) that would limit Repsol’s exposure to the risks and rewards of the 
underlying assets. Figures relating to JOAs are consolidated based on Repsol’s ownership interest.

EAGE Petroleum Resources Management System" system, commonly referred to as its acronym SPE-PRMS (SPE – Society of Petroleum Engineers).

Information on oil and gas exploration and production activities

Repsol simultaneously publishes, together with this Management Report, the “Information on oil and gas exploration and 
production activities” available at www.repsol.com, which contains detailed information on mining acreage, exploration 
and development activity, proven net reserves, future cash flows, and production.

Main events in the period 

Efficiency measures and a focus on the value of the 
asset portfolio have been instrumental in capturing a 
strong adjusted net profit in the current context of price 
volatility. 

For more information, see section 4.1 Results.

Portfolio management
An agreement was reached in 2025 to consolidate the 
Upstream business in the United Kingdom with NEO UK 
and TotalEnergies UK and Repsol divested its 
operations in certain countries (Colombia and Indonesia) 
and in non-strategic assets. These decisions are part of 
the broader portfolio optimization strategy, prioritizing 
assets with greater alignment with its profitability and 
sustainability objectives. These divestments allow 
Repsol to focus resources on key regions and projects 
with greater potential for long-term value generation.

Outlook

Appendices

Reserves
The change in net proven reserves in 2025 was -38 
Mboe, mainly reflecting the net effect of purchases and 
sales of reserves, extensions and discoveries, and 
revisions of previous estimates. The total reserves 
replacement ratio stood at -19% in 2025 (55% in 2024).

Financing
In September, Repsol successfully completed its largest 
bond issuance in dollars, totaling $2.5 billion, through 
its subsidiary Repsol E&P Capital Markets US LLC. The 
transaction, structured in three tranches with maturities 
between 2028 and 2035, was oversubscribed by 6.9 
times the amount offered, reflecting strong interest 
from the U.S. market. The proceeds will be used to 
refinance intragroup debt. This issuance strengthens 
Repsol E&P’s financial position and expands its investor 
base in the United States, consolidating its access to 
international capital markets. The bonds are guaranteed 
by Repsol E&P and are rated BBB+ by S&P and Fitch.

Average production
Average production amounted to 548 Kboe/d in 2025, 
down 4% on 2024, mainly due to divestments in 
Colombia and Indonesia in 2025, in Eagle Ford Southwest 
in June 2024, and in mature assets in Trinidad and 
Tobago in December 2024, as well as maintenance 
activities in the United Kingdom and Norway, and the 
natural decline of the fields. This was partially offset by 
the production start-up of Cypre and Mento in Trinidad 
and Tobago and of León and Castilla in the Gulf of 
Mexico, the integration of the assets in the United 
Kingdom with NEO, the acquisition of Tomoporo-La 
Ceiba in Venezuela in the second quarter of 2024, as well 
as the absence of periods of force majeure in Libya, and 
the connection of new wells in Libya and at Marcellus.

Exploratory activity
The drilling of five exploratory wells was completed in 
2025: two under evaluation (one in Trinidad and Tobago 
and one in Libya), two positives in Bolivia and one with a 
negative result in Libya. The drilling of two delineation 
wells was also completed: one positive in Trinidad and 
Tobago and one under evaluation in Libya.

As of December 31, there was an exploratory drilling 
operation underway in Bolivia (YPC-X43).

Main events in the period

United States: development of Alaska (Pikka) 
and start-up of production in GoM (Leon and 
Castile)

On December 31, 2024, Santos Ltd received approval 
from the Alaska Department of Natural Resources 
(ADNR) for the 2025 Development Plan (POD 2025) for 
the Pikka field in the North Slope Basin, where Repsol 
holds a 49% interest and Santos is the operator with 51%. 
The Plan began on February 1, 2025 and is progressing 
as planned with the drilling of 23 wells (10 production, 
11 injection and two service) of the 45 planned and the 
commissioning of the facilities.

The Nanushuk Processing Plant is 95% complete. All 
other work areas required for the start of production are 
already operational. Production is estimated to begin at 
the end of the first quarter of 2026, reaching a 
sustained gross production level of 80,000 barrels per 
day for Phase I in the third quarter. Although not 
required for the start of production, the seawater 
treatment plant will start operating in May 2026.

Outlook

Appendices

In December, Repsol was awarded 12 exploration blocks in 
the Gulf of Mexico at the Big Beautiful Gulf 1 (BBG1) 
auction, the first deep water oil and gas auction conducted 
by the United States since 2023. Repsol submitted bids for 
13 ultra-deep water blocks and was awarded 12 (3 alone 
and 9 with a partner), with a total of 303 km².

In December, the license for the AC 380 and AC 424 
blocks in the Alaminos Canyon area of the Gulf of 
Mexico expired.

In April, the Salamanca floating production unit was 
mobilized to the Gulf of Mexico to start operations in the 
Leon and Castile fields, where Repsol holds 50% and 
35.62%, respectively. The production capacity is 60,000 
barrels per day of crude oil and 40 million cubic feet of 
gas. The Salamanca platform is the result of a 
refurbishment of an old facility, which has led to an 87% 
reduction in emissions compared to the construction of a 
new unit. In September, the first well began production in 
the León field and, in October, two other wells began 
production, one in León and the other in Castile. This 
strategic project adds significant long-term production 
capacity in the United States and reflects Repsol's focus 
on this key basin for the Upstream business.

Net developed acreage (Km2)

Net production of gas (kboe/d)

Net hydrocarbon production (kboe/d)

In August, Eagle Ford awarded Ensign US Southern 
Drilling LLC a service contract for rig 120 to execute the 
drilling of 17 wells. In September, drilling began on the 
Blunh-Evangeline A platform (seven wells).

In September, Marcellus awarded Precision Drilling 
Company a service contract for pad 566 to execute the 
drilling of 37 wells. It also started drilling the Lovell pad 
(six wells).

In November, in Alaska, Repsol was awarded a total of 
67 exploration blocks, of which 45 correspond to areas 
where the company holds a 100% operating interest. 
The other 22 blocks will be operated by Santos, in which 
Repsol maintains a 49% stake. 

In December, the preparation of the Quokka-1 well 
began, with drilling set to begin in January 2026.

Mexico: increase in the B29 interest

In October, Petronas, and in December Harbour signed 
agreements with Repsol to relinquish their stakes in 
block B29. Repsol now holds 100% of the rights to the 
block, assuming full operational and strategic 
responsibility for the asset (pending authorization for 
the divestiture of partners by the Ministry of Energy, 
SENER).

Main events in the period

Bolivia: variable gas demand

Operations in Bolivia were marked by the variability of 
gas demand, mainly due to the adjustment that 
Petrobras Brazil makes on a recurring basis on its 
volumes between the guaranteed contractual minimum 
(Take or Pay) and the committed daily amount (QDC). 

There are contractual obligations that ensure a 
minimum level of payment and supply even if actual 
consumption fluctuates, providing stability to the 
seller's income.
