the Group to carry out the ordinary, incidental and 
necessary transactions for the winding-down of the 
previously authorized operations until May 27, 2025.

Brazil: progress in the Raia project

In June, the Brazilian regulator (Agencia Nacional do 
Petróleo) technically approved the development plan 
for the Raia project (Block BM-C-33) in the Campos 
basin, where Repsol is a partner along with Equinor, 
Sinopec and Petrobras. In July, Brazil’s environmental 
regulator (IBAMA) granted the installation license for 
the Raia export gas pipeline project, allowing work to 
begin on the 15-kilometer section of shallow water. 

The complete gas pipeline will span approximately 200 
kilometers, connecting Raia's production, storage and 
offloading vessel (FPSO) with the Cabiúnas terminal in 
Macaé. Raia is one of the country's most important 
natural gas developments, with the capacity to 
transport 16 million cubic meters of natural gas per day 
and containing recoverable oil/condensate reserves of 
more than one billion barrels of oil equivalent (boe). The 
project is scheduled to start operating in 2028.

Colombia: withdrawal from the country

In February, the sale of 45% of the CPO-9 asset and 25% 
of SierraCol Energy Arauca LLC was completed. 

For more information, see the section “Assets and 
liabilities held for sale” in Note 19 to the consolidated 
Financial Statements.

Peru: sustained production

In tandem with this, and within the framework of the 
Recapex II project to maintain production at Block 57, 
the seventh and last compressor was successfully 
installed and commissioned, improving the reliability of 
the plant and guaranteeing the production of the block.

Trinidad and Tobago: development 
of Ginger and commissioning of Cypre and 
Mento

The company BpTT, a company owned by BP (70%) and 
Repsol (30%), approved in March the development of 
the Ginger offshore project, a gas field discovered in 
2019. This field will be connected to the Mahogany B 
platform, with a production target of approximately 
62,000 barrels of oil equivalent per day (boe/d). Gas 
production is expected to start in 2027.

In March, gas production began at the Cypre project, 
located in Block 3 off the coast. This first phase connects 
four wells to the Juniper platform, with expected 
production of up to 45,000 boe/d. A second phase with 
three additional wells is planned for the second half of 
the year and will come into production in 2026.

In June, gas production began at the Mento-1ST well, 
located in the SMR (Ska Mento Reggae) block. This well, 
which was discovered in 2020, marks the beginning of 
the development of the Mento field, which includes a 
facility with a capacity for 12 wells. At the same time, a 
drilling campaign will be launched to complete the 
project's remaining seven wells. In August, production 
began on the second well at Mento.

Venezuela: Revocation of the U.S. OFAC 
license and change in the political landscape

In March 2025, the OFAC revoked the Special License 
that allowed the Company to continue its operations in 
Venezuela and issued a Wind-Down License authorizing 

Throughout 2025, the political and economic crisis 
persisted, influenced by the international sanctions 
imposed on PDVSA and the Government of Venezuela. 
This situation worsened following the military activities 
deployed by the United States in the Caribbean under 
the counter-narcotics program.

In January 2026, the National Assembly approved a 
partial reform of the Organic Hydrocarbons Law, which 
includes three main areas of amendment: (i) the 
incorporation of “successful business models” from the 
Anti-Blockade Law; (ii) the proposal of mechanisms to 
ensure the viability of projects which, due to their 
technical or economic characteristics, require special 
considerations (e.g., undeveloped fields); and (iii) the 
proposal to strengthen legal safeguards for investment 
by introducing the possibility of resorting to 
independent mediation and arbitration mechanisms.

In addition, in January and February 2026, the OFAC 
issued several General Licenses enabling operations in 
the oil and gas sector in Venezuela, including those 
involving the Government of Venezuela, Petróleos de 
Venezuela, S.A. (PDVSA), or any entity in which PDVSA 
owns, directly or indirectly, an interest equal to or greater 
than 50%. Specifically, the following licenses have been 
issued: (i) GL 46, later replaced by GL 46A, authorizing the 
marketing of Venezuelan crude oil and derivatives by 
established US entities; (ii) GL 47, allowing necessary 
transactions with US-origin diluents in Venezuela; (iii) GL 
48, authorizing the provision from the US of goods, 
technology, software and services for exploration, 
production and maintenance of facilities; (iv) GL 30B, 
authorizing the operation and use of ports and airports in 
Venezuela; (v) GL 49, authorizing negotiations of and 
entry into contingent contracts for new investments 
(including due diligence), subject to separate 
authorization by the OFAC; and (vi) GL 50, authorizing 
transactions to be carried out in the oil and gas sector in 
Venezuela by the entities and subsidiaries listed in the 
Appendix, which expressly includes Repsol, S.A.

Against this backdrop, Repsol continues to closely 
monitor the country’s political and institutional 
developments, maintaining direct contact with the US 
and Venezuelan administrations, in an effort to align the 
scope of Repsol’s authorized activities in Venezuela with 
the objective of contributing to the country’s energy 
development, while adopting the necessary measures in 
full compliance with the applicable international 
sanctions regulations.

In this regard, several events have taken place from 
year-end to the date of authorization for issue of this 
consolidated Management Report that could have an 
impact in the future on Repsol’s operations and assets in 
the country, in particular the new general license (GL 50) 
that authorizes Repsol to carry out transactions with the 
Government of Venezuela or entities of the PDVSA 
group related to the oil and gas sector in Venezuela. In 
order to effectively apply these licenses and, therefore, 
determine their scope and impact on Repsol’s assets in 
Venezuela, the contracts must be subject to the laws of 
the US and any jurisdiction within the US, and certain 
relevant terms must be negotiated and finalized with 
the Government of Venezuela and/or PDVSA, in 
accordance with that set out in the partial reform of the 
Organic Law on Hydrocarbons, including, among other

Spain: progress in decommissioning assets

In October, the company Dolphin Drilling signed a 
contract with Repsol for the abandonment of 
underwater wells in Spain. To this end, the Borgland 
Dolphin semi-submersible platform will be mobilized to 
Tarragona at the end of 2026 to begin work. The 
contract envisages a scope of seven firm wells and has a 
minimum duration of 220 days, with an option to extend 
it to include three additional wells.

In the meantime, the decommissioning of the 
Casablanca platform and its connections to the wells 
continues, and is expected to be completed by the end 
of March 2026.

United Kingdom: integration into Neo Next 
and Neo Next+ and consolidation of acreage

In April, Repsol started the drilling campaign for three 
development wells at Piper, in which Neo Next holds a 
100% stake.

In July, the alliance between Repsol Resources UK and 
NEO Energy was completed, forming the joint venture 
Neo Next Energy Limited (“Neo Next”). The joint venture, 
owned by Repsol E&P (45%) and NEO UK (55%), is now 
one of the largest oil and gas producers on the United 
Kingdom's continental shelf (UKCS), with a projected 
production for 2025 of around 130,000 boe/d.

NEO UK's production as of December 31, 2024 was 
approximately 68,000 boe/d, while RRUK's was 
34,000 boe/d.

The new entity operates a diversified portfolio that, as 
of the date of this Report, includes 11 production sites, 
as well as significant undeveloped reserves. In addition, 
this alliance will significantly enhance the joint venture's 
operational scale, efficiency and growth prospects, 
while reinforcing Repsol’s long-term goal of maximizing 
the value of its exploration and production assets in the 
United Kingdom.

In September, Neo Next completed the acquisition of 
Gran Tierra North Sea Limited (GTNSL) for $7.5 million. 
GTNSL holds 100% of the UKCS P2358 licence, which 
encompasses the Serenity discovery off the coast of the 
Moray Firth. This asset has development potential 
through connection with the Bleo Holm FPSO floating 
production unit, already operated by Neo Next.

In October, the redeveloped Murlach crude oil field, with 
a subsea connection operated by BP (Neo Next 20% WI), 
was put into production with a gross capacity of 15,000 
boe/d.

In November, Neo Next exercised its pre-emptive right 
over two BP assets in the North Sea to acquire the 
licenses operated by TotalEnergies P111 (Culzean, the 
country's largest gas-producing field) and P2544 
(exploratory permit), after Serica Energy announced its 
intention to acquire 32% for $232 million. Following the 
operation, Neo Next increased its stake to 50.01%. The 
transaction was completed on December 31, 2025.

In December, the Clyde field (Neo Next 100% WI) ended 
its production to begin the abandonment and 
decommissioning of the facilities.

In December 2025, NeoNext agreed with TotalEnergies 
to merge its Upstream assets in the British North Sea. 
This transaction will result in the creation of the NEO 
NEXT+ joint venture, which will position itself as the 
largest independent oil and gas producer on the UK 
Continental Shelf (UKCS), with production expected to be 
around 250,000 barrels of oil equivalent per day in 2026.

The shareholding structure of NEO NEXT+ will be 
divided between TotalEnergies (47.5%), HitecVision 
(28.8%), and Repsol (23.6%). The addition of 
TotalEnergies’ high-quality assets will strengthen the 
new company's balance sheet and operating capabilities. 
In addition, TotalEnergies will retain up to $2.3 billion in 
decommissioning obligations, improving the cash flows 
of the combined business. The transaction, pending 
regulatory approval, is scheduled to close in the first 
half of 2026.

Norway: Seismic campaign in Visund 
and improvements for Mikkel

In July, Repsol, through its operating partner Equinor, 
carried out a 232 km² 4D seismic campaign in the Visund 
field in the North Sea.

In September, Equinor commenced phase 2 of the 
underwater compression project in the Asgard field in 
the Norwegian Sea. The second compressor module was 
installed at a depth of 270 meters, which will allow 
production to be maintained by increasing the pressure 
between the wells and the Asgard B platform. With both 
phases, it is expected to increase the recovery rate to 
90% of the Midgard and Mikkel fields (in which Repsol 
has a 7.65% stake as a non-operating partner).

Algeria: extension of the Block 405A license 
and further progress at Reggane

In January 2025, the contract signed for the 25-year 
extension of operations, with an option for an additional 
10 years, at Block 405a (MLN, EMK and Ourhoud 
licenses), where Repsol is a partner with a 24.5% stake in 
MLN, 6.37% in EMK, and 1.34% in Ourhoud, was 
published in the Algerian Official Gazette (at the time of 
its entry into force).

In February, Repsol received authorization from the 
Algerian government to carry out its investment in Block 
405a. 

In November, the Algerian Council of Ministers approved 
and published in the country's Official Gazette, the 
amendment of Factor A (part of the formula that 
regulates the distribution of production between 
international companies and the state-owned company) 
of the new block 405a contract to start receiving 
hydrocarbon rights. The Indonesian company Pertamina 
is the temporary operator until the new Groupement is 
formed in the first quarter of 2026.

In September, the drilling and development of the 
Reggane, Kahlouche and Kahlouche Sud fields was 
completed at Reggane.

The final well at the Azrafil Sudest field (AZSE-20) 
continues to operate and is expected to be completed 
by the end of the first quarter of 2026. Meanwhile, the 
Tio 4 well remains under evaluation.

Libya: First exploratory tender

In December 2024, exploratory activity resumed, after 
having been suspended for a decade, in the Murzuq 
basin with the drilling of the Nesser-1 well, which began 
on December 31 and was declared negative in 2025.

On March 3, 2025, the NOC started a bidding round 
consisting of 22 blocks (11 onshore and 11 offshore), 
spanning a total area of 235,000 km2.

In 2025, the development drilling campaign continued 
with five active rigs; 30 wells were completed and five 
were pending completion as of December 31, 2025.

For more information, see Note 27 “Geopolitical risks” to 
the 2025 consolidated Financial Statements.

Indonesia: withdrawal form the country

In July, Repsol sold its 24% stake in the Corridor block, 
located in Sumatra (Indonesia) to MedcoEnergi for $425 
million. Corridor is a gas-producing asset that 
contributed 19,000 boe/d to Repsol in 2024, 
representing 3% of its global production and 2% of its 
reserves.

In August, MedcoEnergi acquired Repsol's stakes in the 
Sakakemang (45%) and South Sakakemang (80%) blocks, 
the latter subject to approval by the Indonesian 
government.

Iraq: withdrawal from the country

In May, Repsol sold Talisman (Block K39) BV along with 
its mining rights to the Topkhana development project.

5.2 Industrial

Our activities

• Refining: the transformation of oil into fuels and other 

derivative products.

• Chemicals: production and marketing of a wide variety 

of products (including basic petrochemicals and 
derivatives), with a focus on the circular economy.

• Trading: supply of crude oil, intermediate products, 

chemical raw materials, and lipid residues to Repsol's 
industrial complexes, and third parties; marketing of 
petroleum products, renewable fuels, and 
petrochemical products in international markets; and 
maritime transport for the Group's activity and third 
parties.

• Wholesale, marketing, and gas trading: supply and 

trading of LNG/natural gas, including LNG 
regasification, and marketing to customers in North 
America and the Iberian Peninsula.

• Hydrogen, Renewable Fuels and Circular Economy: 

development, production and marketing of renewable, 
low-carbon footprint and circular products, promoting 
the use of alternative resources obtained in a 
sustainable way, such as renewable and low carbon 
electrolytic hydrogen, biofuels, synthetic fuels, and 
other products that contribute to decarbonization and 
the manufacture of circular materials, promoting 
innovation and the circular economy.

Main events in the period

The activity of industrial businesses has been marked by 
the international context determined by the high 
volatility in the markets for raw materials and products, 
which has led to the adaptation of production, logistics 
and commercial schemes to the changing situation. 

All this has not detracted from the drive towards 
decarbonization, as evidenced by the progress made 
toward the various projects at the industrial complexes: 
final investment decision for the Tarragona Ecoplant, 
progress in the construction of the second renewable 
fuels plant in the Puertollano Industrial Complex, 
acquisition of a 40% stake in Bunge Ibérica, which owns 
various industrial facilities in the Iberian Peninsula, and 
key milestones for the development of renewable 
hydrogen projects in Spain, such as the construction of 
two electrolyzers at the Cartagena and Petronor plants.

For more information, see section 4.1 Results.

Appendices

The Group owns and operates six refineries, five in Spain 
(Cartagena, A Coruña, Bilbao, Puertollano and 
Tarragona) with a total distillation capacity of 896 kboe/
d (including in Tarragona the stake in Asfaltos Españoles 
S.A.), and one in Peru, where it owns a 99.2% stake, with 
an installed capacity of 117 kboe/d.

Performance: volatility in margins

The volatility of the environment has been evident in the 
evolution of the refining margin indicator. 

In Spain, in the first quarter, the margin indicator was 
slightly higher than in the fourth quarter of 2024 ($5.3/
bbl vs $4.8/bbl), although it was below the first quarter 
of 2024 ($11.4/bbl) due to lower margins for diesel. 
After a slight improvement in the second quarter ($5.9/
bbl), mainly due to the effect of lower Brent prices, in 
the third quarter there was a significant increase ($8.8/
bbl) driven by the improvement in diesel due to 
growing concerns about supply disruptions (capacity 
closures, geopolitical conflicts), which was further 
accentuated in the fourth quarter (US sanctions, 
Ukrainian drone attacks on Russian refineries, refinery 
maintenance), leading the indicator to a value of $11.1/
bbl. The average indicator for 2025 (7.9 $/bbl) is higher 
than for 2024, mainly due to the increase in middle 
distillate differentials and the positive effect of a lower 
Brent price. 

Average distillation utilization was 82.8% in Spain, 
compared with 88.1% the previous year, impacted by 
maintenance stoppages, operational incidents 
associated with the presence of water in the Maya 
crude being processed, as well as incidents associated 
with various electrical blackouts on the Iberian 
Peninsula, including the mass blackout that took place 
on April 28.

In July, Petronor completed the shutdown of Plant I 
after two months of work and an investment of more 
than €89 million. Maintenance and technological 
updating tasks were carried out with the aim of 
improving safety, increasing efficiency, and reducing 
emissions. Among the improvements, the installation of 
the new Preflash tower stands out, which will reduce 
CO2 emissions by more than 26,000 tons per year.

In August, the multi-year shutdown began in the 
Hydroskiming area in Cartagena, with the aim of 
carrying out maintenance tasks and incorporating new 
technologies, with a planned investment of €67 million.

In September, Repsol and the Port Authority of A 
Coruña approved the definitive transfer of their 
operations to the outer port in Punta Langosteira, 
currently in the inner port until the end of 2027. It is 
planned to invest €140 million in the second phase of 
the transfer, raising the total investment to more than 
€250 million. The agreement includes a 15-year 
extension in the concession and an occupation of 
127,000 m². The new facilities will allow the operation 
of organic raw materials and low-carbon products, 
promoting the A Coruña Green Port. In 2024, Repsol 
will manage 10 million tons, equivalent to 60% of total 
port traffic.

In November, Petronor began the installation of the new 
polymer-grade propylene splitter to replace the current 
one. The project, which has an investment of €58 million, 
will be launched during the first quarter of 2026 and will 
allow greater purification of propylene, while aiming to 
improve energy efficiency and contributing to the 
decarbonization of the refinery.

In Peru, the refining margin index stood at 10.4 $/bbl, 
compared with 5.9 $/bbl in 2024, mainly due to better 
product spreads. Average distillation utilization was 
89.4% in Peru, compared with 83.9% the previous year, 
as a result of the multi-year shutdown of the refinery 
that began in May 2024.

5.2.2. Chemicals

Main assets

Production at Repsol’s Chemicals business is 
concentrated in three petrochemical complexes, located 
in Spain (Puertollano, Tarragona) and Portugal (Sines), in 
which there is a high level of integration between basic 
chemicals and derived chemicals, as well as with the 
Group’s refining activities in the case of the Spanish 
complexes. Repsol owns subsidiaries and affiliates 
through which it operates plants that manufacture 
polyolefin compounds, synthetic rubber and specialty 
chemicals, the latter through Dynasol, a 50% alliance 
with the Mexican group KUO, with plants in Spain, 
Mexico and China, alongside local partners.

Performance: low demand and

The performance of the Chemicals business was 
influenced by the prevailing international trend of low 
demand and overcapacity, which led to lower activity 
and adjustments in plant operations (also impacted by 
the blackout) and higher margins than in previous 
periods (margin indicator of 252 €/t, which partially 
recovers to its previous level in 2024: 210 €/t). Sales 
stood at 1,820 kt, down 5% on the previous year.

Differentiation of our products 

In July, Dynasol commissioned a new styrene-butadiene 
rubber (sSBR) production line in Gajano (Santander), 
increasing capacity by 20 kt/a. The investment for this 
new line amounts to 72 million euros, making it the 
largest in the history of the complex.

Electrification of the complexes 

In May, the compressor electrification project at the 
Sines olefins unit was completed. With the 
implementation, this project will reduce the direct CO2 
emissions of the center by 95 kt/a.

Along the same lines, in Tarragona, progress has also 
been made in the electrification of large machines, 
including the olefins unit, with its commissioning 
scheduled for mid-2026 and a reduction in direct 
emissions of 165 kt/a. A new compressor with an electric 
motor was also installed at the OP/SM plant.

Both actions meet the requirements to be generators of 
CAEs (Energy Savings Certificates).

5.2.3. Trading

The main function of Trading is the management of a 
diversified portfolio of positions based on experience in 
the markets, hedging activities, as well as technical 
inspection activities for ship safety. The Trading 
portfolio comprises: (i) the supply of crude oil, 
intermediate materials, chemical raw materials and lipid 
residues to industrial complexes and third parties; (ii) 
marketing of petroleum products, renewable fuels and 
petrochemicals in international markets; (iii) chartering 
and maritime transport for the activity of the Group and 
third parties; and (iv) the management of hedging of 
crude oil and other products in the financial derivatives 
markets. Trading has a presence in Madrid, Houston, 
Singapore, Lima, and Shanghai.

In July, Repsol added to its fleet the first diesel-electric 
oil tanker, known as Bahía Candela, designed specifically 
for the supply of new fuels. It is equipped with 12 tanks, 
which allow it to transport up to 8,000 cubic meters of 
different fuels, and a diesel-electric propulsion system, 
complemented by a battery energy storage system. This 
allows fuel supply operations to be carried out in an all-
electric manner.

Performance: environmental uncertainty

In 2025, a total of 1,529 vessels were chartered (1,558 in 
2024) and 660 Time Charter fleet voyages were made 
(593 in 2024).

5.2.4. Wholesale and Gas 
Trading

Main assets and operations

As of December 31, 2025, the Group had regasification 
and transmission assets at its marketing businesses in 
North America, including the Saint John LNG 
regasification plant and gas pipelines in Canada and the 
United States. In the northeastern U.S., where natural gas 
supply is often constrained by a lack of storage capacity, 
cold weather scenarios can cause significant spikes in the 
area’s benchmark prices. The Company’s activity in this 
area focuses on optimizing the margin from the 
marketing and sale of regasified LNG from Saint John 
LNG and natural gas acquired on the market. Repsol also 
markets and trades natural gas in North America from its 
own production in the United States (Marcellus), as well 
as from acquisitions from third parties.

It also allows for the efficient supply of gas to meet the 
Repsol Group’s demand and is responsible for marketing 
and the trading in the Spanish and Portuguese gas 
system and in the international LNG market.

Performance: higher gas prices

In 2025, commercial activity took place amid rising gas 
prices but worse margins. In the United States, volumes 
sold were up on 2024 (616 TBtu in 2025, +4%), although 
supply and marketing increased in Spain and 
internationally (331 TBtu in 2025, +33%).

New agreements and decarbonization 

In February, Venture Global announced that its 
Calcasieu Pass LNG facility would begin commercial 
operations on April 15 of that year, which has allowed 
Repsol to begin receiving the corresponding cargoes 
under the long-term contract, albeit with a significant 
delay compared with what was initially estimated.

The first cargo was received on April 30 and 11 cargoes 
had been received by December 31, which improves 
Repsol's LNG business activities and strengthens 
security of supply.

In September, Repsol became the first supplier in Spain 
to offer bioLNG from biomethane injected into the grid. 
This operation has been carried out from the LNG 
bunkering terminal (in the port of Bilbao) to the vessel 
“Salamanca” of Brittany Ferries,which covers the route 
between Bilbao and Rosslare, in Ireland.

5.2.5. Hydrogen, Renewable 
Fuels, and Circular Economy 

Renewable and low-carbon hydrogen

In May, Repsol reached a strategic milestone with the 
publication of the final approval of the Hy2Use Important 
Project of Common European Interest (IPCEI), which 
includes its renewable hydrogen projects in Petronor and 
Cartagena. These projects, which received a joint 
allocation of €315 million in public aid (€160 million for 
Petronor and €155 million for Cartagena), each have an 
electrolysis capacity of 100 MW. Together, they will 
enable the production of up to 30,000 tons of renewable 
hydrogen per year, while cutting CO2 emissions by up to 
334,000 tons annually. The construction of both 
projects, which are integrated into strategic industrial 
clusters and will contribute significantly to the 
decarbonization of industry and transport, has already 
been approved, and they are expected to enter into 
operation in 2029. Specifically, the Cartagena 
electrolyzer, construction of which has been approved 
jointly with Enagás, will involve an investment of over 
€300 million, while the construction of the electrolyzer 
at the Petronor Refinery in Muskiz will entail an 
investment of €292 million. Overall, both projects are 
estimated to generate around 1,800 jobs, including 
direct, indirect, and induced employment, across their 
various phases.

In June, the final resolution was published whereby the 
Spanish Government has allocated a total of €1,223 
million in NextGenEU funds for the H2 Valles program to 
seven strategic projects located in Aragon, Andalusia, 
Castilla y León, Catalonia and Galicia, of which Repsol has 
been awarded €104.2 million for the T-HYNET project for 
a 150 MW electrolyzer at Tarragona and €40.8 million for 
another electrolyzer in the A Coruña valley.

In July, Repsol announced an investment of €16 million in 
the Puertollano industrial complex, with the aim of 
producing 5,300 tons per year of renewable hydrogen 
from bio-based gas (produced from waste), replacing 
natural gas. This hydrogen will be used at the new 100% 
renewable fuels plant, reducing its emissions intensity. 
This plant is already in an advanced construction phase 
and is estimated to be operational by the end of 2026. 

In August, a major milestone was reached in moving 
forward with the 10 MW electrolyzer project in the Port 
of Bilbao, led by Repsol and in which EVE and Enagás 
Renovable are participating, with the arrival and start of 
the assembly of the electrolyzer itself and the main 
equipment. This new facility, which is scheduled to be 
commissioned in 2026, will produce up to 1,330 tons of 
renewable hydrogen per year, which will be used 
primarily to supply Repsol and Saudi Aramco’s synthetic 
fuels (e-fuels) demonstration plant, currently under 
construction and expected to be fully operational in 
early 2027. Moreover, the renewable hydrogen 
produced there will be used to power Repsol and Saudi 
Aramco's synthetic fuels (e-fuels) demo plant, under 
construction, and will be sent to two hydrogen refueling 
stations that will supply fleets of heavy-duty vehicles 
and the bus line to the Bilbao airport.

Renewable fuels

In March, the acquisition of 40% of Bunge Ibérica, S.A. 
was completed (a company that operates three 
industrial assets dedicated to the production of 
renewable fuels and oils in Bilbao, Barcelona and 
Cartagena) in exchange for $285 million.

In April, an major milestone was reached in the 
construction of the second renewable fuels plant at the 
Puertollano Industrial Complex, with the installation of 
the new reactor. The new plant, which is progressing as 
planned and is expected to be operational in 2026, has 
the capacity to produce up to 200,000 metric tons of 
renewable fuels per year, which will cut CO2 emissions 
by some 750,000 tons per year. 

In September, the first industrial-scale production of 
100% renewable gasoline was achieved at the Tarragona 
industrial complex. Nexa 95 gasoline, of 100% renewable 
origin, is a high-quality product, designed to make the 
most of the engine’s performance and keep it in optimal 
condition. It is already on sale at 30 service stations 
distributed in the Community of Madrid, Catalonia, 
Valencia, the Basque Country, and Aragon. This new fuel 
can be used in any gasoline-powered vehicle without the 
need to modify the car or the refueling infrastructure. Its 
use reduces net CO2 emissions by more than 70% 
compared with conventional gasoline.

In October, Repsol signed an agreement with Norwegian 
Cruise Line Holdings to supply renewable fuels, including 
biofuels and renewable methanol for the next eight 
years. This partnership has the goal to move both 
companies to the forefront of the energy transition 
within the cruise and maritime sectors.

In 2025, the Group's renewable fuel production capacity 
stood at 1.25 million metric tons per year (Mt/y), of 
which 0.25 Mt/y corresponds to the Cartagena plant, 
which is entirely devoted to the production of 
renewable fuels, and 1 Mt/y to other industrial assets 
(including ETBE, HVO, SAF and other renewable fuels).

Circular economy

In January, Repsol announced that it will invest more than 
€800 million in the Tarragona Ecoplant, the first plant 
capable of transforming municipal solid waste into 
renewable methanol and circular products through a 
gasification process. The new plant will have a total 
production capacity of 240,000 tons per year of 
renewable and circular methanol and is scheduled to be 
commissioned by the end of 2029, creating 340 new total 
jobs (direct, indirect and induced) in the operation phase. 
The plant is expected to CO2 emissions by 3.4 Mt in its 
first 10 years of operation. The project is being financed 
under the EU’s Innovation Fund program.

In November, the Tarragona Ecoplant was recognized at 
COP30 in Brazil as one of the six international success 
stories in decarbonization, included in the report of the 
Sustainable Business Group COP (SBCOP).

5.3 Customer

Our activities

•Mobility: marketing and sale of petroleum products, 

autogas, renewable fuels, electric charging solutions, 
and other products and services at service stations 
and wholesale (Direct Sales), offering differentiated 
value to industries such as shipping, large industry, 
and end consumers. 

• Lubricants, aviation, asphalts, and specialized 

products: production and marketing of lubricants, 
lubricant bases, bitumens for asphalts, kerosene and 
SAF (Sustainable Aviation Fuel), extender oils, coke, 
sulfur, paraffins, and propellant gases.

• LPG: acquisition, production, distribution, storage, and 
wholesale and retail sale of liquefied petroleum gases.

• Marketing of electricity and gas: retail supply 

(residential and businesses) of electricity and gas in 
Spain and Portugal.

• New businesses: energy efficiency, electric mobility, 
distributed generation, and development of digital 
products.

Main events in the period 

Repsol continued to develop and strengthen its 
strategy, which is based on and focused on the 
customer. Customers can benefit from Repsol's multi-
energy profile by having a single supplier for all their 
energy needs in mobility and the home (automotive 
fuels, heating fuel, electricity, solar self-consumption or 
electric mobility, etc.).

In 2025, Repsol had around 3.0 million customers in the 
electricity and gas sales segment in the Iberian 
Peninsula and 10,8 million digital customers, with 
Waylet being the driving force behind our digital 
strategy.13 At year-end, a total of 1,558 service stations 
were already 100% renewable fuel, exceeding the target 
set for 2025 of 1,500.

service stations, according to the following

In 2025, agreements with partners such as Amazon,

were consolidated, and new alliances were forged, most 
notably the partnership with Levaduramadre.

Performance: increased sales
At service stations in Spain, sales of gasoline and 
automotive diesel were up 2.9% on 2024, following an 
increase in demand, while direct sales of gasoline and 
diesel for the automotive industry rose by 32%, where 
fraud in the Spanish fuel market also decreased.

Renewable fuels  and circular 
economy
Renewable fuels are an alternative offered by Repsol at 
its service station network to support decarbonization 
and to meet its goal to supplying secure, sustainable, and 
affordable energy.

At the end of the period, Repsol had 1,558 service 
stations supplying 100% renewable fuel from Nexa 
(1,487 in Spain and 71 in Portugal). A total of 248 million 
liters of such fuel were supplied in 2025.

Nexa B diesel is now being sold for the agricultural sector 
in Spain, and HVO Nexa agro-diesel is being sold in 
Portugal.

Throughout the year, Repsol launched several new 
renewable fuels: Nexa 100% Renewable compressed 
natural gas (CNG) and liquefied natural gas (LNG). These

fuels, produced from biomethane, significantly reduce 
CO2 and NOx emissions, and eliminate 96% of solid 
particles. Currently available at two service stations, they 
are expected to reach 20 such stations in Spain by the 
end of 2026. The Diesel e+5 was also launched in the 
period, a fuel of intermediate quality lying between e+ 
and e+10, and already available at 200 service stations.

Following the production, in September, of the first batch 
of 100% renewable gasoline in Tarragona (a global 
milestone), its deployment got underway, reaching 30 
service stations in Spain by the end of the year. Following 
the strategy pursued with Nexa diesel, this gasoline 
combines renewable origin with maximum performance.

On the islands of Gran Canaria, Tenerife, and 
Fuerteventura, the availability of 100% renewable Nexa 
diesel was expanded during the period, with 65% of 
service stations in the Canary Islands now offering this 
fuel.

In December, Repsol was received an award in the 
Energy Excellence category at the Platts Global Energy 
Awards, for overcoming the dual challenge of 
supplying affordable and reliable energy, while 
promoting decarbonization across its operations and 
products in the Iberian Peninsula.

At Repsol, we are focused on the circular economy by 
collecting used cooking oil at authorized service 
stations. By recycling this waste, we guarantee its 
proper treatment, enabling its transformation into 
renewable fuels with the aim of doubling the 
percentage of residential waste oil that is currently 
recycled per year in Spain, which currently barely 
exceeds 4%.

Since the implementation of the project, more than 
195 thousand liters have been collected at the 793 
service stations adhered to the initiative across six 
autonomous regions of Spain. Repsol handles the 
entire process, up to the production of the 100% 
renewable fuel at the Cartagena plant, thus supporting 
its traceability.

Throughout the year, several agreements were reached 
to cover electricity and fuel needs, notably including 
those concluded with:

1. Aena, for the supply of diesel, biofuel and propane 
for the 2025–2026 period (extendable by one year) 
up to a maximum of 700,000 liters of diesel and 
HVO and up to a 30% increase on each of the lots.

2. The Repsol Toyota Rally and Toyota Gazoo Racing 
teams, which competed in the Dakar Rally using 
70% renewable fuels; and the agreement reached 
with Toyota Gazoo Racing was renewed for the 
entire World Rally Raid Championship. In addition, it 
won the Rally of Morocco using Nexa 95 gasoline of 
100% renewable origin.

3. Alsa, to transform the energy model of road 

passenger transport in Spain, which includes the 
goal of reduction of net CO₂ emissions through the 
use of renewable fuels and the study and 
development of initiatives for the supply of 
renewable energies. It also includes the promotion 
of energy storage solutions and the exploration of 
alternatives based on hydrogen and biomethane. 
Synergies will be sought in lubricants and digital 
loyalty and payment platforms, such as Waylet, will 
be integrated.

4. Parque Warner Madrid, to cover its entire energy 

supply, providing electricity, natural gas and 100% 
renewable fuels, reducing its CO2 emissions by 
more than 400 kt per year.

Furthermore, Repsol demonstrated its focus on society 
during the period by collaborating with Omoda&Jaecoo 
and Record Go, for the rental of 300 vehicles to those 
affected by the devastating flash floods in Valencia, 
which includes €100 per month in fuel to be used at 
Repsol service stations.

5.3.2. Lubricants, Aviation, 
Asphalts, and Specialized 
Products

Assets and operations
The production of Lubricants, Asphalts, and Specialized 
Products is mainly concentrated in Spain, although in 
the case of Lubricants there are two additional 
manufacturing hubs through joint ventures: the 
Americas, with Bardahl in Mexico, which covers the 
region, and Southeast Asia, with a presence in Indonesia 
and Singapore through United Oil, and in the Philippines 
with Unioil Lubricants. Both cover areas where the 
lubricants market is expected to grow the most on 
average over the coming years. Repsol also distributes 
lubricants in Eastern Europe through the Valtec 
Premium Lubricants joint venture located in Romania. 
The commercial division has a strong international 
component, with deliveries in more than 90 countries 
around the world. Repsol markets and sells aviation fuel 
in various locations, most notably in Spain, France, 
Portugal and Peru.

Performance
Sales at Lubricants, Aviation, Asphalts and Specialized 
Products were up 3% on 2024, mainly due to greater 
demand for Aviation products.

25,000 metric tons per year, to those already existing in 
Spain, Mexico, Singapore, and Indonesia.

become the new exclusive lubricant supplier for Moto2 
and Moto3. This strategic alliance with MotoGP World

In November, the new lubricant identity was presented

In December, a 44% stake in Valtec Premium Lubricants

Capacity increase in Spain
In April, an investment of €2.5 million in the specialized 
products factory in Palencia was announced for a new 
paraffin pearl manufacturing line. This investment will 
allow for the installation of a new manufacturing line 
which aim to increase production capacity by 33%.

Decarbonization of the airline 
industry
In March, the first Book & Claim operation was 
successfully completed in Spain (with Iberia Express) 
and in April in Latin America (with Avianca), allowing 
emissions reduction without the need to deliver physical 
SAF.

In October, Circulo SAF was presented, a community of 
companies to which Repsol belongs, alongside Iberia, 
Amadeus, BBVA, and Telefónica, to promote the use of 
SAF and reduce emissions from corporate air transport.

Throughout the year, a series of voluntary SAF 
agreements were signed (for volumes exceeding the 
mandatory requirement) with entities such as the Civil 
Guard and the airlines Lufthansa and Scandinavian 
Airlines System.

5.3.3 LPG

LPG has become a key ally in facing the energy 
transition, as a genuine alternative for making 
immediate progress toward decarbonization objectives, 
without losing competitiveness and supporting the 
supply to all geographic areas.

Assets and operations

Repsol is the main LPG14 retail distribution company in 
Spain and the third largest operator in Portugal. It has 
been operating in the French market since 2019.

In 2025, the sale of bottled propane with a tare weight

Self-consumption: decarbonization 
of factories
Six self-consumption photovoltaic plants remained up

Puertollano, Montornés, Pinto, Huelva, Algeciras and 
Alcudia (1,565 MWh, which avoided the emission of 744 
tonnes of CO2).

5.3.4. Sale of electricity and gas 

As part of its multi-energy strategy, Repsol supplies gas 
and electricity with a significant low-emissions origin in 
Spain and Portugal, offering its customers cutting-edge 
digital solutions and energy management initiatives 
through its own retail company and other platforms 
(Gana Energía, CHC Energía, ODF Energía, and Ekiluz).

Performance: profitable growth
Performance in 2025 was marked by portfolio growth 
(an additional 532,000 customers compared with 2024) 
and an increase in total margin, despite higher 
procurement costs and system cost overruns following 
the blackout.

Volumes sold at the end of the year amounted to 8,568 
GWh of electricity (6,735 GWh in 2024) and 2,239 GWh 
of gas (1,900 GWh in 2024),  driven by the greater 
number of customers.

At year-end 2025, Repsol had a portfolio of 3.0 million 
customers, including: 131 thousand from Portugal, 460 
thousand from Gana Energía, 326 thousand from CHC 
Energía, 23 thousand from ODF Energía, and 4 thousand 
from Ekiluz.

In July, Repsol reached an agreement to acquire 70% of 
ODF Energía, an electricity and gas retailer with a long 
track record in this sector as a growth platform in the 
SME segment. The deal has allowed Repsol to 
strengthen its position in this segment, accessing an 
initial portfolio of 22,000 customers.

14In Spain, prices continue to be regulated for piped and bottled LPG of between 8 and 20 kg with a tare weight above 9 kg, excluding bottled 

mixtures for using LPG as fuel. For more information on the legal framework applicable in Spain, see Appendix III of the 2025 consolidated 
Financial Statements.

In April, a new functionality was launched in Waylet 
(Autocharge), allowing for charging without using the 
app, simply by plugging in the cable. It is available for 
1,130 connectors and 1,030 charging points.

In June, a new electricity tariff for electric vehicles was 
launched, with Movistar+ free for one year, and the 
Zunder network, with more than 1,200 charging 
points, was also integrated into Waylet.

In December, strategic agreements were signed with 
Mazda, Toyota and KIA to jointly promote sustainable 
mobility and energy efficiency in Spain. In the case of 
the agreements with Mazda and KIA, the collaboration 
focuses on proposing comprehensive charging 
solutions for their customers, and, in the case of KIA, 
also on the possibility of developing publicly 
accessible charging infrastructure in its dealer 
network. The agreement with Toyota is based on the 
development of multi-energy collaboration projects 
(electric charging, electricity supply, renewable fuels, 
photovoltaic generation, emissions compensation and 
energy efficiency measures) for Toyota customers, 
dealers and employees in Spain.

In addition, Repsol has taken part in events to champion 
sustainable mobility, including the VEM fair in Madrid 
and the sponsorship of the Eco-rally in A Coruña, where 
charging points were installed for more than 50 electric 
vehicles.

5.3.5. New businesses 

Distributed generation
Repsol’s strategy in the Distributed Generation business 
encompasses various models, including Solar360, 
collective self-consumption, Energy Communities, and 
individual self-consumption. During 2025, the following 
milestones were achieved:

• Through Solar360, photovoltaic panels are being 

installed on the rooftops of buildings at the Tarragona 
complex, with an installed capacity of 830 kW. They aim 
to generate 1.2 GWh annually, equivalent to the 
average consumption of 350 households. This project 
will be rolled out to all industrial sites across the 
Iberian Peninsula.

• Significant progress has been made in the promotion 

and construction of collective self-consumption 
communities, both in the form of Local Energy 
Communities and Solar Communities, reaching 65 MWp 
of installed capacity. This enables the supply of locally 
generated solar energy (km 0) to more than 135,000 
households across Spain.

• Moreover, the Zaragoza City Council has awarded 

Repsol the contract to install solar power systems at 
four public car parks, with a total capacity of 2.8 MW 
and 40 charging points. These new solar communities 
will supply municipal facilities and more than 260,000 
households with locally generated energy.

Electric mobility
In 2025, Repsol installed 3,648 publicly accessible 
electric charging points (795 more than in 2024), of 
which 3,013 are up and running, and Waylet users 
already have 6,500 charging points available to them 
(adding the charging points of other interoperated 
networks).

Throughout 2025, Repsol made progress in electric 
mobility with innovative solutions and strategic 
alliances:

• Since January, Repsol has remained Redeia's sole 
partner in electric mobility in Spain, with a 3-year 
contract for the management of 167 charging points.

Main events in the period

Repsol remains focused on its strategy, with a model 
rooted in entering project development during the early 
stages and valorizing the assets by bringing in partners 
or through portfolio optimization, with notable asset 
rotations in Spain and the United States having taken 
place in 2025, which may allow higher returns.

It also completed construction and first power was 
delivered at the Antofagasta project, one of the largest 
in Chile, with a total installed operating capacity of 
364 MW.

In December, Repsol commissioned its first solar 
hybridization project in Spain, which will add 82 MW of 
complementary solar capacity to the wind assets of the 
Delta I project in Aragon (41 MW already in operation). 
This expansion improves the management of the energy 
generated, optimizes the use of existing infrastructure, 
and increases the operational flexibility of the asset.

In 2025, Repsol added 2,154 MW of renewable 
operating capacity with respect to the previous year, 
ending the year with a portfolio of 5,813 MW of wind, 
solar and hydroelectric installed capacity.

Meanwhile, a 50-year extension was granted for the 
water concession on the Torina River (Cantabria) for the 
use of hydro energy storage at the Aguayo I and II 
pumped-storage power plants.

Last but not least, the Escatrón combined-cycle plant 
obtained authorization for 400 MW under a self-
consumption regime to hybridize its facility with 
renewable energy, enabling it to supply power to a data 
storage project promoted by a third party.

Asset rotation in Spain and the United 
States
In April, Repsol completed the sale of a stake in its 400 
MW wind and solar portfolio in exchange for €114 
million. The portfolio includes eight wind farms, with a 
total capacity of 300 MW, in the provinces of Huesca, 
Zaragoza, and Teruel, in northern Spain, as well as two 
solar plants, with a total capacity of 100 MW, in the 
province of Palencia. Repsol retains control of the 
assets. As part of the transaction, in December 2024 
Repsol signed a long-term syndicated financing 
agreement for €348 million.

In July, Repsol completed the sale to Stonepeak of a 
percentage stake in a company that owns a portfolio of 
renewable assets (777 MW solar and storage, in New 
Mexico and Texas) in the United States, in exchange for 
$340 million. Both companies will jointly control the 
company. The operation includes the Frye solar farm 
and the Jicarilla solar and storage complex.

In December, Repsol reached a second agreement with 
Stonepeak for the sale of a percentage stake in a 
company that owns a renewable asset project (629 MW, 
in Texas) in the United States, in exchange for $252.5 
million. Both companies will jointly control the company. 
The transaction will go ahead once regulatory clearance 
has been obtained (See note 19 of the Consolidated 
Financial Statements).

Development progress 
in the United States

In 2025, construction began on Pecan Prairie, a 595 MW 
solar plant located in the state of Texas, which will help 
the Company achieve its plans of reaching 2–3 GW of 
installed capacity  in the country.

The capacity ramp-up at Outpost (which had got 
underway in late 2024) was successfully completed, and 
in the fourth quarter 692 MW at the Pinnington solar 
plant was commissioned. 

Meanwhile, Repsol reached an agreement with Hecate 
Holdings for the sale of 40% of Hecate Energy, thus 
ending the disputes and lawsuits between the two 
parties. This will allow both companies to independently 
advance their respective strategies. For more 
information on the agreement with Hecate Holdings LLC 
to end the dispute related to the investment in Hecate 
Energy Group LLC, see Note 20.2 “Litigation” to the 2025 
consolidated Financial Statements.

Commissioning of projects 
in Spain and Chile

During the period, Repsol began the electricity 
production phase at the Antofagasta wind farm (with 
generation activity levels below its capacity, and 
expected to reach its anticipated generation level in 
2026), its largest wind farm to date and one of the 
largest in Chile. Located in the commune of Taltal, in the 
Antofagasta region, it has a total installed capacity of 
364 MW. With an investment of around €400 million, 
Antofagasta is the first renewable project fully 
developed by Repsol in Chile.

In 2025, operational solar capacity increased by 304 
MW, while wind capacity increased by 393 MW in Spain.

In July, Repsol acquired 805 MW of wind farms from 
Forestalia with the aim of promoting the largest energy 
hybridization project in Spain. Under this project, Repsol’s 
combined-cycle power plant in Escatrón (804 MW) would 
be hybridized with the 15 wind farms acquired from 
Forestalia. This project aims to make it possible to 
harness the synergies of both energy sources, stabilize 
supply, maximize use of the grid connection point, and 
optimize infrastructure and maintenance.

Financing

In November, Repsol signed a long-term syndicated 
financing agreement worth €550 million for various 
wind and solar renewable assets in Spain (706 MW).

Main assets

Repsol is a significant player in the Spanish electricity 
generation market, with a total installed operating 
capacity of 8,030 MW (5,876 MW in 2024), representing a 
37% increase compared with December 31, 2024.

plant (Aguayo II), taking advantage of the existing lower 
and upper reservoirs.

Algeciras (Cádiz) and the other in Escatrón (Zaragoza), 
with a combined capacity of 1,625 MW, as well as its 
cogeneration plants located at the Group’s industrial 
complexes in Tarragona, Santander, Cartagena, Bilbao, 
Puertollano, and La Coruña in relation to its Chemical and 
Refining activity, with a combined capacity of 592 MW.

Performance: higher production and 
prices

Electricity production amounted to 11,584 GWh in 
2025, compared with 7,785 GWh in 2024 (excluding the 
volume corresponding to cogeneration plants). This 
increase is due to greater operational capacity in wind 
and solar assets and the higher production of combined 
cycles.

Renewable generation was up 34% versus 2024.

Electricity market prices in 2025 remained at levels 
similar to those recorded in2024. However, the monthly 
distribution underwent significant changes, including in 
relation to the time profile of the market and also the 
relative performance of the various generation 
technologies, depending on seasonal factors and 
availability of resources.

5.5 Technology 
and digitalization

Technology (focused on low emissions) and 
digitalization (driving AI and Generative AI) are key 
levers for business transformation.

Technology 

Repsol Technology Lab, Repsol's R+D center, worked 
closely with the different businesses on more than 250 
projects in 2025, 61% with the aim of lowering emissions. 
Key highlights:

• 100% renewable gasoline: a technological milestone 
was achieved by producing, for the first time on an 
industrial scale, 100% renewable gasoline from organic 
waste at the Tarragona industrial complex. The design 
allows for an effective deployment in the market, with 
improved properties responding to the premium 
quality expected of the Neotech range of fuels.

• E-Fuels: technological development continues for the 

production of synthetic fuels at the Bilbao 
Decarbonization Hub Demonstration Plant, which is 
scheduled to start in 2026.

• Circular Ethylene (Plastic2Olefins): development of 

chemical recycling technology for the production of 
olefins. Conceptual engineering completed.

• De-risking of first and second generation technologies 
for the production of electrolytic hydrogen produced 
using water and renewable energy. 

•

ICE (Internal Combustion Engine) High Efficiency 
Demonstrator Vehicle: a project that demonstrates 
that it is possible to use renewable fuels efficiently in 
light vehicles, through a propulsion system based on a 
combustion engine, providing a technically and 
economically competitive solution compared with 
other alternatives.

• AI-based autonomous optimizers: end of the initial 

phase of the first Autonomous Optimizer with Control 
(OAC) associated with one of the sections of the glycol 
plant developed entirely by Repsol. 

• Characterization of materials using multiphysics 

imaging: use of hyperspectral cameras and advanced 
algorithmics to characterize new raw materials, 
including municipal solid waste.

• All4Zero, the innovation hub founded by ArcelorMittal, 
Holcim, Iberia and Repsol, which brings together 28 
collaborating entities, is consolidating its position as a 
platform for scaling technologies aimed at 
decarbonization and the circular economy in industrial 
settings. In 2025, it launched four new technological 
challenges and five proofs of concept, and scheduled a 
further three.

• Corporate Venturing investments in Stargate 

Hydrogen OÜ, a start-up with second-generation 
alkaline electrolyzer technology, and Tonic Security 
Ltd, a cybersecurity company. Moreover, convertible 
loans were converted for the companies Sybolid 
Identity S.L. and Qilimanjaro Quantum Tech S.L., both 
engaged in digital identity and quantum computing 
technologies, respectively.

National Innovation Award 2024

Repsol has been recognized with the 2024 National 
Innovation Award by the Spanish Ministry of Science, 
Innovation and Universities “as the leading exponent 
of the ground-breaking transformation of innovation 
in Spain and the world and the undisputed architect 
of the scale of global change that the energy 
transition, decarbonization and the development of 
clean energies have reached”

 Digitalization 

Key figures for the digital program

More than 1,300 digital 
initiatives

More than 75 generative 
AI initiatives.

In 2025, Repsol reached new milestones in its digital 
transformation, having launched more than 1,300 initiatives 
since the start of the Digital Program (end-2017). This 
progress helps to cement the Company’s position as a 
benchmark in innovation within the energy sector. 

The interest in to Generative and Agentic AI is reflected in 
the Competence Center, which, after two years, has 
promoted more than 75 initiatives. 

Key milestones include the launch of the Multi-Agent 
Platform, a pioneering initiative worldwide. Developed in 
collaboration with Accenture, it already has 34 Agents 
organized across three Workflows, allowing teams to 
create work environments where AI agents self-organize, 
coordinate, and solve tasks autonomously, providing 
efficiency and new capabilities in key areas of the business.

The impact of these technologies is reflected in the 
businesses, with Generative AI being applied at Upstream 
to give just one example, and with initiatives such as 
Automated Well Data Ingestion, which automates daily 
drilling and geology reports, integrating data into 
corporate systems and accelerating decision-making in 
exploration.

At industrial, notable solutions include Transcribex, 
allowing for the automatic transcription of audio and 
video meetings or Email 2.0, which organizes and 
classifies emails with advanced algorithms, improving 
documentary efficiency, or Regulatory Management, to 
automate regulatory analysis with generative AI, 
improving efficiency, traceability, and regulatory compliance 
through intelligent assistants and legal bases.

At the Customer area, Energy Buddy centralizes 
information and tools for the sales force, allowing Repsol 
to generate personalized simulations and offer tailor-made 
recommendations for our customers. Meanwhile, the 
Transversal Virtual Assistant aims to integrate customer 
service across all channels (WhatsApp, website, apps, and 
telephone), resolving queries efficiently and swiftly, 
centralizing management, and offering a coherent 
experience for the customer.

In the corporate sphere, we are moving forward with Alex 
2.0, which, following the incorporation of Generative AI, is 
now more interactive and responsive, more effectively 
satisfying the needs of employees. Further highlights 
include RDD2, aimed at third-party due diligence, with the 
aim of automating and optimizing compliance processes 
and control in risk management through Generative AI.

6. Sustainability 

This chapter provides a summary of the Consolidated Statement of Non-Financial Information and 

Sustainability Information set out in Appendix IV to this document. This appendix complies with the 

relevant requirements under the European Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD), and 

under Spanish Law 11/2018, on non-financial information and diversity (still in force). 

6.1 General disclosures 

Governance

Repsol’s commitment to sustainability is reflected in its 
governance structure, in which the leadership of the 
Board of Directors and the Executive Committee is 
accompanied by the oversight function of the Board’s 
Sustainability and Audit and Control Committees, as well 
as the coordination and expert advice of the Sustainability 
Department (SD). The Sustainability Coordination 
Committee—set up in 2025 and featuring representatives 
from the various businesses and corporate areas—
supports the operational integration of sustainability 
throughout the Organization. Its function is to streamline 
the identification and management of material impacts, 
risks and opportunities, facilitating decision-making and 
improving the alignment and connection between expert 
areas and businesses. This governance model allows 
social, environmental and governance criteria to be 
integrated into decision-making processes in line with its 
good governance responsibilities.

To help with the Organization’s alignment with this 
commitment, the remuneration of Repsol employees is 
linked to the achievement of sustainability and 
decarbonization objectives. In 2025, these objectives 
were incorporated into those of each business unit, 
reaching a weighting between 20% and 45%, which, 
together with other objectives, determine the 
employee’s variable remuneration. In the case of the 
CEO, these targets accounted for 25% of his annual 
variable remuneration. In addition, in the Long-Term 
Incentive (LTI) for those employees entitled to receive it 
for the 2025–2028 period, 40% of the targets are linked 
to the energy transition. This approach allows for 
sustainability to be part of Repsol’s performance and 
compensation evaluation criteria.

Repsol has a Code of Ethics and Business Conduct, 
approved by the Board of Directors, following a 
favorable report from the Audit and Control Committee, 
the Sustainability Committee, and the Ethics and 
Compliance Committee. This code sets forth the 
minimum standards of conduct that must guide the 
behavior of all employees and actors along the 
Company’s value chain.

Within the framework of this standard, Repsol has 
fourteen policies, which make up the Company’s 
regulatory corpus that define public commitment and 
management fundamentals, establishing principles and 
guidelines for action for all its employees with the 
purpose of fostering relationships, processes, and 
decision-making in line with the Company’s values. 

Among the policies related to sustainability, the most 
notable are those on Sustainability, Environment, 
People Management, Occupational Health and Safety, 
Human Rights and Community Relations, Tax, and 
Privacy and Personal Data Protection.

Identification of impacts, risks 
and opportunities 

The double materiality assessment, carried out as 
required under the CSRD, has made it possible to 
identify the impacts, risks and opportunities that are 
material in environmental, social and governance 
aspects.

The results show a number of positive impacts, most of 
which are concentrated in the social sphere (own 
personnel, workers in the value chain and affected 
groups) and in governance (business conduct). Most of 
the negative material impacts are in the field of 
environment, especially in relation to climate change, 
water, soil, and air pollution, water management, and 
biodiversity. For all of them, the actions the Company is 
taking to mitigate adverse impacts and enhance positive 
ones are described throughout this section. 

Key risks include those related to climate change 
mitigation, potential regulatory changes, competition 
and reduced sales, adoption of new technologies, 
shortages of raw materials, and decoupling of 
investment in decarbonization.

Likewise, material risks linked to industrial safety, 
cybersecurity, and operational continuity are identified, 
including accidents in facilities, physical or digital attacks, 
vulnerabilities to cyberattacks, and possible operational 
interruptions caused by blockages or incidents in the 
facilities, as well as regulatory non-compliance in areas 
such as accounting, tax, financial, and sustainability, 
including safety and environment, artificial intelligence, 
intellectual property, and other regulations specific to 
the markets in which Repsol operates.

However, the energy transition also offers opportunities 
for the Group, such as optimization through energy 
efficiency, which can increase profitability and 
competitiveness in the market, or investment in low 
carbon-intensity energies. Moreover, access to aid for 
sustainable financing helps to improve alignment with 
sustainability objectives and allows entities to raise 
capital with an ESG risk focus such as Next Generation EU 
funds, Innovation Fund Large Scale, ICO, etc. 

Repsol has defined objectives and taken steps to 
mitigate risks and seize opportunities, as described in 
the following sections of this chapter.

6.2 Environmental information 

6.2.1. Climate change

Repsol’s energy transition strategy is aligned with the 
goal set out in the Paris Agreement to limit global 
temperature to below 2 °C compared with pre-industrial 
levels, making every effort not to exceed 1.5 °C by the 
end of this century. To succeed in this task, it relies on 
various enabling levers, with the aim of steadily 
decarbonizing its own activities (reducing its Scope 1 
and 2 emissions), while also helping to decarbonize the 
value chain, especially for consumers, through the 
development of a portfolio of low- or zero-carbon fuels 
that progressively displaces those of mineral origin 
(thus reducing its Scope 3, category 11 emissions). 
Technological evolution and regulation in the markets in 
which Repsol operates are key and required so that the 
necessary investments deliver an adequate economic 
return.  

In this context, Repsol has the goal of reaching net-zero 
greenhouse gas emissions by 2050, in Scopes 1, 2, and 3. 
The Company has also set two interim absolute 
emissions reduction targets for 2030: direct emissions 
down by 55% compared with 2016, and total emissions 
down by 20% compared with 2018, considering in 
Scope 3 those emissions associated with category 11, 
based on sales of gas and petroleum products. These 
emissions currently account for more than 90% of the 
Company’s total emissions along its value chain (Scopes 
1, 2 and 3).

Since the energy transition involves reducing emissions, 
while also meeting society’s energy demand, 

the Company also uses an internal Carbon Intensity 
Indicator (CII) as a complementary metric to assess 
progress towards decarbonization per unit of energy 
produced and which allows the effect of the key 
decarbonization pillars of its strategy, such as the 
production of electricity and renewable fuels, to be 
incorporated. Repsol has set targets for reducing the CII 
compared with 2016 by 15% in 2025, 25(±1)% by 2030, 
55% by 2040, and 100% by 2050. 

The alignment of Repsol’s energy transition strategy 
with the objective of limiting global warming to 1.5 °C 
above pre-industrial levels can be assessed by reference 
to the C1 scenarios developed by the IPCC 
(Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change). These 
scenarios show a probability greater than 50% of limiting 
global warming to 1.5 °C in 2100 without exceeding this 
limit, or with only limited overshoot during the 21st 
century, and are consistent with global net-zero 
emissions pathways by 2050. The median reduction in oil 
and gas emissions under these scenarios in 2030 is 14% 
compared with 2019.

Repsol’s target of reducing absolute Scope 1, 2 and 3 
emissions by 20% by 2030 compared with 2018 is 
therefore consistent with the IPCC C1 climate scenarios 
that limit global warming to 1.5 °C and achieve net-zero 
emissions by 2050.

The following table shows the decarbonization metrics 
set, their respective targets, and the degree of progress 
made in 2025.15

As can be seen in the table, the CII stood at 65.2 gCO₂e/
MJ, down 15.1% on 2016, meaning that the Company 
has met its CII reduction target.

Repsol has reached the target         
set for 2025 of reducing the Carbon 
Intensity Indicator (CII) by 15% compared 
with 2016

Meanwhile, the methane intensity in 2025 was 0.12%, 
also reaching the target set (0.2%), as well as the target 
for reducing routine flaring emissions set at 50%, which 
stood at 86% in 2025.

Over recent months, and after meeting all its 
decarbonization targets for 2025, Repsol has reviewed 
certain metrics and targets from its latest strategic 
update (now included in this report) in order to adapt 
them to the new global energy context, prevailing 
trends in technology, regulations and demand, and the 
pace of the energy transition. This has been done 
without relinquishing its goal to remain on a 
decarbonization pathway toward net-zero greenhouse 
gas emissions by 2050 and limiting warming to no more 
than 1.5 °C by the end of this century, supported by 
investments that are profitable under these new 
operating conditions.

As a result of these adjustments for 2030, the reduction 
target for the CII has been set at 25(±1)%.16

Repsol’s decarbonization objectives are supported by a 
strategy predicated on the following pillars: 

Pillar 1: Operational excellence and asset portfolio 
management

Energy efficiency, electrification of processes, reduction 
of direct emissions of CO2 and methane from operations 
and adaptation of production and the portfolio of 
Upstream assets towards less carbon-intensive assets 
are key to the decarbonization of the Company’s assets 
and a lever for achieving the goal of reducing Scope 1 
and 2 emissions by 55% by 2030. Furthermore, the 
allocation of capital to the Upstream business is 
consistent with stable production levels in the range of 
550–600 kboe/d throughout this decade. Priority is 
given, as indicated earlier, to investment in assets and 
projects commanding a higher economic margin and 
with lower greenhouse gas emissions, which may allow 
the Company to reduce its carbon intensity over the 
coming years, reaching a carbon intensity of 10 kgCO2e/
boe in 2027. At its industrial businesses (Refining and 
Chemicals), the Company expects to continue 
implementing energy efficiency measures, process 
electrification and increased use of renewable energy.

Pillar 2: Renewable electricity generation

Renewable generation is key to Repsol’s energy 
transition to reduce emissions per unit of energy 
produced. Since the start of this activity in 2018, Repsol 
has built a portfolio of renewable electricity projects 
(wind, solar and hydroelectric), with 5.8 GW of installed 
capacity in 2025 and with the goal of exceeding 
10 GW17 by 2030, especially in Spain and the United 
States. Growth in the coming years has been adjusted to 
the evolution of the environment, marked by higher 
development and financing costs, as well as tax 
incentives in the United States, prioritizing investments 
based on the profitability thresholds set by Repsol. The 
strategy envisages the rotation of the asset portfolio, 
bringing in partners and securing project financing, to 
maximize value generation and support better rates of 
return on net investments.

Pillar 3: Liquid and gaseous renewable fuels 

The competitiveness of Repsol’s refineries, in the first 
European quartile by economic margin, facilitates their 
transformation, aligned with the demand trends 
promoted by the European regulatory framework on 
climate matters applies, in line with the European Green 
Deal and its implementation through the Fit for 55 
legislative package and with a focus on the circular 
economy, with the use of organic waste to produce 
renewable fuels and the recycling of plastics. Until 2030, 
refineries will maintain their activity high due to the 
resilience shown by demand in Spain and the selective 
expansion to adjacent markets, while increasing the 
production of renewable fuels. 

Repsol expects to reach production capacity16 by 2030 
of 1.6–1.8 Mt of renewable fuels, 0.7–0.8 TWh of 
biomethane and 0.6–0.8 GWe of renewable hydrogen, 
marking certain adjustments to the metrics forecast in 
2024 in response to prevailing demand and regulatory 
trends, especially in relation to renewable hydrogen.

Pillar 4: Multi-energy for customers

Repsol is moving towards a multi-energy model, 
focused on the supply of different energy sources to 
adapt to the needs of its customers and to 
decarbonization objectives in its markets of Spain and 
Portugal. 

On the Iberian Peninsula, Repsol has developed one of 
the most extensive 100% renewable fuels networks in 
Europe, with availability at more than 1,550 service 
stations in Spain and Portugal. In Spain, 56% of the 
network offers multi-energy solutions, also including 
more than 3,600 public electric vehicle charging points.

The Company also develops the sustainable aviation 
fuel (SAF) market and already has a 100% renewable 
fuel production plant at its Cartagena refinery, with a 
capacity of 250,000 metric tons per year of co-
production of renewable road diesel and SAF produced 
from oily waste.

In the field of residential energy marketing, Repsol has 
3 million electricity and gas customers in Spain and 
Portugal, with the expectation of reaching 5 million 
by 2030.

16 For more information, see Appendix IV, section 2.1 Climate change.

17 Data revised as a result of the update to the 2026–2028 projections.

Pillar 5. Development of carbon sinks

Carbon sinks represent a pillar that will not be material 
for the Company at least until after 2030. Until then, 
Repsol’s activity is limited to reducing technical, 
regulatory, and business risks through its participation 
in certain projects at an early stage of development.

Repsol’s decarbonization pathway considers the long-
term contribution of carbon sinks (carbon capture and 
storage projects and, eventually, nature-based solutions 
if required to complete net-zero GHG emissions 
by 2050).

To carry out all these initiatives, Repsol plans to invest in 
low‑carbon activities, with the level of investment 
adjusted to the prevailing macroeconomic environment, 
technological developments, regulatory changes, 
project maturity, and progress in asset rotation and 
planned divestments.

Beyond 2030, Repsol conducts resilience analyses of its 
long-term decarbonization strategy and economic 
performance in response to risks arising from the energy 
transition. To this end, it develops scenarios, with 
projections of the main business metrics, under conditions 
of global demand for various energy sources 
corresponding to scenarios of organizations specialized in 
such matters, and demand for energy products in their 
assets in the Iberian Peninsula consistent with the latest 
regulations stemming from the European Green Deal. This 
resilience analysis carried out on the evolution of the 
businesses according to the assumptions of the scenarios 
used, shows that the proposed strategy is resilient to the 
varying paces of the energy transition. 

See Appendix IV, section 2.1 Climate change.

6.2.2. Pollution prevention 

Repsol has established commitments to prevent and 
control polluting emissions into the air, water, and soil, 
minimizing the environmental impact of its operations 
and products, as set out in the Environmental Policy. 

In 2025, a new air emissions target was adopted: to 
reduce nitrogen oxide (NOx) emissions by 30% by 2035 
compared with 2018 in the Refining and Chemicals 
complexes in the Iberian Peninsula.

With regard to the objective set for 2024 to define 
action plans within a period of less than 12 months for 
the management and remediation of soil or 
groundwater in the event of contamination, it should be 
noted its compliance in its first year of implementation.

New target to reduce NOx emissions by 
30% in 2035 in Refining and Chemicals 
complexes in the Iberian Peninsula 
compared with 2018

Repsol continues to implement other actions for 
environmental protection and operational improvement. 
In 2025, progress has been made in the implementation 
of statistical control of fuel inventories at service stations 
owned by and operated by third parties, with progressive 
expansion to new stations. Redundant equipment has 
been installed and improvements have been made to the 
facilities for the control of air emissions, including the 
adaptation of abatement systems (regenerative thermal 
oxidation) in chemical plants. Likewise, improvements 
were made for the protection of the soil, such as the 
replacement of chemical product tanks, measures to 
prevent spills and waterproofing works. In terms of 
prevention of spills and discharges into the water, the 
integrity of the Multibuoy No. 1 Terminal was reinforced 
at La Pampilla, through the installation of automatic 
safety valves, subsea manual valves and pile-supported 
frames on the seabed, with similar actions projected for 
Terminal No. 3 in 2026.

See Appendix IV, section 2.2 Pollution prevention.

6.2.3. Water and marine 
resources 

Repsol assesses, through its own tool known as the 
Repsol Water Tool (RWT), the material impacts, risks, 
and opportunities related to water in the Company’s 
own operations. It also relies on the World Resource 
Institute’s (WRI) Aqueduct Water Risk Atlas to identify 
physical risks related to water stress, in the short and 
long term.

The main actions carried out in the assets include 
initiatives to increase water efficiency and operational 
improvement; water recycling and reuse; use of 
alternative sources and water offset measures. Its 
implementation contributes to the fulfillment of the 
commitments established in the Environmental Policy, 
specifically to the reduction of freshwater withdrawal 
from the environment, especially in areas at water risk, in 
the vicinity of the operations.

Net zero freshwater withdrawal from the 
environment for the Refining and Chemicals 
facilities in the Iberian Peninsula by 2050 
and for the Marcellus asset of the Upstream 
business by 2035, both compared with 2022

Notable progress was made in 2025 in line with the plan 
to achieve the established interim milestones 
compatible with the ambition of reaching net zero 
freshwater withdrawal of conventional sources from the 
environment by 2050 for the Refining and Chemicals 
facilities located on the Iberian Peninsula, and by 2035 
for the Marcellus Upstream asset in the United States. In 
2025, the indicator for freshwater withdrawal of 
conventional sources from the environment amounted 
to 39.7 Hm³ for Refining and Chemicals on the Iberian 
Peninsula and 0.27 Hm³ for Marcellus, representing 
reductions of 10.4% and 84.1%18, respectively, 
compared with the 2022 baseline value.

See Appendix IV, section 2.3 Water and marine 
resources.

18 To a large extent, this reduction percentage is due to a decrease in completion activity at this asset.
