6.2.4. Biodiversity 
and ecosystems 

Together with the double materiality assessment, 
Repsol has identified the Company’s sites with a 
material impact on biodiversity and ecosystems 
following TNFD’s LEAP approach. Those that affect 
sensitive areas, in terms of biodiversity, have also been 
identified, as they are located in or near them, For more 
information, see Appendix IV, section 2.4 Biodiversity 
and ecosystems.

At these sites, actions have been implemented for 
ecosystem restoration and reforestation, to promote 
knowledge of biodiversity and ecosystems, and to 
provide compensation measures. Through these actions, 
the Company contributes to the achievement of the 
commitments set out in the Environmental Policy, in 
particular the protection and conservation of 
biodiversity, ecosystems, and associated ecosystem 
services, as well as the management of impacts on and 
dependencies on them.

New biodiversity targets 

1. To improve the conservation status of 
endangered species 

2. To develop biodiversity action plans in 
biodiversity-sensitive areas or protected 
areas

In 2025, Repsol voluntarily established two measurable 
objectives related to biodiversity and ecosystems to 
address their impacts, risks, and material opportunities, 
aligned with the commitments established in the 
Environmental Policy:

•

Improve the conservation status of at least five 
threatened species by 2030 through the financing, 
implementation and monitoring of projects.

• Have biodiversity action plans or equivalent 

surveillance, monitoring and control plans for tangible 
operated assets that are located in biodiversity-
sensitive areas or protected areas.

See Appendix IV, section 2.4 Biodiversity and 
ecosystems.

6.2.5. Use of resources 
and circular economy 

Repsol has adopted a series of commitments through its 
Environmental Policy to integrate the principles of the 
circular economy into its processes, products, and 
services, which include optimizing the use of resources 
and raw materials, as well as promoting the use of 
alternative and renewable resources obtained in a 
sustainable way. This policy also promotes the 
development of new products with less environmental 
impact and extends to the value chain, encompassing 

19 The circular economy targets for 2030 have been updated: from achieving a renewable fuel production capacity on the Iberian Peninsula from 
more than 2.2 million tons to between 1.6 and 1.8 million tons; from producing more than 150,000 tons of circular or biological origin products   
to between 125,000 and 146,000 tons; and from achieving a biomethane production capacity from organic waste of more than 2.1 TWh to 
between 0.7 and 0.8 TWh, following the update of the 2026–2028 projections.

6.3 Social information 

6.3.1. Own employees 

Repsol has 24,679 employees with the capabilities 
needed to address the energy transition processes and 
the transformation of its business models. People 
management is a pillar that contributes to this process, 
acting as a lever to drive innovation, competitiveness, 
and the aforementioned business transformation.

In this context, the Organization is focused on  the 
development and well-being of its employees by 
aligning people management policies with the corporate 
strategy and promoting a positive, safe, and inclusive 
work environment based on respect for human and 
labor rights, as well as on social dialogue and 
cooperation with employee representatives. To this end, 
Repsol has policies such as the People Management 
Policy and the Occupational Health and Safety Policy, 
among others, which enshrine these commitments.

Repsol’s Global Diversity and Inclusion Framework 
establishes the principles and commitments related to 
equal treatment and opportunities, diversity, equity, and 
non-discrimination in line with the People Policy. Based 
on this framework, objectives and initiatives have been 
defined to promote work environments that comply 
with applicable regulations and encourage the 
participation of people with diverse profiles, ensuring 
responsible diversity management. Some examples of 
these initiatives are the internal Winer Network and 
campaigns promoting respectful and inclusive 
environments for the LGTBI+ community.

In line with the policy, Repsol is committed to attracting, 
developing, and hiring young talent, promoting 
professional opportunities from the early stages of 
training and professional development through 
structured Dual Vocational Training programs, academic 
internships, and Graduate programs. This commitment is 
part of a comprehensive talent management approach 
aimed at offering fair working conditions and 
strengthening employability throughout a professional 
career. In this regard, Repsol promotes the attraction, 
retention, and development of talent through a value 
proposition that considers not only compensation, but 
also benefits, well-being, safety, flexibility, and work–life 
balance. At the same time, it strengthens employability 
through specific training and continuing education plans 
that supports professional development and adaptation 
to future challenges. Since 2024, Repsol has rolled out 
the Company’s talent program as a strategic lever to 
anticipate future needs, attract and retain critical 
profiles, foster continuous learning, evolve leadership, 
and strengthen organizational culture. In 2025, Repsol 
expanded the training offering of Repsol University with 
new courses to support the professional development 
of its employees, expanding and consolidating 54 areas 
of knowledge.

Repsol has set two new targets: 
consolidate an attractive value 
proposition for employees and improve 
the employability of the workforce 
through the training offer

See Appendix IV, section 3.1 Own workforce.

6.3.2. Workers in the 
value chain 

Repsol’s value chain includes suppliers, partners, 
distributors, and customers, marketing a wide range of 
products in more than 90 countries, covering activities 
such as Upstream oil and gas, electricity production and 
sales, refining, chemicals, and product marketing.

Repsol manages its value chain in response to both 
stakeholder expectations and compliance with 
international ethical, labor, environmental, safety, and 
human rights standards. Protecting the rights of 
workers along the value chain is a key part of these 
efforts.

Among the corporate policies, the Code of Ethics and 
Business Conduct establishes the minimum principles 
that the Company expects from its suppliers, promoting 
their knowledge and acceptance before starting a 
contractual relationship and their compliance 
throughout the business relationship.

Repsol has a supply chain risk management model to 
identify, assess, and minimize environmental, social, and 
governance risks at different stages of the procurement 
and contracting process. This model consists of a due 
diligence phase to understand and assess the third 
parties it engages with, and establishes environmental, 
social, and governance requirements in the purchasing 
and contracting process (including specific clauses 
tailored to the nature of the purchase or service) and a 
supplier performance evaluation process, as well as 
audit and control mechanisms for the effective 
implementation of the entire process.

25,251 suppliers screened
from a reputational standpoint

As far as partners are concerned, Repsol conducts a 
complete due diligence process in each mergers and 
acquisitions (M&A) transaction. During this process, 
environmental, social and governance aspects are 
evaluated, among others.

If any such risks are identified, Repsol is able to 
introduce corrective or commitment clauses in the 
contract to achieve certain standards. In some specific 
cases, a contractual follow-up is included to verify this 
commitment after the contract is signed.

The Company endeavors to include local companies 
among its network of suppliers and contractors, thus 
promoting economic and social development in the 
areas where it operates. In 2025, 82% of the volume 
of purchases was made from local suppliers. Repsol also 
promotes social inclusion and sustainability in its supply 
chain by hiring special employment centers and training 
in ESG aspects for its suppliers.

Repsol has defined objectives for the coming years with 
the aim of strengthening good sustainability practices 
along the supply chain, while promoting the inclusion of 
safety and environmental criteria in the supplier 
management process and in all contracts for goods and 
services carrying a high safety and environmental risk.

See Appendix IV, section 3.2 Workers in the value chain.

6.3.3. Affected groups

Repsol incorporates respect for human rights as a core 
principle of its Human Rights and Community Relations 
Policy, which defines its actions in all the countries 
where it operates and along its value chain, and 
recognizes its responsibility to prevent, mitigate, and 
remediate the possible negative impacts of its activities 
on communities that may be affected. 

During the year, the objective of developing an action 
plan to comply with the EU Corporate Sustainability Due 
Diligence Directive was redefined, extending the scope of 
due diligence processes to encompass a greater number 
of activities, including those considered low risk.

Implementing an Action Plan 2025-2028 
to strengthen due diligence processes

The communities in the area of influence of the 
Company’s activities constitute one of the main 
stakeholders with whom Repsol maintains solid 
relationships through a proactive and transparent 
dialogue, seeking shared benefit and contribution to 
local, socioeconomic and environmental development. 

Before starting any new activity, a social baseline study 
is conducted to understand the context and specific 
circumstances and needs of the local community. 
Notably, indigenous communities are considered to be 
especially vulnerable to the Company’s activities. Repsol 
respects and values the uniqueness of these 
communities and their rights to lands and natural 
resources and applies Convention 169 of the 
International Labour Organization (ILO) in the 
relationship with these communities, whether or not it is 
incorporated into the legislation of each country.

The Company conducts social impact assessments and 
implements action plans through participatory and 
consultative processes, while also enabling grievance 
mechanisms, speak-up and dispute resolution channels. It 
also commits to remediate any impacts that may occur as 
a result of its activities.

With the aim of generating a positive impact on the 
environment, in addition to promoting local hiring and 
economic development through the acquisition of local 
goods and services, Repsol implements social 
investment projects that benefit the communities in 
areas of influence of its activity, as well as society as a 
whole.

Repsol aims to increase to 50% the 
average voluntary social investment 
associated with just transition in the 
2026-2030 period

See Appendix IV, section 3.3 Affected communities.

6.3.4. Consumers and end 
customers 

For Repsol, the people-centered multi-energy strategy 
is a key element in promoting the energy transition and 
decarbonization, integrating criteria of efficiency, 
profitability, and sustainability.

In 2025, the Company renewed its brand identity, 
unified protocols across all customer service channels, 
and carried out verifiable actions that provide a 
transparent and functional customer experience.

4,272,964 calls answered in Spain and 
Portugal, with a 89% level of service

Customer service is key to strengthening trust and 
materializing the multi-energy strategy. Repsol offers

on a voice of the customer platform that allows Repsol 
to measure customer satisfaction and apply continuous 
improvements. 

In 2025 alone, more than 4.3 million calls were handled 
in Spain and Portugal, with a service level of 89% and an 
average waiting time of 116 seconds. Furthermore, 
efforts were made to simplify communication, through 
the use of clearer and more relevant messages that 
consider the customer’s perspective, an evolution of 
customer service protocols and guides and a clearer and 
simpler transition to digital channels.

The digitalization process implemented by Repsol is a 
core pillar for optimizing the customer experience, 
combining quantitative and qualitative analysis, 
continuous research, and advanced systems such as the 
voice of the customer program and multi-energy 
tracking, which monitor preferences, behavior, and 
perception.

With regard to the safety of the products sold by 
Repsol, and in compliance with the European REACH 
and CLP regulations, products intended for end users 
have a 24-hour emergency telephone number on their 
labeling, intended to manage security incidents. In 2025, 
18 calls to the emergency telephone number have been 
registered, of which one related to a medium-severity 
incident and 17 related to incidents of no severity. No 
calls were made regarding incidents of low or high 
severity.

	See Appendix IV, section 3.4 Consumers and end 
customers.

6.3.5. Safety

In 2025, Repsol took further positive action to improve 
its safety performance, in response to the objectives set 
out in its Safety and Occupational Health Policy to 
improve the safety of its operations and protect the 
physical integrity of its employees and contractors.

Repsol is working towards the launch in 
2026 of the Life-Saving Rules campaign 
by IOGP to improve levels of prevention 
in critical activities

Repsol has reinforced its strategy for preventing serious 
accidents and reducing fatalities through the Safety 
Excellence Program. Among the main measures 
adopted, the progressive abandonment of the now 
obsolete "zero accidents" concept, the elimination of 
targets based on the total frequency rate and its 
replacement by indicators linked to fatalities and serious 
or permanent injuries, as well as the updating of the 
Company’s risk matrix to contemplate low-probability 
and high-impact scenarios. Moreover, greater awareness 
has been achieved regarding the reporting of high-

potential incidents, and the quality of investigations has 
improved through the incorporation of systemic root-
cause analysis and human factors. The Life-Saving Rules 
campaign by IOGP is also being rolled out to strengthen 
prevention in critical activities.

In parallel, the Company continues to carry out specific 
diagnoses of the safety culture in its assets and 
businesses every four or five years, with independent 
assessments that allow the identification of areas for 
improvement not detected in traditional audits. These 
diagnoses are considered key to preventing major 
accidents and were applied in 2025 in the Venezuela 
business unit, in the Mobility Division of Spain and 
Portugal, and in the industrial complexes of Cartagena 
(Spain) and La Pampilla (Peru). The earnings obtained 
facilitate the development of action plans adapted to the 
cultural reality of each business unit.

In relation to safety and security for people and 
facilities, Repsol has reviewed and updated its plans 
across all countries. These plans include risk mitigation 
measures and are based on ongoing analysis of the 
security situation at each site. The Company has worked 
in collaboration with its partners in the assets and, 
where necessary, with local security forces for the 
protection of its assets, operations, and people.

See Appendix IV, section 3.5 Safety and security.

6.4 Governance information

6.4.1. Business conduct

Repsol believes that ethical behavior and responsible 
practices are essential elements for the sustainable 
development of its activity and for the generation of 
long-term value for its shareholders and stakeholders. 

Code of Ethics and Business Conduct
Repsol’s Code of Ethics and Business Conduct applies to 
all directors, managers and employees, regardless of the 
type of contract that determines their professional or 
employment relationship, and establishes the 
framework of reference for understanding and 
implementing the behaviors and expectations that the 
Company has for each of them. Repsol’s commercial 
partners are an extension of the Company itself and, for 
this reason, must act in accordance with the code and 
any applicable contractual provisions when working on 
behalf or in collaboration with the Company. 

In 2025, a total of 22,126 employees completed the new 
annual mandatory online training on the Code of Ethics 
and Business Conduct.

Ethics and Compliance Committee 
and Channel 
The Ethics and Compliance Committee is a collegiate, 
high-level and multidisciplinary body whose remit 
includes managing the system for monitoring and 
ensuring compliance with the Company’s Code of Ethics 
and Business Conduct. In going about this task, it has 
standalone authority to take action where it sees fit and 
oversees compliance.

The Committee makes the Ethics and Compliance 
Channel available to employees and any third party who 
has something to report or share. This channel is an 
easy-to-access, confidential and, if desired and 
permitted by applicable legislation, anonymous medium 
through which suspected illegal or unethical behavior or 
potential non-compliance with the Code of Ethics and 
Business Conduct, internal regulations or applicable 
legislation can be reported.

In 2025, a total of 311 communications were received 
through the Ethics and Compliance Channel, of which 
75 were investigated at the initiative of the Committee.

Comprehensive Compliance Model
The Compliance function has a framework for action and 
a specialized team that helps to embed a culture of 
compliance and improve the identification and 
monitoring of ethics and compliance risks, especially on 
matters of integrity, money laundering and terrorist 
financing, crime prevention, international sanctions and 
embargoes, competition, and data privacy and 
protection.

Ethical culture, communication, and awareness are 
essential to strengthen Repsol’s Compliance Model. In 
this sense, communication and dissemination actions are 
carried out on internal regulations related to ethics and 
compliance, facilitating employees’ understanding and 
knowledge.

More than 360 employees are part of the 
People Compliance network, who act as 
compliance ambassadors and promote 
the ethical culture within the Company

Taxes paid

Repsol makes a significant tax contribution in the

Repsol’s Board of Directors has established principles of

processes, and controls for compliance.

6.4.3. Cybersecurity

Cybersecurity risks tend to increase worldwide, for 
reasons of a geopolitical and technological nature, 
which is why Repsol has continued to strengthen 
prevention, protection, and response measures.

The deployment of the Strategic Plan for Cybersecurity 
2024–2026 continued throughout 2025, with progress 
made toward its four workstreams: 

1. Strengthen the cybersecurity of critical 

infrastructures and maintain a high level of cyber 
resilience, in order to avoid operational incidents. 

2. Fortify the protection of digital information, the 

control of identities and the cloud for the protection 
of personal information. 

3. Improve the internal cybersecurity capabilities, both 

of the organization and staff, through process 
optimization, training, and awareness. 

4. Comply with new regulations and standards such as 

NIS2 and DORA.   

See Appendix IV, section 4.3 Cybersecurity.

and fiscal deterioration in advanced economies could 
erode the stability achieved. The resilience of the global 
economy has been greater than expected, but its future 
strength will depend on restoring a predictable trade 
framework, strengthening public finances, and 
sustaining investment in innovation and productivity.

Energy sector outlook 

In its latest monthly report for February 2026, Oil 
Market Report, the International Energy Agency (IEA) 
estimates an increase in global oil demand in 2026 of 
0.85 mbl/d, to 104.9 mbl/d. The set of non-OECD 
economies would account for the total growth 
estimated for this year, even offsetting the marginal 
decline expected in the OECD region. After European 
OECD countries maintained their consumption in 2025 
compared with the previous year, the IEA estimates a 
drop of just 70 kbl/d in 2026. 

The IEA notes that Chinese demand looks set to grow by 
220 kbl/d in 2026, having increased its consumption by 
just 210 kbl/d in 2025. In fact, the stagnation in Chinese 
oil demand growth is one of the reasons leading the IEA 
to project an oversupplied market in 2026. The other 
reason for this scenario is the health of non-OPEC 
supply, which in 2026 is expected to grow by 1.17 mbl/
d, an increase similar to that expected for OPEC crude 
oil and natural gas liquids (+1.22 mbl/d), placing global 
supply at 108.6 mbl/d, which would leave the global 
balance at an oversupply of around 3.68 mbl/d on 
average in 2026, the highest level in the agency's 
historical records (going back to 1986). 

Global supply/demand balance in 2026

7. Outlook 
7.1 Outlook for the sector

Macroeconomic outlook 

According to the IMF's latest projections (WEO October 
2025), the global economy has continued to show 
remarkable resilience despite rising trade and 
geopolitical tensions. After growing by 3.3% in 2024, 
global GDP is expected to moderate to 3.2% in 2025 
and 3.1% in 2026, just two tenths below the previous 
year's forecasts and well above the estimates made 
after the announcement of the new US tariffs. The 
impact of these measures has been more limited than 
expected, thanks to the extensive exemptions applied 
by Washington, the rapid reconfiguration of supply 
chains and the boost in private investment, especially in 
artificial intelligence and energy.

Even so, the IMF warns that considering the tariff 
episode closed would be premature. The effective rate 
of tariffs in the US remains high and part of the costs 
could be gradually passed on to the consumer. In 
addition, the cyclical boost from AI and public spending 
on defense and energy transition could sustain 
inflationary pressures for longer than expected. In this 
context, the IMF forecasts a sharper slowdown in the 
second half of 2025, with growth of 1.6% among 
advanced economies, 2.0% in the United States, and 
1.2% in the euro area, while emerging economies would 
grow by 4.2%.

Global inflation would continue to ease, albeit more 
gradually and with notable regional divergences. After 
reaching 5.7% in 2023 and slowing to 4.6% in 2024, the 
IMF projects 4.2% in 2025 and 3.7% in 2026. In most 
advanced economies, headline inflation would converge 
around the 2% target, while in emerging markets it 
would remain slightly higher due to currency 
depreciation and higher food prices.

in its forecasts: the escalation of trade tensions, the 
overvaluation of AI-linked assets, the fragility of the

Outlook

Appendices

In the longer term, the IEA notes in its 2025 World 
Energy Outlook document that geopolitical tensions, 
climate risks and the vulnerability of the energy system 
are placing energy at the center of economic and 
national security. It also states that, despite global 
fragility, oil prices remain contained due to a large 
supply surplus. Countries are seeking to strengthen 
their energy security in different ways: While some are 
focused on renewables and efficiency, others are 
prioritizing an abundant supply of traditional fuels. At 
the same time, energy trade is becoming even more 
important in a context of geopolitical fractures and high 
uncertainty about international trade.

The IEA notes that global energy demand continues to 
grow strongly. In 2024, both renewable energies and 
the consumption of oil, gas, coal and nuclear production 
reached record levels. China has especially boosted the 
use of coal since 2019, contributing to the continued 
rise in emissions.

Thus, according to the scenarios published in the WEO 
2025 by the IEA, under the STEPS baseline scenario, 
total global primary energy demand increases from 
around 15,623 million tons of oil equivalent (Mtoe) in 
2024 to 17,839 Mtoe in 2050. This corresponds to an 
average annual growth rate of 0.5%, just under half the 
growth rate of energy demand over the past decade. 
Demand will continue to increase from 2024 to 2050, 
with 24% growth in emerging economies more than 
offsetting the 7% decline among advanced economies. 
Under the STEPS scenario, demand for each of the major 
fossil fuels—coal, oil and natural gas—would peak 
before 2035, retreating from that point forward. 
Accordingly, in 2035 oil demand would stand at 100.4 
mbl/d, while natural gas demand would reach 4,770 
billion cubic meters (bcm).

All renewable energy sources increase their share over 
time. At present, modern bioenergy (renewable fuels, 
liquid biofuels and solid bioenergy, excluding the 
traditional use of biomass) accounts for more than half 
of renewable energy demand, though solar photovoltaic 
and wind energy will expand rapidly over the coming 
years, especially in the electricity sector. Installed 
capacity across all renewable energy sources will more 
than double under the STEPS scenario.

STEPS forecasts a peak in energy-related CO2 emissions 
before 2035, and they would fall from 38.15 Gt CO2 in 
2024 to 29.63 Gt CO2 in 2050. This would bring the 
global average temperature increase to approximately 
2.4 °C by 2100.

In this edition, the IEA reintroduces the Current Policies 
Scenario (CPS) and abandons the Announced Pledges 
Scenario (APS). In the CPS, which considers only the 
policies that have been adopted in legislation and 
actually implemented so far, global energy demand 
would reach 20,012 Mtoe by 2050, almost 15% above 
the level reached under the STEPS scenario. In this 
scenario, oil demand would not fall throughout the 
analysis period, reaching 112.8 mbl/d by 2050. The same 
is true of natural gas, which would reach 5,596 bcm.

World primary energy matrix under the IEA’s STEPS 
scenario

Global 2024: 15,623 Mtoe

Source: IEA and Repsol Research Department.

World primary energy matrix under the IEA’s STEPS 
scenario

7.2 Potential business 
developments

In February 2024, Repsol presented its Strategic Update 
for the 2024–2027 period, which focuses on a profitable 
energy transition and prioritizes shareholder 
remuneration, underpinned by three pillars: value 
creation, maintaining a strong financial position, and 
investment discipline.

Over these years, a portfolio of projects has been 
deployed that has enabled us to press ahead with our 
strategic commitments: at the Upstream business, with a 
focus on value and volume; and at Industrial, Customer, 
and Low-Carbon Generation, focused on developing new 
platforms and driving the company’s transformation by

The Upstream division will aim to focus on the execution 
of key projects that will improve production levels 
during 2026 (Pikka in Alaska, León/Castile in the Gulf of 
Mexico and Lapa SW in Brazil) and looking further ahead 
(Campos 33 in Brazil). In tandem with this, work will 
continue on improving the efficiency of assets and on an 
investment program aimed at optimizing the use of capital, 
with a disciplined and selective approach.

Meanwhile, the corporate areas will aim to continue to 
promote innovation to optimize processes and 
accompany businesses in the implementation of their 
strategy and projects, adding value through governance 
and control functions, increasing efficiency and flexibly 
managing corporate services. Progress will be made in 
digital transformation (driven by levers such as artificial 
intelligence and its generative aspect), specific programs 
will enhance the attraction, retention and development of 
talent, and cutting-edge technological solutions that 
support the evolution of businesses will continue to be 
deployed, with a focus on low-emission technologies.

7.3 Relevant events in 2026

In 2026, and ahead of the publication of this report, the 
following notable events took place:

• With respect to the events that took place in 
Venezuela during 2026 and up to the date of 
authorization for issue of these consolidated financial 
statements for 2025, see Note 27 to the 2025 
consolidated Financial Statements.

• On the 4th of february of, 2026, after the conditions 

precedent set out in the agreement were met, the sale 
to Stonepeak of the 43.8% interest in a portfolio of 
629 MW operating solar assets in Texas for 253 
million dollars was closed, see Note 19 to the 2025 
consolidated Financial Statements..

combining traditional activities with new low-emissions 
solutions.

In this context, Repsol has announced that it will hold a 
Capital Market Day in March 2026, at which it will present 
an update of its strategic projections for the 2026–2028 
period, in light of developments in the macroeconomic, 
regulatory, and competitive environment. This will steer 
the performance of our businesses, with a focus on 
enhancing competitiveness and efficiency through 
transformational initiatives that support sustainable cash 
generation over time..

In 2026, the Company's transformation and investment 
effort in all businesses may continue, provided that 
macroeconomic and commercial conditions are conducive 
to this. The allocation of capital will be carried out 
rigorously, prioritizing the most profitable and 
transformative projects in the portfolio, with the aim of 
making solid progress on the transformation path toward 
decarbonization.

In the Customer area, efforts will aim to focus on 
improving profitability and cash generation, leveraged 
on a multi-energy proposal that adds value to the 
customer. Repsol will aim to promote growth in 
electricity and natural gas customers, as well as the 
development of multi-energy and low-emission platforms , 
while promoting new businesses and the traditional 
business. Within this framework, our points of sale will 
continue to be transformed and the distribution and 
marketing of renewable fuels will continue to be 
promoted, as a sustainable solution to face the 
challenge of decarbonization of transport. In addition, 
we will accompany our customers in their energy 
transition, offering solutions that balance 
competitiveness, service and sustainability.

The Industrial businesses will aim to continue to drive 
transformation and the circular economy, through the 
development of initiatives aimed at increasing the 
capacity to produce renewable fuels and low-carbon 
materials, always promoting a culture of safety. Among 
the vectors of progress, the start-up in 2026 of the 
advanced biofuels plant in Puertollano, which will 
produce 100% renewable diesel from waste, and the e-
fuels plant in Bilbao, aimed at manufacturing synthetic 
fuels using green hydrogen and captured CO₂, stand out. 
In addition, the expansion of the Sines petrochemical 
complex in Portugal will strengthen the production of 
polymeric materials. On the other hand, after the 
approval of the projects in 2025, the construction of the 
Ecoplant in Tarragona, a pioneer in Europe in converting 
urban waste into renewable methanol through 
gasification, and the electrolyzer in Cartagena, which will 
allow the production of renewable hydrogen, will begin. 
All this will be complemented by the consolidation of the 
production of the renewable fuels plant in Cartagena, 
operational since 2024, and with electrification and 
energy efficiency projects to decarbonize industrial 
processes.

The Low Carbon Generation business will continue to 
play a crucial role, with the organic development of a 
portfolio of solar and wind projects. The new generation 
capacity will be especially focused on Spain and the 
United States. Excellent project execution, together with 
the optimization of the financial structure and systematic 
rotation of the asset portfolio, will maximize the 
profitability of new developments and strengthen the 
growth profile of the business.

7.4 Risks

Main risks

Note: The risks identified in the diagram are described in Appendix III.

(1) Relative economic importance is measured in terms of loss to the 95th percentile (potential loss in acid scenario) according to the probability 
distribution of losses for each of the risks over the next five years.

The diagram above shows the Group's20 main risks in 
terms of their economic, reputational, and people-
related significance, over a five-year time horizon.

For more information on the Integrated Risk 
Management System and a detailed description of the 
Group's main risks over a five-year horizon, see 
Appendix III.

Some of these risks are sensitive to the phenomena of 
energy transition and climate change. To this end, 
relevant risks are linked that are identified in the 
Integrated Risk Management System (IRMS) in 
accordance with the taxonomy of risks related to energy 
transition and climate change.

In addition, given the emerging nature of climate 
change risks, the Group complements the analysis 
conducted for the five-year time horizon with a vision 
for the 2030–2040 decade and 2050.21

For more information on the risks of energy transition and 
climate change, see section 2.1 of Appendix IV.

Among the main emerging risks, those linked to the 
possibility of a low-growth macroeconomic environment 
should be noted; those derived from the dynamics of 
deglobalization; the uncertainty surrounding trade 
policies and their impact on the supply chain; as well as 
the evolution of European Regulation on climate change 
and Sustainability, together with the future 
development of artificial intelligence.

20 The Group has a methodology that allows, by applying a common metric, to obtain an overview of risks, classify them according to their 

relevance, and characterize them in an understandable and robust way. The potential economic, reputational, and people impact that each 
business unit, corporate area, and Repsol as a whole may suffer is quantified, in the event of the occurrence, identifying, where appropriate, 
effective mitigation measures.

21 The Sustainability Committee and the Audit and Control Committee are informed of the methodology used, as well as the risk profile.

Appendix II. Alternative 
Performance Measures

Repsol's financial information contains amounts and 
measures prepared in accordance with applicable 
accounting standards, as well as other measures 
prepared in accordance with the Group's reporting 
model known as Alternative Performance Measures 
(APMs). The APMs are considered “adjusted” figures with 
respect to those presented in accordance with IFRS-EU 
or with the Information on oil and gas exploration and 
production activities, and, therefore, should be 
considered by the reader as complementary, rather than 
as substitutes for them.

APMs are highly useful for users of financial information 
as they are the measures employed by Repsol's 
Management to evaluate its financial performance, cash 
flows, or its financial position when making operational 
or strategic decisions for the Group.

For quarterly historical information on the APMs, see 
www.repsol.com.

1. Repsol’s business segment 
reporting model 

In the fourth quarter of 2025, in the context of the 
growing importance of partner participation in the E&P 
and GBC businesses and the expansion of the joint 
business model, the Group has changed the way it 
manages and evaluates its segments and has therefore 
modified the financial and performance measures that 
are reviewed internally for decision-making. Specifically, 
the results and financial figures of the joint ventures are 
now integrated by the equity method22 into its segment 
results presentation model, and the measurement of 
segment results is modified to reflect the result 
attributable to the parent company. In this way, in 
addition, the Group's financial information is provided 
with greater clarity, aligning it with the presentation of 
the financial statements prepared under IFRS-EU and 
with common practice in the sector.

Additionally, and in line with the change implemented in 
the last quarter of 2025, the 2026 budget, the update 
of the projections to be presented at the Capital 
Markets Day following the publication of the annual 
results in February, and the update of employee 
remuneration targets and plans have all been prepared 
in accordance with the new reporting model.

The information reported on segments corresponding 
to previous periods has been modified to reflect these 
changes to allow adequate comparability between 
periods.

The result metric for each segment is known as 
Adjusted Net Income, which contains the income from 
continuing operations at current cost of supply (CCS), 
net of taxes, the result of investments accounted for by 
the equity method (Profit from investees) and the 
result attributed to minority interests (Non-controlling 
interests) and excluding certain income and expenses 
(“Special items”) described below. The financial result of 
the consolidated companies and the inter-segment 
consolidation adjustments are assigned to the 
Corporation and other results. 

More precisely, the operating results at CCS considers 
the cost of volumes sold to be that corresponding to 
the costs of Supplies and production for the same 
period. It is the criterion commonly used in the sector to 
present the earnings of the businesses of the Industrial 
or Customer segments, which must work with significant 
inventories subject to constant price fluctuation. This 
facilitates comparability with other companies and the 
monitoring of the businesses, regardless of the impact 
of price variations on their inventories. However, for 
accounting purposes, in accordance with EU-IFRS, the 
weighted average cost method is used.

The Inventory effect reflects the difference between 
the the results at CCS and the result at weighted 
average cost. It is presented independently, net of taxes 
and minority interests. 

Special items includes certain material items whose 
separate presentation is considered appropriate in order 
to facilitate analysis of the ordinary business 
performance. This includes gains/losses on disposals, 
restructuring costs, impairments (provisions/reversions) 
of assets, provisions for risks and expenses and other 
relevant income/expenses outside the ordinary 
management of the businesses. These results, which 
include those relating to investments accounted for 
using the equity method, are presented separately, net 
of taxes and non-controlling interests.

For more information, see Note 4 “Business segments” 
to the 2025 consolidated Financial Statements.

22 Previously, the earnings and other financial figures of each segment were presented considering the economic figures of the joint ventures, 
according to their percentage of ownership, with the same methodology and level of detail as the consolidated entities.

2. Financial performance 
metrics

Adjusted net income

Adjusted net income is the primary measure of financial 
performance that the Chief Operating Decision Maker 
(CODM) reviews when making decisions.23 It is a useful 
APM as it allows investors to evaluate the performance 
of the operating segments and allow better 
comparability with other companies operating in our 
sector.

It contains income from continuing operations at current 
cost of supply (CCS) and net of taxes; earnings 
corresponding to investments accounted for using the 
equity method (Earnings of investees), and those 
attributable to non-controlling interests (Non-
controlling interests), and do not include certain 
income and expenses (Special items) described below. 
The financial result of the consolidated companies and 
inter-segment consolidation adjustments are assigned 
to Corporation and other results.

The adjustments included between the Adjusted net 
income and profit attributable to the parent are 
therefore as follows:

•

Inventory effect. In profit at CCS, the cost of volumes 
sold is determined according to the supply and 
production24 costs for the same period. The difference 
between profit at CCS and profit at WAC25 is reflected 
in the so-called Inventory effect, which also includes 
other adjustments to the valuation of inventories 

Special items

€ million

Divestments

Workforce restructuring

Impairment (1)

Provisions and other

TOTAL

•

(provisions, economic hedges, etc.) and is presented 
net of taxes, excluding the effect of Non-controlling 
interests, which are not attributable to the parent 
company. This effect mainly affects the Industrial 
segment.

• Special items. These correspond to significant items 

whose separate presentation is considered convenient 
to facilitate the monitoring of the day-to-day 
management of business operations and allow for a 
reliable comparison between financial years and 
companies operating within the sector. It includes 
capital gains/losses due to divestments (capital gains 
and losses due to transfers or disposals of assets), 
restructuring costs (severance costs, etc.), impairment 
(provisions and reversals resulting from the 
impairment test on fixed assets, tax credits, etc.), 
provisions for risks and expenses (provisions and 
reversals of provisions for fiscal, legal, environmental, 
geopolitical risks, etc.) and other relevant income/
expenses unrelated to the day-to-day running of 
business (provisions for dismantling and remediation; 
exchange rate impacts on tax positions in foreign 
currency; costs and indemnities arising from claims; 
penalties and fines; valuation of derivative financial 
instruments due to accounting asymmetries, etc.). 
Specific results, which include those pertaining to 
investments accounted for using the equity method, 
are presented net of taxes, excluding the effect of 
non-controlling interests, which are not attributable 
to the parent company.

(1) The difference compared to the “(Impairment losses) / Reversals of impairment losses” line item in the IFRS-EU income statement is mainly 
explained by the fact that the latter includes credit risk impairments and impairments of exploratory wells arising from the ordinary course of 
business, and does not include the tax effect of impairments, impairments of deferred tax assets, or impairments of investments accounted for 
using the equity method.

23 Prior to the change in the reporting model described in section 1 above, the measure of business results was called Adjusted Earnings and 

included the Income from continuing operations at current cost of supply ("Current Cost of Supply" (CCS), net of taxes (including the percentage 
corresponding to joint businesses) and not including results attributable to minority interests ("minority interests"). The new measure of business 
results "Adjusted Net Earnings" adequately reflects the nature of its businesses and the way in which their results are analyzed for decision-
making by the Chief Operating Decision Maker, in addition to aligning it with the presentation of the financial statements prepared under 
International Financial Reporting Standards - European Union and with common practice in the sector".

24 To calculate the cost of supply, international quotations on the benchmark markets in which the Company operates are used. The relevant 

average monthly price is applied to each quality of distilled crude. Quotations are obtained from daily crude oil publications according to Platts, 
plus freight costs estimated by Worldscale (an association that publishes world reference prices for freight costs between specific ports). All 
other production costs (fixed and variable costs) are valued at the cost recognized in the accounts.

25 The CMP is an accounting method of stock valuation accepted by European accounting standards, which takes into account purchase prices and 

historical production costs, valuing inventories at the lower of this cost and their market value.

Adjusted EBITDA:

Adjusted EBITDA (Earnings Before Interest, Taxes, 
Depreciation, Amortization and Others) is an indicator 
that measures the company's operating margin before 
deducting interest, taxes, impairments, amortization and 

other items that do not involve cash inflows or outflows 
from operations. By stripping out financial and tax 
figures, as well as accounting expenses that do not 
involve cash outflows, it is used by the CODM to 
evaluate the Company's performance over time.26

26 Prior to the change in the reporting model described in section 1 above, the performance measure was called EBITDA and included the figure 

pertaining to joint businesses based on their percentage of participation. The new “Adjusted EBITDA” indicator corresponds to the measure used 
by the CODM and more reliably reflects the performance of our businesses. It is also aligned with the presentation of the financial statements 
prepared under IFRS-EU and with common practice within the sector.

Adjusted EBITDA is calculated as operating profit + 
amortization + impairment and other items that do not 
involve cash inflows or outflows from operations 
(restructuring, gains/losses on disposals, provisions, 

etc.). Operating income relates to the result from 

operations at weighted average cost (WAC). Where 
Operating result at current cost of replacement (CCS) 
is used, it is known as EBITDA at CCS.

6,052 

This APM is used by Repsol's CODM to assess the 
capacity of operating assets to generate profits. It is 
therefore a measure of the efficiency of the capital 
invested (equity and debt).

ROACE (short for Return on average capital employed) 
is calculated as: (Profit from operations after tax + Profit 

from investments accounted for using the equity 
method) / (Average capital employed during the period 
of operations, which measures the capital invested in 
the Company from internal and external sources and 
which corresponds to Total equity + Net debt). If the 
inventory effect is not considered in its calculation, it is 
known as ROACE at CCS.

DENOMINATOR (€ million)

Total equity

Net debt

Capital employed at period-end

II.Average capital employed (2)

I/II ROACE (%) (3)

(1) Does not include the income tax corresponding to the financial result.
(2) Relates to the average balance of capital employed at the beginning and end of the period.
(3) ROACE at CCS (without considering the Inventory effect) amounted to 8.2% in 2025.

3. Cash metrics27

Free cash flow:

Free cash flow measures cash generation from operating 
and investing activities and is used by the CODM to assess 
the funds available within the Group to pay dividends to 
shareholders and to service debt.

Cash generated 
by the businesses:

The cash generated by the businesses corresponds to 
Free cash flow +/- collections or payments arising from 
transactions mainly with non-controlling interests (dilutions, 
contributions, dividends, etc.). This APM measures the funds 
generated by the businesses before certain financial 
transactions (mainly issuances and redemptions).

The reconciliation of Free cash flow and Cash generated 
with the consolidated statements of cash flow prepared 
under IFRS-EU is presented below:

Payments for investments
(investments):

This CODM uses this APM to measure the investment 
effort in each period, as well as its allocation by 
business. Due to its usefulness in understanding how 
the CODM allocates its resources and to enable more 
reliable comparisons between periods, investments can 

be presented as organic (funds invested in the 
development or maintenance of the Group's projects 
and assets) or inorganic (acquisition of projects, assets 
or companies for the expansion of the Group's 
activities).

4. Financial measures

Debt and financial position 
ratios28:

Net debt and Net debt without leases are the main 
APMs used by the CODM to measure the Company's 
level of indebtedness. It consists of financial liabilities 
(excluding lease liabilities where applicable) less 
financial assets, cash and other cash equivalents.

Sometimes, and due to the relevance of non-controlling 
shareholders in the subgroups of the Upstream and LCG 
segments, this metric may be calculated for the 
corresponding corporate scope.

Gross debt is the amount used to analyze the Group's 
solvency, and includes financial liabilities and the net

(13,283) 

The ratios indicated below are used by the CODM to 
assess both the degree of leverage and the solvency of 
the Group.

It is the main measure to evaluate and compare the 
Company's financial position with other companies in 
the sector.

• Leverage equals net debt divided by Capital 

employed at the end of the period. This ratio can be 
used to determine the financial structure and degree 
of indebtedness with regard to capital contributed by 
shareholders and entities which provide financing. 

• The Solvency ratio is calculated as Liquidity (see 

section 3. “Cash metrics”) divided by gross short-term 
debt, and is used to determine the number of times 
the Group could meet its short-term debt maturities 
with current liquidity. 

28 Prior to the change in the reporting model described in section 1 above, the Group's debt measures included those corresponding to joint 
ventures by their percentage of participation (Net debt, Gross debt, leverage and solvency). The new measures exclude the indebtedness 
corresponding to the joint ventures and correspond to the measures used by the CODM, reflect more clearly the Company's level of indebtedness 
and, in addition, are aligned with the presentation of the financial statements prepared under IFRS-EU and with common practice in the sector.

5. Other measures

Net investment or net Capex is used to measure the 
investment effort in the period, net of the resources 
obtained from the divestment operations that finance 
the group's growth and transition projects. Its measure 
most directly comparable to the IFRS-EU financial 
statements is the cash flow used in investing activities, 
net of certain financing transactions.

It comprises investments and divestments (excluding 
investments in financial assets), cash receipts/
(payments) from transactions with non-controlling 
interests (divestments without loss of control – asset 
rotations –, financing, etc.), and changes in net debt 
resulting from changes in the consolidation perimeter 
(project financing and portfolio management 
transactions, etc.).

Net adjusted free cash flow is calculated as cash flow 
from operations (see section 3. Cash flow 
measurements) less net capex.

Appendix III. Risks

Repsol’s Enterprise Risk 
Management System - ERMS

Repsol has an Enterprise Risk Management System 
which, through the coordinated action of all the units 
involved, enables the main risks associated with the 
Group’s activities to be identified, measured, managed 
and monitored in a manner consistent with the Risk 
Management Policy, and monitors whether the existing 
management systems effectively mitigate them to the 
established levels. Repsol’s Enterprise Risk Management 
System provides a forward-looking, comprehensive and 
reliable view of all the risks that may affect the 
Company and is based on the Risk Management Policy 
approved by the Board of Directors, the principles of 
which are set out in the Integrated Risk Management 
standard approved by the Executive Committee.

The core pillars of the ERMS are:

• Senior Management heads up the enterprise risk 

management process.

• The risk vision is integrated into the management and 

decision-making processes.

• Businesses and corporate areas participate, with 

different levels of responsibility and specialization 
(risk management units, supervisory units, and 
auditing units, in accordance with the Three-Line 
Model29), while the Risk and Control Assurance Unit 
coordinates and governs the system.

• Risks are identified, assessed, and addressed in 

accordance with ISO 31000.

•

It promotes continuous improvement to gain 
efficiency and responsiveness.

Another key element is the risk tolerance declaration, 
which is set out in the Risk Management Policy along with 
the above principles. Repsol aspires to a profile typical of 
an energy company, integrated and diversified, 
differentiating between those in which the Company will 
actively manage and maintain within the defined tolerance 
thresholds, and those for which it declares its high 
ambition for control, minimizing its probability of 
occurrence and its impact, including reputational impact. 
The latter include accident rate, environment, health, 
safety, ethics, and conduct and compliance (including 
tax) risks.

ISO 31000 Risk Management – Principles and guidelines

29The Three Lines model was developed by the Institute of Internal Auditors (IIA) in 2020 with the aim of helping organizations to identify the 

structures and processes that best facilitate the achievement of objectives by promoting robust risk management governance.

The Company’s bodies involved in the definition, 
implementation, monitoring, and supervision of the 
ERMS, as well as their responsibilities, are as detailed 
below:

Figure showing the Company’s bodies involved in the ERMS

Board of Directors

Approves the Company's general policies 
and strategies , including the Risk 
Management Policy and the supervision of 
internal information and control systems.

Audit and Control 
Committee

Directly oversees the activity of the Audit, 
Control and Risk Department, to which the 
Risk and Control Assurance Unit is attached, 
while also periodically reviewing the 
effectiveness of the internal control systems, 
Internal Audit and Risk Management systems 
so that the main risks are properly identified, 
managed, and disclosed.

Sustainability Committee

The Sustainability Committee attached to 
Repsol's Board of Directors supervises and 
advises the Board of Directors on corporate 
social responsibility, the environment, health 
and safety, climate change, and energy 
transition. 

Executive Committee

Approves the necessary governance 
elements in the field of risk management, 
monitors their correct application and tracks 
the Company's performance in terms of risks, 
including the integration of the risk vision 
into decision-making processes.

Risk Management Units

Risk Supervision Units

Risk Audit Units

Responsible for direct risk management in 
daily operations (identification, analysis, 
evaluation and treatment of risks).

Evaluate the design and operation of risk 
management and control systems so that 
they are adequately identified, measured, 
prioritized and controlled, in accordance with 
the Risk Management Policy and other 
current regulations and good industry 
practices.

Supervisory Units: Supervise risk 
management in their field of expertise, i.e., 
they define the management and 
governance model, advise the front lines on 
its application, monitor its performance and 
support compliance with the criteria of the 
established model.
Risk and Control Assurance Area: It defines 
the model for planning, identification, 
valuation and treatment of the ERMS, 
advises on the application of this model, 
monitors the entire process (which includes 
reporting to the governance bodies) and 
supports compliance, alerting the 
management and supervisory units of any 
deviation from the Risk Management Policy 
as identified. It also promotes that the vision 
of risks effectively influences decision-
making processes.

According to the ERMS, the identification, assessment 
and consolidation of risks is carried out at least annually. 
However, the processes are prepared such that if at any 
time it appears that the Group’s risk profile may have 
been substantially altered due to a change in the 
exposure to any particular material risk, the analysis of 
such risks is updated.

Where appropriate, the head of the management unit 
sets in motion appropriate actions or control 
mechanisms to align the risk profile with the 
organization’s expectations, in line with the risk 
tolerance declaration set out in the Risk Management 
Policy. In this way, the company reasonably supports the 
compliance of the objectives of each organizational area 
that could be impacted by risks of a strategic, 
operational, financial, regulatory and legal nature, 
relying on information and internal control systems 
based on the principles of the COSO Reference 
Framework30.

The Risk and Control Assurance unit consolidates the 
individual risk maps to obtain the consolidated Risk Map 
of the Repsol Group, as well as any partial consolidation 
that is agreed to report to the executive and governing 
bodies, which issue, as appropriate, guidelines regarding 
the treatment of certain risks, in view of the risk profile, 
the maturity of its management systems and the 
declaration of risk tolerance contained in the Risk 
Management Policy.

The resulting mitigation actions are driven forward by 
the supervision units and, when they involve 
management units, converge with the treatment 
strategies that the latter have in place. Supervisory units 
include technical and specialist functions in business 
and corporate areas.

In the course of the activities described above, the Risk 
and Internal Controls Assurance unit collects from the 
managing and supervisory units information on their 
performance and expectations in relation to the 
activities carried out to achieve the objectives of the 
ERMS.

This collection of information is complemented, when 
deemed necessary, with campaigns specifically designed 
for the collection of certain data, whether surveys, 
studies of backtesting or any others. Based on this 
information, the area responsible for the ERMS reviews 
its effectiveness and so that the findings result in its 
continuous improvement.

At all stages of the integrated risk management process, 
and in accordance with the scope defined for the 
management system, audit units are responsible for 
assessing the reasonableness and adequacy of the 
design and operation of the management systems. 

control and management of the Repsol Group’s risks 
with the aim, among others, so that that risks are 
adequately identified, prioritized, measured and 
controlled, within the tolerance levels established by 
the Board of Directors in the Risk Management Policy, 
with reference to current regulations and good industry 
practices. As part of their functions, the audit unit plans 
its annual engagements based on various aspects, 
including the state of risks, preferably focusing their 
actions on the most significant ones.

Lastly, it should be noted that the variables of the 
economic models on which the key decision-making 
processes are based, such as the preparation of the 
annual budget and the preparation and periodic 
updating of the strategic plan, are subject to risk 
analysis. Going beyond single-scenario approaches, 
these analyses provide a probabilistic view of the 
achievable result by simulating multiple scenarios in 
which these variables, which may be prices or margins 
and may be loosely or more heavily correlated, take 
different values depending on their prior statistical 
modeling.

System of Internal Control over 
Financial Reporting (ICFR) and 
Sustainability Reporting (ICSR) 

Repsol has a System of Internal Control over Financial 
Reporting (ICFR) whose proper functioning provides 
reasonable assurance as to the reliability of the Group’s 
financial reporting. The ICFR model is defined on the 
basis of the methodological framework set out in COSO 
2013, and its objective is to contribute to supporting 
that the transactions carried out are faithfully recorded, 
in accordance with the corresponding accounting 
framework, providing reasonable assurance in relation 
to the prevention or detection of errors that could have 
a significant impact on the information contained in the 
Consolidated Financial Statements. The Audit, Control 
and Risks department annually evaluates the design and 
functioning of the Group’s ICFR and draws conclusions 
on its effectiveness. Additional information can be 
found under section B 8.2. Internal Control and Risk 
Management Systems in relation to the financial 
information issuance process (ICFR) of the Annual 
Corporate Governance Report.

The Repsol Group has established a system of Internal 
Control over Sustainability Reporting (“ICSR”), designed 
to provide reasonable assurance regarding the reliability 
of the sustainability information reported in accordance 
with the applicable regulatory framework, CNMV 
regulations, the Spanish Companies Act, and the Code 
of Good Governance of Listed Companies issued by the 
of the Spanish National Securities Market Commission 
(see details in Appendix IV, section 1.2 “Governance”).

30 COSO Internal Control – Integrated Framework, 2013. Repsol has an Integrated Internal Control model, inspired by the Committee of Sponsoring 
Organizations of the Treadway Commission (COSO) reference framework, which includes, among others, the Group's formally developed Internal 
Control and Compliance Systems, including the Financial Reporting Internal Control System, the Sustainability Information Control System, the 
Crime Prevention Model and other compliance and criminal prevention models in force.

Main risks

The main risks identified are described in section 7.4 of 
this document:

Financial and market risks

Fluctuations  
in benchmark prices  
for hydrocarbons,  
derivative products 
and other commodities 

The prices of crude oil and gas, as well as those of their derivatives, are subject to exogenous factors and, 
therefore, to volatility as a result, among others, of fluctuations in international supply and demand, 
affected by the geopolitical and macroeconomic environment, the influence of OPEC, technological 
changes, natural disasters, pandemics or the energy transition process. The price of CO2 emission 
allowances may be affected by the macroeconomic and regulatory environment, as well as the emission 
reduction scenarios associated with the energy transition process. 
Note that price deviations from the Group’s projected figures may be favorable or unfavorable.

In 2025, the price of a barrel of Brent averaged $ 69.1 /bbl, lower than that of the previous year (80.8 $/
bbl). However, it was marked by high volatility due to geopolitical factors as well as OPEC+'s production 
strategy. The price of U.S. Henry Hub natural gas averaged 3.43 €/MBtu, higher than that of the previous 
period (2024: 2.27 $/MBtu). In the case of electricity, the wholesale price averaged 66 €/MWh, compared 
with 63 €/MWh in 2024 , influenced by growth in demand that is offset by the increase in self-
consumption and photovoltaic generation. The price of CO2 emission allowances on the ETS1 market 
averaged 74.9 €/t compared with 66.5 €/t in 2024, mainly due to the increase in the price of gas in Europe. 

The reduction in crude oil and gas prices has a negative impact on the profitability of the Exploration and 
Production activity, the valuation of its assets, the ability to generate cash and investment plans. Rising 
prices have the opposite effect. A potential drop in investment could adversely affect Repsol’s ability to 
replace its crude oil and gas reserves. In addition, changes in the prices of crude oil and its derivatives may 
affect the margin of the Refining and Chemical businesses and the value of the inventories stored in the 
Industrial segment. In 2025, the impact of price fluctuations on inventories is reflected in the so-called 
Inventory effect (see section 4 of this document). The price of finished products can also affect demand 
for them. The price of gas and electricity has a fundamental impact on the low-carbon generation 
business and on the electricity and gas marketing businesses and the price of CO2 emission allowances in 
the ETS1 market on the margins of industrial businesses. Likewise, the price of CO2 in the future ETS2 
market may affect the margins and sales of commercial businesses. For more information on the impact of 
changes (positive and negative) on the calculation of the recoverable value of the assets from the 
impairment test (see Note 6.1 of the 2025 consolidated Financial Statements.

For more information on the trend in the price of hydrocarbons, derivative products, and other commodities 
in 2025, see section 3.2 of this report. For more information on the effect of prices on the earnings and 
performance of the business, see sections 4 and 5 of this document. 

Strategic risks

Deviations from 
expected results in 
investment/divestment 
processes

The Repsol Group is exposed to negative impacts on the management of investment, divestment or 
partnership processes that may be caused, among others, by inadequate capture and selection of 
alternatives or an incorrect analysis of each of them. The current context is marked by high volatility and 
uncertainty, which can influence the number and quality of the opportunities available or lead to an 
inadequate estimation of the evolution of the main variables of each initiative, such as demand, 
commodity prices, margin forecasts, exchange rates and interest rates, the efficiency of production 
factors, operating costs, etc. 

This heading includes risks related to the management of the asset portfolio in accordance with Repsol's 
strategic lines, to the management of the portfolio of new and existing asset development projects, to 
the uncertainty associated with the commercial scaling of projects based on emerging technologies, as 
well as risks related to inadequate forecasting of fiscal conditions or the appearance of contingencies in 
the purchase/sale processes of assets or companies.

Regulatory and litigation risks

Regulatory changes  
(including taxation)

The energy industry and the Group’s activity are heavily regulated. Future developments in the regulatory 
framework may significantly affect the Group's activity in aspects such as the energy transition, the 
environment, and the preservation of biodiversity, competition, taxation, the markets where the Group 
operates, the labor environment, industrial and logistical safety and security, cybersecurity, the regulation 
of prices, quotas or the imposition of tariffs, financial and sustainability information communicated to the 
market and modifications to sanctions regimes in force, among others, adversely impacting the 
businesses, results, and financial position of the Repsol Group.

Administrative 
proceedings and 
lawsuits relating to 
regulatory matters

Operational risks

Accident rate

Disputes may arise concerning the application or interpretation of current law and regulations in certain 
realms. In particular, the areas that generate this exposure to the Group include tax regulations and 
their interpretation, energy transition regulations, the wide variety of environmental and safety 
standards (environmental quality of products, air emissions, energy efficiency, extractive technologies, 
discharges to water, soil and groundwater remediation, preservation of biodiversity, waste management 
and risk management linked to substances contained in the products we market), accounting 
regulations and those that regulate financial and sustainability information communicated to the 
market, those related to industrial and intellectual property, the regulation of financial markets, 
competition, good corporate governance, those related to communication and advertising practices, 
those related to money laundering and Financing of terrorism, regulations on labor law, data protection, 
those related to the development and use of artificial intelligence (AI), those relating to cybersecurity 
management and operational resilience in the field of systems, and in general, all those required by 
Public Administrations. In addition, Repsol reports on oil and gas reserve estimates that present an 
uncertainty inherent to the evaluation process and that are subject to judgments and estimates (see 
Note 3.5.2 to the 2025 consolidated Financial Statements). In addition, Repsol may be affected by the 
existence of sanctions and trade embargo regimes adopted by the EU, its Member States, the US or 
other countries, as well as supranational bodies such as the United Nations, on certain countries in which 
it operates and/or companies or individuals based in them. For more information on the regulatory 
framework applicable to the Group's main activities, see Appendix III – Regulatory framework of the 
2025 consolidated Financial Statements and, with regard to tax developments, Note 8. For further 
information on proceedings and litigation, see Notes 8.4 and 20.2 to the 2025 consolidated Financial 
Statements.

Repsol’s industrial and commercial assets (refineries, petrochemical complexes, regasification plants, 
electric power generation plants (cogeneration, combined cycles, hydroelectric plants, wind or 
photovoltaic installations), bases and warehouses, port facilities, pipelines, ships, tankers, service stations, 
etc.), as well as the facilities of Repsol's Upstream business (exploratory or production wells, surface 
facilities, oil platforms, etc.), both onshore and offshore, are exposed to the risk of accidents, such as fires, 
explosions, leaks of toxic products, as well as environmental incidents with a potentially significant 
impact. Such accidents may cause death and injury to employees, contractors, residents in surrounding 
areas, as well as damage to the assets and property owned by Repsol and third parties, as well as damage 
to the environment. These accidents can occur when operating the facilities and when carrying out 
construction projects or decommissioning assets. Repsol is exposed to impacts from any type of damage 
or temporary interruption of service associated with accidents in operations or involving vehicles for land, 
sea-river and air transportation of persons, substances, goods, or equipment.

Deviations in 
organizational and 
employee management

Repsol is exposed to negative impacts arising from the management of the organization and its 
employees, which constitute a key asset for the Group and which, in certain business contexts, may prove 
inadequate for achieving its objectives. Among the triggering factors of such impacts are aspects such as 
the recruitment and retention of talent, the organizational structure, both in terms of design and sizing, 
rising wage costs, cultural adaptation and transformation, protecting the health of our workers, and labor 
relations.

Operational risks

Cyberattacks

The Repsol Group is exposed to all manner of cyberattacks, including, but not limited to, those affecting 
the availability of critical systems and their information on industrial or digital assets, attacks specifically 
directed against industrial asset control systems, those that could affect the confidentiality of our data, 
such as the theft of strategic or sensitive information, those that impact the privacy of the information we 
hold, such as the theft of personal data, whether of customers, employees or other third parties, and last 
but not least, those that may affect the integrity of our data, which could lead to erroneous decision-
making or the occurrence of fraud events.

Deviations when 
undertaking investment 
projects

The project performance phase is exposed to various risks, such as geological, technical, economic, 
commercial, legal and regulatory, in addition to those related to safety, environment and sustainability, all 
of which can compromise the delivery of goods and services, compliance with pre-agreed budgets and 
deadlines, and compliance with defined specifications and/or operational reliability of projects. In 
addition, since the execution of the project also depends on the performance of third parties (including 
suppliers of goods, services and equipment, partners in joint agreements, associates, governments and 
other parties) that are not under the direct control of Repsol, the Group is exposed to execution and 
reputational risks through such entities.

Supply chain

Repsol is exposed to negative impacts associated with the unavailability or scarcity of market goods and 
services, price and cost fluctuations, as well as interruptions and deviations in time and form in the supply 
of goods or the provision of services, including the supply of raw materials, which may eventually force 
the interruption of the affected business activities. Specifically, part of the processing, transportation and 
marketing of crude oil and gas production from Upstream assets is carried out through infrastructure 
(pipelines, processing and purification units or liquefaction terminals) operated by third parties. This 
infrastructure is exposed to various risks, such as unscheduled shutdowns or accidents, which may affect 
the provision of these logistical services by the suppliers concerned. In addition, in certain countries 
where the Group operates that are prone to socio-political instability, there may be a shortage of 
qualified suppliers or contractors, which could in turn affect business. The Company is also exposed to 
risks arising from its relations with other firms along the supply chain, or where Repsol is held jointly liable 
for their actions, including non-compliances with applicable laws and other regulations, external policies 
and standards or administrative requirements.

In addition, there may also be restrictions on the supply of certain raw materials and/or equipment, in 
particular those linked to low-carbon electricity generation technologies and the production of 
renewable fuels. Operational continuity depends on some critical services and supplies provided by third 
parties, such as those in the IT environment or those related to essential supplies, such as electricity. 
Increasing exposure is detected related to the lower availability of skilled labor in the different links of the 
supply chain, which can lead to interruptions and delays in projects. Finally, it should be mentioned that a 
certain impact on the Group related to possible logistical restrictions arising from situations of 
geopolitical instability or armed conflicts that affect the main global transport routes and that result in 
cost overruns or delays in deliveries cannot be ruled out (see Note 27 “Geopolitical risks” to the 2025 
consolidated Financial Statements).

Attacks on people or 
assets

In general, but especially in certain countries where it operates, Repsol is exposed to potential impacts 
arising from acts of direct violence that may endanger the integrity of both the Company's assets and the 
persons linked to it as a result of actions taken by persons or groups partisan to any interests, 
governmental or otherwise, including, without limitation, acts of terrorism (including cyber origin), asset 
blocking, crime, piracy, kidnapping, extortion, theft, and acts of vandalism. For more information, see Note 
27 “Geopolitical risks” to the 2025 consolidated Financial Statements.

Various risks materialized in 2025, including those 
related to the regulatory landscape and litigation. In 
relation to litigation risks, see Note 20.2 to the 2025 
consolidated Financial Statements. During 2025, there 
were several power supply interruptions affecting 
Repsol's industrial assets for reasons beyond the 
Company’s control. On April 22, the Cartagena refinery 
suffered a general blackout due to voltage instabilities 
in the grid, which disrupted the operation of the 
complex for five days.

Subsequently, on April 28, the mass blackout that struck 
the Iberian Peninsula and disconnected it from the 
continental electricity system caused the total 
shutdown of all Repsol's refineries and chemical 
complexes in Spain and Portugal. Lastly, on June 16, the 
Puertollano refinery experienced another blackout due 
to a breakdown at a substation of the electricity 
supplier.

These incidents have had an impact on the Company's  
operations and results (see section 4.1 Results). The 
Company is currently weighing up the merits of 
pursuing legal action if and when liability for the 
blackout events is officially determined.
