1. General disclosures

1.1 Basis for preparation

General basis for the preparation 
of the sustainability statement (BP-1)

The Consolidated Statement of Non-Financial and 
Sustainability Information has been prepared on a 
consolidated basis, with the same scope  as the 
consolidated Financial Statements of Repsol, S. A.

The Consolidated Statement of Non-Financial and 
Sustainability Information of the Repsol Group has been 
prepared in accordance with Directive (EU) 2022/2464, 
as regards corporate sustainability reporting, and in 
accordance with (1) Law 11/2018, (2) Commission 
Delegated Regulation (EU) 2023/2772 supplementing 
Directive 2013/34/EU of the European Parliament and 
of the Council as regards sustainability reporting 
standards, (3) the Regulation on the European 
Taxonomy (Regulation (EU) 2020/852 and its Delegated 
Acts - Regulations (EU) 2021/2139 and 2021/2178, as 
amended by Delegated Regulations (EU) 2022/1214 
and 2023/2485, as well as Regulation (EU) 2023/2486 -
and, on a consolidated basis, the simplification 
amendment introduced by Delegated Regulation (EU) 
2026/73), and fulfils the requirements of Royal Decree 
214/2025, of March 18, which establishes the obligation 
to calculate the carbon footprint and to prepare and 
publish plans to reduce greenhouse gas emissions. 

In addition, selected indicators from the Global 
Reporting Initiative (GRI) standards have been 
considered, such as the GRI 207: Tax 2019, as well as 
indicators that fulfil the requirements of Law 11/2018, 
as indicated in Consolidated Statement of Non-
Financial Information.

Limited assurance refers to the information identified in 
Table 5.4.1 ‘Non‑financial and diversity information 
required by Law 11/2018’ in compliance with the 
applicable legislation (see Independent assurance 
report)

It should be noted that the following companies of the 
Group are included in the scope of consolidation of this 
report of Repsol, S. A. and are exempt from preparing a 
separate Statement of Non-Financial Information in 
accordance with Law 11/2018: 

• Repsol, S. A. 

• Campsa Estaciones de Servicio, S. A.  

• Repsol Butano, S. A.  

• Repsol Soluciones Energéticas, S. A.  

• Repsol Petróleo, S. A.  

• Repsol Lubricantes y Especialidades, S. A.  

• Repsol Materials, S. A.

• Repsol LNG Holding, S. A.  

• Repsol Trading, S. A.

• Repsol Tesorería y Gestión Financiera, S. A.

• Repsol Exploración, S. A.

• Repsol Exploración Inversiones, S. L. 

The report includes information related to the Repsol 
Group’s own operations, as well as aspects related to its 
value chain.

Subsidiary companies and joint operations, with and 
without control of the operation, are deemed to be own 
operations.

A specific scope has been defined for the reported data 
in accordance with the requirements of the CSRD 
Directive relating to the different topics.

For the environmental standards, the approach focuses 
on the assets linked to the companies. The data 
reported for fiscal year 2025 include those 
corresponding to the Group’s own operations. 
Additionally, for ESRS E1, equity‑share‑based data for 
joint ventures are also included for certain metrics.

The Taxonomy report includes controlled companies.

Regarding the social standards, the reported 
information corresponds to the controlled companies, 
and the scope defined for each is detailed below:

• ESRS S1: the scope is restricted exclusively to 

employing companies.

• ESRS S2: it applies to the scope established in the 

internal regulations for the management of 
procurement and contracting, limited to controlled 
companies.

• ESRS S3: it includes all controlled companies with 

assets that may have an impact on communities. With 
regard to social investment, initiatives from Repsol 
Foundation and other companies outside the Group’s 
management control are also considered.

• ESRS S4: it is limited to companies that have end 

customers.

With regard to governance and cybersecurity standards, 
information is reported on all the companies that make 
up the Group.

For information related to responsible taxation, 
controlled companies are reported.

The data on companies or assets that were divested 
during the reporting period are included up to the date 
of divestment. For details on portfolio management, see 
Portfolio management for each business1.

Disclosures in relation to specific circumstances (BP-2)

The information is reported, where appropriate, in each 
section of the Consolidated Statement of Non-Financial 
Information and Sustainability Information.

The format and arrangement of information has been 
adjusted compared to the 2024 report in order to 
facilitate understanding by Repsol’s stakeholders. 
Information from previous reference periods is given 
when such data have been previously reported.

Additionally, methodological adjustments have been 
made to certain metrics and parameters. Specifically, 
changes have been introduced in the materiality 
thresholds of the double materiality assessment and in 
the methodology of the resilience analysis; adjustments 
have been made to the calculation methodologies for 
water intensity, energy intensity, and atmospheric

1.2 Governance

The role of the administrative, management and 
supervisory bodies (GOV-1). Information provided to and 
sustainability matters addressed by the undertaking’s 
administrative, management and supervisory bodies 
(GOV-2)

Repsol’s focus on sustainability is deployed through the 
leadership of the Board of Directors (BoD) and the 
Executive Committee (EC), the supervision of the 
Sustainability and Audit and Control Committees, and 
the coordination of the Sustainability Department (SD), 
across all businesses and corporate areas so that social, 
environmental and governance issues are integrated 
into the Company’s decision-making processes.

Board of Directors

Repsol’s Board of Directors is the highest supervisory and 
decision-making body, holding the Company’s organic 
representation and responsible for steering and 
managing the Company’s business and interests, in all 
matters not reserved for the General Shareholders’ 
Meeting. The specific rules relating to its remit, 
composition, tenure on the Board, the calling and quorum 
of meetings, the manner of passing resolutions and the 
distribution of positions within the Board are set out in 
Articles 31 to 36 of the Bylaws of Repsol, S. A.1 
(hereinafter, the "Bylaws") and in the Regulations of the 
Board of Repsol, S. A.2 (hereinafter, the “Regulations of 
the Board of Directors”).

The greater efficiency and transparency in the exercise 
of the powers and fulfillment of the functions attributed 
to it, justify the creation by the Board of Directors of: (i) 
committees to which decision-making powers are 
attributed on matters whose immediacy and importance 
make it inadvisable to refer them to the full Board; (ii) 
specialized committees, with functions only of 
supervision, report, advice and proposal; as well as (iii) 
those others that, within the internal scope of their 
powers, are attributed to them by law, the Bylaws or the 
Regulations of the Board of Directors. That is why, 
without prejudice to the statutory capacity of the Board 
of Directors to create other committees, the Company 
currently has an Delegated Committee, an Audit and 
Control Committee, a Nomination Committee, a 
Compensation Committee and a Sustainability 
Committee. The specific regulation on the powers and 
functions of the committees of the Board of Directors is 
contained in Articles 33 to 37 of its Regulations and in 
Articles 37 to 39 bis of the Bylaws.

Composition

The Board of Directors is made up of 15 members, in 
compliance with the range set by the Bylaws3, of which 
66.67% are independent4. The detailed composition of 
the Board of Directors, as of December 31, 2025, is 
reported in Section 2.4 Corporate Governance.

Without prejudice to the statutory capacity of the Board 
of Directors to create other committees, the Company 
currently has a Delegated Committee, an Audit and 
Control Committee, a Nomination Committee, a 
Compensation Committee and a Sustainability 
Committee. The composition of the committees of the 
Board of Directors is as follows:

Composition of Board Committees

Diversity on the Board 
of Directors

The Policy on Diversity in the Composition of the 
Repsol, S. A. Board of Directors and the Selection of 
Directors5, approved in 2015 and last updated in 2021 
in accordance with current regulations and recommendations, 
sets out the diversity criteria, in the broad sense, that 
the Board must meet. In accordance with this Policy, the 
process of selecting and appointing directors at Repsol 
is designed to support the election of candidates who 
bring a diversity of skills, knowledge, experience, 
nationalities, age and gender, as well as the appropriate 
competencies to the Board of Directors, enriching 
decision-making and providing plural points of view. The 
Nomination Committee begins with a pre-assessment to 
identify the needs of the Board, determining the 
competencies, knowledge and experience required. 
Based on this evaluation, the Committee defines the 
functions and skills necessary in the candidates to fill 
the vacancies, and may rely on external advice to carry 
out the prior analysis of the Group’s needs and for the 
validation or proposal of candidates. Subsequently, the 
Committee formally submits to the Board of Directors 
the proposals for the appointment and re-election of 
independent external directors, and informs the Board 
of Directors of the proposals for the appointment and 
re-election of the proprietary and executive external 
directors. The Board of Directors reviews and approves 
the Committee’s proposals and, lastly, the approved 
appointments are submitted for ratification by the 
Annual General Meeting.

the rights and obligations of shareholders, as well as the structure, operation and composition of the General Shareholders’ Meeting, the Board of 
Directors and its various committees. They are available on Repsol's corporate website, under: Home > Shareholders and Investors > Corporate 
Governance > Internal Regulations.

2 The Regulations of the Board of Directors are a rule, approved by the Board of Directors, whose purpose is to regulate its structure, powers and 
operation, as well as that of its committees. It is available on Repsol's corporate website, under: Home > Shareholders and Investors > Corporate 
Governance > Internal Regulations.

3 According to the Bylaws, the Board must have between nine (9) and sixteen (16) directors. The Annual General Meeting held on May 31, 2019 set 

the number of members at fifteen (15). The report does not include information on the presence of employee representatives in the entity’s 
governing bodies, as this figure does not exist in the Spanish regulatory framework. According to current legislation, workers can participate in the 
management of the company through collective representation bodies, such as works councils or staff delegates, but they are not entitled to sit 
on boards of directors or other management bodies.

4 The composition of the Board of Directors as of December 31, 2025 is as follows: 10 independent directors, 4 other non-executive directors and 

1 executive director. The percentage of directors in the independent category has decreased from 73.3% in the Annual Corporate Governance 
Report for 2024 to 66.7% in 2025. This change is due to the reclassification of another external director, having exceeded the maximum period of 
12 years to be considered as an independent director.

Equal opportunities

Repsol values generational, cultural, sexual, and gender 
diversity, and the integration of people with different 
abilities as a source of innovation, learning, and respect. 
As of December 31, 2025, the presence of women on its 
Board of Directors is 40%. Thus, the ratio of female to 
male representation on the Board of Directors is 2:3.

Repsol’s Board of Directors, with the support of the 
Nomination Committee, has defined a matrix of 
competencies that reflects the skills, knowledge, and 
experience that are considered necessary for the proper 
performance of its functions. This matrix is periodically 
reviewed to adapt to the needs and challenges of the 
environment and the business.

Board skills matrix

Sustainability is among the competencies identified, 
understood as the ability to contribute to the 
sustainable development of the Company, taking into 
account the economic, social and environmental impacts 
of its activities and the expectations of its stakeholders. 
This competence entails a focus on the principles of 
corporate social responsibility and to the environmental, 
social, and governance (ESG) criteria that govern the 
energy sector.

To identify directors who meet this competence in terms 
of sustainability, the following aspects have been 
considered:

• Knowledge of the main plans and policies of other 
companies in the sector: experience in monitoring 
corporate policies, updating on matters of 
reputational significance and knowledge in corporate 
social responsibility and ESG.

• Knowledge of the relevant regulations related to 

ESG matters.

• The ability to think strategically: evaluate strategic 

options and business plans and budgets.

• Membership of the sustainability committees on the 

Boards of Directors of other large companies, 
especially in the field of energy.

• Knowledge of environmental, social (safety, human 

rights, labor aspects) and governance (ethics, 
compliance and business conduct) matters.

Remit over matters of sustainability encompasses the 
different environmental, social, and governance aspects, 
and therefore it relates to the Company’s material 
impacts, risks and opportunities. 

The skills matrix is presented below:

Sustainability Committee

The Sustainability Committee of Repsol’s Board of 
Directors supervises and advises the Board of Directors 
on environmental, social, and governance issues, 
including climate change and energy transition, security, 
human rights, biodiversity, etc.

Its functions and composition are regulated by Article 
37 of the Regulations of the Board of Directors. In 
accordance with the provisions of these Regulations, the 
Sustainability Committee performs supervisory 
functions related to the energy transition, personal and 
process safety, management and control systems for 
non-financial risks and the reporting of non-financial 
information. This Committee is also responsible for 
guiding the Group’s policy, objectives and guidelines in 
ESG areas; reviewing and reporting to the Board on the 
expectations of the Company’s stakeholders; and 
proposing a Sustainability Policy to the Board.

• Monitoring compliance with 2025-2030 

decarbonization targets within the framework of 
2024-2027 Strategic Update.  

• Company scenario analysis 2023-2050. 

• Sustainability information in the 2024 Consolidated 

Management Report.

• Analysis of the European Commission’s proposals for 

simplifying sustainability reporting.

• The greenhouse gas emissions map for the 2024 fiscal 

year. 

• Analysis and periodic monitoring of safety 

performance: accident rate, scorecard of indicators, 
safety management in contractors and change of 
safety metrics and objectives to improve the learning 
cycle (Safety Excellence Program).

The Committee also regularly7 monitors, inter alia, the 
following matters:

• The summary of investments aligned with the energy 

transition (end of 2024).

• The sustainability strategy and compliance with 

related actions and objectives, together with the 
global and local annual sustainability plans and their 
objectives and the sustainability risk map.

• The results of the double materiality assessment of 

ESG matters.

• The decarbonization roadmap and the fulfillment of 

related actions and targets, as well as emerging risks 
related to the energy transition.

• Performance in natural capital and biodiversity, as 

well as in water management.

• Strategic environmental and safety projects.

• Performance in community relations and human rights 
and its contribution to the Sustainable Development 
Goals (SDGs).

• Performance in ESG ratings.

The Sustainability Committee and the Executive 
Committee regularly monitor information on energy 
transition objectives, risk management, and the degree 
of fulfillment of corporate goals in environmental, 
social, and governance matters, as well as other 
strategic issues related to sustainability. 

Specifically, the Sustainability Committee met five times 
in 2025 and reviewed, among other matters:

• Fulfillment of the energy transition targets by the end 

of 2024.

• The Group’s risk map: short and long term.

• The consolidated map of the Group’s sustainability 
risks at the end of 2024 according to material ESG 
matters.

• The energy transition and climate change risks to 

which the Company is exposed in the 2030 and 2050 
horizons. 

• Review of advances in digitalization in the field of 

sustainability.

• The ratings of the main ESG analysts and rankings  

(ESG performance).

• The Global Sustainability Plan (closing of the 2024 

Plan, and the 2025 Plan) and the Local Sustainability 
Plans.

• The 2024 Sustainable Development Goals 

Contribution Report.

• Progress in the circular economy strategy.   

• Proposal and monitoring of the Company’s 

sustainability objectives (2024 Closing and 2025 
Proposal). 

• Monitoring of progress in human rights, as well as the 
updating of the regulatory framework, including the 
evolution of the Standard for Relations with 
Communities and Human Rights.

• Updating of the energy transition and 
decarbonization metrics and targets.

• The reporting frameworks and regulatory framework 

for the sustainability information of the Consolidated 
Management Report. 

• The process and results of the materiality assessment 

(impacts, risks, opportunities, and environmental 
dependencies8).

• Progress on environmental issues: circular economy, 

biodiversity and water management.

• The main implications for the Company of the COP30 

agreements.

• Analysis of the content of the World Energy Outlook 

2025 (WEO 2025).

In accordance with the provisions of the Regulations of 
the Board of Directors, the Chairman of the Committee 
shall report periodically to the Board of Directors on the 
Committee’s actions.

7 The composition, functions, frequency of its meetings and other aspects related to the functioning of the Sustainability Committee are regulated 

in Article 37 of the Regulations of the Board of Directors.

8 For more information on the list of material impacts, risks and opportunities, see Material impacts, risks, and opportunities and their interaction 

with the strategy and business model.

Audit and Control Committee

Pursuant to the provisions of Article 34 of the 
regulations of the Board of Directors, the Audit and 
Control Committee supports the Board of Directors in 
the performance of its oversight duties through: (i) the 
periodic monitoring and evaluation of the process of 
preparing non-financial information—and the integrity 
of such information; and (ii) the review of the 
effectiveness of internal control systems and non-
financial risk9 control and management systems, among 
others. In addition, among its main activities is the 
review of reports on risk management in the Industrial 
business area and risks related to cybersecurity.

Throughout 2025, issues were jointly presented to the 
Sustainability Committee and the Audit and Control 
Committee that, in accordance with corporate 
governance best practices, must be reviewed by both 
bodies. In this regard, the two committees were briefed 
jointly on the following matters: (i) short- and long-term 
risk map of the Group; (ii) consolidated map of the 
Group’s sustainability risks at the end of 2024 according 
to material ESG matters; (iii) energy transition and 
climate change risks to which the Company is exposed in 
the 2030 and 2050 horizons; (iv) reporting and 
regulatory frameworks of the Consolidated 
Management Report; and (v) the map of emerging and 
climate change risks.

Executive Committee

This body is in charge of proposing and executing the 
global strategy, as well as implementing the basic 
policies established by the Board of Directors. The 
Executive Committee is responsible for implementing 
the Strategic Plan and, in the field of sustainability, has 
among its functions:

• Overseeing the Company’s policies, including those of 

sustainability, safety, the environment and human 
rights.

• Supporting the progress of and defining the Global 
Sustainability Plan and the Safety Excellence Plan.

• Overseeing and proposing to the Board of Directors 

the Company’s strategy in alignment with the energy 
transition, as well as analyzing long-term scenarios 
(2031-2050). 

• Approving and evaluating objectives, including those 

of sustainability, environment and human rights, 
annual budgets and investment plans. 

• Approving, monitoring and evaluating objectives, 

including those of sustainability, which are linked to 
the variable remuneration of the Company’s people.

• Approving the investment qualification 

methodology for its alignment with the energy 
transition and the evaluation of investment proposals 
and their impact on the CII.

• Approving any changes in decarbonization metrics 

and the monitoring, at least once a year, of progress 
towards achieving the energy transition and 
decarbonization targets.

Sustainability Coordination 
Committee

In 2025, Repsol has defined a new body in charge of 
coordinating, facilitating and aligning ESG matters 
between the different businesses and expert areas in 
environmental, social, and governance matters with the 
aim of integrating sustainability into the Company's 
operations and achieving more agile management of 
material impacts, risks and opportunities.

This committee has the participation of the four 
business areas and more than 15 corporate areas, with 
the capacity for internal mobilization so as to more 
diligently and efficiently facilitate decision-making.  

Integration of sustainability-related performance in 
incentive schemes (GOV-3)

To help ensure alignment across the organization, 
employee remuneration is reviewed annually 
by the Executive Committee, the Compensation 
Committee and the Board of Directors, the body that 
approves it, and is linked to compliance with the 
sustainability and decarbonization targets. In the case of 
employees with annual10 variable remuneration, this 
remuneration is based on individual targets of each 
employee and on targets of the business units. 

In 2025, targets related to environmental, social, and 
governance (ESG) matters were integrated into all the 
targets of each business unit, with a weight of between 
20% and 45%. In the particular case of the CEO, the 
weight of the targets related to decarbonization 
and sustainability represented 25% of their total 
variable annual remuneration in 2025. In addition, there 
is a Long-Term Incentive (LTI) for the 2025–2028 period 
in which 40% of the targets are linked to the energy 
transition, which affects all management staff, including 
the CEO, and other employees.

Each type of target is associated with different metrics 
for its measurement, and each metric has an associated 
scale of achievement defined according to its variability 
and strictness. These scales have a minimum threshold 
of compliance below which no incentive entitlement is 
generated and a maximum level set at 100%11.

Likewise, pursuant to the provisions of Article 529 
novodecies of the Capital Companies Act, it is the 
responsibility of the Annual General Meeting to approve 
the Remuneration Policy for directors12 at least every 
three years. Thus, on May 30, 2025, the Annual General 
Meeting approved, at the proposal of the Board of 
Directors, Repsol’s new Remuneration Policy for 
Directors to be applied in 2025, 2026, 2027 and 2028. 

The general principle that defines the Remuneration 
Policy is the pursuit of the reciprocal generation of value 
for the Group and the directors and the alignment of 
their interests in the long term with those of the 
shareholders, for total transparency. 

Repsol’s Remuneration Policy sets out the principles and 
criteria applicable to the remuneration of directors in 
their capacity as directors and for the performance of 
executive functions. These principles and criteria are 

9 For more information on the Internal Control System for Sustainability Information, see Risk management and internal controls for sustainability 

disclosure.

10 It applies to employees of the Company with variable remuneration.
11 See Appendix VI Annual Report on Director Remuneration in this report for further details.
12The Remuneration Policy is available on Repsol’s corporate website, following this path: Home > Shareholders and Investors > Corporate 

Governance >  Board of Directors' compensation.

and the Board of Directors, in order to keep the

Reduction of the Carbon

Renewable generation

Available renewable fuel

(Mta)

Achieve a reduction in the Carbon Intensity Indicator (CII) compared to 
2016 of 15% in 2025 and 28% in 2030, with a specific reduction by 2028.
The CII is calculated as the quotient resulting from dividing the CO2 
equivalent emissions, measured in tonnes (t), corresponding to the 
Company’s operated Scope 1 +2 and Scope 3 corresponding to the use 
of the products obtained from our production of E&P, by the energy of 
these products that we put at the service of society measured in Giga 
Joules (GJ). 
The degree of achievement will be determined according to the value 
obtained at the end of the period with respect to the objective.

Reach a renewable generation capacity of between 9 GW and 10 GW by 
2027 and a specific capacity in 2028. The degree of achievement will be 
determined according to the value obtained at the end of the period 
with respect to the objective.

Achieve an annual production capacity of renewable fuels planned in a 
specific path for the period. The degree of achievement will be 
calculated by comparing the cumulative actual available annual 
production capacity for the period with the cumulative annual available 
production capacity foreseen in the path.

Statement on due diligence (GOV-4)

Repsol has a due diligence process in place to identify, 
prevent, and remediate actual or potential negative 
impacts on people or the environment throughout its 
value chain. This process is underpinned by the Guiding 
Principles on Business and  Human Rights of the UN, 

and the OECD Guidelines for Multinational Enterprises on 
Responsible Business Conduct, as well as other 
international agreements and current human rights 
regulations.

For detailed information on the implementation of the 
due diligence process, see the Index of essential 
elements of the due diligence process. 

Risk management and internal controls over sustainability 
reporting (GOV-5)

The Repsol Group has been working in recent years, 
since the entry into force of Law 11/2018, to strengthen 
the Internal Control System over Sustainability 
Reporting (ICSR), the aim of which is to support the 
reliability of the sustainability information reported. In 
2025, this control model continued to be refined, with 
advances made in the deployment of controls in the 
processes identified as a priority.

The ICSR is defined at Repsol following the same 
principles as the Internal Control System over Financial 
Reporting model and the guidelines of the COSO 
framework (mainly the COSO Integrated Framework and 
COSO ESG). It has the following elements: a governance 
model, a risk identification and assessment process, 
materiality assessment and scope model, inventory of 
controls, including those of information technology, etc. 
With regard to the differentiated responsibility of the 
Company’s units and bodies involved in risk 
management, the ICSR is based on the three-line model, 

the standard recommended by the Global Institute of 
Internal Auditors. The Audit, Control and Risk 
Department evaluates the design and functioning of 
controls, processes and other elements of the Group’s 
ICSR, reporting on these activities to the Audit and 
Control Committee (A&CC).

Responsible bodies

The Board of Directors (BoD) reserves the power to 
approve the risk control and management policy, and 
oversight of internal information and control systems. In 
accordance with the Regulations of the Board of 
Directors, the Audit and Control Committee supervises 
and evaluates the process of preparation and the 
integrity of the financial and non-financial information 
of Repsol, S. A. and its Group, periodically reviewing 
compliance with regulatory requirements, the definition 
of the consolidation perimeter, the effectiveness of 
internal control systems, internal audit, and risk 
management systems, financial and non-financial,so 
that the main risks are identified, managed and made 
known.

Procedures for reviewing and authorizing 
sustainability information and the description 
of the ICSR

Repsol’s ICSR system aims to contribute to the reliability 
of the Company’s sustainability information reported in 
accordance with the current regulatory framework, as 
well as the regulation of the National Securities Market 
Commission (CNMV), the Capital Companies Act, and 
the Good Governance Code of Listed Companies of the 
Spanish National Securities Market Commission.

This model of internal control over sustainability 
information is articulated through an integrated process 
that consists of five components developed through 
seventeen principles in accordance with the provisions 
of the COSO 2013 framework.

The ICSR is integrated into the Organization through the 
establishment of a scheme of roles and responsibilities 
for the different bodies and functions, included in the 
procedures approved and disseminated within the 
Group.

Governance model

At the Sustainability Department (SD), there is a reporting 
unit on sustainability information that governs the 
sustainability information reported, under the supervision 
of the A&CC. This unit:

• Defines the criteria to be used in the reporting of 

sustainability information after analyzing the different 
regulatory frameworks in this field, and informs the 
A&CC of any significant changes in the reporting 
strategy.

• Prepares the sustainability reporting risk map in 

coordination with the unit that manages the 
integrated risk system and the economic-
administrative unit.

• Supports the reasonableness of the reporting criteria 
by verifying compliance with applicable international 
standards.

• Defines procedures and/or guidelines that regulate, 

among other aspects, the roles and responsibilities of 
the areas involved in the sustainability information 
reporting process and the annual reporting milestone 
calendar.

• Assures, in coordination with the Audit, Control and 

Risk Department, that this information has processes 
and systems for adequate identification of risks and 
an adequate set of controls.

The economic-administrative unit governs the financial 
information reported by the Company and is responsible 
for preparing, among other documents, the financial 
statements and the Group’s Management Report and, 
therefore, coordinates the reporting of the financial and 
sustainability information that must be presented 
publicly and so that it is disclosed in compliance with 
the deadlines and other requirements established by 
applicable regulations. In addition, it is responsible for 
monitoring and internally coordinating all the 
requirements of Spanish and European regulators 
(CNMV and ESMA, respectively).

In addition, the economic-administrative unit and the SD 
define, in coordination with the business units and 
corporate areas affected, the processes and controls 
that support consistency between the financial and 
sustainability information presented in the Group’s 
financial statements and Management Report, as well as 
with other documents made public by Repsol.

Supervision units

The Internal Audit unit reports to the Audit and Control 
Committee of the Board of Directors of Repsol. This unit 
has the mission of improving and protecting the value of 
the Organization by providing objective assurance, 
advice and risk-based knowledge, thereby supporting 
the committee in the performance of its duties. Among 
other tasks, this unit oversees the evaluation of the 
design and operation of the controls of the Group’s 
Internal Control System over Sustainability Information, 
reports its results to the Audit and Control Committee 
and the Board of Directors of Repsol.

Business units and corporate areas identified as 
“owners of the controls”

Within the Group, the different business units and 
corporate areas identified as "owners of the controls" 
are responsible for  the validity, execution and proper 
functioning of the processes and controls associated 
with them.

Model components

The documentation making up the ICSR comprises the 
following components:

• The sustainability reporting risk map (reviewed at 

least annually).

• Scope model: corporate perimeter, systems, user-

developed programs, processes, service organizations.

• Documentation of the processes within the scope of 

the ICSR.

•

Inventory of controls identified in the different 
processes.

• Result of the evaluation of the design and operation 

of the controls.

• Certifications of the validity and effectiveness of the 

controls, issued for each year by those responsible for 
each of them.

The ICSR is articulated through a process in which, 
based on the identification and evaluation of the risks of 
reporting sustainability information, a scope model is 
defined that includes the companies covered, the set of 
relevant processes for preparation, the systems and the 
user-developed programs, the review and subsequent 
disclosure of sustainability information, and control 
activities aimed at the prevention and detection of any 
errors.

The Repsol Group has an integrated risk management 
process. This process establishes a uniform 
methodology for the identification and assessment of 
risks by the responsible areas in the Organization. As a 
result of this process, the Repsol Group’s risk map is 
drawn up, of which sustainability risks are a part. For 
more details, see Appendix III Risks in this report.

The main risks that could affect the Group’s 
sustainability information objectives are reflected in the 
sustainability information risk map:

• Leakage of confidential or privileged information.

• Regulatory changes.

•

Inadequate control environment.

• Unavailability and lack of security in information 

systems.

• Errors or omissions in the reporting of sustainability 

information.

Scope and updating

The process of identifying and assessing the causal 
factors of sustainability reporting risks covers all the 
objectives of sustainability information, which could 
impact its reliability.

The inventory of risks and their assessment in terms of 
impact and probability is reviewed annually in 
accordance with the Repsol Group’s integrated risk 
management process, as indicated in Appendix III Risks 
in this report.

When defining the scope of the model, both the risks 
and the exercise of double materiality of the Company 
are considered in order to prioritize control efforts in the 
Company.

Control activities

As indicated above, one of the components of the ICSR 
model is the control activities aimed at mitigating risks 
related to the reporting of sustainability information. In 
this regard, the following types of controls have been 
identified in the ICSR:

General controls

• Entity-level controls: these operate throughout the 
Organization and allow us to achieve global control 
objectives, and cover the risk of “general environment” 
and comply with the COSO 2013 framework. The 
types of general controls include those related to the 
existence and disclosure of a Code of Ethics and 
Business Conduct, a Communications Channel; 
Strategic Company Management; Management of the 
Organizational Structure; People Management and 
Training; Preparation and Management of the Group’s 
Regulations; Corporate Governance; Corporate and 
Power of Attorney Management; Risk Management; 
Audit, Risk and Internal Control Functions.

• General IT Controls: oriented toward supporting the 

reliability, integrity, availability, and confidentiality of 
the information contained in the applications 
considered important to the control model. They are 
grouped into the following typologies: development 
of information systems, modifications to information 
systems, operations and control of access to 
programs and data.

Cross-sectional controls, which in turn can be:

• Company level: primarily governance controls 

exercised by the second lines.

• Reported data: related to the validation roles and the 

calculation methodology used.

Specific controls at the process level from the source of 
the data to its reporting.

Repsol’s policies related to 
sustainability

Repsol has a Code of Ethics and Business Conduct in 
place, approved by the Board of Directors following a 
favorable report from the Audit and Control Committee, 
the Sustainability Committee, and the Ethics and 
Compliance Committee. The Code applies to all 
directors, managers, and employees of the Repsol 
Group, regardless of the type of contract determined by 
their professional or employment relationship. Business 
partners (non-operated joint ventures, contractors, 
suppliers, and other collaborating companies) are part 
of Repsol’s value chain and, for this reason, must act in 
accordance with the Company Code, as well as any 
other applicable contractual provisions when they 
intervene on behalf of or in collaboration with Repsol. 
The Code establishes the minimum standards of 
conduct that must guide the behavior of all its 
recipients as they carry out their professional activity 
and lays down a set of procedures in the event of non-
compliance.

Repsol has fourteen policies, which define the 
Company’s public goals and management fundamentals, 
establishing principles and guidelines for action for all 
Repsol employees with the purpose of fostering 
relationships, processes, and decision-making that are 
aligned with the Company’s values. These policies are 
approved by the Executive Committee, the body 
responsible for the application of these standards. In 
addition, the Company has internal regulations (rules, 
procedures, etc.) for the deployment of policies.

Internal regulations are a management tool, the main 
objective of which is to strengthen control systems and 
promote compliance with external and internal 
requirements; provide criteria for action that make it 
easier for employees to understand their 
responsibilities; to assist in the management and control 
of risks and to contribute to more agile and orderly 
execution of the Company’s activities.

The People and Organization Department is responsible 
for keeping the Company’s regulatory body up to date 
and supports the internal dissemination of regulations 
and their availability to all employees. It also supports 
the review or ratification of policies at least every five 
years.

The ownership of the different regulating documents 
lies with the unit, at the lowest level of management, 
that is responsible for the regulated matter. In order to 
promote the applicability of the regulations, owners 
must review or ratify their rules and procedures when 
necessary, and at least every four years.

Compliance with the regulations is mandatory for all 
employees of Repsol, S. A. and all Repsol Group 
companies worldwide, understood as those in which 
Repsol, S. A. has direct or indirect control of 
management, regardless of the type of legal, 
employment, or commercial relationship, the position 
they occupy and the place where they work. It is also 
mandatory for all those who, not being employees, have 
been temporarily transferred to Repsol to provide 
professional services (secondees) and interns.

Likewise, Repsol’s representatives in the companies in 
which the Group exercises joint control and in the 
consortia must comply with internal regulations, to the 
extent of their capacity in accordance with the 
shareholders'’agreement or the Joint Operating 
Agreement.

Also, employees must promote and encourage, in all 
geographical areas where Repsol is present, the 
adoption of conduct guidelines that are consistent with 
the Group’s rules and regulations among Repsol’s 
partners, suppliers, contractors, and collaborating 
companies.

Repsol’s set of policies is available to employees on the 
Company’s intranet and to other interested parties on 
the corporate website.

The description of the content of the different 
sustainability-related policies, including their general 
objectives, as well as the material subtopics included in 
these policies, defined by Repsol's main impacts, risks, or 
opportunities, as well as their date of approval/review, is 
reported in Sustainability notes.

The sections on environmental, social and governance 
information include policies related to the specific topic 
of each standard.

1.3 Business model and strategy

Strategy, business model and value chain (SBM-1)

Repsol is present throughout the energy value chain 
and markets a range of products in more than 
90 countries. The main products or services offered by 
the Company are detailed below:

• Supply of crude oil, intermediate products, chemical 
raw materials and lipid residues consumed by the 
Group’s industrial complexes and third parties.

• Production and marketing of fuels, biofuels and 

petrochemical products in international markets. The 
Company’s projects include the production of 
renewable hydrogen.

• Development, production and marketing of lubricants, 

aviation fuels, asphalts and petroleum-derived 
specialties.

• Marketing and trading of liquefied natural gas.

• Generation of low-emissions electricity in Spain, the 

USA, Chile and Italy.

• Marketing of retail Electricity and Gas in Spain and 

Portugal.

• Maritime transport for the Group's activity.

• Multi-energy solutions.

Repsol markets wholesale (B2B) and retail (B2C) 
products1 and services in the first segment. The main 
group of customers includes industries and companies 
from various sectors (automotive, food, chemical, 
packaging, aviation, etc.). Customers of retail products 
and services are mainly consumers of fuels and other 
petroleum products (mainly marketed at service 
stations), natural gas, electric mobility, electricity and 
energy services. For more information, see Consumers 
and end customers.

The breakdown in 2025 of ordinary2 income by product 
type and segment is as follows:

1 None of the Company’s products have been listed as prohibited in any of the markets in which it operates.
2 Corresponds to the items of Sales and Revenue from the provision of services in the consolidated  income statement (see Note 4.1 of the 

Consolidated Financial Statements) prepared in accordance with International Financial Reporting Standards (IFRS) issued by the International 
Accounting Standards Board (IASB) and adopted by the European Union (EU). The Company does not break down revenues by significant sector of 
the European Sustainability Standards (hereinafter, ESRS), or additional sectors of the ESRS, as the final list of sectors of the ESRS has not been 
effectively published by the EU. The information will be incorporated into the report once the list is officially made public.

(1) Relates mainly to condensates and natural gas liquids and natural gas. There are no fossil gas revenues aligned with the technical criteria of 

the EU Sustainable Finance Taxonomy. For more information, see Taxonomy Regulation.

One of the key pillars of the Strategic Plan is the 
Company’s decarbonization, which mainly impacts issues 
of climate change and circular economy. Social and 
governance issues support Repsol’s decarbonization 
plan.

Sustainability-related targets

Repsol monitors the effectiveness of its policies and 
actions with respect to material sustainability-related 
IROs by defining their objectives in its Global 
Sustainability Plan.

The Global Sustainability Plan, which is approved by the 
Executive Committee and overseen by the Sustainability 
Committee, incorporates the main sustainability 
objectives. This plan is the framework for action for  
their deployment at the local level in the different 
business units of countries and industrial centers of the 
Iberian Peninsula through the Local Sustainability Plans. 
The details of the plan’s progress are published annually 
on the corporate website.   

The Sustainability Committee of Repsol’s Board of 
Directors is responsible for monitoring and advising on 
policies and objectives in the areas of the environment, 
safety, and social responsibility.

Repsol implements certification, verification, and 
assurance processes through both internal and external 
audits to ensure compliance with national and 
international regulations. In addition, it addresses 
voluntary processes, such as ISO 9001 quality 
certification, ISO 45001 safety, ISO 14001 environment, 
ISO 50001 energy management, and circular economy 
such as ISCC EU and OSC Europe. Repsol is also 
evaluated by different ESG ratings (MSCI, Sustainalytics, 
CHRB, among others), which analyze its objectives and 
management practices, and have made a positive 
assessment of the Company’s performance and strategy. 

Business model and value chain

Repsol’s activities are structured into four segments:

• Upstream: exploration and production of crude oil 

and natural gas reserves, as well as development of 
low-carbon geological solutions, especially CO2 
storage.

•

Industrial: petroleum refining, petrochemical and 
trading, transportation and wholesale marketing of 
crude oil, natural gas and fuels, including the 
development of activities related to new products 
such as hydrogen, renewable fuels and synthetic fuels.

• Customer: mobility (service stations) and marketing of 

fuels (gasoline, diesel, aviation kerosene, liquefied 
petroleum gases, biofuels, etc.), electricity and gas 
and lubricants and other specialties.

• Low-Carbon Generation (LCG): generation of 

electricity from renewable sources (solar, wind, 
hydroelectric and storage) and through combined 
cycles of natural gas.

The Upstream business aims to develop and exploit the 
Company’s crude oil and gas reserves. The main inputs of 
industrial complexes are crude oil, natural gas, 
intermediate materials, chemical raw materials and raw 
materials for the production of biofuels. The trading 
activity aims to support the supply of these inputs to 
industrial complexes (including combined cycle plants) 
and the sale to third parties. Further information on the 
products and services offered can be found in Strategy, 
business model and value chain (SBM-1).

Also, information regarding the current and expected 
benefits for customers, investors, and other 
stakeholders of the Company’s business model and 
value chain is included in Chapter 5 – Business 
performance.

The following figure presents Repsol’s value chain, 
highlighting the main activities that comprise it, both its 
own operations and those upstream and downstream, 
as well as the material actors linked to each of them:

Repsol’s value chain consists of a set of interrelated 
activities that encompass upstream phases, own 
operations, and downstream processes, involving 
different actors identified in the infographic. In the 
upstream phase, the activities cover a broad base of 
suppliers that are responsible for the provision of raw 
materials, manufacturing goods and construction 
services necessary for the development of facilities and 
projects within the scope of the Company’s own 
operations (for more information see S2 Workers in the 
value chain).

In relation to the aforementioned operations, the 
Company carries out Upstream activities for oil and gas, 
where it operates directly or in alliance with partners. 
These activities may be supplemented with 
decarbonization initiatives involving carbon capture 
and storage.

Repsol transforms crude oil and various alternative raw 
materials, like residual plastic and other renewable 
material, through chemical and refining processes, and 
also produces renewable fuels. These activities are 
complemented by transport, storage and trading  
functions—both proprietary and supplier—that allow 
intermediate and final products to be managed and 
mobilized throughout the supply chain.

These operations are integrated with the production and 
sale of electricity, an activity in which the Company 
participates both as a direct operator and through 
agreements with strategic partners. The presence of 
these actors reflects an operating model based on 
cooperation and the shared development of energy 
solutions.

Finally, the marketing and sale of energy products and 
derivatives are managed through different channels, 
which include the distribution of LPG, petroleum 
derivatives, and the network of service stations where 
fossil fuels, renewables, and electricity are primarily sold. 

In the downstream section, industrial and private 
customers, together with society as a whole, constitute 
the final destination of the products and services offered 
by the Company. 

Key actors are involved throughout the value chain: 
suppliers, who are essential for the supply and execution 
of projects; partners, who participate in strategic 
activities in multiple links of the business; shareholders 
and investors, who support the Company’s development 
and transformation; and customers who use the products 
and services. 

Interests and views of stakeholders (SBM-2)

Repsol annually reviews the stakeholder map and has 
tools to obtain information about their opinion. For the 
review of the map, the experts’ criteria of the Company 
are taken into account, the comparison with peer 
companies and the review of internal and external 
documentation with which an analysis of the context 
and the value chain is made, so that all Repsol 
stakeholders are characterized throughout the value 
chain. In 2025, the value chain for energy companies in 
the O&G sector has been taken as a reference, which has 
been adapted to Repsol’s specific circumstances and 
processes. 

The current map identifies nine stakeholders. The 
information that comes from stakeholders is important 
for determining the materiality (impact and financial) of 
the Company’s ESG matters. This information is included 
in the definition of the objectives that are subsequently 
incorporated into Repsol’s Sustainability Plans. 

Annually, the stakeholder map is presented together 
with the results of the double materiality assessment to 
the Sustainability Committee.

Information on the main stakeholders is detailed below: 

Stakeholder engagement processes

2025. Specifically, the following new objectives have 
been defined: 

Environmental

2035 compared to 2018.

•

threatened species by 2030 through the financing, 
implementation and monitoring of specific projects.

• Have 100% of biodiversity action plans on assets 

located in biodiversity-sensitive areas.

Social

• Consolidate an attractive value proposition in 

2025-2028 for employees, reflected in an annual 
global voluntary turnover rate of less than 6%.

•

•

investment associated with just transition in the 
2026-2030 period

Material impacts, risks, and opportunities and their 
interaction with strategy and business model (SBM-3) 

In its double materiality assessment, Repsol has 
identified the impacts, risks, and opportunities that are 
material in environmental, social, and governance 
aspects. 

In this analysis, detailed information is reported with a 
description of the impacts, classified by each ESRS topic 
and subtopic, and indicating whether it is positive or 
negative, current or potential, also detailing the effect 
on the different actors and businesses in the value 
chain.

The results show a greater number of positive impacts, 
most of which are concentrated in the social sphere 
(company personnel, workers in the value chain and 
affected groups) and in governance (corporate 
conduct). Most of the negative material impacts are in 
the environmental field, focused on the issues of climate 
change, pollution, water and biodiversity. For all of 
them, the actions that the Company is carrying out to 
reduce negative impacts and enhance positive ones are 
specified. 

The material risks that may affect the Company’s 
performance in terms of the environment are related to 
the mitigation of climate change and energy 
consumption, in the social sphere with industrial safety, 
and in governance with the possibility of having 
sanctions for regulatory non-compliance and risks 
associated with cyberattacks that affect operations.

Further methodological details are provided at the end 
of this section. 

The results of the materiality assessment have been 
evaluated by the Sustainability Department, the Risk 
team and those responsible for the Company’s 
businesses and corporate areas. In addition, they have 
been presented to the Executive Committee by the 
General Department of Energy Transition, Technology, 
and Institutional Affairs, Deputy to the CEO. In addition, 
it is presented to the Sustainability Committee, a body 
of the Board of Directors, as set out in the internal 
control established in the Internal Control System over 
Sustainability Reporting  (ICSR).

The results are used to identify actions to be integrated 
into Global and Local Sustainability Plans, with the aim 
of maximizing positive impacts and opportunities while 
minimizing risks and negative impacts.

Disclosure requirements in ESRS covered by the 
undertaking’s sustainability statement (IRO-2)

The Index of disclosure requirements fulfilled in the 
preparation of the Consolidated Statement of Non-
Financial Information and Sustainability Information 
(2025 Consolidated Management Report) is based on 
the results of the double materiality assessment, and 
indicates where the corresponding information can be 
found in the report.

Impacts, risks, and opportunities (IRO)

The following table presents the 54 material IROs—
namely the impacts (positive or negative), risks, and 
opportunities—classified by topic and subtopic, 
together with their current or potential effects across 
the most relevant time horizon. It also shows each IRO’s 
impact across the value chain and on each of the 
Company’s businesses, as well as its effect on 
stakeholders. Each material IRO, along with its 
stakeholder impacts, is further detailed in its 
corresponding thematic section.

The effects of the IROs shown in the table are directly or 
indirectly related to the achievement of the objectives 
of the Company’s Strategic Plan. For example, climate 
change and circular economy have a direct effect on 
decarbonization. In the social dimension, the effects are 
primarily related to talent attraction and the 
establishment of trust-based relationships with the 
various stakeholders across the value chain.

The Group has a methodology that provides an overall 
view of risks and quantifies them by economic, 
reputational and people affected impact, across the 
different business areas, identifying effective mitigation 
measures. 

However, the energy transition also presents 
opportunities for the Group, such as energy efficiency 
and access to aid for sustainable financing.

During 2025, there have been no risk events related to 
the aforementioned subtopics that have had material 
financial effects for the Company. For more information 
on risks, see Notes 20 and 27 of the Consolidated 
Financial Statements. For more information on linking 
transition risks to financial statements, see Note 3.5.2 of 
the Consolidated Financial Statements.

In 2025, an opportunity related to access to sustainable 
financing support materialized, with public 
administration funds received to finance the 
development of two 100‑MW electrolyzers at the 
Cartagena and Bilbao refineries to produce renewable 
hydrogen, for a total amount of 315 million euros. These 
grants will become definitive —and therefore not 
subject to repayment— once Repsol demonstrates that 
the investments have been executed under the terms 
established by the Administration; see Note 19 of the 
2025 Consolidated Financial Statements.

The actions put in place to mitigate these risks or take 
advantage of the opportunities described are reported 
in Climate change, Safety and security, Business 
conduct and Cybersecurity. For more information on the 
Company’s resilience, see Material impacts, risks, and 
opportunities and their Interaction with Strategy and 
Business Model. 

1.4 Double materiality 
assessment

Description of the process to identify and assess material 
impacts, risks, and opportunities (IRO-1)

The double materiality assessment carried out at 
Repsol is based on the methodology established in the 
ESRS. This analysis which covers the entire value chain 
is reviewed annually and consists of the following 
phases: 

1. Stakeholder mapping.

2. Context and value chain analysis.

3.

Identification of impacts, risks and opportunities 
(IROs).

4.

Impact and financial materiality assessments.

5. Double materiality matrix development.

6. Determination of the list of data points to be 

reported.

Effects of risks and opportunities on the business 
model

The risks associated with climate change mitigation can 
significantly impact production costs, especially those 
derived from regulatory changes (IRO-11), adoption of 
less efficient technologies (IRO-8), increased 
competition and decreased sales (IRO-7). Likewise, 
decoupling of climate investment (IRO-12) could limit 
trade opportunities. In addition, the scarcity of raw 
materials (IRO-10) can generate problems of access to 
supplies.

However, the energy transition also offers 
opportunities for the Company, such as energy 
efficiency optimization (IRO-3), which can increase 
profitability and competitiveness in the 

market. In addition, access to aid for sustainable 
financing (IRO-13) contributes to improving alignment 
with sustainability objectives and facilitates the raising 
of capital under ESG criteria.

There are additional risks related to potential industrial 
accidents (IRO-25) or attacks on facilities (IRO-27), 
which could result in operational disruptions and 
financial losses. In the area of governance, non-
compliance may result in sanctions (IRO-49) and 
reputational damage. Likewise, the impact on 
operations (IRO-53) could result in negative 
reputational consequences and production losses.

M

METHODOLOGY

Methodology 
of the double materiality 
assessment 

Description of the process for determining 
and assessing material impacts, risks, 
and opportunities

The double materiality process is reviewed annually, in line with 
the requirements of the CSRD. The process is jointly led by the 
Sustainability Department and the Audit, Control and Risk 
Department and relies on the collaboration of experts with 
technical, strategic and cross-cutting knowledge across all the 
sustainability areas established in the ESRS standards 
(hereinafter, ESRS). The process considers both the impacts that 
the Company’s operations may have on stakeholders and the 
potential effects that the external environment may have on the 
Organization.The phases of the analysis are outlined below:

1.

2.

3.

4.

Stakeholder mapping: Nine main stakeholder groups have 
been identified across the value chain.  

Context and value chain analysis: A review of the internal 
and external context has been conducted to identify 
possible significant aspects. The corporate map has been  
revisited and updated including all Repsol companies 
within the scope of the double materiality assessment. The 
value chain has been defined using as reference the O&G 
value chain model, while adapting it to Repsol’s specific 
context and characteristics. The double materiality process 
has been tailored to incorporate critical activities, sensitive 
areas and key commercial relationships. Besides, 
environmental and social risks have been assessed across 
the entire value chain, using specific methodologies, such 
as the LEAP approach, to identify high-risk locations and 
prioritize adverse impacts, while enabling alignment with 
ESRS requirements.

Review of possible material topics and IROs: An expanded 
identification of IROs has been carried out, promoting 
alignment and traceability with the topics, subtopics and 
sub-subtopics defined in the ESRS. In addition, Repsol has 
identified entity-specific topics of its activity, and added 
them to the expanded list of IROs. For each IRO, the 
affected stakeholder, the value chain segment impacted 
and, where relevant, the geographical area have been 
identified.

Impact and financial materiality: IROs have been assessed 
from both an impact and financial perspective. The 
materiality of each topic and subtopic is determined by the 
IROs associated with each of them. For both impact and 
financial materiality, three time horizons have been 
identified—short (1 year), medium (1–5 years) and long (>5 
years)—with specific adaptations for climate change 
(medium‑term 2030 and long‑term 2050), being the 
horizon with the greatest impact the only one which has 
been assessed.

For impact materiality, the following dimensions have been 
assessed: scale (severity or benefit of the impact), scope 
(number of people or geographical extent affected), the 
remediability to restore the situation prior to the produced 
impact (only for negative impacts) and the probability of 
occurrence (100% for current impacts and/or involving 
human rights). The final value of the impact is the outcome 
of multiplying the severity (average of scale, scope, and 
remediability) by the probability of occurrence. Where an 
impact involves risks to life or the possibility of causing a 
serious and irreversible injury or harm, severity takes 
precedence over probability in the impact assessment.

In addition. a panel of external experts supplemented the 
impact materiality assessment with additional insights.

For financial materiality, risks and opportunities have been 
assessed using three methodologies: risk map, quantitative 
estimations and qualitative assessment, prioritizing 
matters according to their magnitude and likelihood within 
the time horizon with the highest valuation or potential 
economic impact (based on historical data or expert 
judgment).

To determine the threshold value, Repsol has established a 
cut-off aligned with the value used in the financial 
materiality equivalent to a score of 3 on a 1‑to‑5 scale. The 
same threshold has been applied to impact materiality 
following a sensitivity analysis across different scenarios.

5.

6.

Drawing up the double materiality matrix: The matrix 
results from consolidating the outcomes of the IRO 
assessments, identifying material and non‑material topics 
and subtopics. The results are reviewed by the Executive 
Committee and by the Sustainability Committee. 

List of data points to be reported: Based on the material 
topics and subtopics, the ESRS‑required data points 
disclosed in this report were identified.  

The results of the 2025 double materiality assessment differ 
from those of 2024 due to methodological changes—such as 
revised time horizons and the alignment of the materiality 
threshold with financial statement parameters—resulting in a 
reduction of material IROs from 61 in 2024 to 54 in 2025.

2. Environmental information

2.1 Climate change 

2.1.1 Governance

Integration of sustainability-related performance in 
incentive schemes (ESRS 2 GOV-3)

Repsol has set targets and metrics in relation to 
decarbonization and sustainability, integrating them 
into its annual variable remuneration system. More 
precisely, 25% of the CEO’s annual variable 
remuneration is tied to sustainability and 
decarbonization objectives, with 15% tied to the latter. 
Within this percentage, 5% is allocated to the increase in 
the installed capacity of renewable generation 
envisaged in the 2025 Annual Budget and 5% to the 
development of other low-carbon platforms and 5% to 
the reduction of the Carbon Intensity Indicator (CII)1. 
The degree of achievement of these objectives is 
assessed by the Compensation Committee based on the 
evidence provided. 

For each metric, a quantitative compliance level scale is 
established, depending on its nature. The details can be 
found in Targets and metrics set out in the CEO's 2025 
Annual Variable Remuneration in relation to 
sustainability. 

In addition, Repsol has implemented a Long-Term 
Incentive (LTI) for the period 2025-2028, which affects 
all management staff, including the CEO, and other 
employees, in which 40% of the objectives are tied to 
the energy transition. This LTI includes specific metrics 
such as the reduction of the CII, renewable generation 
capacity (GW), and renewable fuel production capacity 
(Mt/a).      

2.1.2 Strategy 

The energy sector faces the challenge of supplying 
secure and affordable energy in a context of growing 
energy demand, driven global economic growth. At the 
same time, energy efficiency and a transition to 
decarbonized energies are needed in order to meet the 
urgency of curbing the impact of greenhouse gas 
emissions on the climate. The pace of this energy 
transition is mainly determined by technological 
development and the implementation of enabling policies 
on a global and regional scale. 

Transition plan for climate change mitigation (E1-1)

In 2019, Repsol began its transformation to align itself 
with an energy transition compatible with the global 
ambition to achieve net zero emissions. Since then, it has 
made this commitment concrete through different 
strategic plans, the most recent being 2024–2027 
Strategic Update approved by the Board of Directors on 
February 21, 2024, which reinforces the Company’s 
decarbonization objectives. 

This strategic process was completed with the 
presentation of the Energy Transition Plan and its 
submission to an advisory vote at the Annual General 
Meeting on May 10, 2024, in which it obtained the 
support of 70% of the capital present or represented.

Transition metrics and targets are regularly reviewed to 
adapt them, where necessary, to the environment in 
which energy sector operates. During the last few 
months, metrics and objectives for the 2026–2028 
period have been reviewed, and are now included in this 
report and will be disclosed to the market in greater 
detail in March 2026.

Repsol reaffirms its focus on climate change mitigation 
and its ambition to reach net zero GHG emissions by 
2050, in line with the Paris Agreement, which seeks to 
limit global warming to 1.5°C and achieve climate 
neutrality by the second half of the century2. 

Repsol’s energy transition strategy enables the 
Company to progressively decarbonize its own activities 
(reducing its Scope 1 and 2 emissions) while also 
contributing to the decarbonization of the value chain, 
especially for consumers, through the development of 
low- or zero-carbon3 products (Scope 3, category 11). 

In this context, Repsol set the goal of achieving net zero 
emissions by 2050, which involves reducing greenhouse 
gas emissions to net zero in Scopes 1, 2 and 3. To make 
progress towards this goal, the Company set two 
targets for 2030: reducing direct emissions by 55% 
(Scope 1 and 2 under operational control) compared to 
2016 and reducing total emissions (Scopes 1, 2 and 3) 
by 20% compared with 2018. Scopes 1 and 2 include 
emissions from operated assets and Scope 3 the 
emissions associated with category 11 for the use of the 
products sold (gas and petroleum products).4 

1 Under Repsol's criteria, low-carbon investments include all investments in projects that contribute to the Company's decarbonization, and refer 

to projects that do not generate emissions or do so in a very low proportion compared to their fossil energy alternatives. It does not fully 
correspond to the European Taxonomy's definition of sustainable investments.

2 The goal of achieving carbon neutrality is to achieve a balance in the second half of the century between anthropogenic emissions by sources and 
anthropogenic removals by sinks, on the basis of equity and in the context of sustainable development and efforts to eradicate poverty, as set out 
in Article 4.1 of the Paris Agreement. The Methodology for climate change reporting and metrics provides detailed information on the analysis 
used to determine the strategy’s alignment with the 1.5°C objective.

3 Low- or zero-carbon products refer to those that generate low or no greenhouse gas emissions throughout their life cycle, compared to 

conventional products.  

4 In this metric, Scope 3 refers to Category 11 (use of sold products). The absolute Scope 1, 2 and 3 emissions metric covers 91% of the Company’s 

total operated emissions.

Since the energy transition requires reducing greenhouse 
gas (GHG) emissions while simultaneously meeting 
society’s energy demand, the Company also uses the CII as 
a key metric to assess progress towards decarbonization 
per unit of primary energy consumption. This indicator 
reflects the combined impact of the main pillars of 
decarbonization, which are key to its climate strategy, 
namely  the development of electricity and renewable 
fuels. 

Repsol has set targets to reduce its Carbon Intensity 
Indicator (CII)5 by 15% for 2025 compared to 2016: 25% 
±1% for 2030, 55% for 2040 and 100% for 2050. 

Repsol thus has two complementary indicators that 
incorporate Scope 3 emissions with different perimeters 
and that strengthen its decarbonization strategy.

It is noteworthy that by 2025 all the established 
decarbonization targets have been achieved: a) reducing 
the CII by 15%; b) reducing methane intensity to 0.20% 
in the operated assets of Upstream; and c) reducing 
emissions from routine gas flaring by 50% in the same 
assets compared to 2018. These last two targets 
contribute to the 2030 Scope 1 and 2 emissions 
reduction target.

Decarbonization pillars and levers 

Repsol’s energy transition strategy is structured around 
five strategic pillars which represent the main areas of 
action in the energy transition towards net zero 
emissions.

For each pillar, decarbonization levers are deployed, 
which are concrete initiatives that generate effective and 
quantifiable reductions in Scope 1, 2 and 3 emissions.

Each pillar integrates both these levers and other mainly 
commercial actions that, although they do not directly 
reduce emissions, support the transition in the 
Company’s energy model. 

This structure makes it possible to clearly distinguish 
between the strategic framework (pillars) and the 
operational mechanisms (levers) that drive the 
achievement of the 2030 interim targets, as set out in 
Actions and resources in relation to climate change 
policies (E1-3) and Targets related to climate change 
mitigation and adaptation (E1-4). 

Pillar 1. Operational excellence and asset portfolio 
management 

Energy efficiency, electrification of processes, reduction 
of direct CO2 and methane emissions from current 
operations and optimization of Upstream asset portfolio 
towards less carbon-intensive assets are the levers for 
achieving the target of a 55% reduction in Scope 1 and 2 
emissions by 2030 compared with the 2016 baseline 
year.

Capital allocation to the Upstream business is consistent 
with stable production levels in the range of 550–600 
kboe/d throughout this decade. As previously indicated, 
investment is prioritized in higher‑margin assets and 
projects with lower greenhouse gas emissions, which will 
enable a reduction in carbon intensity over the coming 
years, reaching an intensity of 10 kgCO2e/boe in 2027. 

At its industrial businesses (Refining and Chemicals), the 
Company plans to continue implementing measures, 
electrification of processes, and increasing the use of 
renewable electricity.

Pillar 2. Renewable electricity generation 

Renewable generation is key to Repsol’s energy 
transition to reduce emissions per unit of energy 
produced. Since the start of this activity in 2018, Repsol 
has built a portfolio of renewable electricity projects 
(wind, solar and hydro), with 5.8 GW of installed 
capacity in 2025 and projected to exceed 106 GW by 
2030, especially in Spain and the USA. Growth in the 
coming years has been adjusted to developments in the 
environment, marked by higher development and 
financing costs, as well as lower tax incentives in the 
United States, prioritizing investments according to the 
profitability thresholds established by Repsol.

The strategy contemplates the rotation of asset 
portfolios, with the incorporation of partners and the 
financing of projects to achieve better rates of return on 
net investments made.

Pillar 3. Liquid and gaseous renewable fuels

The competitiveness of Repsol’s refineries, in the top 
European quartile by economic7 margin, enables their 
transformation, in alignment with demand trends 
promoted by regulation and focusing on the circular 
economy, with the use of organic waste to produce 
renewable fuels and the recycling of plastics. The 
refineries will continue  to operate through to 2030, due 
to the resilience shown by demand in Spain and the 
selective expansion to adjacent markets, while 
increasing the production of renewable fuels. 

5 The CII includes in its numerator the Scope 1 and 2 emissions operated, as well as the Scope 3 emissions of categories 11, 12 and 1 related to the 
primary energy produced by the Company. These categories account for approximately 37% of total Scope 3 emissions. Together, the CII covers 
41% of the Company’s total operated emissions. 

6 Data reviewed as a result of the update to the 2026–2028 projections.
7 Strategic Update 2024-2027

Repsol plans to reach a production capacity6 in Europe 
by  2030 of 1.6-1.8 Mt of renewable fuels, 0.7-0.8 TWh 
of biomethane and 0.6-0.8 GWe of renewable hydrogen, 
with these metrics adjusted from 2024 projections to 
reflect demand and regulatory trends, particularly with 
regard to renewable hydrogen. The targets for 
renewable fuel and biomethane production are detailed 
in Targets related to resource use and the circular 
economy (E5-3).  

Pillar 4. Multi-energy for customers  

Repsol is moving toward a multi-energy model focused 
on the supply from different energy sources to meet 
both the needs of its customers and European 
decarbonization goals. This approach allows the 
Company to reduce emissions throughout its value 
chain (Scope 3) by diversifying its portfolio of energy 
products and services.  

Repsol is a leading player in the liquid and gaseous fuels 
market in the Iberian Peninsula, and has expanded its 
offer to include renewable fuels, residential and mobility 
electricity, and other low-carbon products.

Repsol has developed in the Iberian Peninsula one of the 
most extensive 100% renewable fuel networks in 
Europe, with availability at more than 1,550 service 
stations, with 56% of the network in Spain offering 
multi-energy solutions that include 3,600 public 
charging points for electric vehicles.

The Company is also developing the sustainable aviation 
fuel (SAF) market and now has a 100% renewable fuel 
production plant at its Cartagena refinery, with a 
capacity of 250,000 tons per year for the co-production 
of renewable road diesel and SAF from oily waste. 

In the field of residential energy marketing, Repsol has 3 
million electricity and gas customers in Spain and 
Portugal, with the aim of reaching 5 million by 2030.

Pillar 5. Development of carbon sinks

Carbon sinks constitute a pillar that will not be material 
for the Company at least until after 2030. Until then, 
Repsol’s activity is limited to reducing technical, 
regulatory, and business risks through its participation 
in certain projects at an early development stage.

Repsol’s decarbonization pathway considers the 
long‑term contribution of carbon sinks (CO₂ capture and 
storage projects and, eventually, natural solutions if 
needed) to complete net-zero GHG emissions by 2050).

By 2030, the Company's portfolio of marketed products 
will reflect a shift towards more decarbonized 
alternatives. These will include renewable electricity and 
renewable fuels (liquid fuels, biogas and hydrogen), 
where 20% of the portfolio will consist of low-carbon 
products (based on energy content), together with a 
reduction in sales of natural gas and petroleum 
products, which are the levers associated with the 
Company's reduction of Scope 3 emissions.

% Product mix (based on energy content) 

1 Low-carbon molecules correspond to products marketed by the 
Company, whose use does not generate net CO2 emissions such as 
biofuels, renewable hydrogen, synthetic fuels, chemical products, 
lubricants and asphalts.

Repsol assesses progress in the execution of its energy 
transition strategy through the various indicators 
detailed in sections E1-4 and E1-6, including absolute 
Scope 1, 2 and 3 emissions, operational emissions 
(Scopes 1 and 2) and the CII.

•

In 2025, Repsol's total emissions (Scopes 1, 2 and 3, 
category 11)4 amounted to 184.7 MtCO₂e, down 
17.4% on 2018, advancing toward the target of –20% 
by 2030.

• Scope 1 and 2 emissions stood at 13.9 MtCO₂e in 

2025, down 45.3% on 2016 and on track to reach the 
-55% reduction commitment of these emissions for 
2030.

• Meanwhile, the CII stood at 65.2 gCO₂e/MJ, which 

represents a decline of 15.1% compared with 2016, 
exceeding the target of –15% in 2025 and with a focus 
on achieving a reduction of 25% ±1 in 2030.

These results reflect sustained progress in the 
decarbonization of the Company's energy portfolio and 
the consolidation of its strategy to reach net zero 
emissions by 2050, as detailed in Targets related to 
climate change mitigation and adaptation (E1-4).

Investments

Repsol has classified the following investments as low-
carbon: energy efficiency and process electrification, 
renewable electricity generation, production and 
marketing of renewable fuels (biofuels, renewable 
hydrogen, synthetic fuels, biogas), chemicals (long-life 
polymers) and non-combustible products, CCS, 
marketing of renewable electricity, distributed 
generation and renewable electric mobility, and R&D 
and corporate venturing investments in low-carbon 
technologies.
