excluded from the EU Paris-aligned Benchmarks in 
accordance with the exclusion criteria laid down in 
Articles 12(1)(d) to (g) and 12(2) of Commission 
Delegated Regulation (EU) 2020/1818.

The Company has a robust financing structure that 
allows it to undertake its investments and it maintains 
access to diversified sources of capital at a competitive 
cost (financial credits, sustainable bonds, grants, etc.), 
which strengthens its ability to meet its climate and 
operational commitments. 

The allocation of resources is under continuous 
evaluation in order to prioritize profitable projects that 
have the greatest impact on the energy transition and 
that contribute to the fulfillment of the Company’s 
decarbonization plan. This approach, which is 
underpinned by a robust financial management 
framework for the resilience and sustainability of 
Repsol’s operations in the face of changes in either the 
market or the regulatory environment.

Any investment or divestment submitted to the 
Executive Committee and the Board of Directors for 
approval includes a report prepared by the 
Sustainability Department which assesses the carbon 
intensity of the project and its impact on the Company’s 
CII. Based on this methodology, investments are 
classified into three categories:

• Aligned with the energy transition, if they do not 
negatively affect achievement of the targets for 
reduction of the CII or if they contribute to achieving 
these targets.

• Enabling the energy transition when they have a 

negative impact of less than 1% on the CII and this can 
be offset with other initiatives. For Upstream, 
investments are subject to additional conditions, such 
as the limitation of the useful life of exploitable 
reserves and the exclusion of extra-heavy crudes, tar 
sands and offshore projects in the Arctic.

• Misaligned, if they do not meet the requirements of 

the above categories.

This approach allows for investment decisions that are 
aligned with the CII reduction target.

In 2025, using the investment qualification 
methodology, a report from the Sustainability 
Department was incorporated into 16 investment 
proposals (8 from Upstream, 5 from Low-Carbon 
Generation and 3 from Industrial). Of these, 75% were 
aligned, 12.5% facilitating, and 12.5% misaligned. 

To promote investment decisions consistent with its 
decarbonization goals, Repsol applies an internal carbon 
price to all projects. 

1.5 ºC alignment

Repsol has assessed its energy transition strategy 
against scenarios of the Intergovernmental Panel on 
Climate Change (IPCC) that are compatible with limiting 
global warming to 1.5 °C by the end of this century. In 
particular, the analysis is based on mitigation scenarios 
classified as C1, which represent global pathways with a 
probability greater than 50% of not exceeding this 
threshold by the end of the century, and with a transient 
and limited overshoot of temperature above 1.5 °C. 
There are other scenarios, classified as C2, also 
compatible with 1.5 ºC, but involve a more prolonged 
temperature overshoot.

The C1 scenarios envisage a global reduction in 
greenhouse gas emissions of 40–50% in 2030 compared 
with 2019, with steeper declines in coal (≈ 75%) and 
smaller declines in oil and gas (≈ 10%). 

Of the total of 97 IPCC C1 scenarios, those that achieve 
a net reduction of more than 95% of emissions from the 
energy sector by 2050 (19 scenarios) show a median 
reduction in greenhouse gas emissions from oil and gas 
of 14% in 2030. Similar results are observed when 
restricting the analysis to the nine scenarios with 
assumptions that Repsol considers more realistic in 
terms of technological availability and carbon costs.

Meanwhile, the International Energy Agency’s (IEA) Net 
Zero Emissions (NZE) scenario, updated in the WEO 
2025 and aligned with a limit of 1.5 °C13 and emissions 
neutrality by 2050, does not project a specific data for 
2030. By interpolation, a reduction of 22% in oil and gas 
is estimated for 2030 compared with 2019.14

From this analysis, it can be concluded that Repsol’s 
goal of achieving a 20% reduction in absolute Scope 1, 2 
and 3 emissions by 2030 compared with 2018, which 
covers more than 91% of the Company’s total emissions, 
falls within the range of the set of IPCC and IEA 
scenarios compatible with limiting global warming to 
1.5 °C by the end of this century and achieving net zero 
emissions by 2050.

Reduction of oil and gas emissions in 19 C1 Net 
Zero scenarios by 20501

below that threshold towards the end of the century, and a relevant dependence on Carbon Removal Technologies (CDR). Therefore, it is a 1.5°C 
overshoot scenario, and not an IPCC category C1 scenario, characterized by limited overshoot and less reliance on negative emissions.

2030 in the WEO 2025.

Qualitative analysis of locked-in emissions

Calculations of potential locked-in emissions associated 
with the main assets and products marketed by Repsol 
are based on an estimate of future GHG emissions over 
their useful life. Oil and gas products could be subject to 
drastic demand reductions over the long term that 
would affect the value of the Company assets that 
produce them and could eventually require halting its 
activity, leaving the assets and their emissions 
“locked in”.

In its climate transition risks and resilience analysis, 
Repsol classifies the risk associated with potential 
locked-in emissions as significant and manages its 
portfolio of assets to strengthen its resilience to this 
risk. The resilience of the assets for the production, 
transformation and sale of oil and gas products has 
been assessed, including both the emissions generated 
directly by those that are operated (Scope 1 and 2) and 
those derived from the use of the products sold 
(Scope 3). The risk of locked-in emissions is taken into 
account when setting decarbonization targets:

• With respect to assets, Scope 1 and 2 emissions from 

the facilities operated are part of the reduction 
trajectory detailed in Targets related to climate 
change mitigation and adaptation (E1-4). 

• Regarding products, of the 20% reduction target for 
Scope 1, 2 and 3 emissions compared with the 2018 
baseline year, 17.4% of the reduction corresponds to 
emissions associated with the use of the products 
sold. 

The analysis of the Company’s future emissions is 
classified by business: Upstream or Downstream:

Upstream

The Company’s strategy is focused on optimizing its 
portfolio of exploration and production assets, giving 
priority to those with higher profitability and lower 
carbon intensity, with a greater geographical 
concentration to gain scale. Priority is given to projects 
for the development of discovered reserves and 
resources that are profitable at moderate oil and gas 
prices and exploration adjacent to existing assets and 
infrastructure, excluding new exploratory frontiers, tar 
sands and the Arctic offshore.

As a result, Scope 1 and 2 emissions have already been 
reduced, reaching an intensity that is now within the 
first quartile of the sector. This intensity will continue to 
decline in the coming years, with projections of reaching 
10 kgCO2e/boe for 2027.15

For the Upstream business, Scope 3 emissions derive 
from the use of hydrocarbon production. With the 

strategy described, Repsol’s production will remain at 
levels of 550-600 kboed throughout this decade, after 
having fallen from a peak of 709 kboed in 2019. From 
2035, a gradual reduction is expected reaching around 
100 kboed in 2050 due to the natural decline of assets 
without undertaking new long-term developments in 
scenarios compatible with the warming limited to 1.5 ºC.    

Downstream 

Most of Repsol’s downstream assets are located in Spain 
and Portugal, where the European regulatory framework 
on climate matters applies, in line with the European 
Green Deal and  its implementation through the Fit for 
55 legislative package with emission reduction targets 
for 2030, aimed at achieving climate neutrality by 2050. 
Within this framework, Repsol manages the locked-in 
emissions of the products sold (Scope 3, category 1116) 
by progressively reducing refining activity from 2030 
and the gradual replacement of conventional products 
with low-carbon products such as renewable liquid and 
gaseous fuels and synthetic fuels. 

The EU regulation also promotes the reduction of 
Scope 1 and 2 emissions through greater energy 
efficiency, the electrification of processes with 
renewable energies and, in the longer term, through the 
capture, use and storage of CO2, or the trend to reduce 
the processing of crude oil in refineries.

Material impacts, risks and opportunities and their 
interaction with strategy and the business model (ESRS 2 
SBM-3)

The resilience analysis carried out in 2025 covers all the 
Company’s own operations, in all segments of the value 
chain where it operates, and in its four business lines 
(Upstream, Industrial, Customer, and Low-Carbon 
Generation).

The Company’s ability to adapt to the progressive 
decarbonization of the energy system in different 
transition scenarios is assessed, promoting operational 
continuity, economic competitiveness and sustainable 
development in all business areas.

This study has as its starting point the analysis of 
transition and physical risks17 from the double 
materiality assessment. However, given that this analysis 
concludes that exposure to physical risks derived from 
climate change is not material in any of the scenarios 
and time horizons analyzed, (as they do not generate 
significant impacts on the operational continuity or 
economic competitiveness of the Company’s key assets), 
these physical risks have not been included in the scope 
of the resilience analysis. 

15 Source: Strategic Update 2024-2027.

16 Scope 3 category 11 emissions account for 84% of the Company’s current total emissions. 

17 Resilience analysis and the identification of transition risks are complementary but distinct exercises. Although they are based on the same 

scenarios and assumptions, resilience analysis is a scenario analysis, so it does not assess each risk individually, but rather analyzes in an integrated 
manner the capacity for adaptation and value creation of the Company’s businesses under different rhythms of the energy transition, based on 
aggregate variables, such as demand, prices or margins, with an effect on the projection of economic-financial metrics.

Details of the analysis of identified material risks can be 
found in Description of the processes to identify and 
assess climate-related material impacts, risks, and 
opportunities (ESRS 2 IRO-1). 

The analysis covers the period between the present and 
2050, in correspondence with the temporal scope of the 
climate risk analysis and the Company’s decarbonization 
objectives.

Three reference climate scenarios from the expert 
consultancy S&P Energy updated in 2025 have been 
selected, which are applied differently in each business 
as explained in the following paragraphs. These 
scenarios, recognized in the sector, unlike other 
reference scenarios, contain production, demand and 
price projections of different energy sources and 
products with sufficient granularity at the level of 
geography, seasonality and raw material/product to 
allow for developing a more complete and consistent 
analysis.

Across all businesses, until 2030, an internal demand 
and price scenario is applied that coincides with the 
most recently used for the adjustment of metrics in 
Repsol'’ 2026-2028 projection update, which is 
expected to be published in March 2026. 

From 2030, the following is expected:

• For the Upstream and Low-Carbon Generation 

businesses, three transition scenarios are developed. 
Starting in 2040, Repsol’s metrics are adjusted to the 
environmental conditions of the aforementioned S&P 
Energy scenarios. In the 2030–2040 period, Repsol’s 
2030 scenario metrics are gradually converged 
toward the 2040 metrics of the S&P Energy scenarios.

• For the Industrial and Customer businesses, with 
activity mainly concentrated in the EU, a single 
scenario is constructed on the basis of an energy 
demand environment arising from the EU Green Deal, 
as noted, up to the year 2050.

Based on these scenarios, the gross impact (before 
mitigation) of transition climate risks in 2030, 2040 and 
2050 is first estimated. Next, the net impact is evaluated 
after introducing the performance of the Company’s 
asset portfolio to adapt to the environmental 
conditions, with investments and capital employed 
differentiated by scenario.

The results of the analysis show the performance of the 
different businesses, as detailed below, as well as the 
Company’s resilience to risks arising from changes in the 
pace of the energy transition:

•

In an environment for Industrial and Customer 
businesses determined by the European Green Deal 
and the Fit for 55 legislative package, investments are 
envisaged in renewable fuel projects that preserve 
their economic value, where they are currently 
positioned in the first quartile within the EU and with 
the largest market share in Spain.

• The Upstream business maintains its value‑creation 

capacity well into the next decade, and subsequently 
sees it decline in line with the reduction in production 
and the decrease in prices assumed due to lower 
global demand, with varying intensity under each 
scenario.

• Meanwhile, the Low-Carbon Generation and Electricity 

Distribution businesses increase their capacity to 
create value over time due to the growth and 
investment strategy proposed and the trend towards 
electrification in the scenarios posed.

The resilience analysis carried out, which projects the 
performance of businesses based on the assumptions of 
the scenarios used, shows that the proposed strategy is 
resilient in the event of changes in the pace of the 
energy transition. The main areas of uncertainty are 
those related to baseline scenario assumptions, such as 
population growth, economic growth, or technological

2.1.3 Impact, risk and opportunity 
management
Description of the processes to identify and assess 
material climate-related material impacts, risks, and 
opportunities (ESRS 2 IRO-1)

Repsol has carried out a double materiality assessment,  
incorporating the opinion of the Company’s expert 
areas, as well as its stakeholders, to assess the impacts, 
risks, and opportunities (IROs) related to the energy 
transition, in the short, medium and long term. 

The following material impacts have been identified in 
relation to climate change: 

• Repsol’s sale of the fossil fuels demanded by the 

market generates indirect emissions of CO2  (Scope 3)  
(IRO-9).

•

Impact on global warming (IRO-4) of Repsol’s 
activities in the areas of Upstream and Industrial, as 
well as those related to mobility, which generate 
Scope 1 and 2 (direct company operations) and Scope 
3 (mobility and supply chain) emissions.

• Repsol’s operating model involves an intensive use of 

energy from non-renewable sources, which 
contributes significantly to its direct greenhouse 
gases emissions (IRO-1)  and can generate exposure 
to variations in energy costs and an increasingly 
demanding regulatory environment.

• Repsol’s leadership position in the market can serve as 
an example for other companies and institutions when 
adopting their energy transition measures (IRO-5), 
promoting a shift toward more sustainable operations 
and contributing to the reduction of greenhouse gas 
emissions and to collective progress toward 
decarbonization.

• Commitment and awareness of the workforce in terms 

of decarbonization due to the link of their variable 
remuneration to this target (IRO-6).

• Repsol’s renewable energy services, from wind, solar 

and hydro sources, contribute positively to the energy 
market (IRO-2) by increasing the supply and 
consumption of renewable energy, thus favoring the 
reduction of greenhouse gas emissions and the move 
towards a low-carbon energy model.

In relation to financial materiality, the Group’s general 
risk map, focused on a five-year period, is 
complemented by a specific analysis in the medium 
(2030–2040) and long-term (2040–2050), for these 
risks, including an estimate of their economic impact, in 
order to anticipate potential negative impacts and take 
advantage of opportunities.   

Repsol assesses the physical risks of climate change and 
the risks of the energy transition separately through the 
methodologies described below.

Physical risk analysis of climate change

In 2022, Repsol developed its own, semi-quantitative 
methodology for the detailed analysis of the physical 
risks of climate change, both in existing facilities and in 
investment projects subject to the Taxonomy 
Regulation. These analyses are complemented, from 
2024, by quantitative analyses of a broader scope, 
including both operated and non-operated facilities, 
carried out by an expert consultancy. 

First, an identification is made of the climatic 
phenomena to which Repsol’s assets are potentially 
exposed because they are located in certain 
geographical locations. The starting point is the list of 
phenomena offered by the current regulations, 
discarding those that, in the opinion of the experts, are 
less relevant to Repsol’s assets.

The following natural phenomena are included:

1. Surface water flooding

2. Riverine flooding

3.  Coastal inundation

4.  Soil movement

5.  Extreme wind (storms, tornadoes)

6.  Cyclone

7.  Wildfire

8. Drought

9. Heat wave

10. Freeze-Thaw

11. Changing wind patterns

12. Permafrost thawing

13. Cold waves

14. Hail

The climate projections of certain physical variables 
related mainly to temperature, precipitation and wind 
speed that are associated with these phenomena have 
been studied. The climate projections that have been 
used are, among others, those from Copernicus services 
(the EU’s Earth observation program, coordinated and 
managed by the European Commission) as well as 
external consultancy services that provide additional 
information on the behavior of these physical variables 
and their combinations. In addition, account has been 
taken of the global warming scenarios described by the 
Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC), with 
a wide range of emissions (SSP5-8.5, SSP2-4.5 and 
SSP1-2.6 scenarios) selected in the 2030, 2040 and 
2050 time horizons.

Based on this information, simulations have been carried 
out to determine the potential increase in the impact on  
the Company’s facilities resulting of the increase in the 
frequency or intensity of these phenomena, with an 
estimate made of the economic value at risk for each of 
them in the different assets. This study, carried out in 
the absence of mitigation or adaptation measures, is 
first carried out on a representative sample of the assets 
in the current portfolio of all the Company’s businesses, 
which is then extrapolated to other assets that are 
compatible in location and typology. In addition, more 
detailed analyses of assets subject to the European 
taxonomy regulation have been carried out.

These studies were updated in 2025, including changes 
in the asset portfolio resulting from the Company’s 
investments and divestments during the last year. The 
conclusion of these studies is that the increase in risk 
from acute or chronic climate change events is not 
material18 for the Repsol Group in any of the analyzed 
scenarios or time horizons. 

Risk analysis of the energy transition

Transition risks and opportunities are important, 
especially in the medium and long term, which is why 
Repsol reviews them on an annual basis. The objective of 
this process is to identify, evaluate and make sure that 
these risks are known and taken into account for 
decision-making through resilience analysis and the 
Company’s strategy. 

In the short term, these risks are part of the Company’s 
risk map, with a time range of five years, and are studied 
using quantitative techniques (determination of 
frequencies, probabilities and economic losses for each 
activity) and qualitative techniques (for the 
determination of reputational and people impacts), 
using the expert knowledge of the different areas of the 
Company.

In the medium and long term, a specific 
analysis of the transition risks is carried out for each of 
the four major business areas. To this end, a taxonomy 
has been defined following the guidelines of the CSRD 
Directive, which includes regulatory, legal, 
technological, market and reputational risks, up to a 
total of 13 risks classified according to their nature, that 
could impact the Company across the different time 
horizons considered. These risks are described in the 
table below19:

Regulatory 
and legal

Regulatory changes affecting the Company’s 
results

Increase in litigation arising from the effects 
of climate change

Inefficient, delayed or premature adoption of 
new practices, processes or emerging 
technologies

Technological

Shortage or unavailability of raw materials, 
natural resources, goods or services

Limitations in technology deployment due to 
lack of infrastructure

Misalignment between portfolio management 
strategy and the pace of the energy transition

Market

Changes linked to end‑consumer or 
intermediary preferences

Potential difficulty or constraints in securing 
funding

High competition in markets associated with 
the energy transition

Stigmatization of the energy sector

Reputation

Failure to meet the company’s commitments 
or errors in reporting

Challenges related to talent management

Conflicts of interest with stakeholders

First, a group of business experts individually analyzes 
the causes and consequences related to each of the 
risks. The most significant risks are identified and a level 
of probability and impact is assessed for each of them, 
through qualitative scales, as well as their duration and 
evolution over time. 

Subsequently, these assessments are verified with other 
experts of the Company in the fields of strategy, 
markets, regulation, finance, reputation, legal affairs, 
technology and sustainability, in order to obtain a 
common vision on the potential consequences of the 
materialization of risks for the Company in the horizons 
2030–2040 and 2040–2050. 

These analyses are carried out taking as a reference the 
projections of the latest update of the Strategic Plan 
announced by the Company, as well as the 
decarbonization route, and as the environmental 
scenario the NZE scenario of S&P Energy20, aligned with 
limiting global warming to 1.5 ºC with limited overshoot, 
which enables the determination of the residual risk to 
which the Company would be exposed in these horizons 
and scenarios. These risks are taken into account when 
conducting the climate change resilience analysis, taking 
account of variables such as trends in the demand for 

18 To determine the materiality of these risks, the same financial materiality criterion has been used as a reference as in the double materiality

Time horizon 

Regulatory changes (IRO-11) that affect operations, either derived from the obligation to adopt measures to 
mitigate climate change (e.g., limitation of the use of hydrocarbons, limitation of emissions or discharges, 
limitation of the use of natural resources) consistent with the decarbonization commitments undertaken by 
each country, of a fiscal nature (for example, emissions trading systems or increased tax burdens) and/or 
regulatory-related associated with the development of new businesses

long term

Technological

Inefficient or late adoption of new practices, processes, or developing technologies (IRO-8). It refers to 
investments in technologies for energy production, distribution and storage that may become obsolete or 
prematurely adopted before demand develops

Scarcity or unavailability of raw materials, natural resources, goods or services (IRO-10) required for the 
development of key technologies for renewable electrification such as batteries, photovoltaic panels, etc.

production of renewable fuels and synthetic fuels (water, lipid raw materials, waste). It also includes the 
difficulty of access or increase in the price of other elements, goods and services required to carry out the 
energy transition

Short term

Market

the actual pace of the energy transition if differing from expectations

Medium term

High competition in markets associated with the energy transition leading to a decrease in sales  (IRO-7)

Short term

Material transition opportunities

Availability of aid for sustainable financing (IRO-13) of low-carbon energy generation projects (for example, 
European Next Generation funds) and access to ESG investors

Policies related to climate change mitigation and 
adaptation (E1-2)

The management of the impacts, risks, and 
opportunities arising from climate change is included in 
the Sustainability Policy, which establishes as priority 
objectives the promotion of a fair energy transition that 
takes into account its impact on workers, local 
communities and society in general while reducing and 
avoiding greenhouse gas emissions, and the transition 
to low-carbon business models that reduce or mitigate 
the adverse effects of climate change and integrate 
sustainability into all businesses. 

21 Risks analyzed under a residual approach.

In this policy, Repsol also takes on the challenge of 
contributing to universal access to safe, affordable and 
sustainable energy. Accordingly, it emphasizes that all 
reduction technologies will be necessary to achieve this 
goal, so that the energy transition is carried out in an 
orderly manner and at the lowest cost to society.

In relation to energy efficiency and the deployment of 
renewable energies, Repsol applies its Environmental 
Policy to all its businesses and organizational levels,

which underscores the need to take account of 
stakeholder expectations and to “provide energy and 
products to society to meet its needs efficiently, 
guaranteeing adequate protection of the environment 
and the sustainable use of resources without 
compromising the well-being of future generations”.

In this policy, the Company commits to identifying, 
evaluating, and managing, in accordance with the 
mitigation22 hierarchy, the impacts, dependencies, risks, 
and opportunities on the environment, in the activities 
and places where Repsol operates, throughout the value 
chain, as well as in the products and services offered to 
society.

Repsol is also committed to assessing the risks 
associated with climate change and implementing 
climate change mitigation and adaptation measures in 
its operations when such risks are material.

The Environmental Policy also includes Repsol’s 
commitment to integrate the principles of the circular 
economy into its processes, products, and services, 
optimizing the use of resources and raw materials, 
including energy; encouraging the use of alternative and 
renewable resources obtained in a sustainable way; and 
promoting the development of new products with less 
environmental impact. This applies to all the activities 
carried out by the Company, all its products, all its 
businesses and all the locations where it operates.

In addition, Repsol undertakes to promote 
environmental commitments in accordance with those 
defined internally throughout the value chain, 
customers, partners, suppliers of products and services, 
and other stakeholders.

The commitments included in this document, together 
with the Sustainability Policy, contribute to achieving 
the Company’s goal of net zero emissions by 2050, in 
line with the Paris Agreement, as well as the other 
decarbonization objectives specified in the transition 
plan. 

The Sustainability Department ensures the 
implementation of the Sustainability and Environment 
policies. 

Actions and resources in relation to climate change 
policies (E1-3)

Below are the actions that Repsol has implemented in 
2025 to mitigate climate change and its impact in 2025, 
along with forecasts for 2030, as well as the resources 
allocated for their execution. The information covers 
different lines of action depending on the scope of the 
emissions (1, 2 and 3) and the nature of the measures, 
considering both current investments and future 
projections (see Investments).

Scope 1 and 2 emissions 

1. Energy efficiency23

In the industrial field, which includes the complexes 
located in Spain, Portugal and Peru within the value chain 
downstream, measures are implemented such as the 
cleaning of heat exchangers, the optimization of energy 
integration, the use of heat from high-temperature 
streams, the improvement of furnace efficiency and the 
optimization of steam networks. Further, at Upstream, 
which covers assets in various geographies within the 
upstream value chain, notable initiatives include reducing 
methane emissions and minimizing routine flaring of gas.

Some of the actions with significant reductions in 2025 
are the following:

Industrial

Upstream

• Optimization of heat recovery from the furnaces of 
the Platforming Unit at the A Coruña refinery. The 
new APEX preheater captures waste heat to raise the 
temperature of the air entering the combustion 
process, resulting in fuel savings and a reduction of 
3 ktCO2e/year.

• Reduction of intermediate diesel reprocessing at the 
Puertollano refinery. The improvements allow for a 
cleaner product and thus reduce reprocessing costs in 
intermediate units and the consequent reduction of 
6 ktCO2e/year.

• Operational and energy efficiency improvements at 

Yme, including the optimization of the use of electric 
power, the reduction of diesel consumption and the 
recovery of the gas vent from the glycol dehydrator 
together with measures to stabilize production and 
decrease energy consumption in the facilities, 
resulting in a reduction of 24 ktCO2e/year.

• Dynamic optimization of the compressor 

configuration in Block 57 (Peru), adjusting its 
operational strategy daily to maximize the flow of 
compressed gas with minimum energy consumption, 
with a reduction of 12 ktCO2e/year.

Energy efficiency actions at the Company level achieved

2. Electrification of processes24

The replacement of steam turbines with electric motors 
to drive pumps and compressors is enabling greater 
energy efficiency in refining and chemical plants in Spain 
and Portugal. 

This substitution is particularly economically viable in 
scenarios of high CO₂ prices and availability of 
renewable electricity at a competitive cost.

On the other hand, the reduction in steam consumption 
can penalize the profitability of cogeneration plants, 
leading, in some cases, to the shutdown of these units.

Key action in 2025:

• Replacement of the condensing steam turbine in the 
Mildhydrocracker Unit of the Petronor refinery with a 
standard induction motor. The new motor has allowed 
to optimize the operation of the unit's compressor. 
This change represents a reduction of 13 ktCO2e/year.

Electrification has achieved a reduction of 74 ktCO2e 
in 2025.

3. Use of renewables25

Renewable electricity is a lever for decarbonization of 
Scope 1 emissions, if it replaces electricity generation in 
cogeneration; and Scope 2, if it replaces the purchase of 
electricity on the market that continues to have a fossil 
component. This substitution can be done through on-
site generation in self-consumption facilities (solar 
photovoltaic and wind), or through renewable energy 
purchase agreements (PPAs), which guarantee supply 
and offer price stability.

By 2030, biomethane projects (Waste to Energy) are 
included in this lever, considering both the use of 
biomethane for renewable H2 production and for steam 
production to replace natural gas.

Key action in 2025:

• Purchase of electricity produced from renewable 

energy in industrial sites in Spain by means of PPAs. 
This results in a reduction of 48 ktCO2e/year.

4. Renewable hydrogen26

Renewable hydrogen is a lever for the decarbonization 
of industrial assets, by progressively replacing the 
current production of hydrogen from natural gas, which 
generates CO2 emissions, with hydrogen produced in 
electrolyzers with water and renewable electricity or 
from biomethane. These initiatives contribute to the 
emission reduction both in industrial processes and the 
use of products.

The first electrolyzer projects linked to industrial 
complexes in Spain and Portugal have already begun. In 
October 2023, the Company put into operation a 
2.5 MW electrolyzer at the Petronor industrial complex 
(Bilbao), intended for the production of renewable 
hydrogen for industrial use by third parties, and 
therefore this hydrogen is neither used nor consumed at 
Repsol’s facilities.

The 10 MW electrolyzer in Bilbao, associated with the 
synthetic fuels demonstration plant, is currently in the 
execution phase, as well as the 4 MW electrolyzer in 
Sines (Portugal), which is expected to come into 
operation in 2026.

In addition, Repsol has several larger-scale projects in 
different stages of development and execution. These 
include the 100 MW electrolyzers in Cartagena and 
Bilbao, which are expected to reach the final investment 
decision (FID) in 2025, and are recognized as an 
Important Project of Common European Interest (IPCEI) 
and is scheduled to come into operation from 2029.

5. Carbon sinks27

CO₂ capture and storage is a technological lever for the 
decarbonization of industrial and Exploration and 
Production assets, applicable when it is technically and 
economically feasible. As part of Repsol’s medium- and 
long-term strategy, which already includes two projects 
under evaluation in Spain and the United States, and 
aims to develop capabilities that would enable CO₂ 
storage, always subject to regulatory maturity and 
demand for storage services.

Scope 3 emissions

Repsol’s Scope 3 emissions are mostly associated with 
the use of the energy products it markets (petroleum-
based fuels and natural gas), so their reduction requires 
action on the volume and mix of sales, in line with 
energy transition scenarios and European regulation. 
Within the framework of the Scope 1, 2 and 3 emissions 
reduction target, the Company combines portfolio 
management decisions, evolution of the commercial 
offer and growth in lower carbon intensity solutions to 
progressively reduce emissions derived from the use of 
its products. 

1. Sales of petroleum products28

Repsol’s oil products are marketed mainly in the Iberian 
Peninsula, operating within Repsol’s marketing of 
petroleum products is subject to the European Union’s 
policy and regulatory framework. This regulatory 
environment sets the trend with respect to the 
evolution of the transport sector and the associated 
demand for fuels. Nonetheless, this demand shows 
notable resilience in the Iberian Peninsula in the short 
term, with stable projections throughout this decade 
and point to a progressive decrease in the demand for 
conventional fossil fuels in the medium and long term, 
with the consequent increase in renewable fuels for all 
mobility and electric mobility, mainly in light road 
transport.

Renewable energy production 

As stated in Strategy, the energy transition involves 
supporting the supply of energy with the lowest 
possible level of emissions. To this end, Repsol is making 
progress in the transformation of its energy portfolio 
through investments in the production of electricity and 
renewable fuels, which is reflected in the reduction of 
the CII.

• Repsol is investing in the transformation of its 

industrial sites and its commercial network to increase 
the production and marketing of renewable fuels, 
driven by the European regulatory framework. In 
addition to the Cartagena plant, in operation since 
2024 with a capacity of 250,000 tons per year, there is 
the construction of a second 100% renewable fuel 
plant in Puertollano, with a capacity of 200,000 tons 
per year and expected to commence operations in 
2026; and the final investment decision for the 
Ecoplant in Tarragona was made in 2025. This project, 
which is to be launched in 2029 with an investment of 
more than €800 million, will allow the treatment of 
urban solid waste for the production of 240,000 
tonnes of renewable and circular methanol per year. In 
2025, renewable fuel production capacity reached 
1.25 Mt/y.

• Repsol began to generate renewable electricity in 

2018, an activity that is necessary to meet its carbon 
intensity reduction targets and to take advantage of 
growth opportunities. In 2025, it reached a total 
installed capacity of 5.5 GW in Spain, the United 
States and Chile. An installed capacity of more than 
10 GW is expected to be reached by 2030.  

2.1.4 Metrics and targets 

Targets related to climate change mitigation and 
adaptation (E1-4) 

In 2024, Repsol set a new absolute emissions reduction 
target for Scopes 1, 2 and 3 as part of its strategy to 
address climate risks and opportunities, thus further 
attesting to its alignment with the global targets of 
limiting warming to 1.5 °C and net-zero greenhouse gas  
emissions by 2050. This target is a response, to a large 
extent, to the demands of certain interest groups and 
the CSRD Directive. Its approach is complementary to 
the CO2e emissions reduction target per unit of energy 
generated (carbon intensity) set in 2019, and to other 
specific targets for reducing Scope 1 and 2 emissions, 
methane and routine flare emissions. 

The following describes the metrics that the Company 
uses to assess the progress of impacts, risks, and 
opportunities (IROs) related to the energy transition and 
decarbonization (see Materiality assessment).

All decarbonization goals are consistent with the 
Company’s business and operational projections, which 
are shared with stakeholders in their strategic updates. 
In addition, they  contribute directly to the objectives of 
the Sustainability Policy and the Environment Policy, 
directing actions towards optimizing the use of 
resources, improving energy efficiency and achieving 
climate neutrality.

1. Absolute Scope 1, 2 and 3 emissions (MtCO2e)4,30

Repsol uses this metric31 of absolute greenhouse gas 
emissions of Scopes 1, 2 and 3, to show its progress in 
decarbonization and as the form most accepted by 
certain stakeholders, in the absence of a recognized 
standard, to demonstrate its alignment with the global 
objectives of limiting warming to 1.5 °C and achieving 
net-zero GHG emissions by 2050, as detailed in 
Transition plan for climate change mitigation (E1-1). 

By 2030, Repsol has set a goal to reduce absolute 
Scope 1, 2 and 3 GHG emissions by 20%, compared with 
the 201832 baseline, with an estimated contribution of 
5% in Scopes 1 and 2 and 15% in Scope 3.

on current projections, the Company estimates a 
reduction of more than 90% in absolute emissions with 
the mitigation levers and technologies currently 
envisaged. The remaining percentage, around 10%, is 
expected to be addressed through greenhouse gas 
removal and abatement solutions, including carbon 
capture and storage technologies and natural sinks.

prior to the pandemic.

Scope 1, 2 and 3 emission reduction levers to reach the 2030 target

2. Carbon Intensity Indicator (gCO2e/MJ)24

In 2019, Repsol defined the CII in gCO2e/MJ. This metric is 
calculated by adding Scope 1 and 2 (operational) and 
Scope 3 greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions (based on 
products obtained from Repsol’s oil and gas production) 
in the numerator, and the energy generated by the 
Company in the denominator (see Methodology for 
climate change reporting and metrics).

In the CII numerator, approximately 37% of the 
Company’s Scope 3 emissions and 41% of total 
emissions are covered, as the Company’s oil product 
sales are higher than its oil production33.

The denominator of the CII represents the energy that 
Repsol makes available to society as final products 
derived from the production of primary energy from oil 
and gas and low-carbon energy sources.

By 2050, the goal is to reduce the carbon intensity of the 
primary energy we produce to net zero, with interim 
targets in 2025, 2030 and 2040. 

The CII contributes directly to Sustainability and 
Environmental Policies by offering an integrated and 
quantifiable metric that guides the Company’s progress 
towards net-zero GHG emissions, along with the Scope 1, 
2 and 3 absolute emissions metric. This indicator 
reinforces the commitment to reduce GHG emissions 
(Scopes 1, 2 and 3), aligning with the principles of 
continuous improvement set out in the Environmental 
Policy. In addition, the CII facilitates the strategic 

management of our energy transition by providing a tool 
to assess the impact of decarbonization actions that are 
not identifiable through the reduction of absolute 
emissions (renewable power generation, renewable fuels), 
as noted, which supports the Sustainability Policy by 
prioritizing the integration of the energy transition and 
climate resilience into the Company’s operations.  

In October 2021, the CII reduction targets were set at 15% 
for 2025, 28% for 2030, 55% for 2040 and 100% for 
205034, compared to the base year of 2016. These 
objectives were ratified in the February 2024 strategic 
update.

Over the last few months, and after meeting all of its 
decarbonization targets for 2025, Repsol has reviewed 
certain metrics and objectives from the latest strategic 
update (which are included in this report) in order to 
adapt them to the better understanding of the global 
energy context, regulation and demand trends, and the 
pace of the energy transition. And it does so without 
departing from the path of decarbonization toward net- 
zero GHG emissions by 2050, and undertaking 
investments that are profitable in the current 
environment. 

By 2030, as a result, the reduction target for the CII has 
been set at 25% ±1. 

In 2025, the CII stood at 65.2 gCO₂e/MJ, representing a 
15.1% reduction compared to 2016 (13.4% in 2024), 
thus meeting the 15% reduction target.

33 Refining Capacity = Product Sales = ~1 M Boed; Net liquid production ~ 0.25 M boed.
34 Net zero will be achieved by reducing numerator emissions by at least 90% (emissions covering 42% of the Company’s current total emissions) 

and neutralizing residual emissions.

 3. Absolute Scope 1 and 2 emissions (MtCO2e)24

These are the direct emissions from facilities in which 
Repsol is the operator (Scope 1) and the indirect 
emissions related to the consumption of electricity, heat 
or steam acquired for Repsol’s operations (Scope 2), 
where the Company has the greatest capacity to 
undertake decarbonization actions. This metric is 
detailed in Methodology for climate change reporting 
and metrics.

84% of Repsol’s Scope 1 and 2 emissions fall under the 
scope of the EU ETS, which requires external 
verification, carried out after the publication of this 
report. Additionally, 94% of Scope 1 and 2 emissions are 
certified under the ISO 14064 standard.

In 2021, the Company pledged to reduce these 
emissions by 55% by 2030 compared with 2016. 

The base year, 2016, was selected in 2019 (the year the 
Company’s goal of net zero emissions 2050 was 
announced) as the baseline for this target due to its 
historical significance: it represents the first full year 

Scope 1 and 2 emission reduction levers

following the acquisition of Talisman Energy, which 
significantly increased CO2 and methane emissions from 
the Company’s Upstream business and Scope 3 emissions. 
In 2016, Repsol’s total Scope 1 and 2 emissions amounted 
to 25.4 MtCO₂e.

In 2023, Repsol reaffirmed its goal of reaching net zero 
Scope 1 and 2 emissions in its operated assets by 2050, 
adding to the commitment of the Oil and Gas 
Decarbonization Charter (OGDC), announced during 
COP28. 

By 2030, it is estimated that most of the reduction in 
these emissions will be Scope 1, although a reduction in 
Scope 2 emissions of 200 ktCO2e compared to 2025. 

These reductions also contribute to the objective of 
reducing absolute emissions of Scopes 1, 2 and 3, with 
Scopes 1 and 2 representing 8% of the total.  

The reduction in absolute Scope 1 and 2 emissions in 
2025 was 45.3% compared with 2016.

4. Methane intensity (% of natural gas sales)24

5. Routine flare emissions (tCO2e)24

This metric35 is based on the methodology applied by 
OGCI (Oil and & Gas Climate Initiative), in accordance 
with the 2023 reporting framework36. This indicator is 
applied to upstream oil and gas assets operated by 
Repsol. 

Repsol set the target of reducing methane intensity to 
0.20% by 2025 in its operated Exploration and 
Production assets, with 2017 as the baseline. This 
objective has become an international standard 
recognized by industry and other significant 
stakeholders (UNEP, EDF, CATF, European Commission). 

In 2025, the methane intensity was 0.12%, reaching the 
target set. Work will continue in order to improve the 
identification, quantification and reduction of methane 
losses by 2030.

Gas shipments to the flares (flaring) generate CO₂, CH₄, 
N₂O and water vapor in their combustion (see 
Methodology for climate change reporting and 
metrics). The emissions reduction metric is applied to 
the Upstream oil and gas assets operated by Repsol. 

In 2025, routine flaring emissions accounted for less 
than 0.3% of the Company’s Scope 1 and 2 emissions 
inventory. However, its significance, like that of 
methane, is particularly significant because it helps 
minimize product losses and improve operational 
excellence and safety.

Since 2016, Repsol has been a member of the World 
Bank’s Zero Routine Flaring by 2030 initiative37, through 
which it is committed to seeking technically and 
economically viable solutions to minimize routine flaring 
of gas as soon as possible and no later than 2030. 

35 The methodology for this metric is detailed in Methodology for climate change reporting and metrics.
36 https://www.ogci.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/11/OGCI_Reporting_Framework_2023_-_FINAL_FINAL.pdf.
37 https://www.worldbank.org/en/programs/zero-routine-flaring-by-2030.

(2) ESRS Perimeter: This perimeter refers to the emissions reported in accordance with the requirements of ESRS E1, which establish the criteria for defining the greenhouse 

gas emissions inventory. According to these criteria, the inventory includes 100% of the emissions corresponding to assets operated by the Company, as well as the 

proportional share of emissions associated with non‑operated assets, based on Repsol’s participation in the corresponding joint operating agreements (JOA).

This perimeter has been adjusted compared to what was published in 2024 in order to align the presentation of information with the requirements of ESRS E1 and to 

improve the comparability and understanding of the data. For comparative purposes, figures for fiscal year 2024 recalculated according to this perimeter are included.

(3) Operational perimeter: Reported emissions of assets operated on a 100% basis, following the guidance of the GHG Protocol.

included.

Emissions reported as operated are calculated under 
the operational criteria established by the GHG 
Protocol, the most widely used standard globally by 
companies in all sectors, providing a consistent and 
transparent framework for GHG accounting and 
reporting. This approach includes all businesses, units, 
assets, and operations in which Repsol exercises direct 
or indirect control over management or operational 
responsibility, accounting for 100% of the emissions 
generated in these operations. 

In addition, the same breakdown is reported for the 
perimeter defined in the ESRS standards, which include 
emissions according to operational criteria by adding  
the emissions of JOAs not operated on an equity basis.

In 2025, the changes shown in emissions have been 
mainly due to divestments and the implementation of 
operational improvement measures.

Repsol accounts for the emissions of these assets up to 
the effective date of their sale. In addition, and in order 
to be able to compare with the 2024 data, the most 
significant changes during the year are specified:

• Divestment in the UK: In July 2025, the strategic union 

between Repsol Resources UK and NEO Energy is 
completed. The joint venture has been renamed 
NEO NEXT. This change in the portfolio has an impact 
of -0.5 MtCO2e on the operated emissions. 

• The combined cycle plants of the Low-Carbon 

Generation business have increased activity notably  
following the blackout that occurred in Spain in April. 
This has resulted in an increase of +0.8 MtCO2e.

In addition, energy efficiency measures, methane 
emission reductions and reduction of gas flaring have 
been implemented in Upstream assets, supplemented 
by optimization initiatives in the Industrial business.

GHG removals and GHG mitigation projects financed 
through carbon credits (E1-7)

Currently, the Company does not have any tons of CO2 
stored that are derived from CO2 removal projects from 
the atmosphere implemented in its own operations or in 
which it has contributed in upstream and downstream 
stages of its value chain.  

As part of its commitment to the goal of net zero 
emissions of Scopes 1, 2 and 3, Repsol envisages that up 
to 8% of these emissions (around 20 MtCO2e) cannot be 
eliminated by 2050 through technologies applied to its 
energy39 production, in which case it would offset with 
its own natural climate solutions projects (NCS) or 
through credits. These credits would be aligned with the 
most demanding integrity frameworks, such as Article 6 
of the Paris Agreement and/or the European Union’s 
CRCF, guaranteeing transparency, additionality, 
permanence and methodological rigor. Repsol 
undertakes to report in a clear and transparent manner 
any possible use of these credits.

In any case, Repsol’s decarbonization strategy prioritizes 
avoiding and reducing its own emissions and those in its 
value chain through technological development and 
operational efficiency. At least until after 2030, Repsol 
will not use emission offset mechanisms. 

Notwithstanding the foregoing, Repsol has 
implemented commercial initiatives that allow its 
customers to voluntarily contribute to the mitigation of 
their carbon emissions. Although these credits finance 
projects that capture or avoid CO2 emissions, such as 
forestry projects, their use is not linked to Repsol’s40 
global decarbonization strategy, but is offered as an 
additional and voluntary option for customers who wish 
to contribute to the mitigation of emissions that come 
from the use of our products. 

In 2025, the Company has cancelled 20,340 tCO2 in the 
form of international carbon credits to cover the demand 
for these initiatives, entirely of the AFOLU, REDD+ type 
and verified by Verra’s41 VCS42 (Verified Carbon 
Standard), with additional CCB+ Gold certification43.

It should be noted that these credits are not used to 
offset GHG emissions under the E1-6 requirement, nor as 
a means to achieve the Company’s emissions reduction 
goals outlined in the E1-4.

Internal carbon pricing (E1-8)

Repsol has internal mechanisms to promote the 
allocation of capital to low-carbon investments, such as 
the internal carbon price and the investment 
qualification methodology for their alignment with the 
energy transition. 

The Company has established an internal carbon price 
for investment decisions for new projects. This 
mechanism is included in the Company’s economic bases 
for investment evaluation and is applied in the 
investment decisions of all projects.

The internal carbon price applies to the analysis of new 
investments on the potential impact of the Company’s 
Scope 1 and 2 emissions even where there is no 
regulated carbon price. It acts as a shadow price by 
incentivizing emissions reductions and investment in 
low-carbon technologies, so that a higher carbon price 
promotes projects with lower emissions over the rest. 

The internal carbon price in the scope of EU operations 
is established based on the current price and future 
forecasts for the European Union Emissions Trading 
Scheme (EU ETS), which is currently set at around $92 /t 
in 2026 and $110/t in 2030 in nominal terms. This price 
is aligned with the EU ETS price path used by the 
Company in its financial statements to perform the 
asset impairment test. See Note 3.5.1 Estimate of the 
recoverable value of assets and Note 6 on asset 
impairment of the Consolidated Financial Statements. 

For the rest of the world, in countries without more 
stringent specific regulation, an internal carbon price of 
$60/tCO2e is applied in the period 2026-2030. This 
price applies mainly to Upstream projects and, when it 
refers to methane emission reduction and routine flaring 
projects, it especially facilitates investment, since the 
real cost of abatement is often less than $60/tCO2e.  

Whenever an investment decision is made, the new 
project is analyzed with respect to the effect it could 
have on the Company’s direct emissions perimeter. To 
this end, the internal carbon price is used in the 
valuation of any investment that may have an impact on 
the Company’s Scope 1 and 2 (which currently 
corresponds to a volume of 13.9 MtCO2e).

39 See Targets related to climate change mitigation and adaptation (E1-4).
40 The CO2 removed or reduced, resulting from the carbon credits offered to our customers, is not discounted from Scope 3 emissions.
41 Verra VCU: Carbon credits generated under the Verified Carbon Standard (VCS), which guarantee the rigorous verification of emission reductions 

and their contribution to global climate mitigation. 

42 All of the carbon credits purchased correspond to emission reduction projects. 
43 CCB + Gold: Additional certification to Verra’s Climate, Community and Biodiversity (CCB) standard, so that carbon projects generate additional 

climate, social and environmental benefits.

METHODOLOGY

Methodology for climate change 
reporting and metrics

Methodology for aligning with the 1.5 °C target

The analysis of the alignment of the energy transition strategy 
with the objective of limiting global warming to 1.5 °C is based 
on the IPCC mitigation scenarios (AR6), which constitute the 
most recognized international scientific reference for assessing 
global emissions trajectories.

Scenario selection: The C1 family of scenarios published in the 
IIASA (International Institute for Applied Systems Analysis) 
database has been taken as a reference, which groups the 
trajectories with a probability greater than 50% of limiting 
global warming to 1.5 °C by 2100, without exceeding this 
threshold or doing so in a limited way (≤ 0.1 °C) during a 
transitional period. This family is distinguished from others (C2–
C7) by its level of climate ambition and its compatibility with 
the objectives of the Paris Agreement.

The C1 scenarios include emissions from all sectors—energy, 
industry, transportation, AFOLU, and land use—and all primary 
sources (coal, oil, gas, nuclear, and renewables), allowing for a 
comprehensive analysis of global decarbonization.

Statistical treatment and reference analysis: From the set of 
97 C1 scenarios, the median of the projected reduction of oil 
and gas emissions to 2030 has been calculated, considering this 
as the most robust trend measure against extreme values or 
asymmetric distributions. The median has been preferred over 
the arithmetic mean, as it offers greater stability in the face of 
the dispersion between underlying models and methodologies.

The dispersion analysis shows an interquartile range (Q1–Q3) of 
–25% to –11% and an absolute deviation of less than 9%, which 
confirms the statistical consistency of the whole and the 
representativeness of the median as a reference for the 
evaluation of corporate objectives.

Filtering Neutrality-Compatible Scenarios: Not all C1 scenarios 
are consistent with net zero in 2050. Therefore, the subset of 
19 C1 scenarios that achieve a reduction of more than 95% of 
emissions from the global energy system by 2050, considered 
compatible with net zero emissions trajectories, has been 
identified.

From this subset, a second filter has been applied to rule out 
assumptions considered technologically extreme or of low 
plausibility, including:

• Scenarios with more than 1.5 Gt of CO₂ captured and stored 

(CCS) in 2030.

• Scenarios with high dependence on nuclear energy in the 

energy matrix.

• Scenarios with carbon prices above USD 750/tCO₂ in 2050, 
given their disproportionate impact on the economic and 
social viability of the transition.

The application of these filters has resulted in a final set of 
9 representative scenarios, on which the median reduction in oil 
and gas emissions for 2030 has been recalculated, remaining 
around –14% compared to 2019.

Cross-validation and external consistency: As a verification 
exercise, the consistency of the results has been contrasted 
with the NZE (Net Zero Emissions by 2050) scenario prepared 
by the International Energy Agency (IEA), which is deterministic 
in nature and widely used as a sectoral reference. This scenario, 
updated in the World Energy Outlook 2025, projects global 
reductions in oil and gas emissions of 22% and coal emissions of 
32% by 2030 compared to 2019, reinforcing the consistency of 
the median obtained from the IPCC’s probabilistic scenarios.

Methodological conclusion: The methodology used combines 
scientific selection criteria (probability of meeting the 1.5 °C 
target), statistical consistency (median and interquartile 
intervals) and technological and economic plausibility (CCS 
technologies, nuclear and carbon prices). This approach ensures 
that the comparison between corporate objectives and the 
trajectories of the IPCC is made on a scientifically grounded 
basis.

Decarbonization targets

1. Absolute Scope 1, 2 and 3 emissions

 This metric includes: 

• Scope 1 and 2 (on an operational basis): Direct and indirect 

emissions  from assets operated by the Company are 
accounted for following the GHG Protocol, considering the 
assets operated by the Company. Repsol applies the 
operational control approach, including 100% of the 
emissions from the operations under its control, regardless of 
its shareholding or financial participation in such operations. 
Emissions arising from non-operated Joint Operating 
Agreements (JOA) assets are not included and account for 
0.7 MtCO2e in 2025. 

• Scope 3, Category 11 (GHG Protocol): These are the 
emissions from the use of the fossil products sold.   

2. Carbon Intensity Indicator  

The formula for calculating the CII is expressed as follows:

Carbon Intensity Indicator (gCO2 e/MJ)

Scope 1 and 2 operational

+

Scope 3 (Upstream 
production)

–

Carbon sinks

CII = 

Energy products (Upstream production)

+

Low carbon energy sources (electrons and 
molecules)

M

More precisely, the CII numerator includes:

• Operational Scope 1 and 2: Direct and indirect emissions 

from Upstream, Refining and Chemical assets, as well as from 
the non-renewable electricity generation operated by Repsol. 
For Scope 2 emissions, the market-based approach 
corresponding to the energy mix of the suppliers that supply 
steam and electricity is used. 

• Scope 3: Emissions derived from the use of products that can 
be obtained from Repsol’s oil and gas production. In the case 
of oil production, the yields in products are those of its own 
refining and chemical processes (category 11- upstream 
production based). For the production of natural gas, all 
emissions resulting from its combustion (category 11) are 
counted. In addition, emissions from third-party hydrogen 
plants supplying the Company's refineries (category 1- 
hydrogen purchases) are included, as well as emissions from 
final disposal after the use of chemicals (category 12). 

• Carbon sinks: Emissions stored through carbon capture, use 

and storage managed by the Company. Repsol’s 
decarbonization strategy does not rely on carbon offsets 
from natural-based solutions or carbon markets, except to 
address residual emissions once all available energy 
decarbonization options have been exhausted. Carbon 
offsets have not been included in our decarbonization path 
for this decade and are only contemplated in the long term 
to offset residual emissions in order to reach carbon 
neutrality by 2050. As for the denominator of the CII, it 
includes the following:

• Energy products (Upstream production): It includes the 

energy contained in the production of oil and natural gas. For 
oil, since we operate an integrated value chain, the energy of 
the products that can be obtained in our refining and 
chemical production scheme is included. For chemicals and 
other non-energy products (such as lubricants and asphalt, 
among others), the energy contained in the oil used in their 
production is counted. In the case of natural gas, given that 
we do not have an integrated value chain until final 
commercialization and that there is no net purchase of gas 
from third parties, the energy contained in the gas produced 
is included.

• Low-carbon energy sources: It includes energy produced 

from renewable electricity generation sources (solar, wind 
and hydro), energy from natural gas used in combined cycles 
and surplus cogeneration, as well as energy from renewable 
fuels (renewable hydrogen, biomethane, biofuels and 
synthetic fuels). Renewable electrical energy is accounted for 
by the substitution method, as the equivalent amount of 
non-renewable energy needed to generate the same 
electricity in a conventional thermal plant, thus allowing 
consistent and comparable assessments of energy 
contributions.

For greater homogeneity of the financial information by 
business segments and non-financial segments, the 
calculation of both energy production and Scope 3 emissions 
is prepared using the same scope of entities and assets used 
by the Company, namely:

• Companies/assets controlled by Repsol: 100% of energy 

production and Scope 3 emissions (category 11) are 
accounted for.

METHODOLOGY

• Companies/assets under joint control: Energy production and 

Scope 3 emissions (category 11) are accounted for in 
proportion to Repsol’s percentage of ownership.

Interests in companies/assets in which the Company does not 
have control or joint control, constituting a mere financial 
interest: Energy production and Scope 3 emissions (category 
11) are not counted. As of today, Repsol does not have assets 
under this category that should be considered for the 
calculation of the CII.

3. Absolute Scope 1 and 2 emissions

The perimeter of this target follows the operational criteria 
established by the GHG Protocol44, which entails the inclusion 
of all business units, assets and operations in which Repsol has 
direct control over management and/or operational 
responsibility, accounting for 100% of the emissions generated. 
Emissions corresponding to non-operated JOAs are excluded 
from this objective. Emissions are calculated using recognized 
methodologies, which are reported in Gross Scope 1, 2 and 3 
and total GHG emissions (E1-6).

To reinforce the robustness and transparency of this data, 
Repsol collaborates with external organizations that provide 
methodologies and technical validation, such as Oil&Gas 
Methane Partnership (OGMP), which supports best practices in 
the measurement and reporting of methane emissions. These 
external validations, together with international standards 
alignment, reinforce the operational excellence and credibility 
of the Company’s emissions reporting. 

This metric is validated annually by an external auditor 
according to the ISO 14064 standard, covering more than 90% 
of Scope 1 and 2 inventory in this verification. The coverage of 
this verification is subject to different portfolio changes 
throughout the year.

4. Methane intensity 

As most of the methane emissions from production operations 
come from natural gas production, this metric accounts for gas 
production in the denominator, while the numerator includes 
emissions associated with both gas and oil production. Repsol’s 
methane emissions represent 4% of equivalent Scope 1 and 2 
GHG emissions, but its relevance lies on the operational 
excellence, which should aim to reduce methane losses to 
practically zero, with the consequent environmental and 
economic benefit.

Emissions are accounted for "from the wellhead to the point of 
sale," in line with the IEA’s outlook for natural gas, which 
includes oil and gas production, as well as natural gas gathering 
and processing. The methane intensity metric is, in sum, the 
quotient between methane emissions and natural gas output at 
the point of sale, both expressed in volume and as a 
percentage. Additionally, the numerator calculation (m3 CH4) 
complies with the OGMP methodology by implementing 
innovative quantification technologies that have been validated 
by UNEP, having awarded Repsol the Gold Standard. For more 
information on this methodology, see the technical guides on 
the OGMP website.

44 The GHG Protocol guides for Scope 1, 2 and 3 can be found in Corporate Standard; Scope 2; Scope 3.

M

METHODOLOGY

5. Routine flaring emissions 

Metrics related to energy production 

Direct measurements of gas volume are made using flowmeters 
and sensors installed in flare systems in all operations. The 
calculation of emissions is based on standardized formulas and 
factors, detailed in the Company’s internal emissions guidance. 
For the segregation of emissions derived from routine operations 
and, therefore, likely to be reduced with more efficient operating 
strategies, the Company applies the definitions of the Global 
Flaring and Methane Reduction Partnership (GFMR) technical 
guide, which defines the different types of flaring in crude oil 
assets. 

Energy consumption metrics

The calculation of the energy consumed is obtained by 
multiplying the consumption of each fuel by its lower calorific 
value. This comes from specific analyses of each fuel or from 
reference guides (IPCC, API and Concawe). The bases for the 
calculation of energy and the scope are similar to those of 
section E1-6, where the origin of the emission sources and their 
calculation methodology are developed. The energy consumed 
that is generated by third parties (Scope 2) is calculated from 
the purchase invoices and includes:  

• Steam consumed from outside: In the case of Repsol, it has 
fossil origins since it is generated in hydrogen production 
facilities by reforming natural gas or other hydrocarbons.  

• Electricity consumed from outside: Depending on the 

marketer and the type of contract, this energy can be fossil, 
renewable or nuclear. 

The consumption of electricity, heat, steam and cooling 
purchased or acquired from fossil sources corresponds to that 
indicated on the supplier's invoice, except for assets that 
consume electricity directly from the grid for which the 
electricity mix is used according to the geographical area. 

Nuclear energy consumption corresponds to that indicated on 
the supplier’s invoice, except for assets that consume electricity 
directly from the grid for which the electricity mix is used 
according to the geographical area.    

The consumption of electricity, heat, steam and cooling 
purchased or acquired from renewable sources corresponds to 
that indicated on the marketer’s bill, including the business 
units and assets that consume 100% renewable electricity, 
except for assets that consume electricity directly from the 
grid, for which the electricity mix is used according to the 
geographical area.

For all the cases described, the type of energy consumed is 
related to the supply contract of each asset. This will depend on 
whether there are Power Purchase Agreements (PPAs) or 
Guarantees of Origin (GoO) certificates. If there are none, the 
residual or "remaining" emission factor (where the energy 
generated and sold specifically with GoOs is discounted) of the 
marketer (or of the country in the case of Direct Market 
Consumer assets) will be used. 

The energy consumption data for non-operated JOAs is 
estimated from 2024 emissions using emission factors 
corresponding to the geographical areas where they are located 
and using the production ratio between 2025 and 2024. 

Non-renewable energy production includes energy generated at 
Repsol's assets in the Industrial, Exploration and Production, and 
combined cycle power plant areas. Renewable energy production 
comprises energy generated from wind farms, solar plants, 
hydroelectric power stations, and renewable fuels produced at 
industrial facilities. Non-combustible renewable energy is 
expressed in terms of equivalent fossil energy, considering the 
average efficiency of a conventional thermal power plant to allow 
comparability with the Company’s other energy outputs. This 
procedure is the so-called substitution method, widely used and 
recognized by organizations outside the sector45. 

GHG emissions  

Scope 1 and 2 emissions are those derived from operations and 
processes at the Company’s facilities. These emissions can be 
classified into:  

Direct emissions (Scope 1) 

Those emitted directly at Repsol facilities, from the following 
sources: 

• By fuel consumption: The combustion reaction of any fuel 

results in heat, CO2, CH4, N2O, and water vapor. They make up 
the bulk of Scope 1 emissions. These emissions are calculated 
from direct measurements, such as fuel consumption and 
chemical composition, which means they can be accurately 
determined. Combustion occurs in equipment such as engines, 
turbines, furnaces and boilers and other small-scale 
consumption such as pilot gas and purge gas in flares.  

• By combustion of gas sent to flare.

• Process: Emissions from the process itself, characteristics of 

the Refining business such as, for example, the production of 
hydrogen from natural gas. These are reported by direct and 
analytical measurement of the gas or by process unit balance.  

• Vents: Emissions inherent in the equipment's design or 

operating practices, such as pneumatic devices or purges. 
They are mainly methane emissions and are quantified by 
direct measurement.

• Fugitive: Fugitive emissions come from involuntary leaks in 
equipment used in operations. This includes those from 
flanges, valve packings, compressor elements, pressure relief 
devices that vent to the atmosphere, etc. They are mainly 
methane emissions and are quantified by direct measurement 
through Leak Detection & Repair (LDAR) campaigns.

Indirect emissions (Scope 2)

Emissions from energy exchanges with third parties to or from 
Repsol facilities. Emissions due to the import/export of steam 
and electric power are considered. As it is energy exchanged 
with a third party, it is quantified through invoices, from which 
the emission factors of the marketing companies are also 
extracted and used to calculate the associated emissions. In a 
manner similar to energy consumption, the residual mix 
emission factor of the marketer or the country in the case of 
Direct Market Consumers is used for this calculation.

The (location-based) Scope 2 methodology quantifies GHG 
emissions based on average emission factors of energy 
generation for given locations, including local, subnational or 
national borders (Annex I of Directive 2013/34/EU). 

45 The substitution method is used by the USA Department of Energy, Science Based Targets initiative Energy Institute and the Our World in Data 

initiative which includes the University of Oxford.

M

The (market-based) Scope 2 methodology quantifies the GHG 
emissions produced by generators from which the reporting 
entity purchases under contract with a marketer (Annex I of 
Directive 2013/34/EU). 

Generally speaking, Scope 1 and 2 GHG emissions are quantified 
following the guidelines of international standards widely 
recognized in the industry, such as IPCC, API, and Concawe. 

Similarly, the breakdown at the emission source level and by 
business can only be accurately determined for emissions from 
operated assets as they are under the Company's control and 
the source and usage of each fuel is known.  

In the specific case of methane (CH₄), the Company reports 
these emissions using advanced quantification methods, such 
as direct measurements or equivalent alternatives recognized 
by the OGMP 2.0 initiative. For more information on the 
methodologies used, you can consult the publicly available 
technical guides on the OGMP website. 

As for the global warming factors for methane and N₂O, as of 
2024, the IPCC's Sixth Assessment Report (AR6) is used as a 
reference, which establishes a global warming factor (GWP) of 
28.9 for methane and 273 for nitrous oxide (N₂O) over a 100-
year time horizon, highlighting the greater climate impact of 
these gases compared to CO₂. 

JOA’s non-operated Scope 1 and 2 emissions are estimated 
based on information provided by third parties in 2024 and 
updated using 2025 equity production.   

Indirect emissions (Scope 3) 

The reported Scope 3 emissions are aligned with the principles 
established by the GHG Protocol Corporate Value Chain 
(Scope 3) Accounting and Reporting Standard and reflect the 
methodological approach followed to quantify the Company’s 
indirect value chain emissions.  

After analyzing the entire emissions inventory for 2024, 
including the 15 Scope 3 categories, and considering that no 
significant changes have taken place that could have altered 
the materiality of these categories, the material categories and 
the corresponding calculation methods are detailed below.

A materiality threshold has been defined for those categories 
that account for more than 1.5% of total Scope 3 emissions, 
while ensuring that more than 95% of the emissions within this 
scope are always considered. 

1: Purchased goods and services 

It includes purchases of crude oil, other main raw materials for 
Refining and Chemicals (hydrogen, ethylene, benzene, etc.), 
purchases of biofuels and services contracted by the Company. 
For the calculation of crude oil, biofuels and raw materials 
emissions, primary activity data from material balances are used 
together with emission factors from IOGP 2024 (International 
Association for Oil & Gas Producers) by regions and Ecoinvent 
3.12. In the case of hydrogen, an emission factor is used based 
on the European Union’s BREF Best Available Techniques (BAT) 
Reference Document for the Refining of Mineral Oil and Gas  
(JRC 2015). The calculation of contracted services is based on 
the methodology spend-based using data from the Company’s 
economic purchasing activity and emission factors from the 
Carbon Footprint 2023 Evolution 2019-2023 document, May 
2025. Ministry for the Ecological Transition and the 
Demographic Challenge, in which the Ministry for the 
Environment calculates and publishes its own carbon footprint.    

METHODOLOGY

Category 11: Use of sold products  

This is calculated taking into account the sale of the Company’s 
petroleum products (Customer, Mobility Peru, Operators and 
Exports businesses) and net gas production by the Exploration and 
Production business. To obtain category 11 emissions, the lower 
calorific value (GJ/t) and emission factor (tCO2/GJ) of each of 
the products is applied (2006 IPCC Guidelines for National 
Greenhouse Gas Inventories, Volume 2 – Energy; Table 1.2 and 
2.2). All products are taken into account with the exception of 
chemical products, lubricants, asphalts and specialties, as they do 
not generate emissions in their use. 

Biogenic emissions 

These emissions are related to the value chain of biogenic 
matter, both to feed Repsol processes and to be sold as biofuel. 

Biogenic emissions associated with Scope 1 and 2 are not 
included as they are not material. 

In the case of Scope 3, we distinguish:

• Biogenic CO₂ emissions associated with the use phase of 
biofuels, calculated based on biofuel sales data and the 
required bio content in the marketed fuels. For the calculation 
of CO2 emissions associated with the use of these fuels, the 
lower calorific value (GJ/t) according to the RED II directive 
and the emission factor (tCO2/GJ) of each of the products is 
applied (2006 IPCC Guidelines for National Greenhouse Gas 
Inventories, Volume 2 – Energy; 2.2). The result is 4.2 MtCO2 
and it is not included in the results presented for Scope 3. 

• Methane and N2O emissions associated with the biomass 
combustion use stage, calculated by applying the lower 
calorific value (GJ/t) according to the RED II directive and the 
corresponding emission factor for each GHG and product 
(2006 IPCC Guidelines for National Greenhouse Gas 
Inventories,  Volume – Energy; 2.2), with a result of 15 ktCO2e. 

• Life cycle emissions of biomass not from combustion or 

degradation, already included in the results presented for 
Scope 3 and corresponding to the purchase of bio feedstocks 
with associated emissions of 1.1 MtCO2e and calculated with 
DEFRA factors. 

Other considerations 

The methodology for calculating Scope 3, Category 11 emissions is 
based on the Company’s product sales.

The percentage of Scope 3 emissions calculated using primary 
data obtained from suppliers or other partners in the value chain 
is 91.7%, including data provided by flight travel agencies for 
category 6, category 15 emissions as data provided directly by 
the joint ventures, and considering category 11 emissions based 
on internal fuel sales data. 

Methane (CH4) and nitrous oxide (N2O) are operationally 
relevant greenhouse gases, however, the detailed breakdown of 
these gases loses relevance for the quantification of Scope 3 
emissions due to their materiality. Despite this, most of the 
emission factors used to calculate Scope 3 emissions include 
both CH4 and N2O (DEFRA, IOGP, Ecoinvent).  

Similarly, the breakdown at the emission source level and by 
business can only be accurately determined for emissions from 
operated assets as they are under the Company’s control and the 
source and usage of each fuel is known. Scope 3 emissions, on the 
other hand, are indirect and therefore the only way to estimate 
them is by means of global emission factors in CO2e, which is why 
this breakdown is not available.
