2.2 Pollution prevention

2.2.1 Impact, risk and opportunity 
management
Description of processes to identify and assess pollution-
related material impacts, risks, and opportunities (ESRS 2 
IRO-1)

Repsol has carried out a double materiality assessment,  
incorporating the opinion of its stakeholders, to assess 
its impacts, risks, and opportunities (IRO) related to the 
emissions into air, water and soil of potentially polluting 
substances derived from its activity, in the short, 
medium and long term. 

In this analysis, environmental experts from each 
business area have participated and the Company’s sites 
and businesses that are operated have been examined, 
excluding the rest of its own operations from the scope. 
The amount of pollutants emitted, their dispersion into 
the environment, the legal limits established for 
emissions, the existence of pollution receptors and their 
vulnerability in relation to these pollutants were taken 
into account.

The following aspects have been identified as the IRO 
materials with a negative environmental impact in terms 
of pollution:

• Emissions of pollutants generated in its own 

operations into water (IRO-14).

• Air emissions from both its production processes 

(IRO-15) and those generated by third parties due to 
the use of the fuel products offered by the Company 
(IRO-16).

• Emissions to the ground due to accidental releases or 

deviations from established procedures (IRO-17).

In addition to the double materiality assessment, 
a methodology has been developed to determine where 
the emissions of the substances that the Company 
considers a priority occur, as well as the business areas 
that contribute significantly to each IRO analyzed:

• For air emissions, based on the volume of emissions 

recorded in Repsol’s internal environmental 
management tool, it has been identified that the 
Industrial and E&P businesses contribute significantly 
to the material IROs identified.

• For water emissions, based on local water-related risks, 
assessed with the Repsol Water Tool, weighted with 
respect to the relative volume of discharge from each 
facility, it has been identified that the Industrial 
business contributes significantly to the material IROs 
identified. For more information on the Repsol Water 
Tool, see Impact, risk and opportunity management.

• For emissions to the soil, indirectly, given the technical 

difficulty to measure them due to their accidental 
nature,  and based on the estimated costs to manage 
contaminated soils, it has been identified that the 
Industrial business and the Customer business 
contribute significantly to the material IROs identified.

In relation to air pollution, sulfur oxides (SO2), nitrogen 
oxides (NOx), volatile organic compounds (VOCs) and 
particles are identified as priority substances. In water 
pollution, hydrocarbons, suspended solids and 
substances that cause an increase in chemical oxygen 
demand (COD) are a priority. In soil contamination, in the 
event of an accidental release, priority is given to 
prevention by controlling all substances that may cause 
damage to health and the environment in case there is 
evidence of their presence, including hydrocarbons. 

The Company also monitors other substances emitted in 
compliance with the requirements of applicable 
legislation, such as the European Industrial Emissions 
Directive (Integrated Pollution Prevention and Control 
E-PRTR).1 

These assessments have not required consultations with 
potentially affected communities.

Policies related to pollution (E2-1)

Repsol has embraced numerous commitments in its 
Environmental Policy, which governs its actions in all its 
activities and locations where it operates, throughout 
the value chain, as well as in the products and services it 
offers. 

Specifically, the Company undertakes to prevent and 
control the impacts associated to air, water and soil 
emissions of pollutants derived from our operations  
and to reduce the potential environmental impact 
arising from the use of the products we manufacture, 
avoiding incidents and emergency situations. This 
commitment relates to the material IROs mentioned 
above.

The policy also promotes and requires environmental 
commitments in line with one’s own in the value chain, 
partners, suppliers, contractors, distributors and other 
business relationships. 

For more information, see Repsol’s policies related to 
sustainability.

1 European Pollutant Release and Transfer Register

The following is information on actions taken and 
planned by Repsol in relation to pollution. Their 
implementation contributes to the achievement of the 
commitments established in the Environmental Policy 
regarding the prevention and control of the impacts 
associated with 

emissions of air, water and soil pollutants derived from 
their operations.

3.– Implementation of statistical control of fuel 
inventories at service stations in Spain and Portugal. In 
2025, the expansion of the service to the NMC network 
(new CODO model of stations operated by third parties 
in which Repsol owns the land) was completed, and 
annually, the service will be extended to service stations 
in its own network and the NMC network that are built 
or acquired. This initiative contributes to the objective of 
defining, within a period of less than 12 months, action 
plans for the management and remediation of soil or 
groundwater in the event of contamination.

preventing and minimizing the risks of soil 
contamination in the event of possible spills and  
discharges at the Escatrón and Bahía de Algeciras power 
plants (Spain). In 2025, tanks (hydrochloric acid, caustic 
soda, citric acid, and hypochlorite) were replaced and 
chemical areas (chlorite, hydrochloric acid, and sulfuric 
acid) have been improved, and over the next year, the 
reworking of faulty routes and sections, identification 
and listing of manholes, installation of new tanks, and 
improvement of the HCl and NaOH pumping area in the 
demineralized water plant (demi) will be completed. 
At the Bahía de Algeciras plant (Spain), repair work was 
carried out in 2025 on the circulating water discharge 
channel and inspection chambers of the acid drainage 
network of the demineralized water plant and the 
waterproofing of the acid and oily ponds.

control of water emissions in 2025 are detailed below:

Prevention, reduction and control of emissions to water actions

Prevention of spills and 
discharges in the maritime

Main actions in 2025:

5.- A notable action carried out in 2025 was the 
implementation of improvements in the integrity of the 
La Pampilla refinery complex (Peru) for the prevention 
of spills and discharges.  For example, the installation of 
automatically removable safety valves on hose strings, 
the incorporation of underwater manual valves in the 
PLEM system or the assembly of guided frames for the 
parts incorporated into the seabed (PLETs). In 2026, the 
same improvements are expected to be implemented at 
the Multibuoy No. 3 terminal.

Repsol also carries out other actions for which no 
specific goals have been set, but which help to prevent 
polluting emissions:

•

•

It develops and markets a range of products that add 
to its performance the minimization of polluting 
emissions in its life cycle. For example, renewable fuels 
and electricity, Blue+ fuels, LPG, AutoGas, LNG and 
low-emission diesel for boilers (bioenergy e+10), 
among others.

It minimizes potential environmental impacts on 
populations and natural spaces through a process of 
selecting the location of its new facilities, regulated 
by its internal regulations on the assessment of 
environmental, social, and health impacts, and also 
subject to the applicable laws on such impacts.

•

•

It implements comprehensive management of 
discharges through advanced wastewater treatment 
systems, which include the segregation of effluents 
according to their level of contamination and the 
application of specific and effective treatments, with 
the aim of maximizing internal reuse and minimizing 
the pollutant load discharged.

It establishes internal safety standards for the design 
and operation of its activities, minimizing the risks of 
accidents that may lead to air, water or soil 
contamination due to the unintentional release of 
polluting substances.

None of the above actions require significant capital 
expenditures (capex) or operating expenses (opex), 
according to the financial materiality criteria adopted by 
the Company.

2.2.2 Metrics and targets

Targets related to pollution (E2-3)

Repsol is making progress in the prevention and control 
of polluting emissions through the requirements of the 
applicable regulations. This involves the adoption of the 
best available technologies (BAT) referenced by the 
European Union’s sectoral BREF3 documents. 

In addition, Repsol has voluntarily set two targets4 to 
mitigate its material IROs in line with the commitments 
set out in the Environmental Policy regarding the 
prevention and control of impacts associated with 
emissions to the air, water, and soil of polluting 
substances derived from its operations.  

3 The Best Available Techniques Reference (BREF) documents are the reference documents published by the European Union within the framework 
of Directive (EU) 2024/1785 on Industrial Emissions (IED), which include the best available technologies from the different industrial sectors and 
apply across Europe.

4 These targets are not based on conclusive scientific evidence and interest groups have not been involved in their development; there have been 

no changes in the target or metrics from the initial definitions.

(1) Owned operations that contribute significantly to the identified material impacts (see Impact, risk and opportunity management).

As further information to the targets described:

1.– Define action plans for the management and 
remediation of soil or groundwater in the event of 
contamination within less than 12 months.

In 2024, Repsol established this goal to prevent and 
control contamination of soil or groundwater, aligned 
with IROs 14 and 17 (as described in Materiality 
assessment). This objective is applicable to the 
Company’s own operations that contribute significantly 
to the identified material impacts. Its definition is not 
based on the emission of specific pollutant loads.

By means of this target, the Company considers that the 
extent of contaminants present in the soil and/or 
groundwater is minimized, as is the potential impact 
that contaminants may have on existing receptors. 

This target is monitored through the Company’s 
environmental situation reporting tool. To assess 
compliance, the percentage (%) of action plans that 
have been defined within a period of less than 
12 months from the confirmation of the contamination 
shall be determined annually. The aim is to achieve a 
value of 100% in this indicator, starting from a reference 
value of 0%, which is established annually.

Progress toward achieving the 2025 target remains on 
track, according to the initial planning, and no 
significant deviations have been identified from the 
initially planned performance.

2.– Reduce emissions of nitrogen oxides (NOx) by 30% by 
2035 compared with 2018 at the Refining and Chemical 
industrial complexes in the Iberian Peninsula.

In 2025, Repsol set this target for air pollution, in line 
with the IRO-15 (as described in Materiality 
assessment), which will first apply in 2026. This target is 
not based on the emission of specific pollutant loads.

It is an absolute target linked to the decarbonization 
plan, the GHG emission reduction levers and the 
development of new production processes related to 
the use of renewable energy and renewable raw 
materials of the Industrial business. In its definition, the 
projected reduction of NOx emissions achievable 
through the aforementioned levers has been estimated.  
To this end, a correlation between CO2 emissions and 
NOx was used, based on historical emissions for both 
gases in the different facilities included within the scope 
of the target.      

The target will be reviewed if there are changes in 
current regulations, technological advances and market 
trends, modifications in Repsol’s Strategic Plan, or 
changes in the facilities operated in the Industrial 
business that have a significant impact.

To monitor this target, the internal environmental 
parameter reporting tool will be used, from which NOx 
emissions data will be extracted annually.

In the case of water pollution, Repsol has not adopted 
specific targets beyond those established for water 
management, as defined in Water and marine resources.  
Businesses that are material to these issues operate in 
highly regulated environments, where the applicable 
regulations define both the limits to be reached and the 
technologies to be implemented.

(1) In non-operated own operations, the data have been estimated following the methodology described in Methodology for pollution prevention 
reporting and metrics. In the case of air pollutant emissions, the reported value corresponds to the estimated consolidated value that exceeds the 
threshold for assets and is approximately equivalent to 10% of the actual data for the operated own operations.
(2) The 2024 figure for the pollutant Ni and compounds (such as Ni) has been updated after an error in its original report was identified and 
corrected.
(3) The 2024 figure for the pollutant Chlorides (as total Cl) has been updated after an error was identified in its original report and corrected.

In 2025, certain variations are observed in air and water 
pollutants, mainly due to methodological revisions, 
operational variations, or asset divestments. The increase 
in NMVOC is due to improved calculations based on more 
complete venting data; NH₃ decreases as it falls below the 
reporting threshold in the operating asset that reported 
it the previous year after applying the CONCAWE (2019) 
methodology, which confirms the absence of significant 
emission sources. Variations in metals (Cd, Cr, Hg, Ni 
and Zn) result from the update and standardization of 
emission factors in accordance with this guideline, while 
the strict application of the compounds defined in 
Annex II of the PRTR reduces the scope of PAHs, placing 
them below the reporting threshold.

In the case of As and Pb, differences are explained by the 
divestment of assets operated in the UK. The variation in 
nitrogen, AOX, chlorides, cyanides and fluorides 
discharged into water corresponds to normal operational 
variations. Finally, the reduction in phenols is due to one 
of the operating assets reporting an annual average 
concentration below the detection limit.

Anticipated financial effects from pollution-related 
material risks and opportunities (E2-6)

The expenses and disbursements related to the oil spill at 
the La Pampilla refinery facilities in Peru are described in 
Environmental risks - oil spill in Peru in Note 20.1 to the 
Group’s 2025 consolidated Financial Statements.

M

METHODOLOGY

Methodology for pollution prevention 
reporting and metrics 

Repsol has a process for reporting environmental parameters 
(water, emissions, and waste), which is carried out in the Enablon 
digital tool, that allows the data to be uploaded and validated, 
thus ensuring two levels of data review. In turn, this process, in 
some cases, has connections from the source systems of the 
operating sites, making the process efficient and reducing errors 
in data management. This data reporting process is regulated by 
an internal procedure that describes the process, roles, 
responsibilities, and dates for reporting periods.

The data reported for Repsol’s own operations in which the 
Company has operational control follow the internal processes of 
the operating sites, which are subject to compliance with Annex II 
of Regulation (EC) No 166/2006 of the European Parliament and 
of the Council (European Pollutant Release and Transfer Register, 
European PRTR Regulation), and must be reported to the 
authorities. 100% of emissions is accounted for, regardless of the 
percentage of shareholding. 

The information is obtained by direct measurement, calculation 
and estimation, depending on the parameter and the process of 
each operating site. The reported quantities that have exceeded 
the threshold have mostly been obtained by direct measurement 
or calculation, in accordance with the requirements of PRTR-
Europe. The data for 2025 have been obtained using the 
methods indicated above and, to a minimum extent, estimated 
data from actual measurements projected for the year.

The table includes data corresponding to those pollutants that, 
by operating site, have exceeded the thresholds established in 
the regulation, consolidated at the Company’s aggregate.

Repsol does not have information on the metrics of the assets 
managed through Joint Operating Agreements (JOA). To obtain 
this data, information has been requested from operators, but 
complete or verified information has not been received, so 
estimates of them have been made.

The estimation of pollutants emissions has been made taking into 
account the production of the assets. Production-based ratios 
have been calculated for operated assets and applied to non-
operated assets. Where information was available on assets 
operated under similar conditions and in the same location, the 
average ratio of these assets was applied; and where there was 
no comparable information by location or type of operation, an 
average ratio of several assets with a similar type of operation 
was used.

Once the estimated pollutant data for non-operated own 
operations have been obtained, the data has been compared with 
the threshold set out in Annex II of Regulation (EC) No 166/2006 
of the European Parliament and of the Council (European 
Pollutant Release and Transfer Register, European RTRP 
Regulation) emitted into the atmosphere, water and soil, with 
only NOx exceeding the threshold in some assets. Therefore, the 
NOx data is reported, but not added to the consolidated data on 
an operational control basis.

2.3 Water and marine resources

2.3.1 Impact, risk and opportunity 
management

Description of the processes to identify and assess 
material impacts, risks and opportunities related to water 
management (ESRS 2 IRO-1)

Repsol has carried out a double materiality assessment,  
incorporating the opinion of its stakeholders, to assess 
its impacts, risks, and opportunities (IROs), including 
those related to water in the short, medium and 
long term.

In carrying out this assessment, environmental experts 
from each business area have participated. The water 
withdrawal data of the Company’s operated sites and 
businesses have been examined, excluding the rest of its 
own operations from the scope. 

In the water subtopic, the following material negative 
impact has been identified:

• Freshwater withdrawal from natural sources (surface 
and groundwater) in areas of water risk that reduces 
the availability of water resources in the river basin 
(IRO-18).

Repsol Water Tool

In addition to the double materiality assessment, an in-
house tool called the Repsol Water Tool (RWT) has been 
used to identify and evaluate current and potential 
impacts, as well as material water-related risks and 
opportunities in the Company’s own operations. 

The analysis conducted identified the businesses for 
which water is a material issue, namely the Refining and 
Chemicals business in the Iberian Peninsula and the 
Marcellus asset of the Upstream business in the USA. 
Particular attention has been given to the amount of 
freshwater withdrawn from the environment, as this is 
considered to be the Company’s most significant impact 
in this topic. 

For more details, see Methodology for water and 
marine resources reporting and metrics. 

The results of the IRO assessment are taken into 
account when setting water-related policies and 
targets. These, in turn, are aligned with the Company’s 
Strategic Plan for its transformation and an energy 
transition compatible with the global ambition to 
achieve net zero emissions.

Policies related to water management (E3-1) 

Repsol has established commitments in its 
Environmental Policy that govern its actions in all its 
activities and places where it operates, throughout the 
value chain, as well as in the products and services it 
offers. These commitments give rise to the actions  
necessary to manage water-related environmental 
impacts. 

The Company is focused on water management that 
includes the use and sourcing of water throughout the 
life cycle of its operations, products and services, and 
the long-term protection of available water resources, 
both terrestrial and marine. Repsol will fulfill this 
commitment by increasing the use of sustainable supply 
from alternative water sources, including both internal 
and external reuse of water, thereby supporting the 
prevention and reduction of water pollution resulting 
from our activities through the treatment of effluents. 

The Company has also set the ambition to reduce 
freshwater withdrawal from the environment, especially 
in areas at water risk.

These commitments are related to the material IROs 
associated with the reduction in the volume of available 
water resources, which is reported in this section.

The Environmental Policy includes in its scope all the 
Company’s facilities located in areas of high water stress 
and envisages practices related to the sustainability of 
the oceans and the seas.

The policy also pursues and requires environmental 
practices in the value chain that are in line with the 
Company’s own, including partners, suppliers, 
contractors, distributors and other business 
relationships. 

For more information, see Repsol’s policies related to 
sustainability.

Actions and resources related to water management 
(E3-2)

The following tables provide information on the actions 
taken and planned by Repsol related to water 
management.

Its implementation contributes to meeting the 
commitments established in the Environmental Policy, 
specifically to reducing freshwater withdrawal from the 
environment, particularly in water-stressed areas, in the 
vicinity of the Company’s operations. 

In particular, actions 1.1, 1.2, 1.3, 2.1, 2.2, 2.3, 2.4 and 
3.1-3.6 are aimed at fulfilling the ambition of zero net 
freshwater withdrawal from the environment for the 
industrial complexes of the Refining and Chemicals 
business located in the Iberian Peninsula. Actions 2.5, 
2.6 and 4.1 have been established in order to achieve 
the goal of zero net freshwater withdrawal from the 
environment for the Marcellus E&P asset.

2.4.– Improvement of instrumentation and increase of 
hydraulic capacity for internal water distribution 
(2024-2027).

Upstream

2.5.– Adaptation of the facilities of the Marcellus asset 
by increasing the water storage capacity (estimated at 
72,000 barrels) in order to increase water recycling in 

the processes. This involves the construction of three 
new water storage tanks at a central location and the 
transformation of the current operating process. The 
application was submitted to the local regulatory 
authority for approval.

In 2026 (depending on the permit approval schedule), 
the construction phase will begin. An external 
contractor will be responsible for the installation of the 
necessary containment systems, tanks, piping, and 
automation systems (2024-2026).

2.6.– Optimization of the design of the Marcellus asset 
completion site. This will involve reducing the discharge 
time of water transported by truck in order to increase 
the availability of recycled water for use in operations. 
The project has evaluated the success of this first 
distribution optimization, which proved to be effective. 
Work is currently underway to adopt this new optimized 
layout of the facilities in upcoming completion activities 
from 2025 onwards, where possible (2024-2026).2

1 The time horizon has been modified from 2023-2025 to 2023-2026 due to an update in the project planning that entails new actions to be 

developed in 2026.

2 The time horizon has been changed from 2024-2025 to 2024-2026 to indicate that the results of the study conducted in 2024 will be applied to 

new completion activities taking place in 2026.

will be operated for validation between 2026 
and 2027.

2.3.2 Metrics and targets

Water management targets (E3-3) 

Repsol has voluntarily defined two absolute5 targets in 
relation to water to address its material impacts, risks, 
and opportunities in line with the ambition set out in the 
Environmental Policy to reduce freshwater withdrawal 
from the environment, especially in water-stressed areas, 
in the vicinity of operations.

The scope of these targets encompasses operations that 
are material in the management of water-related IROs 
identified in the double materiality assessment.

Both targets take into account the improvement of water 
quality, since the reduction in freshwater withdrawal 
from the environment6 minimizes the impact on the 
conditions (quality and quantity) of the water available in 
ecosystems. In addition, since withdrawal and 
consumption are two directly related variables, the aim is 
also to reduce the consumption of freshwater. 

Water-related targets

To define the targets, the use has been made of the 
methodology recommended by the CEO Water Mandate in 
the guide Setting Enterprise Water Targets , published in 
2021 and developed by the World Resources Institute 
(WRI), Pacific Institute, CDP, The Nature Conservancy 
and WWF. 

The indicator for tracking both targets is monitored 
using the Company’s environmental parameters 
reporting tool.

In 2025, the volumes of water consumption, recycled 
and reused water decreased. This was mainly due to the 
divestment of the assets operated in the United 
Kingdom following the agreement to consolidate the 
Upstream business with Neo UK, as part of the creation 
of a new joint venture in that country.

the update of the water stress level in accordance with 
WRI Aqueduct 4.0. The reclassification derived from this 
update means that volumes previously associated with 

lower stress categories are now located in areas of 
greater criticality, thus increasing the total reported 
under this criterion.

compared to the previous year due to specific 
operational needs in certain E&P assets. This change 
must be interpreted in relation to the trend in the 
previous year: in 2024, a reduction of 225,063 m³ was 
recorded, while in 2025 a proportional increase was 
observed with the aim of maintaining a stable storage 
volume that allows operating needs to be met.

M

Methodology for water and marine 
resources reporting and metrics

Repsol Water Tool

The analysis methodology of the Repsol Water Tool (RWT) 
includes the following features:

• The degree of measurement of the volume and quality of 

water flows in the facilities.

• The type of water (fresh and non-freshwater) and the 
volumes associated with each indicator (withdrawal, 
discharge, reuse).

• The quality of the water withdrawn, the existence of 

alternative sources of water supply and water treatment 
technologies (withdrawn, discharged and reused), the 
potential alteration of water quality (physical, chemical and 
biological parameters) downstream of the facility.

• The characterization of the withdrawal sources: type of 

source, type of water, location, impact of the withdrawal in 
relation to the average volume of the water body, degree of 
dependence on that source of supply, location of the source 
with respect to protected areas and key areas for 
biodiversity, presence of threatened species, importance of 
the water source for local communities and current and 
future level of water stress.

• The characterization of the discharge: direct/indirect return 
to the original source, the reuse of the discharge by a third 

party.

• The degree of competition for the resource and the future 

availability of water (guarantee of supply in the coming years, 

projections of future water demand).

• Regulatory and legal changes, sanctioning and reputational 

aspects.

For this assessment, no consultations were necessary with the 
potentially affected communities.

For the identification of physical risks, the values of water stress 
(current and future) are taken into account both for the water 
withdrawal sources and for the points of discharge, as obtained 
from the Aqueduct Water Risk Atlas tool of the World Resource 
Institute (WRI).

To define the state of water bodies, the criteria established in 
the annexes of the European Directive 2000/60/EC (Water 
Framework Directive) have been used.

The input data entered into RWT is based on direct measurements 
and assessments made by those responsible for water 
management at each facility.

Water metrics

The reporting criteria for water metrics are set out in a specific 
internal guide for the water vector. It defines metrics and 
methodologies so that the reporting of these metrics complies 
with the legal requirements for sustainability reporting, 
supporting the homogeneity and traceability of the information 
throughout the Company. Some analytical techniques reviewed 
are Standard Methods (SM) of the Examination of Water and 
Wastewater (from the APHA, AWWA, WPCF associations), series 
2000 to 9000, Water Testing Standards (ASTM) and European 
Standards (EN) and standards published by the International 
Organization for Standardization  (ISO). These criteria are 
determined for the internal report and will not prevail over the 
criteria established by the competent authorities for the reports 
that must be issued by the operational sites in compliance with 
the applicable legislation.

METHODOLOGY

For the calculation of the metrics, the information is reported 
by category, according to the type of water (fresh or non-fresh), 
type of withdrawal source and type of effluent receiver, 
additionally indicating the use of water for the particular cases 
in which it is reused or recycled or, for example, if it is used for 
cooling processes in specific circuits. This segregation, based on 
the Global Reporting Initiative (GRI) water standard, allows the 
requested metrics to be calculated from the granularity of the 
Company’s water management.

The methodology used to calculate the metrics in Repsol’s 
operations under operational control establishes that 100% of 
the corresponding contribution is accounted for, regardless of 
the percentage of shareholding held by the Company, and is 
applied in accordance with the following criteria:

• Water consumption: it is calculated as the difference 

between withdrawals and discharges, which includes both 
fresh and non-freshwater.

• Water consumption in areas with water risk: it is calculated by 
evaluating the level of water stress in the areas where both 
the withdrawal sources  and the discharge points are located, 
using the Aqueduct Water Risk Atlas tool by World Resource 
Institute’s (WRI). Regions with a high (40-80%) or very high 
(greater than 80%) percentage of water withdrawals are 
deemed to be areas at water risk. This information relates to 
to areas where water has been identified as a matter of 
material importance.

• Recycled water: treated or untreated water that has been 
used more than once in the same process, before being 
discharged outside the Company’s boundaries.

• Reused water: treated or untreated water that has been used 
more than once in different processes of the Company or by 
a third party, before being discharged outside its limits.

• Stored water: water withdrawn from the environment that, 
without having been included in the report of the water 
consumption metric, is temporarily accumulated to enable 
water availability in operations and to be used when necessary 
for a certain operational purpose, whether present or future.

• Changes in water storage: the difference between the 
volume stored at the beginning and at the end of the 
reporting period.

• Water intensity: the ratio of total water consumption (m3) to 

net income.

In assets managed through Joint Operating Agreements (JOAs), 
where Repsol does not exercise operational control, direct 
information on the metrics is not available through the internal 
reporting process. To obtain this data, information has been 
requested from the pertinent operators; however, the 
information received is incomplete and unverified.

To meet the reporting requirements, estimates of the relevant 
metrics have been made. In the specific case of water metrics, it 
is only possible to estimate consumption, since other indicators, 
such as the volume of water recycled, reused or stored, depend 
on the particular way that each asset is managed, which is 
conditioned by operational factors such as location, type of 
operation (onshore/offshore) and the nature of the asset 
(conventional/unconventional).

Water consumption was estimated based on production, 
applying ratios from assets operated under comparable 
conditions and, where no equivalent information was available, 
using average ratios from assets with similar characteristics. 

Given that the estimate of water consumption in these assets 
represents less than 5% of the Company’s total, it has been 
deemed non-material and therefore excluded from the 
Company’s consolidated reporting.

2025  Consolidated Management Report

2.4 Biodiversity and 
ecosystems

2.4.1 Strategy 

Transition plan, strategy and business model (E4-1)

Repsol has carried out a resilience assessment of its 
business model and strategy in relation to the risks 
associated with biodiversity and ecosystems in order to 
assess its ability to address these risks under different 
scenarios and to facilitate the viability and continuity of 
its projects and operations. Stakeholders were not 
involved in the elaboration of this study.

The scope of the analysis includes the Company’s 
operated sites and businesses, leaving out of scope the 
rest of its own operations. The time horizon used is five 
years.

The risks considered are those assessed in the double 
materiality assessment, where the annual economic 
impact is evaluated, generally based on the operating 
result before tax, and the probability of occurrence of 
the event, based on statistics of historical events or, 
failing that, on expert criteria. 

The risks assessed are listed below. None of them were 
deemed material in the double materiality assessment:

• Potential risk of non-compliance with regulations 

related to biodiversity and the ecosystem protection 
in owned onshore operations or those located in 
sensitive marine environments, which may result in 
economic sanctions, suspension of activities, loss of 
licenses, reputational damage, and increased 
operating costs.   

• Potential risk that onshore and offshore operations 
will be restricted, disrupted, or impeded in areas 
classified as sensitive or prioritized for biodiversity 
conservation, which could lead to delays in obtaining 
permits, project redesigns, or even to cancellation.

• The deterioration of coastal or river protective 

ecosystems may become an additional risk factor that 
could worsen potential damage to infrastructure in 
the event of heavy rainfall or waves.

• Loss of vegetation as a result of deforestation, 

construction activities and natural erosion may lead to 
a decrease in soil stability. This can be an additional 
risk factor in exposure to landslides, which could lead 
to damage to infrastructure, increase operational 
downtime and raise repair costs.

Therefore, this assessment has identified transition and 
physical risks, and it has been determined that there are 
no systemic risks related to biodiversity and ecosystems 
that affect the Company. 

Repsol’s strategy and business model could be affected 
by the following:

• Greater complexity when selecting geographical 

locations to undertake new projects.

•

•

•

•

Increased regulatory requirements that may result in 
greater difficulty in obtaining environmental 
authorizations for new projects or for renewing/
maintaining existing ones. 

Increased cost of capital for investments (capex).

Increase in management costs (capex and opex) of 
impacts related to biodiversity and ecosystems.

Impact on the Company’s reputation associated with 
lower investor attraction.

Repsol has considered several scenarios to assess the 
resilience of its strategy and business model, although it 
does not intend to establish which will be the most 
likely. The Company has adopted the scenarios set out in 
the Taskforce on Nature-related Financial Disclosures 
(TNFD)1 reporting framework as a reference for 
developing its long-term forecasts. TNFD’s approach 
focuses on two critical uncertainties: 1) the deterioration 
of ecosystem services, which is correlated with physical 
risks, and 2) the alignment of market and non-market 
forces, which is associated with transition risks. Four 
different scenarios are thus envisaged: 

• Ahead of the game: moderate deterioration of 

ecosystem services and high alignment of market and 
non-market forces.

• Go fast or go home: serious deterioration of 

ecosystem services and high alignment of market and 
non-market forces.

• Sand in the gears: serious deterioration of ecosystem 

services and low alignment of market and non-market 
forces.

• Back of the list: moderate deterioration of ecosystem 

services and low alignment of market and non-market 
forces.

In the assessment of the potential level of risk, a scale 
has been considered ranging from negligible, when it 
does not affect the normal functioning of the business, 
to extreme, which would involve a total or substantial 
loss of the operation. In no case does the potential level 
of risk obtained over five years exceed the materiality 
threshold, in other words., there are no situations that 
could affect the normal operation of the business, that 
would imply an impact or operational change, or a 
partial loss of the operation or prolonged closure. 

For impact, risk and opportunities management, the 
Company undertakes commitments in its Environmental 
Policy and takes actions.

The Company is prepared for the most adverse 
scenarios thanks to its Strategic Plan, the 
decarbonization roadmap and the actions it carries out 
to manage its biodiversity- and ecosystem-related 
dependencies, impacts, risks, and opportunities.

1 Taskforce on Nature-related Financial Disclosures (TNFD) Recommendations and Guidance on scenario analysis

2.4.2 Impact, risk and opportunity 
management

Description of the processes to identify and assess 
biodiversity- and ecosystem-related impacts, risks, and 
opportunities (ESRS 2 IRO-1)

Repsol has carried out a double materiality assessment, 
incorporating the views of its stakeholders, to assess its 
short-, medium-, and long-term biodiversity and 
ecosystem-related impacts, risks, and opportunities 
(IROs). 

Environmental experts from each business area have 
participated in this assessment and the Company’s 
operated sites and businesses have been examined, with 
other own operations left out of scope. IRO-related 
metrics have been taken into account based on 
information from the sites and businesses.

The following material negative impacts have been 
identified in relation to biodiversity and ecosystems, 
which are associated with the operations of the 
Industrial and LCG businesses:

• Temporary loss of ecosystems and the services they 
provide as a result of the modification of the terrain 
and/or waterbeds, and the physical presence resulting 
from Repsol’s activities that affect a sensitive area, 
which may alter local flora and fauna (IRO-19).

• Alteration of the presence and population size of 

species due to the Company’s activities (IRO-21).

The material positive impact identified is as follows:

•

Improvement of the conservation status of natural 
habitats and species populations, including those 
threatened, through the implementation of specific 
projects and nature-based solutions (NBSs) in the 
areas where the Company operates (IRO-20).

In addition to these material IROs, others have been 
identified that are related to other drivers of 
biodiversity loss (direct exploitation or climate change, 
for example) that can be consulted in climate change 
impact, risk and opportunity management and in water 
and marine resources impact, risk and opportunities 
management. The double materiality assessment does 
not identify material negative impacts related to land 
deterioration, desertification or soil sealing.

LEAP Analysis

Complementary to the double materiality assessment, a 
deeper understanding and identification has been 
achieved of the impacts and dependencies that result in 
different types of risks and opportunities for the 
Company with respect to nature, following TNFD’s LEAP 
approach. For more details, see Methodology for 
biodiversity and ecosystems reporting and metrics.

The LEAP analysis identifies sites with a material impact 
on biodiversity and ecosystems, and those that affect 
biodiversity-sensitive areas because they are located in 
or near them, as shown in the following table:

The table above shows the main habitats2 (according to 
the IUCN3 classification scheme) where the Company’s 
sites with material impacts on biodiversity are located 
and an assessment of their ecological status. To 
determine the latter, the Mean Species Abundance 
(MSA) has been used as an indicator, as modelled by 
GLOBIO4 and available on its website. Its maximum 
value is calculated in the environment of each operation 
to which a semi-quantitative level has been attributed 
(Very low: <0,1; Low: 0.1-0.2; Moderate: 0.2-0.5; High: 
0.5-0.7; Very high: 0,7-1). The MSA is an indicator of the 
integrity of local biodiversity and represents the degree 
of alteration in the composition of species. 

Repsol also accounts for the conservation and 
protection of biodiversity in the management of its own 
projects and operations by applying the mitigation 
hierarchy (avoid, minimize, restore, and compensate) 
during projects’ life cycle, especially in those located in 
protected areas. Examples of these measures are 
reported in Actions and resources relating to 
biodiversity and ecosystems.

Repsol analyzes the potential presence of threatened 
species included in the Red List of the International 
Union for Conservation of Nature in the area of 
influence of its facilities using the Integrated 
Biodiversity Assessment Tool (IBAT) and has concluded 
that its operations may affect them. The results of this 
analysis are made public on the corporate website. To 
minimize and compensate for this impact, the Company 
implements different management measures which are 
also available in the actions and resources section.

Policies related to biodiversity and ecosystems (E4-2)

Repsol has established commitments in its 
Environmental Policy that govern its actions in all the 
places where it operates, throughout the value chain, as 
well as in the products and services it offers. These 
commitments give rise to the necessary actions to 
manage the impacts, dependencies, risks and material 
environmental opportunities related to biodiversity and 
ecosystems.

The Policy includes the following commitments that are 
related to material IROs of alteration of the presence 
and population size of species and temporary loss of 
terrestrial ecosystems and the services they provide as a 
result of the modification of terrain and/or waterbeds:

• The sustainable use of resources.

• The protection and conservation of biodiversity, 
ecosystems and associated ecosystem services.

• The management of the impacts and dependencies on 
the above and the communities and stakeholders that 
may be affected.

• Special attention to impacts that take place within or 

near protected areas.

• Consideration of the different drivers of biodiversity 

loss, such as climate change, land/freshwater/sea use 
change, direct exploitation, the introduction of 
invasive species and pollution.

Therefore, the contribution to the direct drivers of 
biodiversity loss, impacts on the status of species, 
impacts on the extent and state of ecosystems, and 
impacts and dependencies on ecosystem services have 
been considered when establishing the commitments of 
the Policy and the Company’s management must take 
them into account when they are material. In this way, 
Repsol seeks to contribute to goals 1, 4, 6, 7, 8 and 15 of 
the Global Biodiversity Framework (GBF), given the 
importance that Repsol attributes to this framework.

The Policy prioritizes the application of the mitigation 
hierarchy, which entails the use of ecosystem 
restoration as a practice to manage deforestation. 
Examples can be found in Actions and resources 
relating to biodiversity and ecosystems.

The same policy has also made commitments in relation 
to the sustainability of the oceans and seas and 
promotes the sustainable sourcing and use of resources, 
although it does not explicitly address production, 
procurement or consumption from ecosystems 
managed to maintain or improve conditions for 
biodiversity, since most of the input resources to the 
Company are technical materials. For more details, see 
Resource use and circular economy.

The policy also promotes and requires environmental 
commitments in the value chain that are in line with 
Repsol’s own commitments, including partners, 
suppliers, contractors, distributors and other business 
relationships. 

The consequences of impacts related to biodiversity and 
ecosystems on affected communities are addressed 
following the guidelines established in the Human 
Rights and Community Relations Policy.

Actions and resources related to biodiversity and 
ecosystems (E4-3) 

The following tables include the actions undertaken and 
planned by Repsol related to biodiversity and 
ecosystems. Specifically, those associated with the 
biodiversity loss due to land, sea, or freshwater use 
change, or the alteration of the presence and population 
size of species are included. 	Actions related to activities 
whose impact on biodiversity is due to other drivers are 
reported in Climate change and Water and marine 
resources.

The implementation of these actions contributes to the 
achievement of the commitments undertaken in the 
Environmental Policy, specifically to the protection and 
conservation of biodiversity, ecosystems and associated 
ecosystem services and the management of impacts and 
dependencies on them.

Specifically, actions 2.1 to 2.8 contribute to the 
objective of improving the conservation status of 
endangered species through the financing, 
implementation and monitoring of specific projects.

2 This represents a change in the methodology used in 2024, which was based on local expertise and knowledge for assigning a biome, whereas in 

the 2025 report, the source of information has a greater degree of detail based on the cartography, with a recognized scientific basis. (Jung et al., 
2020. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41597-020-00599-8 ) and an internationally accepted taxonomy (IUCN 2012).

Ecosystem restoration and reforestation actions

Description of the action

Scope

1.- Voluntary (1.1 to 1.4) and 
mandatory (1.5) ecosystem 
restoration and reforestation 
actions

Industrial centers of Petronor, Spain, 
(1.3) and La Pampilla, Peru, (1.4), and 
the E&P business units of Mexico 
(1.1), Indonesia (1.2) and Marcellus 
(1.5)

action

Recovery of 
degraded 
ecosystems and 
planting of forest 
stands

Main actions in 2025:

1.1.– Restoration of an interior mangrove in the San 
Pedro River (Tabasco, Mexico)- Phase 3. Support to 
community nurseries for the production and 
establishment of mangrove species in the Wanha 
Biosphere Reserve in Tabasco. Community participation 
has been essential for implementation. About 
10,000 plants of various mangrove species have been 
produced and established, and 20 km of riverbanks 
within the reserve have been cleaned and restored 
(2025-2026).

1.2.– Planting trees in Indonesia. It is an initiative that 
is part of the community development program of 
the Sakakemang block. This program seeks to increase 
vegetation cover, improve carbon absorption, water 
harvesting capacity, reduce soil erosion and flood risk, 
and strengthen the participation of the local community 
in environmental protection. In 2025, about 
10,000 trees have been planted in the village of 
Tampang Baru (South Sumatra). Species planted 
include mango, mahogany, sengon, jackfruit, and 
avocado (2025).

Compensation actions

Description of the action

Scope

2.- Voluntary compensation 
measures (2.4) or linked to 
environmental impact 
statements (other actions)

Activities carried out by the Low-
Carbon Generation business, with a 
focus on different solar and wind 
energy projects and their associated 
districts with the integration of all 
local stakeholders (neighbors, 
associations and administrations) 

Main actions in 2025:

2.1.– Recovery of necrophagous birds in the region of 
Cariñena (Aragon, Spain). In 2022, in collaboration with 
the NGO Acobija, a five-year R&D project began for the 
restoration of the natural food habitat of necrophagous 
species and the improvement of the ecological 
connectivity of the biological corridor of the Iberian 
System. The target species are the red kite and the 
Egyptian vulture. Among the activities carried out to 
date, the creation of an Extra Feeding Point (EFP), the 
planting of native vegetation to provide camouflage to 
the observation huts, and the installation of perches in 
the EFP to promote the presence of the species 
targeted by the project stand out. It is a compensation 
measure aligned with the Environmental Impact 
Declaration, and among the indicators used are the total 
feed contributions made and feed consumed or species 
sightings (2022-2026).

1.3.– Reforestation of the Monte Mello area devastated 
by the fires that occurred in 2019 in Muskiz (Basque 
Country, Spain). Since 2021, Petronor has been 
supporting the reforestation of the area affected by the 
fire, the origin of which is not related to the refinery’s 
activity. In this fourth year, about three hectares have 
been recovered and approximately 1,000 native trees 
planted thanks to the participation of students from the 
municipality’s schools (the total number of hectares 
restored and trees planted is 7.5 and 3,500, 
respectively) (2021-2025).

1.4.– Sembrando Bosques program. Development of 
forest ecosystems in areas surrounding the La Pampilla 
refinery (Peru). In partnership with the Municipality of 
Ventanilla and a local NGO, more than 5,000 trees of 
different species have been planted with the aim of 
forming two urban forests in that municipality 
(2024-2025).

1.5.– Active ecosystem restoration activities in Marcellus 
(USA). Restoration by 2025 of a total of 9 hectares of 
well and pipeline land linked to construction activities, 
or the decommissioning of operations at the Marcellus 
unconventional production asset, USA (2025).

Implementation 
status

Time 
horizons

See the detail 
in each action

Improvement of the 
condition of the local 
populations of species that 
are the focus of the 
activities and/or their 
habitats in the regional 
environments where the 
Company operates

2.2.–  Reinforcement of the Bonelli’s eagle in the Sierra 
de Guara (Aragon, Spain). In collaboration with the NGO 
GREFA and the regional administration, the project 
includes the release of 25 Bonelli’s eagle chicks. At the 
end of the third year of implementation, it has been 
verified that the releases have worked and that a 
survival rate close to 60% is maintained. The main 
indicators of the project come from the monitoring of 
individuals by radio tagging. During this year, the 
settling of two potentially breeding pairs in the territory 
may be completed. The capture and marking of several 
wild specimens has provided important data for the 
monitoring and conservation of the species. This action 
is part of the plan of additional measures defined in the 
Environmental Impact Statements of the wind farms 
associated with the Delta I project (2022-2026).

2.3.– Stewardship of steppe bird territory (Spain). 
Management of agricultural areas for the 
implementation of agroforestry measures to strengthen 
the habitat of steppe birds (black-bellied sandgrouse, 
little bustard, bustard, etc.). These actions are part of 
the plan of additional measures defined in 
Environmental Impact Statements for the Company’s 
various photovoltaic parks in Spain. Indicators related to 
population control and the use of space of the identified 
birds are monitored. During 2025, through this 
management modality, these management measures 
have been established in more than 190 new hectares 
compared to 2024, when the action affected 
348 hectares (2021-2046).

2.4.– LIFE Red Kite Project (Spain). The aim is to 
determine the influence of wind energy production on 
the population dynamics of this endangered bird with 
the aim of improving impact mitigation measures. This is 
a voluntary compensation action. In 2025, the 
methodological and fieldwork part has been completed, 
so that during 2026 the follow-up will be carried out for 
the purpose of obtaining conclusions and publishing the 
study (2024-2026).

2.5.– Los Berciales Crane Reserve (Spain). Actions on the 
Los Berciales farm, which is owned by ADENEX, for the 
protection of the common crane. In 2025, the control, 
monitoring and maintenance of the holm oaks planted in 
previous years was carried out. Throughout the year, 
several education and awareness days were held on the 
crane, with the aim of involving local schools and the 
population of nearby municipalities (2022-20265).

2.6.– Support service for the collection of birds 
(Guadalajara, Spain). Implementation, in coordination 
with the competent administrations, of a support 
service for the transfer of injured threatened fauna to 
the recovery center in Guadalajara. During the year 
2025, the process has been initiated. This is a 
compensation measure aligned with the Environmental 
Impact Statement of the Trillo project (2025-2045).

2.7.– Creation of a colony of lesser kestrel using the 
colony environment method and applying new release 
techniques (Spain). This release system generates an 
environment that imitates that of a wild colony, favoring 
the presence of individuals. During the year 2025, the 
construction of the colony has been carried out to enable 
the start of the consolidation campaign through the use 
of foster birds in the coming years. This action is part of 
the measures proposed in the Environmental Impact 
Statement of the Escatrón project (2025-2031).

2.8.– Support for the campaign to mark and monitor the 
little bustard in the province of Cádiz (Spain). This action 
is part of the measures proposed in the Environmental 
Impact Statement of the SIGMA project, which aims to 
monitor and sample this species, using GPS monitoring 
systems, to correctly characterize its population. All 
local stakeholders (neighbors, associations and 
administrations) are part of the project (2025-2027).

2.9.– Pasture management by sheep in photovoltaic 
plants (Spain). Promotion of the natural recovery of the 
soil with respect to previous intensive agricultural use. 
This is an action that is part of the plan of additional 
measures defined in the Environmental Impact 
Statements of different photovoltaic parks of the 
Company in Spain (since 2022 and throughout the 
operation of the photovoltaic plants).

Improvement of knowledge

Main actions in 2025:

3.1.– Publication of the book Mammals of the Lower 
Urubamba (Peru). A compilation of a total of 153 species 
of mammals in the vicinity of Block 57, the result of 
more than 10 years of research work to understand their 
behavior and their impact on the local ecosystem and 
native communities. As a result of these assessments, an 
endemic primate species was discovered: the Urubamba 
titi monkey (Callicebus urubambensis) (2024-20256).

3.2.– Generation of knowledge on population dynamics 
and the abundance of terrestrial micro-mammals 
(Bolivia). Study of the diversity, structure and 
composition of terrestrial micromammal communities 
(rodents and didelphids) in the Caipipendi area and 
preparation of technical sheets, including information

on their ecological roles and current threats. The results 
of this study will be published during the first quarter of 
2026 (2025).

3.3.– Study of shad populations and fishery resources in 
the Caipipendi area (Bolivia). Analysis of the ecological 
roles and conservation of fishery species in the 
Caipipendi area with emphasis on the shad present in 
the Pilcomayo River. The results of this study will be 
published in the first quarter of 2026 (2025).

planning based on previous results and annual actions.
6 The time horizon has been extended to 2025 due to an update in the project planning and the final publication of the book.

3.4.– Biological monitoring and replanting of the 
Kinteroni and Sagari projects (Peru). Biological 
monitoring campaigns of vegetation, birds, beetles, 
bats, large mammals, amphibians, reptiles and aquatic 
fauna. In addition, Lepidoptera are evaluated in 
Kinteroni, while epiphytic plants, ants, and rodents are 
included in the monitoring activities in Sagari. The 
information collected is used to assess the effectiveness 
of the mitigation measures defined. These activities 
have been carried out since 2012 in Kinteroni and since 
2017 in Sagari, with continuity in both cases until 2040 
(2012-2040).

3.5.– KnowSandeel 2.0 and 3.0 Project (Norway). R&D 
project in collaboration with the IMR (Institute of Marine 
Research), among other entities and operators, which 
aims to determine how sensitive the larva of sandeel 
(tobis in Norwegian) is to exposure to oil. By 2025, a 
protocol for the production of eggs, larvae and juveniles 
was prepared, exposure experiments conducted, 
manuscript on oil sensitivity in early larvae prepared and 
behavioral studies completed. Individuals were  
collected for genetic and microchemical studies and a 
scientific paper has been published (Komiyama et al., 
2025) on the use of drones to investigate the natural 
behavior of sand lance banks (2023-20267).

Other biodiversity and ecosystem management actions

Description of the action

Scope

4.- Other biodiversity management actions 
other than restorations, reforestations, 
compensation activities, or mandatory (4.3.) or 
voluntary dissemination of knowledge (other 
actions)

Actions carried out by several 
industrial complexes and 
hydroelectric power plants in 
Spain (see detail)

status

result

See detail in

Main actions in 2025:

4.1.– Control of invasive alien species (Galicia, Spain). 
Initiative of the La Coruña refinery with the University of 
La Coruña for the control of populations of the invasive 
Helichrysum petiolare species in the Costa de Dexo 
natural monument through parcel division, controlled 
eradication, work with volunteers, etc. (2024-2025). 

4.2.–  Collaboration agreement with the Association for 
the Recovery of the Native Forest (ARBA) (Murcia, 
Spain). Various actions organized by the Cartagena 
industrial complex to regenerate the natural 
environment in the vicinity of the Portman lighthouse 
and nearby areas (western slope, Lastre beach, etc.). The 
agreement was signed in 2025 and its continuity will be 
evaluated on a year-by-year basis (2025).

4.3.– Ichthyological safeguarding in hydroelectric power 
plants (Asturias and Castilla y León, Spain). Rescue 
operations for fish fauna in the canals of the 
hydroelectric power plants of Arenas de Cabrales and 
Camarmeña for their subsequent release into the river 
channel to maintain fish populations upstream of the 
facilities. The activity has been carried out annually since 
2017 prior to the maintenance campaigns for the 
diversion channels and will continue throughout the 
operational period (2017- onwards).

4.4.– Assessment of the ecological status of surface 
water bodies in hydroelectric power plants (Spain). 
Determination of the ecological status/potential of 
reservoirs and rivers associated with hydroelectric 
power plants through the analysis of biological, 
physicochemical, and hydromorphological quality 
elements. This is a voluntary measure that has been 
implemented annually since 2015 and that allows for 
the monitoring of the state of rivers and reservoirs in 
order to prevent potential impacts on ecosystems 
directly related to the activities (2015 – onwards).

4.5.– Technological and environmental renovation in 
hydraulic power plants in Cantabria and Asturias. A 
series of environmental improvements are planned for 
the plants in La Paraya (2025-2026), Urdón and Torina 
(2025-2029) that consist of the covering of the 
Llananzanes canal (La Paraya), and the Urdón and Torina 
canals; and the construction of dam crossing elements 
(ramps in the Lorria and Llananzanes dams, fish ladder in 
the Rioaller dam) in La Paraya and escape ramps for 
animals in the three diversion channels and loading 
chamber at this same plant.

None of the actions included in the above tables require 
significant capital expenditures (capex) or operating 
expenses (opex) according to  the financial materiality 
criteria adopted by the Company.

Actions 1.1 and 2.1 to 2.9 in the tables above are 
compensations that have involved operating expenses 
of 465,775 euros. On the other hand, actions 1.2, 1.3, 
2.1, 2.2, 3.1 and 3.4 are examples of interaction and 
integration of local stakeholders in their planning.

7 The final time horizon has been modified from 2027 to 2026 due to the update of the project planning and the Company’s participation in the 

project.

2.4.3 Metrics and targets

Targets related to biodiversity and ecosystems (E4-4)

By 2025, Repsol has voluntarily established two 
measurable targets8 related to biodiversity and 
ecosystems to meet its material IROs. These objectives 
are aligned with the commitments established in the 
Environmental Policy to protect and conserve 
biodiversity, ecosystems and 

Biodiversity and ecosystem-related objectives

the associated ecosystem services, managing the 
impacts and dependencies on them and the 
communities and stakeholders that may be affected 
throughout the life cycle of operations, products and 
services, with special attention focused on impacts that 
take place within or near protected areas.

developed previously.

Further details on the targets described:

1.– Improve the conservation status of endangered 
species. 

The Company undertakes conservation projects to 
minimize, restore and compensate for the magnitude of 
possible direct impacts on threatened species, 
improving their populations and habitats. This objective, 
as part of the Company’s voluntary goals, supports the 
Global Biodiversity Framework (objective 4), which seeks 
to reduce the risk of extinction of threatened species 
through their recovery and conservation, although it 
does not entail external validation. No ecological 
thresholds have been applied when setting the target, 
the first year of which will be 2026. 

This objective applies to its own operations, with priority 
given to those that contribute significantly to the 
material impacts identified in Impact, risk and 
opportunity management. Its compliance will be 
measured with an absolute value of the number of 
threatened species with documented improvement in 
their conservation status. The improvement in 
conservation will be linked to parameters related to the 
increase in the local or regional population of a species, 
the improvement of the quality or surface area of the 
habitat available for it, etc. 

To determine whether a species is threatened or not, the 
categorization made by IUCN in its Red List, as well as 
regional or national threat criteria, will be followed. No 
intermediate milestones have been established for this 
objective, and the year 2020 has been set as baseline.

2.– Adopt action plans for biodiversity in assets located 
in sensitive areas for biodiversity.

Beyond the commitment defined by the Company years 
ago to observe voluntary exclusion areas9 , Repsol has 
established a relative (%) and continuous target in 2025 
related to the management of impacts on biodiversity 
and ecosystems in sensitive areas.

The Company believes that it avoids, minimizes, restores 
and compensates for the magnitude of the possible 
impacts on biodiversity and ecosystems through the 
actions associated with this target. Repsol will consider 
as equivalent plans different biodiversity impact 
management measures such as monitoring, surveillance 
plans, the implementation of complementary impact 
measures, etc.

8 The defined objectives are not based on conclusive scientific evidence and stakeholders have not been involved in their development.
9 Information on biodiversity protection can be found on the Repsol website, following this path: Home > Sustainability > Sustainability Pillars > 

Environment > Biodiversity.

This target is associated with various management 
measures and is aligned with objectives 1, 7 and 21 of 
the Global Biodiversity Framework10. No ecological 
thresholds have been applied in setting the target.

This objective is applicable to the Company’s own 
operations, with priority given to those that contribute 
significantly to the material impacts identified in Impact, 
risk and opportunity management. It has been defined 
on a voluntary basis, including not only legally 
protected areas, but also Key Biodiversity Areas (KBAs), 
many without legal protection. Compliance will be 
assessed by identifying assets located in sensitive or 
protected areas and verifying whether they have a 
Biodiversity Action Plan or equivalent plan. 

No intermediate milestones have been established for 
this objective and the base year is established annually.

To define this objective, the methodology used is that 
recommended by the guide elaborated by IPIECA and 
IOGP entitled A guide to developing biodiversity action 
plans (2022).

Metrics11 related to biodiversity and ecosystems (E4-5)

Repsol participates in the Proteus consortium, for which 
the UN Environment Program World Conservation 
Monitoring Center (UNEP-WCMC) makes available to 
participating companies information related to the 
potential distribution of species included in the IUCN Red 
List and to the protected natural spaces and areas 
included in the World Database of Protected Areas 
(WDPA) and Key Biodiversity Areas (KBAs).

This information is obtained and analyzed with the 
Integrated Biodiversity Assessment Tool (IBAT), which is 
recognized as a source of quality and reliable data, and is 
used by Repsol for decision making.

From the table above, it can be seen that the total area 
of sites located next to or within biodiversity-sensitive 
areas during 2025 amounts to 206,936 hectares. The 
change compared to the previous year 
(429,155 hectares) is mainly due to variations in the 
number of the Company’s own operations (several exits 
in Upstream, and the commissioning of new areas in 
Wind Generation), and to changes in methodology due 
to the availability of more detailed information on 
unconventional assets in the United States12.

The total altered area (understood as that which has 
been subject to human activity that has modified its 
status compared to an initial reference status) of the 
Company’s sites and businesses that have been operated 
in 2025  (see details of the scope and calculation of the 
metric in Methodology for biodiversity and ecosystems 
reporting and metrics) is 39,086 hectares (28,796 in 
2024). This increase is due to the commissioning of new 
renewables areas in 2025. In addition, restoration and 
compensation actions have been carried out on a total 
of 778 hectares (594 in 2024) of restored or 
rehabilitated area (actions 1.1, 1.3, 1.5 and 2.3 of the 
tables of Actions and resources related to biodiversity 
and ecosystems). This table also includes compensation 
actions other than the restoration or recovery of 
habitats and ecosystems.

information on surface areas of each of the individual concession blocks making up to total in each county.

M

METHODOLOGY

Methodology for biodiversity and 
ecosystems reporting and metrics

LEAP approach analysis

The LEAP analysis takes into account the Company’s own 
hydrocarbon production operations, refineries, chemical plants, 
LPG factories, specialty, asphalt and lubricant plants, 
hydroelectric and combined cycle plants, renewable energy 
facilities (solar and wind), service stations and office buildings. 
In this LEAP analysis, it has not been necessary to carry out 
consultations with potentially affected communities.

The first component of the LEAP assessment (L for Locate ) 
involves creating a list of assets located in sensitive areas or 
with potentially material impacts or dependencies. Each of the 
activities has been evaluated using tools such as ENCORE13, and 
guides such as those published by the World Business Council 
for Sustainable Development14, which collect general 
information by type of activity. 

Likewise, the activities have been analyzed according to their 
location, based on geo-referenced information, identifying 
biomes of importance for biodiversity and interaction with 
sensitive areas for nature. According to TNFD, a site is sensitive 
to nature if it impacts on: 

• Areas of importance for biodiversity.

• Areas of high ecosystem integrity. 

• Areas with a rapid decline in this integrity. 

• Areas with a high water risk. 

• Areas of importance for the provision of ecosystem services, 
including benefits to local and indigenous communities and 
other stakeholders.

A conservative approach has been followed by taking into 
account areas of influence recommended by a third party in the 
determination of sensitive areas.

The second evaluation component of the LEAP approach (E for 
Evaluate) allows for the measurement of dependencies and 
impacts on biodiversity and ecosystems. 

The ENCORE tool  has been used for the dependencies, 
attributing a semi-quantitative level to them for each 
ecosystem service. 

For impacts, the Company has developed the Reads15 tool, 
which can be used to assess the environmental impacts of 
projects and activities during the construction, operation and 
decommissioning stages.

Reads enables users to understand the effects of a project or 
activity on climate, water resources, ecosystem services and 
social well-being. Based on these assessments, Reads calculates 
an indicator of impact on biodiversity taking into account the 
different drivers of biodiversity loss: land use change, 
greenhouse gas emissions, direct exploitation of natural 
resources, and pollution. In addition, it can go beyond sectoral 
averages by incorporating site-level data to assess 
environmental impacts.

In recognition of the importance of the Global Biodiversity 
Framework (GBF), Repsol seeks to contribute to the GBF’s 2030 
and 2050 goals through its efforts. Thus, for example, the 
Company includes in this report (see table of Sites with a 
material impact on biodiversity and ecosystems) the impacts 
and dependencies on biodiversity and ecosystems, in line with 
Target 15 of the Global Biodiversity Framework.

Finally, the third evaluation component of the LEAP approach 
(A stands for Assess) aims to identify and assess nature-related 
risks and opportunities. An identification of the typology of risks 
collected by TNFD has been carried out, which includes physical 
risks, related to dependencies, and transition risks, associated 
with the impacts. It has been determined that there are no 
systemic nature-related risks affecting the Company.

The Company has been making progress over the past two 
years in the application of LEAP analysis, and intends to 
continue advancing and make it more robust year by year. To 
this end, throughout this year, progress has been made in the 
number and types of assets modelled in Reads, as well as in the 
frequency of updating the data to have the most up-to-date 
quantification of impacts possible; the dependency analysis has 
been updated with the new version of ENCORE; the 
measurement of the main habitats where the Company's sites 
are located following the IUCN classification scheme has been 
incorporated; etc. Despite the continuous effort to improve this 
approach, the analysis does have limitations, or challenges. For 
example, ecosystem condition data are not available for 
offshore sites. Although progress has been made in the last 
year, polygonal geographic information is not available for all 
sites, but only point information. So manual and visual data 
curation is required.  

13 Exploring Natural Capital Opportunities, Risks and Exposure. For more information: https://encorenature.org/en
14 Roadmap to Nature Positive: Foundations for the energy system, 2023
15  Information on this methodology can be found on Repsol’s corporate website, following this path: Home > Sustainability > Sustainability pillars 

> Environment > Reads.

M

METHODOLOGY

Sites located in or adjacent 
to biodiversity-sensitive areas

In relation to the table that contains information relating to the 
sites located within biodiversity-sensitive areas or adjacent to 
them for the Company’s own operations, it should be noted that 
the areas of the hydrocarbon production sites correspond to 
the geo-mining domain concession blocks, regardless of the 
exact location of the operation within said area. The areas for 
photovoltaic and wind power plants comprise the entire 
perimeter of these sites. Other areas have been determined 
from official sources, such as the cadastral registry, and, only in 
isolated cases, digital measurement has been used on aerial 
photography (for example, fuel distribution facilities in 
seaports) with the limitation of precision of this approximation. 
It should be emphasized that the location of sites with respect 
to sensitive areas has been visually checked, and digitally 
documented, except for service stations, where conservative 
assumptions of size have been made (assigning a larger size to 
each site, specifically the cadastral plot) and an automatic 
relative location assignment has been applied, which may be 
subject to some error, but is insignificant in comparison to the 
total area recorded for the other activities in the table.

For calculation of the total altered area (or disturbed area 
according to TNFD nomenclature) of the Company’s sites and 
businesses, account has been taken of the area occupied by the 
assets where there is a limitation on carrying out other types of 
activities, as well as possible exclusion zones for other activities 
in the vicinity of the facilities such as, for example, fishing 
exclusion zones around offshore platforms. The metric is 
applied to sites belonging to businesses whose main driver of 
impact on biodiversity is land use change, change in freshwater 
use, or change in sea use, and facilities with a different driver of 
impact on biodiversity from the aforementioned, but that 
occupy a significant area.

Estimates of E&P’s non-operated Joint Operating 
Agreement spatial footprint

The spatial footprint of non-operated Joint Operating 
Agreements of E&P has been estimated based on the Hectares/
Production ratio calculated for the operated assets. The most 
appropriate value of the ratio has been used for the calculation 
according to the geographical location, the facility type 
(onshore/offshore and conventional/unconventional), and the 
production stage of the non-operated Joint Operating 
Agreements. For smaller operations located in Spain, actual 
data from the cadastral registry were used. The estimated 
spatial footprint for non-operated Joint Operating Agreements 
would account for 4.15% of the total owned operations, so they 
have been left out of scope due to their own weight in the total 
value of the metric, and uncertainty as to the estimation.
