2.5 Resource use and 
circular economy

2.5.1 Impact, risk and opportunity 
management

Description of the processes to identify and assess 
material impacts, risks, and opportunities related to 
resource use and the circular economy (ESRS 2 IRO-1)

Repsol has carried out a double materiality assessment, 
incorporating the opinion of its stakeholders, to 
determine the material impacts, risks, and opportunities 
(IROs) related to the input of resources used in its 
activities and the manufacture of products, as well as 
for the waste streams it generates, in the short, medium 
and long term.

In carrying out this analysis, the Company’s sites and 
businesses that are operated have been examined, while 
other own operations are out of the scope. 

This information makes it possible to determine that the 
contribution to the material IROs occurs in the 
Upstream and Industrial businesses, in which:

• There is an influx of raw materials in the form of 

mineral hydrocarbons. 

• The mineral hydrocarbons used can be replaced by 

other raw materials of renewable origin or derived 
from waste.

Likewise, the categories of products manufactured by 
Repsol have been reviewed, with the finding that some 
of those produced by the Chemicals and Customer 
businesses are related to the material IROs, as detailed 
in Product and material outflows (E5-5). 

In this analysis, it has not been necessary to carry out 
consultations with potentially affected communities.

The following material IROs have been identified in the 
field of resource use and circular economy:

• As a positive impact, the development of circular or 

bio-based products and renewable fuels from 
renewable and recycled secondary raw materials, as it 
reduces the adverse effects associated with the 
disposal of waste generated by society (IRO-22).

• As a negative impact, the extraction of non-renewable 

resources from nature, which contributes to the 
reduction of the world’s reserves of them (IRO-23).

• As a negative impact, the offering of products made 
with non-renewable nor recycled materials (IRO-24).

In addition to the double materiality assessment, own 
operations have been identified in which non-renewable 
resources are used to a greater extent and in which 
there are products and services for which circular 
economy criteria can be applied. To this end, the 
quantities and types of resources that enter the 
activities and the circularity characteristics of the 
different families of manufactured products have been 
analyzed.

Policies related to resource use and the circular economy 
(E5-1)

Repsol has established commitments in its 
Environmental Policy that govern its actions in all its 
activities and places where it operates, throughout the 
value chain, as well as in the products and services it 
offers. The necessary actions to manage the material 
environmental impacts related to the use of resources 
and the circular economy arise from these goals.

In particular, Repsol is focused on integrating the 
principles of the circular economy into its processes, 
products, and services, optimizing the use of resources 
and raw materials, including energy and water; fostering 
the use of alternative and renewable resources obtained 
in a circular manner; and promoting the development of 
new products with less environmental impact. This goals 
relates to the material IROs mentioned above. In 
addition, it is aligned with the industrial transformation 
that Repsol is carrying out and could imply a relative 
reduction in the use of virgin and non-renewable 
resources, thanks to the fact that a portion1 of the 
demand for Repsol’s products will be met with products 
manufactured from renewable and/or non-virgin raw 
materials.

The policy also promotes and requires environmental 
commitments in the value chain in line with the 
Company’s own, including partners, suppliers, 
contractors, distributors and other business 
relationships. 

Actions and resources related to resource use and the 
circular economy (E5-2)

The following tables provide information on the actions 
taken and planned by Repsol related to the use of 
resources and the circular economy.

Its implementation contributes to the achievement of 
the commitments established in the Environmental 
Policy, specifically to the integration of the principles of 
the circular economy in its processes, products and 
services, the optimization of the use of resources and 
raw materials, the use of alternative and renewable 
resources obtained in a sustainable way; and the 
development of new products with less impact on the 
environment. 

Specifically, actions 1.1 and 1.2 contribute to the target 
of reaching a production volume of between 125,000 
and 146,000 tons by 2030 of circular or bio-based 
products that incorporate alternative raw materials such 
as waste plastic or other renewable materials. Actions 
2.1, 2.2 and 2.3 have a positive impact on meeting the 
target of reaching a renewable fuel production capacity 
in the Iberian Peninsula of between 1.6 and 1.8 million 
tons by 2030. Lastly, action 3.1 supports the target of 
reaching a biomethane production capacity by 2030, 
from organic waste, of between 0.7 and 0.8 TWh.

1 With regard to the progressive advancement towards Repsol’s objectives reflected in Metrics and targets.

Promotion of the use of circular materials 
and recovery of resources for their 
reintegration into production processes

Main actions in 2025:

1.1.– Use of Reciclex®, a polymer from Repsol Materials, 
S. A., with 60% post-consumer recycled plastic, in the 
containers of lubricants in the Master range and in the 1, 
4 and 5-liter containers of Repsol lubricants. These 
containers are purchased from regular suppliers who, in 
turn, buy the Reciclex® from Repsol Materials, S. A. Since 
2024, this action is carried out on a continuous basis. 

1.2.– Recovery of used cooking oil for biofuel production 
at service stations in Spain (ESOPO project). In 2024, the 
Customer business launched the project to collect used 
cooking oil at service stations in the Autonomous 
Communities of Madrid, Castile-La Mancha and Galicia. 
During 2025, the service has been extended to Aragon 
and Castile and León in line with the scaling plan 
(2024-2030).

In 2025, progress has been made in construction, with 
significant milestones such as the installation of the new 
reactor and the modification of auxiliary services to 
integrate it into the production scheme. The plant is 
expected to be operational in 2026 (2024-2026). 

2.2.– Construction of a synthetic fuel (renewable fuel) 
demo plant in the Port of Bilbao (Spain), with the 
participation of Saudi Aramco as a partner, with a 
production capacity of 2,000 tons/year from the capture 
of CO2 emissions and renewable hydrogen. By 2025, 
construction has progressed by more than 90% and the 
plant is expected to commence operations in 2026 
(2024-2026). 

Biogas production and marketing actions

2.3.–Approval of the investment in the Ecoplant, the 
first European plant capable of producing renewable 
methanol to transform urban waste into renewable fuels 
and circular products using gasification technology 
developed by Enerkem Inc., a company in which Repsol 
holds a majority stake. The new plant, located in 
Tarragona (Spain), will have a total production capacity 
of 240,000 tons per year of renewable methanol and 
circular products and is expected to reduce CO2eq 
emissions by 3.4 million tons in its first ten years of 
operation. In 2025, its investment has been approved 
and the plant is scheduled to commence operations in 
2029 (2025-2029).

4.1.– Construction and commissioning of a waste recovery 
demo plant to manufacture eco-aggregates, a circular 
construction material, at the Petronor industrial complex

the incineration of municipal solid waste. This plant will

captured CO2, with a production capacity of 56,000 tons/
year of eco-aggregates (calcium carbonate). In 2025, 
construction has begun and the plant is expected to be 
commissioned in 2026.

2.5.2 Metrics and targets

Targets related to resource use and the circular economy 
(E5-3) 

In 2024, Repsol updated its Strategic Plan for 
2024-2027, defining several voluntary objectives2 to 
manage its impacts, risks, and material opportunities in 
line with the commitments set out in the Environmental 
Policy, namely: to integrate the principles of the circular 
economy into processes, products, and services; 
optimize the use of resources and raw materials; 
optimize the use of alternative and renewable resources 
obtained circularly; and develop new products with less 
impact on the environment.

These targets, which are of an absolute nature, are 
aligned with IRO-22, as described in Materiality 
assessment, and relate to both the inflow and outflow of

resources, encouraging the use of circular materials, the 
minimization of primary raw materials, and the circular 
sourcing and use of renewable resources. The calculation 
and tracking of these indicators is monitored through the 
project management tool of the Industrial business. Its 
achievement serves as a lever for the reduction of the 
Company’s CO2 emissions in all its scopes.

Repsol has reviewed the objectives and eliminated 
intermediate milestones to adapt them to the global 
energy context, changes in regulation and demand, and 
the pace of the energy transition, without ceasing to 
make progress in achieving the commitments in the 
Environmental Policy, while making investments that are 
profitable in the new environment. 

2 The defined targets are not based on conclusive scientific evidence and stakeholders have not been involved in their development.

(1) Own operations to the Industrial business in the Iberian Peninsula in which renewable fuels are produced.

Further details on the targets described:

1.– Reach a renewable fuel production capacity of 
between 1.6 and 1.8 million tons in the Iberian Peninsula 
by 2030.

Renewable fuels are produced from sustainable sourced 
waste raw materials or from the capture of CO2 
emissions. This target has been set with reference to a 
renewable fuel production capacity value of 1 million 
tons in 2023. It includes co-processing, ETBE, and 
renewable H2 as intermediates.

The objective originally defined in the 2024-2027 
Strategic Plan set the objective of achieving a 
production capacity of renewable fuels in the Iberian 
Peninsula of more than 2.2 million tons by 2030.

Progress toward this goal in 2025 is in line with initial 
planning and no significant changes in performance 
have been identified.

The production capacity of renewable fuels stood at 
1.25 million tons at the end of 2025 (1.2 million tons 
in 2024).

2.- Reach a production volume of between 125,000 and 
146,000 tons by 2030 of circular or biological origin 
products incorporating alternative raw materials such as 
waste plastic or other renewable materials.

Repsol’s circular or biological origin products include 
alternative raw materials, such as plastic waste or 
renewable raw materials, where renewable fuels and 
biomethane do not count toward this objective. This 
objective has been defined with respect to a reference 
value for the production of circular or biological origin 
products of 9,372 tons in 2023 and account is taken of 
changes in regulations, technology and markets 
associated with circular or biological origin products in 
the definition.

The objective originally defined in the 2024-2027 
Strategic Plan set the objective of achieving a 
production capacity of circular or biological origin 
products incorporating alternative raw materials such as 
waste plastic or other renewable materials of more than 
150,000 tons by 2030.

The target, which was initially communicated as circular 
or bio-based products, is adapted to the terminology in 
line with the current state of reference standards. These 
products are obtained through mechanical recycling, 
which includes a percentage of recycled material 
content certified by RecyClass; chemical recycling, 
which includes a percentage of recycled material 
content, allocated by mass balance and certified by 
ISCC Plus; or recycling of bio-based materials, which 
includes a percentage of renewable or bio material 
content, assigned by mass balance and certified by ISCC 
EU (Renewable Energy Directive 2018/2001).

In 2025, the production of circular and bio-based 
products increased by 106% compared to the previous 
year, thanks to the identification of opportunities in very 
specific voluntary markets. The strategy of the Company 
remains focused on growing in these markets, and 
regulatory support continues to be essential in order to 
develop them structurally and avoid exposure to 
decisions on voluntary goals in the end market.

The production of circular and biological-based 
products came to 25,137 tons at the end of 2025 
(12,227 tons in 2024).

3.- Reach a biomethane production capacity in 2030, 
from organic waste, of between 0.7 and 0.8 TWh.

Biomethane is a renewable alternative to natural gas 
that originates from organic matter, such as agricultural 
and livestock waste. In addition to its use as a substitute 
for conventional gas, it has other industrial applications, 
such as the production of renewable fuels and 
renewable hydrogen or the manufacture of chemical 
products, with an associated reduction in greenhouse 
gases. This target has been set against a biomethane 
production reference value of 0 TWh in 2023.

The target originally defined in the 2024-2027 Strategic 
Plan set the objective of achieving a production capacity 
of biomethane from organic waste of more than 
2.1 TWh by 2030.

For compliance assessment, progress is checked in 
accordance with the initial planning, with significant 
changes in performance identified. In 2024, Repsol 
acquired a 40% stake in Genia Bioenergy, including 19 of 
its biomethane plants and an additional 11 plant 
projects in an early stage of development. In 2025, 
progress was made with the processing of 
environmental permits for two plants in accordance with 
the initial planning to achieve the objective.

Resource inflows (E5-4)

Repsol’s activities are structured into four business 
segments which are reported under Business model and 
strategy. 

Resources used include hydrocarbons of mineral and 
biological origin, as well as other materials necessary for 
the manufacture of their products, such as steel, 
cement, chemicals. It also uses water captured from 
both natural sources of salt water (oceans and seas) and 
fresh water (surface and underground resources), as 
well as from other alternative sources of water (effluent 
from water treatment plants or effluent from crude oil 
and gas production processes).

Product and material outflows (E5-5)

The notable criteria that define the circularity capacity of 
products are their durability, reusability, repairability, 
ability to be disassembled, remanufactured, restored, 
reintroduced into natural biological cycles or their ability 
to optimize their consumption. These concepts in some 
cases are not applicable to energy products such as fuels, 
although they are applicable to other ranges of the 
Company’s products.

Below is the expected durability of Repsol’s product 
groups and how they compare with industry averages:

Expected durability of Repsol’s product groups, relative to industry averages 

Product group

Asphalt bitumens

Expected durability

The ranges of bituminous products for asphalt marketed by Repsol are manufactured in 
accordance with applicable regulations and their durability is at the industry average, with the 
exception of the Repsol Perform - Conventional Modified Bitumen range, which makes it 
possible to manufacture bituminous mixtures with greater mechanical and functional 
performance and adapt them to the increase in traffic and the greater demands derived from it.   
This results in greater durability, less need for the use of resources and savings in maintenance 
costs compared to conventional bitumen.

Specialties (products for specific 
applications in the rubber, automotive, 
asphalt, waxes and candles, 
agriculture, construction, packaging 
and propellants, among others

The ranges of bituminous products for asphalt commercialized by Repsol are manufactured in 
accordance with applicable regulations and their durability is in the industry average.
However, it must be considered that these products are intended for processing by third parties 
for the manufacture of final products that are intended for users. The life cycle of these final 
products is not related to the products supplied by Repsol.

Lubricants

The ranges of lubricant products commercialized by Repsol are manufactured in accordance 
with applicable regulations and their durability is at the industry average, with the exception of 
Repsol’s product ranges labeled "Long Life", which extend their service life compared to 
conventional lubricants.

Polymers and monomers derived from 
petrochemicals (styrenes, polyols, 
polyolefins, others)

The ranges of polymer and monomer products commercialized by Repsol are manufactured in 
accordance with applicable regulations and their durability is at the industry average. 
However, it must be considered that these products are intended for processing by third parties 
for the manufacture of final products and their lifespan is not related to the products supplied 
by Repsol.

Crude oil and gas production

The crude oil and gas produced by Repsol is manufactured in accordance with applicable 
regulations and its duration is at the industry average. 
However, it must be considered that these products are intended for processing by third parties 
for the manufacture of final products that are intended for users. The life cycle of these final 
products is not related to the products supplied by Repsol.

Fuels

Not applicable (product intended for single use only)

Electrical energy

Not applicable (product intended for single use only)

The concept of repairability and its correspondence with 
scales established as a reference does not apply to the 
products offered by Repsol, with the exception of 
certain insignificant cases such as the 

liquefied petroleum gas cylinders, which are reusable 
and repairable.

M

METHODOLOGY

Methodology for resource use and circular 
economy reporting and metrics3

Resource inflows

Materials that enter a Repsol business area for the first time are 
considered, avoiding counting those that are transferred to 
another business area once transformed. Each batch of raw 
materials taken into account in the metric has been accounted 
for, recorded, and managed independently.

Packaging data is not included because quantities are not 
significant. In the case of Chemicals, packaging represents less 
than 0.5% of the amount of materials used and it is estimated 
that this amount is lower for E&P and Refining, since the 
materials used in these businesses are not packaged.

For the Upstream business, data from direct measurements 
corresponding to the gross crude oil and natural gas production 
are reported and consolidated monthly. 

For the Refining business, data obtained from material balances 
from direct measurements recorded in management control 
systems are reported.

In the case of the Chemicals business, data from direct 
measurements, recorded in the management control system, 
classified as raw materials, auxiliary materials and other market 
materials, are reported.

In both Refining and Chemicals, the natural gas used as a raw 
material and as a fuel is taken into account.

The Low-Carbon Generation business does not use significant 
amounts of non-renewable resources such as hydrocarbons, so it 
is excluded from the metric.

Similarly, critical minerals are excluded because Repsol estimates 
a non-significant risk to the technologies used in this business 
according to its Critical Minerals Observatory report (2024 and 
2025) and based on the estimated quantities used in 2024.

3 The measurement of these metrics has not been validated by an external body other than the provider of the verification.

Environmentally sustainable capex
according to the EU Taxonomy 

See the Taxonomy Regulation for more details on this 
process. 

2.6 Taxonomy Regulation 

As explained in Climate change, Repsol’s investments 
that directly contribute to meeting its decarbonization 
goals are called low-carbon investments and include 
energy efficiency, generation and commercialization of 
renewable electricity, production and commercialization 
of renewable fuels, renewable hydrogen, synthetic fuels, 
chemicals (long-life polymers), other circular economy 
projects, carbon capture and storage (CCS), distributed 
generation, renewable electric mobility, and 
investments in R&D and corporate venturing in low-
carbon technologies.   

This vision of the decarbonization of our activity is less 
restrictive1 than that laid down by the European Union 
in its framework for facilitating sustainable investments, 
called the Sustainable Finance Taxonomy. The taxonomy 
defines a series of "eligible" economic activities, which 
are encompassed in 16 macro-sectors, and establishes 
the technical selection criteria to determine whether, if 
eligible, they are "aligned" activities: that is, those that 
contribute substantially to at least one of the six 
environmental objectives defined by the EU, do not 
pose significant damage to the other objectives and also 
comply with a minimum of social safeguards. 

The percentage of these activities is quantified by three 
economic indicators: revenues, investment (capex) and 
operating costs (opex). In 2025, the percentage of 
activities aligned according to the Sustainable Finance 
Taxonomy out of the Group’s total has risen to a 
percentage of 28% capex (40% in 2024), 0.8% of 
revenues (0.8% in 2024) and of 5% opex (4% in 2024).

In terms of its energy transformation and transition, 
capex is the most significant indicator for Repsol. In 
2025, the greatest weight was held by investments in 
wind and solar photovoltaic generation, followed by 
investments in the production of biofuels for transport 
and biogas. Capex aligned with the taxonomy decreased 
by twelve percentage points compared to 2024 as a 
result of the more intensive organic development of 
Repsol’s project portfolio in Spain and the USA and the 
organic acquisition of Connect Gen in 2024.

3. Social information

3.1 Own workforce

3.1.1 Strategy

Interests and views of stakeholders (ESRS 2 SBM-2) 

Repsol has 24,679 employees, and the necessary 
capabilities to tackle the processes of energy transition 
and transformation of its business models. Repsol 
considers its own workforce to be a key stakeholder, and 
it therefore systematically integrates their interests, 
views and rights, including their human rights, in the 
definition of the Company’s strategy and business 
model. The Company has permanent channels of 
dialogue and listening, such as the People and 
Organization teams, the Employee Service, the 
Employee Voice, committees, task forces and digital 
surveys that help in identifying their expectations and 
needs. See Interests and views of stakeholders (SBM-2).

The vast majority of Repsol employees are salaried 
(99.5%), that is, they maintain an employment 
relationship with Repsol under a permanent (90%) or 
temporary (10%) contract, the latter through the 
contracting modalities legally established in each 
country. This distribution shows Repsol’s preference for 
the direct hiring of its own workforce, as opposed to 
external forms of labor through third parties or the 
hiring of self-employed workers, who are residual and 
not material1.

People management at Repsol is a pillar that 
contributes to the transformation of the Company’s 
business model and the achievement of its objectives, 
acting as a lever for the energy transition, innovation 
and competitiveness.

Accordingly, the Organization is focused on the 
development and well-being of its employees, aligning 
people management policies with corporate strategy 
and promoting a positive, safe and inclusive work 
environment, based on respect for human and labor 
rights and social dialogue, and cooperation with 
workers’ representatives.

All the information presented below relates solely to 
the Company’s salaried employees.

Material impacts, risks, and opportunities and their 
interaction with the strategy and business model (ESRS 2 
SBM-3) 

Repsol has carried out a double materiality assessment 
in which the following impacts related to its own 
workforce have been identified:

• Creation of jobs that cover various areas and levels of 

specialization, providing direct and indirect 
employment throughout the supply chain and 
associated services, improving the quality of life of 
individuals and their families and strengthening the 
economy by increasing purchasing power and 
stimulating consumption (IRO-29).

• Strong and continuous social dialogue with workers' 

representatives that fosters a positive work 
environment, facilitates proactive problem solving, 
the implementation of improvements in working 
conditions and allows decisions aligned with 
employee needs (IRO-30).

1 Less than 1.5%

•

•

Improvement of the physical and mental health of 
employees, and increase of their motivation, 
engagement and productivity through initiatives that 
promote healthy habits and comprehensive well-
being (IRO 31). 

Improving the living conditions of Repsol employees 
by offering a comprehensive value proposition that 
includes competitive economic conditions, 
performance-based bonuses, health and well-being 
programs, and initiatives that facilitate work-life  
balance (IRO-32).

• Promotion of equality, diversity and inclusion beyond 
what is established by local legislation and market 
demands, improvement of working conditions and 
opportunities for professional growth, in a way that 
strengthens motivation and job  satisfaction (IRO-33).

• Training aligned with market demands, which 

strengthens human capital and facilitates that 
employees have the necessary skills. This enhances 
performance and adaptability, improves job 
satisfaction and enables meeting challenges 
effectively, contributing to innovation, growth, 
productivity and operational efficiency (IRO-34).

•

•

Improving the employability and inclusion of young 
people through Repsol’s young talent program, which 
fosters an inclusive workforce and promotes 
generational renewal in the future through the 
creation of a talent pool (IRO-35).

Improvement of working conditions, creating a fairer, 
safer and more equitable environment for all 
employees through the promotion of an environment 
of respect for human rights (IRO-36).

The positive impacts, as described in Adoption of 
measures related to material impacts, encompass the 
entire workforce. 

No negative impacts, risks or opportunities of significant 
importance to the Company have been identified arising 
from the impacts or dependencies of the Company’s 
own workforce.

The Company has no operations at risk of forced or child 
labor in any country or business where Repsol operates.

3.1.2 Impact, risk and opportunity 
management

Policies related to the Company’s own workforce (S1-1) 

All of the policies and standards listed below apply to 
all Repsol salaried employees and are global in scope. 
They are reviewed periodically, taking into account the 
business needs and the concerns and needs expressed 
by employees through the different communication 
channels set up by the Company.

Annual Report on

Remuneration

The Code of Ethics and Business Conduct, Repsol’s 
People Management Policy, the Human Rights and 
Community Relations Policy, the Sustainability Policy, 
the Safety and Occupational Health Policy and the 
Global Framework for Diversity and Inclusion are 
described in Repsol's policies related to sustainability2.

The Global Compensation and Well-being Framework 
establishes a total compensation system that integrates 
fixed and variable short- and long-term remuneration, 
along with a defined set of benefits. This framework 
ensures compliance with the regulations applicable in all 
the geographies where the Company operates and is 
periodically reviewed to adapt to market movements 
and the macroeconomic context.

There are different remuneration schemes based on the 
activity performed, which include commercial incentives 
and variable compensation. Variable remuneration is 
linked to both Company and individual performance, 
with sustainability indicators. In addition, there are 
monetary and non-monetary recognition programs, and 
a benefits package that includes pension plans, medical 
and life insurance and food assistance, among others.

The occupational health norm sets out guidelines to 
promote and maintain a healthy, safe work environment, 
protecting the physical, mental and social health of 
employees and encouraging a healthy lifestyle both 
inside and outside the work environment. The standard 
includes access to adequate medical resources and 
services and action plans for medical emergencies. 

The labor management standard establishes the 
structure for the administration of labor relations, 
promoting efficient and organized management of 
employees. It regulates aspects such as the processes of 
hiring and modification of the employment relationship, 
professional progression, mobility and international 
assignments, and sets out the criteria for the management 
of documentation and employee data while safeguarding 
privacy. 

The implementation of the Global Compensation and 
Well-being Framework and the aforementioned standards 
is the responsibility of the People and Organization 
function. All of them are available on the Repsol intranet 
where the person in charge is identified, and employees 
can provide comments or suggestions on it. For more 
information, see Business model and strategy 

Processes for engaging with own workers and workers’ 
representatives on impacts (S1-2). Processes to repair 
negative impacts and channels for own workers to express 
their concerns (S1-3)

Repsol has its own channels to capture employees’ 
interests and perspectives, both to activate 
collaboration and to facilitate the remediation of 
incidents when appropriate. These channels are 
common in both cases and allow any opinion, interest or 
incident to be managed through the established 
procedures. Additionally, all Repsol employees have 
communication channels to capture and manage their 
needs and concerns, which are available on an ongoing 
basis: People and Organization (HR) teams in each 
business and area, Employee Service (SAE), etc.

In addition, the Ethics and Conduct Channel allows both 
employees and third parties to express concerns, report 
possible breaches and communicate negative issues 
confidentially. Furthermore, as established by the Code 
of Ethics and Business Conduct, any type of retaliation is 
prohibited. For more information, see Business conduct.

In 2024, the Our Voice program was launched, designed 
to collect, measure and analyze employee opinions on 
key aspects for the Organization, such as engagement 
and job satisfaction. By 2025, this program has 
established itself as a tool for assessing employee 
engagement, well-being and critical moments of 
professional experience (onboarding, mobility, training, 
definition of objectives and communication of earnings). 
In 2025, a survey was carried out with more than 20,000 
employees, with a response rate of 64%, which reflected 
an overall engagement rate of 78% and a well-being 
index of 81%, indicators that show strong occupational 
health. In addition, the intention to remain in Repsol 
(90%) stands out, 19 points above the external average, 
confirming a strong connection of talent with the 
Company.

Repsol maintains a constant dialogue and guarantees 
the participation of employees through their legal 
representatives, in the definition of working conditions 
and in the creation of an environment based on equality, 
respect and talent development. This dialogue is 
articulated under the principles of mutual respect and 
open and transparent dialogue, through commissions, 
committees and negotiation panels (78 in Spain) and, in 
any case, through the continuous relationship of the 
social agents with the Labor Relations teams of each 
country/work center or with the head of People and 
Organization of each business unit/corporate area.

The People and Organization Department is responsible 
for leading the people strategy, managing actual and 
potential impacts and facilitating all channels of 
collaboration.

For more information on the processes of collaboration 
with the Company’s employees and workers’ 
representatives, see Stakeholder engagement 
processes.

Adoption of measures related to material impacts on own 
workforce, approaches to mitigate material risks and take 
advantage of material opportunities related to own 
workforce and the effectiveness of such actions (S1-4). 
Goals related to managing material negative impacts, 
driving positive impacts, and managing material risks and 
opportunities (S1-5)  

This section describes the actions and objectives 
implemented by Repsol to manage material impacts and 
contribute to the achievement of Repsol’s policy goals.

The information is structured according to the following 
subtopics: working conditions and equal treatment and 
opportunities for all.

Repsol has provided the human, financial, and 
technological means to carry out all the initiatives 
outlined below. Operating expenses are not disclosed 
because they are not material.

2 Repsol has policies that regulate different areas of people management, which provide a coherent framework in all dimensions that affect 

employees.

The actions and targets described below apply in all 
business units, areas and regions where the Company 
operates, without exceptions. However, the scope of 
certain actions may be conditioned by local regulatory 
frameworks or by country-specific circumstances. These 
objectives and actions are updated and reviewed 
annually based on the needs. 

The targets described have not been validated by an 
external body. The control is carried out by the People 
and Organization Department and no third parties have 
been involved in its definition. 

The current progress of the objectives is maintained as 
planned and no relevant changes have been detected 
that could affect their compliance.

Equal treatment and opportunities for all3

Repsol has a Global Framework for Diversity and 
Inclusion, which establishes the principles and 
commitments  in terms of equal treatment and 
opportunities, diversity, equity, and non-discrimination. 
In line with this framework, objectives and initiatives 
have been defined aimed at promoting work 
environments that are compliant with applicable 
regulations and favor the participation of people with 
different profiles, thereby ensuring the responsible 
management of diversity.

Equal treatment involves offering working conditions 
and opportunities based on objective criteria and 
avoiding any form of discrimination. The initiatives that 
materialize this undertaking are detailed below. 

Measures related to equal treatment and opportunities for all (1)

Action

Description of the action

Associated target

Expected result

Graduate 
Program

The Graduate Program is an initiative to incorporate 
recent graduates who join Repsol through direct hiring, 
and by 2025, 71 employees have been hired. 

Recruitment of young 
professionals (2)

Internal Winer
network 

LGBTI+ 
campaigns

The Internal WINER network is a space designed to 
foster talent and promote a culture of equal 
opportunities and an inclusive environment. Its 
purpose is to support initiatives that encourage 
balanced representation and promote the active 
participation of all individuals by helping to identify 
and eliminate potential barriers.
Throughout the year, various initiatives and actions 
aimed at strengthening gender equality have been 
promoted. The network is made up of 405 men and 
women.

In 2025, different actions and campaigns have been 
carried out aimed at making the LGTBI+ community 
visible and promoting respectful and inclusive 
environments: protocol for the accompaniment of 
transgender people, communication campaigns, 
content on sexual infections during health week, 
information points in Tarragona and Petronor, diversity 
forum in A Coruña, Proud network meetings in Mexico, 
Peru, Campus, Puertollano and Portugal, Yes We Trans 
label, We co-founded REDI Portugal. 

Unconscious bias 
training programs

In 2025, the program has continued to advance with 
training in unconscious biases (3), reaching a total of 
157 people trained in 2024 and 258 in 2025. 

These actions are part of 
Repsol’s Global 
Framework for Diversity 
and Inclusion.

of reinforcing generational

in terms of LGBTI+ inclusion. 

Increase the number of

management decisions are 
based on objective

principles of inclusion.

equal to the age of 30 in 2024 and 2025.

implementation of the principle of equal opportunities and equal treatment of men and women in matters of employment and occupation.

objectives, which apply to all business units, areas, and 
geographies where the Company operates, to promote 
diversity, ensure parity in hiring, foster female 
leadership, and incorporate young professionals, in 
order to support balanced representation in the 
Organization.

undertaken internships in the Company as an effective path to employment and generational renewal.

Working conditions

Repsol’s goal is to attract, retain and develop talent, 
providing all employees a competitive value proposition 
that includes attractive total remuneration, safe and 
flexible working conditions that promote 
comprehensive well-being and work-life balance. In 
addition, it promotes healthy lifestyle habits and 
people’s motivation.

This goal takes the shape of the following actions and 
objectives aimed at fostering healthy lifestyles and 
boosting individuals’ motivation.

At the same time, they strengthen employability via 
specific plans and ongoing training that advances 
professional development and the ability to tackle the 
challenges of the future.  

Since 2024, Repsol has deployed the Company's talent 
program as a strategic lever to anticipate future needs, 
attract and retain critical profiles, promote continuous 
learning, evolve leadership, and strengthen 
organizational culture. All this is supported by data-
driven management.

Lastly, all these initiatives, objectives and goals will 
contribute to advancing towards our 2030 goal of 
achieving our purpose of being a company recognized 
as a benchmark employer in the sectors in which it 
operates to attract and retain talent. The Merco Talento 
ranking is one of the indicators that show Repsol’s 
consolidation in this area, with the Company ranked as 
the third best in Spain for the third consecutive year.

Likewise, Repsol is the only company on the IBEX and in 
the energy sector that has remained among the top 
five positions in the Merco Talent index in Spain since 
2013. In the 2025 edition, the Company has been 
recognized as the second best company for the quality 
of its Human Resources team among the 200 
organizations evaluated.

(1) The total number of employee departures with respect to the 
number of employees with an active contract as of December 31, 
2025 is counted as global turnover.

The ratio is influenced by the Service Station business, 
which is characterized by high turnover rates due to the 
need to reinforce temporary contracts at certain times 
of the year due to the inherent seasonality of the retail 
business. For this reason, the employee turnover rate is 
also attached, excluding the service stations in Spain, 
Portugal and Peru, for which the companies Campsared 
(Spain), Recosac (Peru) and Gespost (Portugal) are 
excluded and the rate is reduced from 19% to 4%, and 
from 5.9% to 1% when only voluntary employee 
resignations are counted.

Coverage of collective bargaining and social dialogue 
(S1-8)

In relation to collective bargaining, Repsol includes its 
employees in collective agreements in Spain, Peru, 
Portugal, Brazil, France, Italy, Belgium and Norway, all of 
which are represented by an internal body or by the 
sectoral union. In 2025, employees covered by a 
collective bargaining agreement represent 80,19% of 
the Group’s total workforce. In 2024, the percentage of 
employees covered by collective agreements came to 
79.17% of the Group’s total workforce(including the 
United Kingdom and Indonesia).

In Spain, Repsol has 17 collective bargaining 
agreements and one Framework Agreement6, which 
constitute the basis on which the reciprocal interests of 
the Company and the employee representatives are 
based. For more information on the participatory bodies 
of workers’ union representation, see Coverage of 
collective bargaining and social dialogue.

The percentage of employees covered by collective 
bargaining agreements broken down by country can be 
found in Collective Bargaining Agreements. For more 
information on the existence of agreements, see 
Processes to repair negative impacts and channels for 
own workers to express their concerns (S1-3)

Adequate wages (S1-10)

Repsol guarantees that all its employees receive 
adequate remuneration understood as the sum of the 
base salary and other components defined according to 
the group, such as incentives, participation in profits, 
and social benefits (for example, health insurance and 
pension plans), with the aim of ensuring financial 
stability and covering essential needs. Salary references 
are established through objective market studies and 
internal criteria, thus ensuring external competitiveness 
and consistency with the Company’s remuneration 
policy.

In 2025, the percentage of people with disabilities is 
2.05% (505 people); this is a global figure and meets the 
legal requirements in force in the countries where 
Repsol operates. The information was compiled from 
internal systems and local reports, considering only 
current contracts and applying the official criteria of 
each jurisdiction. These regulatory differences can affect 
global comparability, so the data is presented in 
aggregate and accompanied by this contextualization 
for transparency.  

Training and capacity building metrics (S1-13)

Repsol focuses its performance process on assessment 
and continuous development of employees. Individual 
objectives, aligned with those of the unit and the 
Strategic Plan, are set in collaboration with managers and 
reviewed regularly to support progress. The process 
includes review to support progress and constant 
feedback between managers and employees, promoting 
recognition of achievements and professional 
development.

Work-life balance metrics (S1-15)

In all countries where the Repsol Group operates, 100% 
of employees can take family leave (maternity, paternity, 
parental leave or leave for carers), whether under the 
country’s legislation, local collective bargaining 
agreements or the global minimum work-life balance 
rules that apply in all Group companies to improve on or 
complete local regulations. In 2025, 4,241 employees 
took some form of leave (17.18% of the Group's total 
workforce), of whom 67.74% were men, 32.23% were 
women, and 0.024% did not provide information on their 
gender. This means that of the total number of male 
employees, 19.92% took leave; of the total number of 
female employees, 13.34% took leave; and of the total 
number of employees who did not provide information 
on their gender, 10.00% took leave.  In 2024, 4,288 
employees took leave (16.75% of the Group's total 
workforce), of whom 68.70% were men, 31.27% were 
women and 0.02% did not provide information on their 
gender. This means that of the total number of male 
employees, 19.31% took leave; of the total number of 
female employees, 12.98%; and of the total number of 
employees who did not provide information on their 
gender, 16.67%.

Compensation metrics: pay gap and total compensation 
(S1-16)

Repsol operates in Spain, Peru and Portugal, in different 
sectors and under different corporate entities subject to 
different collective agreements, which means very 
different salary values, depending on the company and 
the sector. In certain categories, employees receive the 
salary values directly determined by collective 
bargaining agreements, which in no case establish pay 
differences based on gender. Analyzing together the 
wage realities of different sectors under different 
agreements can lead to inappropriate interpretations.

Comparing similar situations with each other, that is, 
employees with the same seniority/age and belonging 
to the same business and professional category, the 
salary difference not explained on the basis of objective 
criteria between men and women is reduced globally to 
5%, 6% and 4% in 2025, 2024 and 2023, respectively. 

See Methodology for own workforce reporting and 
metrics for more information on the calculations of the 
pay gap and remuneration ratio.

In the rest of the countries, 10 reports were recorded 
that led to an investigation of potential harassment. In

Repsol has complaint mechanisms such as the Ethics 
Channel regulated by the Code of Ethics and Business 
Conduct, which allows cases of harassment and 
discrimination to be reported securely and 
confidentially. This mechanism guarantees the 
protection of the whistleblower throughout the process 
and is complemented by internal channels for employee 
service and social dialogue. In addition, other avenues 
are contemplated, such as lawsuits, labor inspections or 
the supervisory work of workers’ representatives. 

The total number of discrimination cases includes 
workplace incidents of discrimination on the basis of 
gender, racial or ethnic origin, nationality, religion or 
belief, disability, age, sexual orientation or other 
relevant forms of discrimination. This includes cases of 
harassment as a specific form of discrimination.

During the reference period, Repsol has not detected 
any serious human rights-related cases involving its own 
workforce, including situations of forced labor, human 
trafficking, or child labor.

M

Methodology for own workforce reporting 
and metrics 

All indicators relating to employees in Appendix IV exclude the 
following non-material companies7: Saint John LNG Limited 
Partnership, Alba Emission Free Energy S. A, Klikin Deals Spain, 
S. L. U., Energía Distribuida del Norte S. A., Gaolania Servicios, S. L 
y Evsare, Tecnologías de Recarga S .L. These exclusions do not 
apply to the health indicators in S1-14.

The total number of employees presented in this report at the 
end of 2025 corresponds to Headcount, while the figures 
included in the Consolidated Annual Financial Statements are 
reported in terms of FTE (Full-Time Equivalent) and PMA. For 
more details, see Note 5.5 of the Consolidated Financial 
Statements. 

Voluntary turnover

Calculation of voluntary turnover is the number of departures/
number of active people. Voluntary turnover is solely counted for 
employees with a permanent contract, given that they represent 
the structural workforce of the Company and they allow for more 
precisely measuring the effectiveness of the value proposition, 
loyalty of talent, and employment stability. Including temporary 
employees would introduce an element of volatility that would 
be unrelated to people management owing to the seasonal or 
contractual nature of their relationship, and it would distort the 
internal and external comparability of the indicator.

Overall gross pay gap

This calculation encompasses total cash remuneration per hour 
and stock bonuses on a cash basis, that is, sums actually received 
by active employees, in euros. Employees on international 
assignment and employees who left before December 31 of the 
year in question are excluded. 

Repsol operates in Spain, Peru and Portugal, in different sectors 
and under different corporate entities subject to different 
collective agreements, which means very different salaries, 
depending on the company and the sector. In certain categories, 
employees receive the salary values directly determined by 
collective bargaining agreements, which in no case establish pay 
differences based on gender. 

METHODOLOGY

Jointly analyzing wage realities of different sectors and under 
different agreements leads to inappropriate interpretations. 
Considering more comparable situations, that is, employees 
belonging to the same business, with the same professional 
category and similar seniority/age, the salary difference is 
reduced to 5%, 6%, 4% and 5% in 2025, 2024, 2023 and 2022, 
respectively. The decrease in the gap in 2025 is due to the exit of 
Repsol Resources UK from the reporting scope and the gender 
distribution of employees.

Country-adjusted pay gap (%)

The total cash remuneration per hour and stock bonuses received 
by employees and expressed in euros, has been considered on a 
cash basis. Employees on international assignment and 
employees who have left before December 31 of the year in 
question are excluded.   

In order to offer a value that allows a comparison between similar 
situations, the pay gap is calculated by comparing the 
remuneration of men and women with the same main 
determining metrics (business/company, professional category, 
age/seniority).

The pay gap has been calculated as follows: (average male 
remuneration - average female remuneration / average male 
remuneration) x 100. 

Total annual remuneration ratio

The methodology for calculating the total annual remuneration 
ratio (S1‑16) was revised to fully adapt it to the requirements of 
the CSDR Directive and to the specifications of ESRS S1. As a 
result of this methodological adjustment, the 2024 figure is again 
provided in order to support comparability and fully document 
year-on-year changes.

The indicator shows the relationship between the total monetary 
remuneration of the person with the highest remuneration and 
the median of the population. For the calculation, the total 
monetary remuneration plus stock bonuses have been included 
on a cash basis, that is, the amounts actually received by 
employees during the year. The person with the highest 
remuneration has been identified considering all employees, 
excluding expatriate personnel from other origins and employees 
who left before December 31, 2025.

7 Less than 1.5%

3.2 Workers in the value chain
3.2.1 Strategy

Repsol’s value chain includes suppliers, partners, 
distributors, and customers, marketing a wide range of 
products, and encompassing activities such as oil and 
gas Upstream, electricity production and sales, refining, 
chemicals, and product marketing.

Supply chain description

Repsol’s supply chain is global, mirroring the scope of its 
operations. It includes suppliers with registered offices in 
any region of the world, with a predominance of local 
purchasing and contracting.

The main suppliers, those considered key1, have their 
registered offices in Europe and North America.

Interests and views of stakeholders (ESRS 2 SBM-2) 

The map of interest groups, revised annually, consists of 
nine groups and 40 subgroups. The suppliers, 
contractors and partners are the most significant 
stakeholders in relation to value chain workers, as 
described in Processes for engaging with value chain 
workers (S2-2), Business model and strategy, 
Materiality assessment and Affected communities.

Material impacts, risks, and opportunities2 and their 
interaction with the strategy and the business model 
(ESRS 2 SBM-3) 

The information reported in this section relates mainly to 
partners and suppliers of Tier 1 materials and services3. 
Currently, partial information is available at Tier 2 level 
and, occasionally, at Tier 3 level, in which case it is 
specifically detailed. Repsol is working to expand 
information on workers in the value chain in the future. 

The double materiality assessment is detailed in Business 
model and strategy and Materiality assessment. 

The material negative impacts identified are:

•

Industrial accidents that can negatively affect workers 
throughout Repsol’s value chain (IRO-37). 

The positive material impacts associated with workers in 
the value chain are the following:

•

Inclusion of contractual clauses requiring respect for 
human rights in agreements with suppliers and 
contractors (IRO-38).

• Promotion of the improvement of the working 

conditions, health and safety of workers in the value 
chain through the inclusion of ESG criteria (IRO-39).

The types of workers who are potentially impacted by 
value chain activities (also VC) are the following 
(including particularly vulnerable chain VC workers):4

• Workers who are not part of the Company’s own 

workforce, but work at the Company’s site or at its 
facilities.

• Workers belonging to entities that carry out their 

activities in the previous phases of the value chain. 

• Workers belonging to entities that carry out their 

activities in the downstream stages of the value chain, 
such as those involved in logistics or distribution 
activities or franchising. 

All these workers are potentially exposed to different 
impacts, which may be generalized or systemic 
(countries and types of procurement with human rights 
and social risks), or of a one-off nature (for example, due 
to an industrial incident or the detection of reputational 
risk in a supplier).

With respect to systemic impacts, the countries at 
highest social risk in which Repsol operates are Peru, 
Venezuela, and Bolivia. The activities with the greatest 
social risk are those related to security detection 
services and systems, textile production, and mineral 
extraction.5

More detail is provided in Adoption of measures related 
to material impacts on workers in the value chain 
(S2-4).

3.2.2 Impact, risk and opportunity 
management

Policies related to workers in the value chain (S2-1)

The policies related to workers in the value chain, which 
are detailed below, reflect Repsol’s respect for human 
rights, as well as to the main international standards and 
agreements in this area. 

Repsol’s policies are developed in management 
processes, such as collaboration with workers in the 
value chain or those for repairing negative impacts. The 
processes include measures that make it possible to 
remedy possible human rights incidents that affect 
workers in the value chain. Among these measures are 
due diligence, the inclusion of the different 
requirements in all bidding processes through the 

1 A key supplier is considered to be one with an awarded amount greater than 50 M€ in the reporting year.
2 In the assessment of impacts, risks, and opportunities related to workers in the value chain carried out through double materiality assessment, 

there are no material risks and opportunities, so the section focuses on impact management.

3 Tier 1 is the supplier that maintains the direct contractual relationship with Repsol; Tier 2 is the supplier of Tier 1; and Tier 3 is the supplier of 

Tier 2 and successive ones.
4 See Affected communities.
5 Calculated from the volume of orders for the current year, the list of risk countries defined internally in the Company, and the registered office of 

the suppliers.

General Conditions of Purchase and Contract6 or 
standardized and/or specific clauses, and performance 
evaluation.

Repsol intends for third parties subject to the codes of 
ethics and policies detailed below to share such conduct 
and commitments with their own employees.

Repsol’s Code of Ethics and Conduct 

The purpose of Repsol’s  Code of Ethics and Business 
Conduct is to establish the framework for 
understanding and putting into practice the conduct 
and standards that the Company expects from each of 
its employees. 

Partners, contractors, suppliers, and other collaborating 
companies are an extension of Repsol and, for this 
reason, must act in accordance with this code and any 
other applicable contractual provisions when acting on 
behalf of or in collaboration with the Company. Repsol 
also encourages business partners to develop and 
implement ethical programs that are consistent with its 
own standards, as detailed in Business conduct.

Code of Ethics and Business Conduct for Suppliers 

The Code of Ethics and Business Conduct for Suppliers7 
establishes the minimum principles that the Company 
expects from its suppliers, promoting their knowledge 
and acceptance before starting the contractual 
relationship, as well as their compliance throughout the 
business relationship. This Code articulates the minimum 
behavior guidelines that are reasonably expected of 
suppliers throughout their supply chain, reflecting their 
commitment to respect of human rights and the main 
international standards and agreements in this area: the 
International Bill of Human Rights8, the principles 
relating to rights established by the International 
Labour Organization (ILO) concerning fundamental 
principles and rights at work and the eight Fundamental 
Conventions9 that implement them.

The Code includes, among others, provisions on forced 
labor, child labor and human trafficking, gender 
equality, freedom of association, the right to collective 
bargaining, integrity and ethical behavior, measures 
against bribery, corruption and conflicts of interest, 
environmental protection and security, as well as the 
protection and transparency of information to promote 
a sustainable economy. 

The Code of Ethics and Business Conduct for Suppliers, 
which is available on the corporate website, must be 
accepted by all suppliers who pass the due diligence 
phase and participate in a bidding process with any part 
of the Company, where the obligations undertaken by 
the supplier shall also applicable to its employees, 
subcontractors and subcontractors.

Human Rights and Community Relations Policy 

The Human Rights and Community Relations Policy is 
aligned with the main international treaties and 
standards in this area. Repsol expects its partners, 
suppliers, contractors, distributors and other agents 
with whom it maintains business relationships to respect 
human rights of their workers in the carrying out of their 
activities anywhere in the world and, consequently, to 
address any negative impacts they have have a role in, 
as detailed in Affected communities.

Commercial Relations Policy with Third Parties 

The purpose of the Commercial Relations with Third 
Parties Policy is to establish relationships based on the 
law, ethical principles, integrity and values of the 
Company with the partners, suppliers, contractors and 
customers who collaborate with Repsol in the 
development of business activities and with whom it 
establishes commercial or business relationships. It 
applies to all Repsol employees and to the third parties 
with whom the Company is related.

Safety and Occupational Health Policy 

The Safety and Occupational Health Policy establishes 
the objective of carrying out all the Company’s activities 
while preserving the integrity of people and avoiding 
any damage to the environment and surroundings, as 
well as supporting a healthy and safe work environment 
from both a physical and emotional point of view. 
Repsol seeks to ensure, as far as possible, that its 
suppliers, contractors, and partners share the 
commitments  set out in the Policy and provide a safe 
environment for their workers.

In the event of a conflict between safety or health and 
operational results, all employees and contractors have 
the responsibility to choose safety, with management 
always supporting this choice. 

6 The General Terms and Conditions of Purchase and Contract are intended to regulate the relations between Repsol and its suppliers. They apply 

to all suppliers and subcontracted companies. They establish the obligation of the supplier to respect internationally recognized human rights 
that include, at least, the rights set forth in the International Bill of Human Rights and the principles relating to fundamental rights established in 
the Declaration of the International Labour Organization. In addition, they establish the requirement to comply with all the provisions related to 
respect for human rights that are in force in the Repsol Group’s internal regulations and practice, such as the elimination of child labor, all forms of 
forced labor, human trafficking, slavery or servitude throughout the life cycle of the goods and services contracted and non-discrimination on the 
basis of nationality, race, ethnicity, religion, political affiliation, marital status, social status, age, disability, culture, sex, gender identity, sexual 
orientation, or any other status. They also include the requirement for the supplier to comply with the current provisions relating to safety and the 
environment, as well as the applicable regulations and internal practice of the Repsol Group. They are available on the corporate website, 
following this path: Home > Sustainability > Sustainability pillars > People > Human rights > Business relationships > Suppliers and contractors.
7 The Supplier Code of Ethics and Conduct is available on Repsol’s corporate website, following this path: Home > Sustainability > Sustainability

8 The International Bill of Human Rights is known as the Universal Declaration of Human Rights (1948) along with the instruments that develop it: 

The International Covenant on Civil and Political Rights (1976) and the International Covenant on Economic, Social and Cultural Rights (1976).
9 The ILO's eight Core Conventions include: Freedom of association and protection of the right to organize convention (1948), Right to organize 

and collective bargaining convention (1949), Forced labor convention (1930), Abolition of forced labor convention (1957), Minimum age 
convention (1973), Worst forms of child labor convention (1999), Equal remuneration convention (1951), Discrimination convention (1958).

Among other things, it outlines Repsol’s goals of  
involving employees, contractors and other 
stakeholders in continuous improvement and in the

and systems, as detailed in Safety and security.

Processes for engaging with value chain workers (S2-2) 

Collaboration with suppliers and contractors 

Repsol manages its supply chain by responding to both 
the expectations of stakeholders and complying with 
ethical, labor, environmental, safety and human rights 
standards. 

The expectations of the different stakeholders, 
including the Company’s suppliers, are met through 
materiality assessment and the establishment of a 
collaboration process, as outlined in the internal 
regulations.

The collaboration process is carried out in two phases: 
initially, by the Purchasing areas and, subsequently, by 
the areas that use the goods or services requested 
through the person responsible for the contract. For this 
reason, there is no single person responsible for the 
process.

Purchasing carries out a first phase of search, selection 
and due diligence of the supplier and, subsequently, the 
contracting process (bidding, awarding and 
contracting). During the process, the supplier’s 
expectations are addressed, and are reflected in the 
commitment formalized by the two parties. In order to 
comply with current legislation regarding the illegal 
assignment of workers10, Repsol’s internal Purchasing 
and Contracting regulations regulate the nature of the 
individual relationship with the suppliers’ workers, which 
should be conducted through the representative of the 
contracted company. 

The regulations also provide that the supplier may not 
subcontract the execution of the service subject to the 
contract without the prior written consent of Repsol.

According to internal purchasing regulations, the person 
responsible for the contract must maintain a 
relationship with the supplier’s representative 
throughout the duration of the contract so as to ensure 
fulfillment of the expectations of both parties and the 
addressing of any new needs that arise during the 
lifetime of the contract. The frequency of contact 
between the contractor and the supplier’s 
representative is defined by the parties according to the 
type of service. The person responsible for the contract 
has the following responsibilities: 

• Act as Repsol’s technical representative with the 

supplier during the contract and keep a written record 
of any significant communications with the supplier.

• Perform material inspection/service verification, 

validating that the goods or services listed on the 
goods receipt have actually been received.

• Promote compliance with all contractual obligations. 

• Periodically evaluate suppliers’ performance to 

identify risk situations that may require corrective 
actions, termination or suspension of the relationship. 

The evaluation of the effectiveness of the collaboration 
process is carried out in two phases: for the duration of 
the contract, by the person responsible for the contract 
and the supplier; and in performance evaluation, by the 
person responsible for the contract, as described in 
Adoption of measures related to material impacts on 
workers in the value chain (S2-4). 

Collaboration with suppliers on safety 

Within the framework of the Safety Excellence11 
Program, Repsol maintains continuous collaboration 
with its contractors regarding safety. In addition, group 
communication actions are developed on specific 
aspects of interest to relevant contractors, as detailed in 
Safety and security.

Collaboration with partners

The scope of this section is limited to new Repsol 
partners.

Before establishing new relationships with potential 
partners through a Mergers and Acquisitions (M&A) 
process, Repsol conducts a complete due diligence 
process on each transaction. The responsibility for 
coordinating and leading the M&A process lies with the 
M&A Department or the Business Development 
Department, depending on the nature of the 
transaction.

In this process, social, environmental and business 
conduct aspects, among others, are evaluated. The 
process includes requesting information through an 
initial questionnaire, analyzing the information provided 
generally through a Virtual Data Room, question-and-
answer sessions, and specific meetings with the workers 
responsible for the different areas.

Relations with partners are contractually regulated in 
the Shareholders’ Agreement, which includes clauses on 
issues related to governance, corporate policies, ethical 
compliance, sustainability, anti-corruption, and auditing. 
In the event that risks or aspects for improvement 
related to social, environmental, or governance issues 
have been identified in the due diligence process, 
Repsol has the prerogative of introducing corrective or 
commitment clauses in the Shareholders' Agreement to 
achieve the necessary standards, along with the 
contractual mechanism, in order to verify corrective 
measures or commitments subsequent to the signing of 
the contract.

10 Art. 43 of the Workers’ Statute
11 Led by the Department of Safety Excellence Program

Processes to repair negative impacts and channels for 
value chain workers to voice their concerns (S2-3)  

Repsol has collaboration processes with suppliers and 
contractors that take into account the actual and 
potential impacts that may affect workers in the value 
chain. In this way, it encourages suppliers, contractors 
and partners to collaborate with their own workers. 
These processes are designed with the aim to include all 
the workers in the value chain, separately from workers 
engaged in industrial activity, which is where negative 
impacts have been identified. Details are provided 
below on some of the main processes carried out to 
repair negative and potential impacts:

Ethics and Compliance Channel 

Repsol is committed to the culture of compliance and 
ethical integrity and understands that, in this context, 
individuals who report possible irregular or unlawful 
behavior play a key role in discovering and preventing 
such violations, protecting the proper functioning of the 
Company and contributing to the well-being of society 
as a whole. 

The Company has an Ethics and Compliance Channel, as 
well as an internal information system. This channel is 
available on Repsol’s corporate website to anyone, 
including employees and third parties, suppliers, 
contractors and subcontractors.

The existence of the channel is included in the Code of 
Ethics and Business Conduct of Suppliers for its 
knowledge and acceptance according to the approved 
regulations. It is shared at different stages of the 
purchasing and contracting processes. 

For more information on the functioning of the Ethics 
and Compliance Channel and on the process of 
managing reports, see Ethics and Compliance Channel 
and Process of managing communications.

Operational grievance mechanisms 

Grievance mechanisms are a valuable source of 
information for detecting potential human rights 
impacts on communities, suppliers and contractors, 
partners, customers or other stakeholders.

In the event that the Company detects any adverse 
impacts caused by activities directly related to its 
business, it undertakes to address them in agreement 
with the affected parties in order to achieve an effective 
and satisfactory remedy.  

In such a case, the affected population is identified, the 
nature and magnitude of the impacts are evaluated 
following internationally accepted standard 
methodologies and a remediation solution is proposed, 
which is discussed with the affected parties to obtain 
their approval and reach a satisfactory agreement 
between the parties. 

Adoption of measures related to material impacts on 
workers in the value chain, approaches to manage material 
risks and take advantage of material opportunities related 
to workers in the value chain and the effectiveness of such 
actions (S2-4)  

Among the most key measures are the following:

Evaluating the results of due diligence measures is 
crucial in order to identify any significant evidence of 
compliance. If any risk is detected, possible mitigation 
measures are identified and assessed. The final decision 
on the formalization of the relationship with the third 
party rests with the contracting area. In addition, a regular 
update of due diligence measures is required every two 
years or when there are significant changes in the 
circumstances of the third party. For this reason, 
continuous monitoring of active counterparties is 
carried out as the case may require.

The time horizon tied to this action is not limited, given 
that it is integral to the ESG strategy.

Managing negative impacts in the supply chain

Repsol has a supply chain risk management model to 
identify, assess, and minimize environmental, social, and 
governance (ESG)  risks at different stages of the 
purchasing process. 

Application of the due diligence standard

The Repsol Group’s third-party due diligence standard 
establishes the necessary measures to allow adequate 
knowledge of the third parties with whom the Company 
engages or will engage, in relation to compliance risks. 
This standard aims to provide sufficient information for 
decision-making and the evaluation and mitigation of 
potential compliance risks associated with third parties, 
both before formalizing the relationship and during its 
validity. The standard is applicable to all employees of 
the Repsol Group, regardless of their type of contract, 
position, or place of work, provided that in the 
performance of their duties they interact with third 
parties (suppliers, partners, and customers, among 
others).

The due diligence process obliges contracting areas to 
familiarize themselves with the third parties and to 
apply appropriate due diligence measures based on the 
risk or indication of risk assigned to the third party. 
Knowledge of third parties, their activity and 
background is facilitated through searches in public and 
private databases, integrity reports, and external 
questionnaires. As a result of these analyses, additional 
measures may be applied according to the criteria of the 
Compliance department12 and in consideration of the 
risk for operations.

Managing supply chain risk

12 The Gas Trading and Wholesale businesses have integrated compliance departments, which have autonomy in decision-making regarding the 

due diligence of the third parties involved in their operations.

In the due diligence phase, suppliers are registered in 
the procurement management systems, having to pass 
an analysis related to aspects of integrity, anti-
corruption, bribery, sanctions, international embargoes 
and social aspects. In 2025, a reputational analysis was 
carried out on 25,25113 suppliers worldwide (in 2024, 
28,899 suppliers were evaluated). As a result, relevant 
information has been found regarding these aspects of 
63 companies with which purchases or contracts have 
been made. This process is continuous for suppliers 
registered in purchasing systems. The relevant aspects 
identified in the reputational analysis are managed 
jointly by the Compliance and Purchasing areas, and 
help to make decisions in the different phases of the 
purchasing process.

After the reputational analysis, the supplier is assigned a 
rating with respect to operations that are significant to 
the Company, based on economic and financial criteria.

In the purchase and contracting phase, through specific 
contractual clauses, the Company requires a compliance 
with international standards, and provisions relating to 
safety, the environment, data protection, information 
security, cybersecurity, ethical conduct and respect for 
human rights in its internal regulations. In addition, the 
supplier’s acceptance of the General Conditions of 
Purchase and Contract before or at the time of 
submitting its offer is encouraged. Likewise, both the 
award letter and the order refer to these General 
Conditions of Purchase and Contract, and are 
considered a binding contract between parties.

Depending on the purpose of the award, it may be 
necessary to complete various compliance 
questionnaires that allow suppliers to share information 
on the control mechanisms they have put into place to 
mitigate different risks on the safety of operations and/
or compliance with additional legal requirements (data 
protection, illegal transfer of workers, cybersecurity, 
international sanctions on software and technology 
goods, etc.). 

During the bidding process, in acquisitions of goods and 
services of high risk in safety and environment, it is 
required to introduce a minimum weight in the 
weighting criteria of the matrix of analysis, evaluation, 
comparison and alignment of bids,  with a minimum 
acceptable value required. In the tender process, safety 
and environmental reference persons evaluate suppliers 
in significant aspects of the new contract. Subsequently, 
specific requirements are established in the contractual 
clauses. In addition, in the Upstream business, a target 
for 2026 has been defined that perfects this process 
and is described in more detail in Targets related to 
managing material negative impacts, driving positive 
impacts, and managing material risks and opportunities 
(S2-5).

Supporting the effective implementation of the impact 
management process 

There is a process of periodic evaluation of the 
performance of suppliers in terms of safety, 
environment, quality and management in order to 
identify risk situations that may require corrective 
actions, termination or suspension of the relationship. In 
the case of goods and services with a high risk in safety 
and the environment, an action plan is drawn up jointly 
with the supplier if the evaluation falls short of a certain 
threshold. 

In 2025, 2,744 performance evaluations were carried out 
on 1,683 suppliers (in 2024, 2,381 performance 
evaluations were carried out on 1,569 suppliers).  

Within the performance evaluation procedure, there is a 
supplier disqualification process, whereby any current 
and future business relationship with a given supplier 
can be blocked for a period of time or even indefinitely. 
In 2025, no provider was disqualified for ESG reasons. 
There were no severe impacts on human rights, nor 
breaches of contract due to social or environmental 
impacts in the year related to the previous and 
subsequent phases of its value chain. 

The Company has auditing procedures, both internal 
and external, as well as internal control procedures to 
verify and oversee that the established requirements are 
met.

Different areas participate in the process of managing 
material impacts related to workers in the value chain, 
supported by financial and technological resources:

• Contracting areas: Responsible for adopting the 
minimum diligence measures based on the risk 
associated with the third party prior to the 
formalization of the commercial relationship and for 
as long as it continues.

• Compliance function: Responsible for preparing a 
report on compliance, in the event that significant 
evidence is identified that may be indicative of 
conduct that is unethical, incompatible with or 
contrary to Repsol’s principles of action, identifying 
Repsol’s main risks of compliance and, where 
appropriate, including appropriate risk mitigation 
measures, in order to prevent or mitigate the 
materialization of the identified risks. It is also 
responsible for managing the Ethics and Compliance 
Channel and, if necessary, for making a proposal of 
recommended improvement and action plans.

• Safety and Environment function: Responsible for 

defining which activities and purchases are of high risk 
in safety and the environment, as well as evaluating 
the risk and other significant aspects of suppliers prior 
to contracting.

13 The calculations of all the metrics reported are detailed in Methodology for value chain workers reporting and metrics at the end of this section.

• Purchasing and Contracting Function: Responsible for

The time horizon tied to this action is not limited, given
