Training and awareness for those involved in 
purchasing processes

In order to promote ESG training for employees in the 
Value Chain (VC), Repsol has collaborated with the 
Spanish Network of the United Nations Global Compact 
to boost the participation of small and medium-sized 
companies in its global supply chain in a training 
program. 

The training itinerary covers general aspects of 
sustainability, SDGs, the 10 Principles of the Global 
Compact (human rights, labor standards, environment 
and the fight against corruption), communication and 
sustainability reporting.

Repsol has collaborated since the beginning of the 
program in 2023 with the aim of training workers in 
SMEs integrated into the supply chains of large Spanish 
companies that act as promoters of the project. 

Likewise, objectives are established annually to train 
employees in the Purchasing department in the areas of 
ethics and conduct, cybersecurity risks, and other 
pertinent sustainability topics.

In industrial complexes, LPG factories and renewable 
generation assets, mandatory training is provided on the 
risks of the facilities where workers are employed, 
regardless of whether they are company personnel or 
not.  

The time horizon tied to this action is not limited, given 
that it is integral to the ESG strategy

Positive impact on the supply chain 

Repsol promotes the participation of local companies in 
its network of suppliers and contractors and, in doing so, 
favors the generation of employment and the 
protection of workers’ rights in local communities, 
improving economic and social development in the 
areas in which it operates. 

Local employment makes it possible to establish links 
that promote sustainability and joint growth. 
Geographical proximity optimizes logistics efficiency 
and allows Repsol and its local partners to adapt quickly 
to changing market dynamics. In addition to the direct 
contribution to employment, Repsol creates indirect job 
opportunities through its projects.  

During 2025, 82% (in 2024, it accounted for 85%) of the 
total volume of the Company’s purchases has been 
made locally, covering a wide variety of services 
essential for operations across all geographies. The 
number of local suppliers represents 85% (in 2024, it 
accounted for 85%) of the Company’s total. The 
distribution by region is as follows:

3.2.3 Metrics and targets

Targets related to managing material negative impacts, 
driving positive impacts, and managing material risks and 
opportunities (S2-5)  

Repsol works with its value chain to implement criteria 
and initiatives in the areas of human rights, the 
environment, safety and security, the Sustainable 
Development Goals, and the circular economy. 

To this end, it defines environmental, social, and safety 
objectives throughout the purchasing and contracting 
process. These objectives are reviewed annually 
together with the affected areas (Sustainability, 
Business, Compliance, etc. and, in some cases, suppliers), 
and are prioritized according to impact.  

These objectives are reflected in Repsol’s Global 
Sustainability Plan, which includes short- and medium-
term objectives for the business, supply chain, 
employees and society. The Company monitors them 
and, using the defined metrics, reports annually on their 
performance.

Objectives for workers in the value chain

Strengthen good sustainability practices in the supply chain of the 
Low-Carbon Generation business by measuring the environmental, 
social, and governance management of suppliers, through the 
evaluation of the sustainability index, which considers aspects of 
safety, environment, human rights, climate change and pollution.

M

METHODOLOGY

Methodology for value chain workers 
reporting and metrics

Number of suppliers that are subject to reputational 
analysis

Absolute metric calculated from the accounting of reputational 
analyses carried out. Metric referring to the management scope 
of the Purchasing function calculated according to the 
Company’s internal regulatory criteria. Metric verified by the 
Company’s auditor in the Consolidated Management Report of 
previous years. 

Number of performance evaluations of suppliers and 
contractors and Number of suppliers and contractors 
that have undergone a performance evaluation 

Absolute metrics, they count the number of evaluations 
performed by the contractor as well as the number of suppliers 
corresponding to the evaluations, respectively. Both metrics refer 
to the management perimeter of the Purchasing function, 
calculated according to the Company’s internal regulatory 
criteria. Metrics verified by the Company’s auditor in the 
Consolidated Management Report of previous years. 

The number of suppliers with whom the contractual 
relationship has been terminated as a result of social 
impacts metric

Absolute metric, referring to the management scope of the 
Purchasing function calculated according to the Company’s 
internal regulatory criteria. Metric verified by an external auditor 
in the Consolidated Management Reports of previous years. In 
2025, no contractual relationship was terminated as a result of 
social impacts.

The number of suppliers with whom the contractual 
relationship has been terminated as a result of negative 
environmental impacts metric 
Absolute metric, referring to the management scope of the 
Purchasing function calculated according to the Company’s 

internal regulatory criteria. Metric verified by an external auditor 

in the Consolidated Management Reports of previous years. In 

2025, no contractual relationship was terminated as a result of 

negative environmental impacts.

Volume of local purchases (%)

Calculated as a ratio between purchases (volume awarded) in 
which the supplier’s country coincides with the country of the 
operation and the total purchases made in the country of 
operation. Metric referring to the management scope of the 
Purchasing function calculated according to the Company’s 
internal regulatory criteria. Metric verified by the Company’s 
auditor in the Consolidated Management Reports of previous 
years.  

Percentage of local suppliers

Calculated as a ratio between the number of local suppliers (in 
which the supplier’s country coincides with the country of the 
operation), and the total number of suppliers in the country in 
question. Metric referring to the management scope of the 
Purchasing function calculated according to the Company’s 
internal regulatory criteria. Metric verified by the Company’s 
auditor in the Consolidated Management Reports of previous 
years.

% Volume awarded corresponding to suppliers with 
sustainability indicator

Calculated as a relative value between the volume awarded to 
suppliers with a quantified sustainability indicator and that 
corresponding to the total number of suppliers awarded in the 
reporting year in the Low-Carbon Generation business. Metric 
referring to the management scope of the Purchasing function 
calculated according to the Company’s internal regulatory 
criteria. Internal metric, not verified by an external auditor. The 
loading of the information by the supplier, the calculations and 
the visualization by Repsol are done with support on external 
web platforms. No considerations are made in the calculation of 
the metric. This objective was defined exclusively by Repsol, 
although the need to define actions to improve the score of the 
sustainability indicator has been agreed with strategic suppliers.

% Volume awarded corresponding to suppliers with 
prior evaluation in terms of safety and environmental 
(Industrial) 

Calculated as a relative value between the Volume awarded to 
suppliers with prior safety and environmental assessment and the 
Volume awarded in critical safety and environmental activities in 
the reporting year in the Industrial business. Metric referring to 
the management scope of the Purchasing function calculated 
according to the Company’s internal regulatory criteria. Internal 
metric, not verified by an external auditor. No other 
considerations are made in the calculation of the metric. 
Objective defined exclusively by Repsol, without involving third 
parties. A baseline cannot be established because a new process 
is being implemented.

 % Volume awarded corresponding to suppliers with 
prior safety and environment assessment (E&P)

Calculated as a relative value between the Volume awarded to 
suppliers with prior safety and environment assessment and the 
Volume awarded in high-risk safety and environment activities in 
the reporting year in the Upstream business. Metric referring to 
the management scope of the Purchasing function calculated 
according to the Company’s internal regulatory criteria. Internal 
metric, not verified by an external auditor. No other 
considerations are made in the calculation of the metric. 
Objective defined exclusively by Repsol, without involving third 
parties. A baseline cannot be established because a new process 
is being implemented.

2025  Consolidated Management Report

3.3 Affected communities

3.3.1 Strategy
Interests and views of stakeholders (ESRS 2 SBM-2)

Repsol seeks to build and maintain solid relationships 
with its various stakeholders based on an honest, 
proactive, and transparent dialogue that expresses 
respect and seeks shared benefit through contribution to 
local, socioeconomic, and environmental development. 

Interaction with stakeholders is key to maintaining the 
strength of the Company’s business model. For this 
reason, work is done to identify and understand their 
expectations regarding Repsol’s sustainability and 
human rights performance, and thus be able to 
incorporate them into decision-making processes. 

The communities in the area of influence of the activities 
constitute one of the main stakeholders in the 
Company’s business strategy. In order to define the area 
of influence, Repsol applies a criterion of physical 
proximity to its operations and the environment that 
may be affected by them. In addition, resources and 
processes are allocated for managing human rights-
related risks, impacts, and opportunities.

Before commencing any activity that may involve 
interaction or impact on the community, Repsol 
conducts a social baseline study that helps to learn 
about and examine the context and social, economic, 
and cultural realities of the territory. Based on these 
studies and in collaboration with local authorities and 
social organizations, stakeholders are identified to find 
out their expectations, needs, concerns and aspirations, 
as well as their vulnerability to impacts.

These groups include neighbors, local authorities and 
associations, customers and suppliers, as well as all 
those legitimate rights holders on whom the Company 
may have an impact, with a special focus on the most 
vulnerable groups, namely those who, due to their 
nature or intrinsic or external environmental conditions, 
may find it more difficult to enjoy their fundamental 
human rights or see them compromised. 

Notably, indigenous communities are considered to be 
especially vulnerable to the Company’s activities. Repsol 
respects and values the unique nature of these 
communities, their right to participation and their rights 
to lands and natural resources, as well as the right to 
their autonomy and self-government and to promote 
their cultures. Actions in environments with indigenous 
communities are governed by Convention 169 of the 
International Labour Organization (ILO), regardless of 
whether or not it is envisaged in the legislation of each 
country.

As a result of communication with key stakeholders, and 
especially those who may be considered vulnerable, 
actual and potential impacts are identified and the 
business strategy is defined in this regard. 

Repsol takes into account the opinion of nearby 
communities about the impacts that its operations may 
generate. Early engagement allows modifications to be 
incorporated preventively in the development of 
projects when necessary to limit impacts. In particular, it 
is common in Low-Carbon Generation projects to 
reconsider the location of installations. And, as a unique 
case, we have the no-go decision that was reached, in a 
culturally sensitive area for the Wayuu Indigenous 
Community during an exploration project in La Guajira, 
Colombia.  

Under the Global Plan and the Local Sustainability 
Plans, annual objectives are established that take 
account of the expectations of local communities.

Material impacts, risks, and opportunities and their 
interaction with strategy and business model (ESRS 2 
SBM-3) 

Repsol has carried out a double materiality assessment, 
in which the material impacts, risks, and opportunities in 
relation to sustainability have been identified. 

This assessment is based on the findings made during 
the implementation of the human rights due diligence 
process to identify, prevent, mitigate, and remediate the 
risks and social impacts associated with its operations. 
This internal processes management system has a 
preventive approach and is based on carrying out social 
impact assessments (on human rights), the 
implementation of social action plans, their monitoring 
and accountability. Moreover, Repsol has consultation 
mechanisms in place to encourage the participation of 
local communities in the process, as well as channels for 
reporting grievances and conflict resolution.

This process includes the following actions: 

• Exhaustive analysis of the context and the social, 

economic and cultural realities of each area as well as 
potentially affected stakeholders, analyzing their 
degree of vulnerability.

•

Identification and assessment of risks, and of negative 
and positive impacts. 

• Design and implementation of mitigation plans for 

risks and negative impacts, or remediation when they 
have materialized. 

• Development and implementation of consultation 

mechanisms, grievance mechanisms, and other 
communication tools. 

•

Identification of social opportunities to maximize 
positive impacts.

These actions are combined interaction with rights 
holders, local communities and other stakeholders, such 
as NGOs and local administrations, foundations and 
institutions from different fields in all the operated 
projects. This engagement focuses on effective risk and 
impact management and on improving the quality of life 
and sustainable development of the communities in the 
vicinity of operations, thus contributing to obtaining 
and maintaining the social license to operate.

As a result of the materiality assessment, the following 
negative impacts derived from the Company’s activity 
have been identified as material:

• Operations carried out by Repsol or its contractors 

may negatively affect indigenous communities. These 
impacts may include the alteration of their traditional 
ways of life, cultural heritage, or effects on the use of 
lands and natural resources (IRO-40).

• The change in land use caused by our projects can 
generate disruptions in the quality, way of life and 
rights of communities (impact on the landscape, 
eventual increase in the cost of land, depreciation of 
assets belonging to local communities) (IRO-42).

Risk management

Repsol conducts impact assessments, the results of

Local communities

The analysis of human rights risks is part of the 
Company’s integrated risk management system, through 
which the different businesses and corporate areas 
identify, measure, manage and supervise the main 
material risks. 

This system is based on a Risk Management Policy that 
identifies the risks of rejection, including ethical and 
behavioral risks, such as those associated with human 
rights abuses. Their probability of occurrence or 
associated impact must be minimized, according to the 
commitment set out in Repsol’s Human Rights and 
Community Relations Policy and also its Code of Ethics 
and Business Conduct. 

In accordance with the Enterprise Risk Management 
System, the risk profile is reported for formal validation 
at least annually to the units responsible for direct risk 
management in day-to-day operations. These units are 
responsible for promoting, when appropriate, the 
corresponding actions or developing the necessary 
control mechanisms to align the risk profile with the 
expectations of the organization, according to the risk 
tolerance statement reflected in the Risk Management 
Policy. Similarly, and at least annually, the Audit and 
Control Committee evaluates the effectiveness of the 
management and control systems for these risks and 
ESG risks are also presented to the Sustainability 
Committee. In addition, the Executive Committee is part 
of the Enterprise Risk Management System. Accordingly, 
its functions include supervising the Company’s 
performance in matters related to these risks.

Other supervisory units include the Sustainability 
Department, which promotes the integration of risk 
vision to effectively influence decision-making 
processes, and the risk auditing units, which evaluate 
the design and operation of risk management and 
control systems. 

Repsol uses its influence to extend the implementation 
of human rights due diligence throughout the value 
chain. 

In non-operated assets, commitments, policies and 
practices are shared with partners and they are offered 
the Company’s knowledge, experience and techniques 
necessary for alignment with corporate standards. 

Among partners, suppliers, and contractors, Repsol also 
promotes respect for human rights through the 
inclusion of specific clauses in its contracts, the conduct 
of social audits, and training and awareness on human 
rights. For more information, see Workers in the value 
chain.

The Company collaborates with other organizations and 
industry initiatives to share best practices and make 
progress in the implementation of international 
standards. In particular, Repsol actively participates in 
different IPIECA working groups, including those on 
social responsibility and SDGs. In addition, this year, for 
the first time, Repsol carried out a social impact 
measurement based on the Prosper4ALL methodology 
and received the Socially Committed seal. 

The commitment to human rights is embraced by the 
Board of Directors and the Executive Committee, and it 
encompasses all the people who work at the Company, 
all of whom must respect human rights in all their 
decisions and activities. This goal also applies to 
contractors, suppliers and partners. For more 
information, see Material impacts, risks, and 
opportunities and their interaction with the strategy 
and business model.

3.3.2 Impact, risk and opportunity 
management
Policies related to affected communities (S3-1) 

Repsol incorporates respect for human rights 
recognized in international treaties and standards as a 
core principle of its policy. To this end, it defines its 
regulatory framework based on the most exacting 
international standards, such as the United Nations 
Guiding Principles on Business and Human Rights, and 
on permanent and transparent dialogue with all its 
stakeholders.

The Human Rights and Community Relations Policy, 
approved in 2008 and updated in 2023 to adapt it to 
new regulatory standards, is the reference framework 
that embodies the commitment of top management and 
the guidelines for the management of material impacts, 
risks, and opportunities in this area. It expresses the 
corporate commitment to: 

•

Integrating human rights due diligence into all of the 
Company’s business activities and relationships, in all 
countries where it operates, and in all phases of the 
life cycle, including decommissioning. 

• Remedying negative impacts on human rights 

generated by the activity. 

• Building strong relationships and respect the human 

rights of all people potentially affected by the 
Company’s activity, including the communities in the 
areas of influence, the people who work at Repsol, as 
well as its customers, among others.

• Contributing to the sustainable socio-economic 

development of local communities and the 
preservation of their environment. 

• Working with partners and suppliers in any activity to 

drive compliance with human rights due diligence and 
to remediate adverse impacts related to operations, 
products or services. 

The implementation of the policy is the responsibility of 
the Company’s Executive Committee and the process of 
monitoring and oversight of its application and result is 
carried out by the Audit and Internal Control Unit. The 
scope of application of the policy extends to all 
activities and business relationships, and in all phases of 
the life cycle of activities, from preliminary studies, 
design, construction, commissioning, production and 
decommissioning or transfer of assets. 

The results of the double materiality assessment were 
taken into account when drawing up the policy, thus 
ensuring that the demands of stakeholders such as 
investors, the financial community and rating agencies, 
among others, are taken account of.

Knowing this policy and the processes derived from it is 
key to proper risk and impact management. During 
2025, in addition to the 22,126 people who completed 
the ethics and conduct course, 214 people took specific 
courses on human rights.

Furthermore, specific actions are carried out to 
disseminate the policy within the framework of dialogue 
with stakeholders at the beginning of the projects, in 
particularly sensitive contexts with the presence of 
indigenous communities, or in those with a greater risk 
of impact or significant impacts. For example, in the 
Bolivia business unit, regular activities are carried out to 
raise awareness among contractors and communities 
about human rights and, in particular, about the 
commitments set out in our Human Rights and 
Community Relations Policy.  

The Code of Ethics and Business Conduct, which is 
based on our values, regulates our responsibilities in 
terms of harassment and discrimination and equal 
opportunities, among other matters. 

The Code of Ethics and Business Conduct for Suppliers, 
which is available on Repsol’s corporate website, lays 
down the minimum principles that the Company expects 
from its suppliers. Prior to entering into a contractual 
relationship with a supplier, The Company seeks to 
promote their awareness of and compliance with these 
principles throughout the commercial relationship. The 
code’s acceptance is required during the registration 
process in order to work with the Company. The Code is 
oriented toward mutual benefit, and includes, in the 
social sphere, obligations such as the rejection of child 
labor and forced labor, freedom of association and the 
right to collective bargaining, as well as ethical behavior 
and measures against bribery, corruption and conflicts 
of interest.

Regarding the impact that suppliers may have on the 
communities in the area of influence, this code explicitly 
includes obligations set out in the Human Rights and 
Community Relations Policy: 

• Recognize and respect internationally recognized 

human rights and set out in this policy. 

• Respect the rights, cultural diversity and customs of 
local communities, indigenous peoples and other 
vulnerable groups that may be influenced by their 
activities. 

• Workers and any stakeholders must have at their 

disposal a grievance mechanism, be suitably informed 
of it, which allows them to communicate any 
complaint or breach of the code without risk of 
retaliation, and which allows its communication and 
management in a consistent, fair, accessible and 
prompt manner. 

In 2025, a comprehensive review of the internal 
standard on human rights and the relationship with 
communities was carried out, with two objectives:

•

Incorporating consolidated good practices derived 
from the implementation of due diligence in the 
different businesses and those requested by different 
stakeholders.

• Fostering alignment with new regulations regarding 

sustainability reporting and due diligence in 
sustainability. 

The degree of detail in the definition of the due 
diligence process has been increased, the section on 
partners and contractors has been reinforced, the need 
for stakeholder participation is emphasized, lessons 
learned in the remediation process derived from the La 
Pampilla spill have been included, as well as specific 
mentions of vulnerable groups such as children or 
women.

Repsol has been a signatory of the United Nations 
Voluntary Principles on Security and Human Rights since 
2013, with the aim of guaranteeing the security of 
operations in sensitive or conflict zones through working 
procedures that support respect for human rights.

Processes for engaging with affected communities (S3-2)  

Repsol applies the Environmental, Social, and Health 
Impact Assessment (ESHIA) standard, which aims to 
ensure that environmental, social, and health impacts 
are properly identified and mitigated. This process is 
carried out through a participatory and proactive 
dialogue with stakeholders, providing them with 
relevant information and engaging them in the actions 
to be undertaken as defined in the prevention, 
mitigation and surveillance plan derived from the 
impact studies. Measures to repair environmental, 
social and health impacts are defined in a participatory 
manner with the people potentially affected, taking 
into account their needs and priorities, without being 
limited to direct monetary compensation. 

Here are some examples of participation processes:

• At the Low-Carbon Generation and Upstream 

businesses, there is the role of community liaison 
officer, who is responsible for establishing and 
maintaining a dialogue between the Company and the 
community in which it is operating, based on trust and 
respect.

•

In the Industrial area, there are channels for direct 
dialogue, such as public advisory panels, in which the 
concerns and interests of the neighbors are 
addressed. Each panel is a permanent channel of 
dialogue between society and the Company; an 
advisory body that seeks to improve the population’s 
knowledge of the Company’s activity and promote 
informative actions for a better awareness of the 
industrial operation and answering questions that are 
frequently asked by society about the industry. 

• Collaboration with local institutions that safeguard 

the interests of the community in order to manage the 
risks, impacts, and opportunities of the activity. 

In addition to these mechanisms for consultation and 
participation with local communities, there are local 
mechanisms and channels for reporting and resolving 
conflicts in the form of operational grievance 
mechanisms that allow us to anticipate potential 
controversies, by gathering stakeholders’ concerns, 
doubts, queries and complaints.

In the particular case of the management of indigenous 
communities, a key aspect is respect for their right to 
free, prior and informed consultation. Repsol verifies the 
level of acceptance of indigenous organizations and 
actively seeks the consent of those potentially affected.

Repsol operates in areas featuring the presence of 
indigenous communities in six countries: Algeria, one 
operation; Bolivia, one operation; Chile, one operation; 
United States, two operations; Libya, two operations; 
and Peru, one operation.

All of the above-mentioned operations have one of the 
following elements: public consultation and 
consultation plans, baseline studies, social impact 
assessments and action plans, relocation plans, 
community development plans, grievance and 
complaint procedures and other community information 
center documents. 100% of significant assets have local 
community development programs based on local 
community needs and stakeholder engagement plans 
based on their geographical distribution. 

As in 2024, no incidents involving violations of the rights 
of indigenous peoples were recorded in 2025 through 
the Company’s channels.

A key tool that Repsol uses to enhance positive impacts 
on the communities affected by its activity are social 
investment initiatives, of which entrepreneurship 
projects in local communities are prioritized to avoid 
future dependencies. The goal is to promote sustainable 
socioeconomic development through planning based on 
dialogue and consensus with communities, which are 
factors that determine the scope of investment.

This process is regulated by the internal standard for 
the management of social investment, which is aimed at 
guaranteeing transparency and enhancing positive 
impact.

For more information on social investment, see Metrics 
related to affected groups.

In order to manage potential impacts arising from the 
relationship between the communities and the security 
forces that protect our assets, Repsol requires that 
100% of the employees of private security companies 
who provide services at its facilities have human rights 
training. In accordance with the Voluntary Principles on 
Security and Human Rights to which the Company 
adheres, this training is verified or courses are held for 
security personnel. 

Processes to remediate negative impacts and channels for 
affected communities to raise concerns (S3-3) 

Repsol has grievance mechanisms designed with the 
participation of communities, taking into account their 
customs and traditions so that they are accessible and 
effective. 

Relevant complaints are handled at the local level and 
transferred to the expert team in communities and 
human rights of the Sustainability Department, and, if 
necessary, they are escalated to the Executive 
Committee for further scrutiny and handling. In addition, 
as set out in the Code of Ethics and Business Conduct, 
any form of retaliation is prohibited.

In 2025, a total of 56 complaints were received from local 
communities (excluding complaints relating to the incident 
at Terminal 2 of the La Pampilla refinery in Peru, of which 
82 were this year) and 98% of these complaints were 
resolved.     

Community complaints

Moreover, there were no significant disputes during the 
period with local communities and indigenous peoples 
or non-technical shutdowns as a result of the 
Organization’s activities. There were also no involuntary 
resettlements in the activities operated by the 
Company.

In the event of significant negative impacts, the 
Company conducts a due diligence process to manage 
remediation in accordance with the United Nations 
Guiding Principles on Business and Human Rights. 

Following the accidental spill that occurred at 
Terminal 2 of the La Pampilla refinery, a Social 
Management Plan was launched. The remediation phase 
concluded with individual compensation provided to 
more than 99% of the affected persons identified by the 
Government.

The third phase of the Social Management Plan began in 
2023, continuing in 2024 and 2025 with the ImpulsaRed 
program for the development and socioeconomic 
recovery of the communities affected by the spill. The 
design of the Social Management Plan was fed with the 
results of the human rights impact assessment carried 
out in 2022 for the five districts affected by the spill: 
Ventanilla, Santa Rosa, Ancón, Aucallama and Chancay, 
and in permanent dialogue with the target population 
of the program. 

It is an initiative born from collaboration with affected 
communities through field work with workshops held in 
the five districts and interviews with community 
representatives. The program promotes training and the 
creation of enterprises. 

Meanwhile, a grievance mechanism was designed and 
implemented with different channels: in-person 
(reporting desk, community liaison officers), telephone, 
electronic messaging, email inbox and Repsol 
Commitment portal. In 2025, remediation management 
has continued, dealing with 82 new claims (all of which 
have been resolved).

In addition, Repsol makes itself available to national and 
international human rights protection bodies and 
institutions, whether judicial or extra-judicial, to work 
with them to seek out solutions. Repsol not only 
commits not to hinder their normal operations, but also 
to establish a collaborative relationship that benefits 
the soundness of the process and, therefore, the 
reparation of the people affected. 

Repsol also works closely with partners or contractors in 
the event of any impact, and supports them in ensuring 
effective remediation for all affected people. 

In 2025, there were no severe human rights incidents. 
The Company continues to carry out actions related to 
the remediation and management of opportunities 
related to the spill that took place at the La Pampilla 
facility in 2022. For more information, see Processes for 
engaging with affected communities.

Taking action on material impacts on affected 
communities, and approaches to managing material risks 
and pursuing material opportunities related to affected 
communities, and effectiveness of those actions (S3-4) 

Repsol conducts Environmental, Social and Health 
Impact Assessments (ESHIA) on its new projects and 
assets, with the aim of identifying and adequately 
mitigating the impacts through measures included in 
the prevention and mitigation plans.

Before undertaking a new project or activity, a 
preliminary analysis is carried out: it addresses the 
social, environmental, and health situation, the legal 
requirements and the vulnerability of the environment 
and of stakeholders. This is the starting process for 
identifying and evaluating the risks and impacts and 
designing the necessary mitigation measures, involving 
the relevant areas, in accordance with the Company’s 
internal regulations in force since 2011. 

The social impact assessment takes into account, among 
other aspects, the right to land and its natural 
resources, the right to a healthy environment and the 
right to protection of the identity and culture of 
communities, as well as the special rights of indigenous 
communities. Since 2014, a proprietary methodology for 
assessing human rights impacts has been available. 

Before starting each activity, viable alternative designs 
that minimize land acquisition and restrictions on land 
and subsoil use are considered, to avoid resettlements 
and adverse impacts on communities and land users. If 
the above is not possible, an assessment of potential 
impacts is carried out and consideration given to 
whether continuing with the project is appropriate.

Seven new impact assessments addressing social and 
human rights aspects were carried out in 2025 relating 
to the assets of the Renewables business in Alto Bierzo 
Sil, Benavieja, Bufarán, Oencia, Páramo del Sil and 
Ampliación, Eólica Ponte and Cerrato, all of them in 
Spain.

As in 2024, in 2025, no human rights violations of 
communities nearby our operations were reported.

In addition, Repsol undertakes social investment 
initiatives in order to share value with society in general 
and, in particular, through projects that benefit the 
communities near the operations, and especially 
vulnerable groups that may be identified. These 
initiatives, including Impulsared, have contributed to 
achieving the goal of contributing to the 2030 Agenda 
and benefiting 10 million people from 2021 to 2025. 
The following table provides more information on these 
actions:

in January 2022, the activities continue in the last phase 
of development.

alongside national and international communities and 
entities, the lines of action were identified of 
Impulsared, a social and economic reactivation program 
in the five districts of the area affected by the spill.

This is a comprehensive Repsol program that promotes 
the training of technical, business, and innovative skills 
and sustainable development in the districts of 
Ventanilla, Santa Rosa, Ancón, Aucallama, and Chancay 
in the department of Lima (Peru).

It is expected to improve the lives of both the participants 
and their communities. In addition to technical training, it 
helps and encourages the creation of companies to 
generate income in a sustainable way over time. 

It consists of four lines of action:

• Fishing, commerce and crafts.

• Wellness and nutrition.

• Environment and circular economy

• Sustainable tourism.

The program began in 2023 with an initial pilot project 
and, to promote it, strategic alliances were entered into 
with 14 local entities.

The total investment to date has been more than US$4 
million, reaching 10,000 direct and indirect beneficiaries, 
resulting in the creation of 52 enterprises with nearly 
400 partners.

The 2025-2026 phase of the Impulsared project is 
currently underway.

3.3.3 Metrics and targets
Targets related to managing material negative impacts, 
advancing positive impacts, and managing material risks 
and opportunities (S3-5) 

Last year, the target was set to implement an action 
plan to comply with the Sustainability Due Diligence 
Directive. In 2025, all scheduled activities were fulfilled 
and the decision was reached to redefine this objective 
by setting a more ambitious target that aims to expand 
the scope of due diligence processes to a greater 
number of activities, including those considered low risk. 
Based on this, a due diligence review plan has been 
established that has the following milestones:

• Digitization of the social reporting process. 

• Evaluation and improvement of the effectiveness of 

grievance mechanisms.

•

Implementation of the due diligence process in the 
value chain.

• Analysis of tools and methodologies to evaluate the 
effectiveness of engagement and the perception of 
communities in relation to our social management 
processes.

•

Implementation of the due diligence process in low-
risk businesses.

In addition, upholding the Repsol’s commitment to 
generate a positive impact on society through social 
investment, an objective has been defined aimed at 
promoting social investment associated with the 
Company’s commitment to a fair transition.

These objectives affect the entire Company. In relation 
to the previous objective of drawing up an action plan 
to comply with the Sustainability Due Diligence 
Directive, this year the scheduled activities have been 
completed:

• Update of the internal regulations on Relations with 

Communities and Human Rights (approved in 
December 2025).

•

Inventory of own operations, subsidiaries and business 
partners related to the activity chain.

• Progress in the definition of a risk control model 

pending the publication of the final text of the CSDD 
Directive.

Repsol annually defines global and local human rights 
objectives, which are integrated into the global 
sustainability plan and the 16 Local Plans for 2025  
defined by various areas and businesses. The objectives 
of these plans are established through proposals and 
dialogue with the local business units according to their 
objectives and taking into account the particular 
contexts and characteristics of the stakeholders in each 
case. The Company monitors them and reports annually 
on their performance.

Metrics related to affected groups

For more information, see Methodology for affected 
communities reporting and metrics.

Mandatory social investment

Repsol makes contributions due to legal or regulatory 
requirements of the country or that are stipulated in the 
operating contract. These contributions can be 
managed entirely by the Company (through social 
programs) or by a third party (national hydrocarbons 
company, government institution or agency, etc.) to 
which a payment of the amount is made.

M

METHODOLOGY

Methodology for affected communities 
reporting and metrics 

Social investment

This section includes the social investment values of the Repsol 
Group and its Foundation, as well as those of certain assets that 
are not managed or controlled by Repsol, but which are 
considered material for the Company and for the community. In 
these cases, only the expense disbursed by the Company is 
reported.

These social investment values are presented with the following 
breakdowns: countries where social investment is made, type of 
social investment, type of contribution and contribution to the 
SDGs. 

The Company’s social investment has been managed since 2019 
through a social investment tool, where managers upload 
project information, the number of beneficiaries, the type of 
entity receiving the investment, the SDGs associated with it 
and, if any, the management costs or in-kind contributions. 

Data from the different businesses and countries is included in 
local currency, and once the information consolidation process 
is complete, the conversion is made to euros using the 
Company’s official exchange rate.

3.4 Consumers and end 
customers1,2

3.4.1 Strategy3

Interests and views of stakeholders (ESRS 2 SBM-2)

Repsol has evolved towards a multi-energy proposition 
that offers a comprehensive solution, setting aside a 
product-centric vision in order to focus on people and 
their energy needs: electricity, gas, mobility and self-
generation.

For Repsol, the multi-energy strategy is a key element in 
promoting the energy transition and decarbonization in a 
way that integrates efficiency, profitability, and 
sustainability. This approach recognizes the transitional 
role that conventional energy sources, such as oil and 
gas, continue to play in the progress towards a more 
diversified energy system.

In 2025, the multi-energy strategy take the form of a 
brand upgrade, with a new visual and verbal identity. 
New protocols were established across physical, 
telephone, and digital channels. The aim is to 
measurably improve the user experience, not through 
aspirational messaging, but through verifiable actions 
that prioritize functionality and transparency, such as 
the creation of new spaces for customers at service 
stations or the new design of the Waylet front page with 
a new information structure that allows users easier 
access to all services.

Material impacts, risks, and opportunities4 and their 
interaction with strategy and business model (ESRS 2 
SBM-3) 

The double materiality assessment carried out includes 
the assessment of impacts, risks, and opportunities 
based on their interaction with Repsol customers and 
consumers. This analysis identifies both the positive and 
negative impacts of the Company. Specifically, in 
relation to customers and end-consumers, after an 
analysis of all the risks, opportunities, and impacts, the 
following positive impacts have been deemed material: 

• Greater ease for customers to self-manage their 

incidents through platforms such as chatbots, online  
forms, etc. (IRO-44).

•

•

Improvement of the customer purchasing experience 
through the transparency and availability of extensive 
information in digital customer contact environments 
(IRO-45).

Increased safety throughout the product life cycle 
owing to the identification of potential hazards and 
risks in the design phase (IRO-46).

These impacts affect all types of customers equally, and 
Repsol maintains similar communication channels with 
all of them. The case of vulnerable electricity customers 
is noteworthy, for whom a specific protocol has been 
established for listening to and managing their 
incidents, as explained in Processes for engaging with 
consumers and end users (S4-2).

3.4.2 Managing impacts, risks 
and opportunities
Policies related to consumers and end users (S4-1)5

The Sustainability Policy is a key Repsol policy that applies 
to all the Company’s customers, consumers, and end users.

This policy includes the following commitments:

• Knowing, analyzing and managing the expectations of 

the Company’s different stakeholders, including 
customers and society in general. Repsol has different 
communication channels, available and accessible, to 
inform, involve, and engage in ongoing dialogue with 
its stakeholders.

• Responding transparently to stakeholders through 
economic, environmental and social performance 
indicators that are published in corporate reports 
following international reporting standards.

• Promoting and requiring sustainability commitments 

in accordance/similar to those defined internally 
throughout its value chain: customers, partners, 
suppliers of products and services and other 
stakeholders.

In line with these commitments, and within the framework 
of Repsol’s relationship with consumers and customers, 
the Company has internal regulations in the field of 
product safety, specifically the standard for safe product 
management, the application of which is the responsibility 
of the Director General of Energy Transition, Technology, 
Institutional Affairs, and Deputy CEO.

This standard defines the requirements and 
responsibilities for the proper management of risks 
during the design and supply of products, and during the 
manufacture, handling, marketing and final use of 
products that may affect the health and safety of 
people, facilities and the environment.

The standard applies to all chemical products (hereinafter, 
"products"), whether substances or mixtures, designed, 
sourced, manufactured, handled, used, and marketed by 
Repsol. Therefore, areas within the business and areas of 
research and development that are in charge of the 
design, supply, manufacture, marketing or use of the 
products are responsible for complying with the 
standard and with specific regulations (including 
legislation) applicable to product safety.

The main objectives of this standard are to guide risk 
management to avoid any harm to people and the 
environment. In particular, the aim is to prevent major 
accidents through proper process safety management, 
and to involve employees, contractors and other 
stakeholders in continuous improvement and in the 
definition of health and safety management programs 
and systems.

1 The information provided in this standard is mainly that relating to the management carried out in Spain and Portugal, which encompasses most 

of Repsol’s contacts and users registered in the different Repsol apps, specifying the information relating to Mexico and Peru. 

2 According to the CSRD Directive, “customer and end consumer”refers to individuals who acquire, consume or use goods and services for personal use.
3 This reporting requirement includes information that affects the Company’s global strategy, therefore, includes Mexico and Peru.
4  In  the  assessment  of  impacts,  risks,  and  opportunities  related  to  consumers  and  end  customers  carried  out  through  the  double  materiality 

assessment, no material risks and opportunities have been detected, so the subsection focuses on impact management.

5 This reporting requirement includes information that affects the Company as a whole, therefore, it includes Mexico and Peru.

Repsol has a Human Rights and Community Relations 
Policy in which it pledges to respect the rights 
internationally recognized in international treaties6 and 
standards7 on business and human rights in all its 
activities and business relationships. Repsol is therefore 
committed to respecting the human rights of its 
customers, and rejecting any type of discrimination 
against customers and users of products or services and 
respecting their right to privacy at all times, while 
protecting and making proper use of their entrusted 
personal data.

In 2025, there were no severe human rights impacts 
related to consumers or end users, as indicated in 
Governance.

Processes for engaging with consumers and end users 
(S4-2)

Repsol seeks a standardized and comprehensive service 
model with customers and consumers. This transversal 
management is led by the Chief Customer Officer8, who is 
the senior operational role for managing its proper 
implementation. Customer service is the responsibility of 
all the people in the Organization and the general 
management offers coverage to the different lines of 
business. Specifically, it is the Customer Care function 
that carries out comprehensive management for the 
different businesses. 

Customer service channels deployed 

Customer service is structured through various 
interaction channels where quality, listening skills and 
understanding of user needs are prioritized, while 
seeking personalization and customer satisfaction in 
every case.

The main contact channel in 2025 was the telephone, 
accounting for 58% of the service (2024: 57%), 
compared with 42% (2024: 43%) for all other channels 
(email, apps, web forms, chatbot, etc.). This channel 
provides consumers with different free customer service 
telephone numbers which they can use to contact for 
inquiries related to the Company’s products and 
services. In 2025, in Spain and Portugal, a total of 
4,272,964 calls were served (2024: 4,418,326), with a 
service level (number of interactions attended to total 
interactions received) of 89% and with an average 
waiting time (average time that the customer waits 
before being served) of 116 seconds.     

In addition, for those customers who require autonomy, 
Repsol provides digital channels, including automated 
telephone services to manage transactions at the 
Electricity and Gas business or requesting orders in LPG. 

Meanwhile, further progress was made toward certain 
initiatives that had got underway in 2024. For instance, 
customers of the Waylet app can use a Waylet chatbot, 
while LPG customers can request orders via WhatsApp. 

Moreover, Electricity and Gas customers could make 
payments on electricity and gas bills via WhatsApp starting 
in 2025. 

In addition to these digital functionalities, there are 
other mobile apps that Repsol makes available to its 
customers, such as Waylet, Vivit, Repsol Contigo, Repsol 
You and Pide tu Bombona.

The service in all these channels is multilingual, with the 
official regional languages of Spain, as well as English 
and French, to cover the spectrum of current customer 
needs. This includes the provision of a special sign 
language interpretation channel for people with hearing 
difficulties, in line with Repsol’s principles of inclusion 
and social responsibility. 

Customer service processes

Regardless of the input channel, any interaction is 
recorded in the corresponding customer management 
platform. This registration is done manually or 
automatically and includes a classification based on the 
reason for the contact by the customer. 

Interactions are resolved at the front line of care and 
those requiring some processing or specific action are 
escalated to a specific management team. Furthermore, 
customers of businesses such as Electricity and Gas, in 
order to avoid possible fraud, have to pass a security 
protocol or have an authorization if they are not 
contract holders. 

The interactions that are recorded on the different 
customer service channels also include the rating and a 
satisfaction score with the key objective of monitoring 
and managing the interactions.

Voice of the customer platform

These communication channels are complemented by a 
customer voice platform that is used across the different 
businesses. The purpose ascertain in real time customer 
perceptions and assessments of the care they receive in 
order to manage and improve it.

6 International human rights treaties: International Bill of Human Rights, Convention on the Elimination of All Forms of Discrimination Against 

Women, International Convention on the Elimination of All Forms of Racial Discrimination, Convention Against Torture and Other Cruel, Inhuman 
or Degrading Treatment or Punishment, Convention on the Rights of the Child. Principles relating to the rights set out in the Declaration of the 
International Labour Organization (ILO), as well as the eight Fundamental Conventions that develop them: Freedom of Association and Collective 
Bargaining (Conventions 87 and 98), Elimination of Forced Labor (Conventions 29 and 105), Effective Abolition of Child Labor (Conventions 138 
and 182) and Elimination of Discrimination (Conventions 100 and 111). ILO Convention No. 169. Humanitarian law in conflict zones. In addition, 
Repsol will also respect all international treaties of regional human rights protection systems in whose countries it operates.

7 International standards on business and human rights: the United Nations Guiding Principles on Business and Human Rights, the OECD Guidelines 
for Multinational Enterprises, the International Finance Corporation (IFC) Performance Standards on Environmental and Social Sustainability and 
the 10 Principles of the Global Compact.

8 In the case of Peru, the management of end customers is led by the Director of the Mobility business unit under the D. E. Repsol Peru.

In November 2025, a QR code was deployed in the multi-
energy offices so that customers can give feedback at the 
different points of sale and in-person service . 

Throughout 2025, a total of 2,574,700 responses were 
received through all the channels operating in the Iberian 
Peninsula. 

From a weighted average of customer ratings at the 
main touchpoints, a global CSAT (Customer Satisfaction 
Score) is created that is part of the objectives of 
Repsol’s General Customer Department. 

In addition, based on all this information or customer 
voice, the different teams implement experience 
improvement actions that allow the CSAT index to be 
improved by between 3% and 5%.

Services to vulnerable customers

Within the Customer Service function, and specifically in 
the Electricity and Gas business, customer management 
covers vulnerable customers, severely vulnerable 
customers, and customers at risk of social exclusion. 

The management of vulnerable customers is regulated, 
so these customers must meet a series of legal 
requirements to prove their economic vulnerability. 

REGSITI, the regulated retailer of the Repsol Group that 
offers its customers the PVPC tariff, manages requests 
for information or requests for subscription to these 
types through customer service. Consumers have 
channels such as telephone, face-to-face, email or post, 
among others, to make these queries.

In 2025, a total of 9,430 requests were handled (10,471 
in 2024), of which 8,011 were resolved (8,666 in 2024). Of 
these, 3,836 related to vulnerable customers (4,196 in 
2024) and 4,175 to severely vulnerable customers (4,470 
in 2024).

Product safety management

Repsol has a 24‑hour emergency phone line for 
managing any potential customer incidents related to 
product safety. This phone line, as well as the product 
labeling, comply with the requirements established in 
the REACH9 and CLP10 regulations.

The treatment of these incidents, as well as their 
management, control and response, is regulated by an 
internal procedure, by which all incidents are analyzed in 
order to proceed with the study and implementation of 
the measures deemed appropriate depending on the 
degree of severity of the incident. If the incidents have a 
high, medium or low degree of severity, the affected 
business is notified so that it can proceed with their 
management. In the case of incidents with a zero degree 
of severity, no immediate action is taken. 

Likewise, to support the transmission of the information 
on the safety data sheets to the clients, in addition to 
the traditional sending by email or paper, an algorithm is 
being tested so that every time a new sale is made, the 
safety data sheet is sent to the email address provided 
by the customer. 

Through the labeling of packaged products, 
transmission of information throughout the supply chain 
is ensured. The information consists of safety 
instructions such as pictograms and safety information 
for the use or handling of the substances and a 24-hour 
emergency hotline. This information reaches end users 
as long as the packaging is not tampered with and 
remains unaltered.

Processes to remediate negative impacts and channels for 
consumers and end users to raise concerns (S4-3)

Repsol has multiple channels and procedures in place for 
managing complaints. Each of the areas that have a 
relationship with customers has procedures that allow the 
collection and management of claims and complaints to 
process their resolution and redress. 

These complaints, and other interactions, are managed 
and coordinated by an expert and internal Customer 
Service team. Direct management with customers and 
users is articulated, with internal coordination and 
management, through teams external to Repsol, which 
are the ones who handle interactions with consumers 
and end customers.

Complaints management

Complaints are received through the entry agents 
authorized by Repsol: customer service, technical 
support and development (ATD) technicians, 
commercial departments, etc. In addition, customers can 
use the different available channels such as telephone, 
email, web, social networks, mail, in person, etc., to file a 
complaint. 

The complaints handling process is as follows:

• Logging of the complaint in the customer management 
systems, with detailed categorization of each case, by 
process, applicant, origin and levels of classification.

• Resolution of the complaint and/or claim at the first 

line of support or assignment to those responsible for 
managing, resolving, and following up on the most 
complex complaints. 

• Gathering of information, analysis of the root cause, 

proposal and implementation of containment, 
corrective and improvement actions.

• Depending on the implementation of new processes 

or the results of root cause analysis, quantitative 
monitoring is carried out in terms of the volume of 
incoming different types of claims and the reason for 
them. To this end, measurement systems through 
data panels or our own audits on these calls or 
contacts are decisive in understanding their evolution 
and degree of adjustment. 

During 2025, complaints registered in Spain and 
Portugal totaled 217,470, of which 97% were closed. 
Compared with 2024 (213,307 complaints), there was a 
2% increase in claims received and registered this year. 
Of these complaints, in Electricity and Gas in Spain, 
25% were resolved in favor of the customer.

9 Regulation (EC) No 1907/2006 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 18 December 2006 concerning the Registration, Evaluation, 

Authorisation and Restriction of Chemicals

10 Regulation (EC) No 1272/2008 on the classification, labelling and packaging of substances and mixtures

The following chart shows complaints by type:  

The typology of cases is as follows:

Types of customer complaints

Types of complaints and queries in Peru

As for the complaints received in Mexico, a total of 
6,404 (4,439 logged in 2024), the vast majority (around 
90% of calls and emails) related to billing at service 
stations, while the remaining 10% concerned matters 
such as requests from suppliers, job applications, or 
complaints relating to specific issues, such as the level 
of sound at service stations.

In Peru, a total of 2,500 cases were registered in 2025 
(3,107 in 2024), by telephone (9%), email (79%) and in 
person (13%), through which customers make their 
queries, requests, suggestions and/or complaints/claims.  

Managing the voice of the customer

On the voice of the customer platform, both in the 
Service Station business in Spain and Portugal and in 
the Electricity and Gas business, where the largest 
number of points of sale and customer contact exist, 
there is a process for responding to customers who 
convey dissatisfaction and who express the desire to be 
contacted to be listened to and have their concerns 
resolved.

This process of managing dissatisfaction is gradually 
being extended to the points of contact of the different 
businesses in the Iberian Peninsula. In 2026, it will be 
extended to Electric Mobility and LPG and work is 
underway to establish an internal protocol for its 
regulation with a comprehensive vision of customer 
responses, which will enter into force in 2026.  

Complaints related to service stations in Mexico are 
monitored in real time thanks to an IT system that 
records all calls and emails that reach Customer Service. 
This allows Repsol to consult cases in real time and 
monitor them. This system is also capable of generating 
reports for the evaluation of the service.

In the case of Peru, monitoring case handling is carried 
out by the loyalty team in order to identify cases 
handled outside the service level and, in this way, 
identify areas for improvement. These can address 
different aspects: improvements in the operation of the 
system, training of personnel, management of new 
resources, among others.

Taking action on material impacts on consumers and end 
users, and approaches to managing material risks and 
pursuing material opportunities related to consumers and 
end users, and effectiveness of those actions (S4-4)

Repsol relies on digitalization, in addition to physical 
points of sale and service, as a lever for its customer 
management strategy. In this context, as a means of 
accessing the information that customers need, we are 
actively working to improve the experience of searching 
for information and managing or purchasing products or 
services through the transparency and breadth of 
information available on platforms such as Waylet, the 
corporate website or the Customer Area in accordance 
with the Company’s regulatory framework.  

In 2025, Repsol undertook to enhance its brand. One of 
the main levers was the simplification of communication 
through the use of clearer and more relevant messages 
that consider the customer’s perspective, an upgrading 
of customer service protocols and guides, and a clearer 
and simpler transition to digital channels. 

In the quest to improve the experience offered to 
Repsol customers across digital channels, a continuous 
and comprehensive approach is implemented that 
combines both quantitative analytics techniques and 
qualitative research. In addition, the Company has a 
specialized UX (User Experience) team dedicated 
entirely to improving the digital experience:

• Quantitative analytics: Web analytics tools are used to 

collect data on user behavior. This includes metrics 
such as conversion rate, time spent on site, and user 
journey. By analyzing this data, patterns and trends 
are identified that allow us to understand how the 
Company’s customers interact with products and 
services. This information is an aid to informed 
decision-making about improvements in the interface, 
content and functionalities. 

• Qualitative research: The quantitative findings are 
complemented by qualitative research, including 
surveys, interviews, and focus groups. These 
techniques allow for delving deeper into customers’ 
motivations, needs and expectations. Through direct 
feedback, areas for improvement and opportunities to 
personalize the experience are identified. By 
integrating both approaches, customized strategies 
are designed that respond to users’ individual 
preferences. 

To measure the experience with the platforms, there are 
two main sources of information: 

1. For apps, with customer ratings and opinions in 

reviews on app marketplaces.

2. For some processes or specific points of contact 

through apps or websites, such as the purchase of 
services with Waylet, the Vivit app, or electric 
charging, customers have the opportunity to provide 
feedback through a satisfaction survey.

Both sources provide Repsol with important information 
to implement actions focused on the energy needs of 
our customers, which allow us to improve their 
experience at all points of contact with the Company.

In order to anticipate and promote transparency, Repsol 
has a “customer community” that allows it to carry out 
continuous qualitative research with a group of users or 
potential customers and to inquire about how these 
users understand the different value propositions, learn 
about their relationship with energy and, in this way, 
adapt or design the Company’s communication and 
information dissemination.

In addition, to deepen the understanding of the 
physical-digital experience, identify levers for 
improvement, detect market trends and optimize the 
multichannel strategy, the Multi-energy tracking system 
was implemented in 2025 and will continue to be used 
moving forward. It is a continuous monitoring and 
analysis system that can track the behavior, perception 
and experience of consumers with respect to different 
sources of energy they use in their home or while 
mobile. Through this tracking system, 1,000 managers 
responsible for home energy contracting and regular 
drivers are surveyed per month and KPIs are evaluated 
such as the Net Promoter Score (NPS), satisfaction by 
attributes, reasons for selection and brand awareness.

Customer management assessment

With respect to efficient management of 
communication, active listening, and complaint 
channels, Repsol has established customer satisfaction 
control mechanisms so that the processes provide 
effective redress in their application and results. 

Firstly, as noted, a record is made of interactions 
occurring in the different customer service channels, 
with a rating and a satisfaction score also obtained . 
Based on an analysis of these assessments, 
improvement actions are proposed.  

Customer Service and its internal teams carry out a daily 
operational monitoring of the management of external 
suppliers and their service provision with the aim of 
ensuring optimal levels of care and service for 
customers and consumers.  

In the case of customer service in Mexico, meetings are 
held between the external service and the internal 
Repsol team, where analysis is focused on specific cases 
of complaints from service stations that could not be 
resolved by the supplier, with a view to improving 
processes. 

Likewise, on a monthly basis, the operators of this 
service are evaluated through service indicators to 
check the quality of the service and the correct 
management of complaints related to service stations.

In Peru, in 2025 and with a main focus on the level of 
service, actions were implemented aimed at expediting 
the handling of registered cases. 

In this regard, it should be noted that Repsol in Peru is 
now ranked first in the CX Index in the service station 
category, thus achieving recognition as the company 
that offers the best customer experience in the country.

Product safety assessment

Managing the safety of the products sold is a priority for 
Repsol and is present through all stages of the life cycle 
of products. In addition to the applicable legislation in 
force, the Company has internal11 regulations that set 
out the requirements to support adequate risk 
management, from the beginning of research to design 
a product until it is marketed. 

During the design phase and following the REACH and 
CLP regulations, hazards are identified and potential 
risks arising from use are assessed, in order to take 
appropriate management measures, carrying out 
(eco)toxicological studies to identify possible adverse 
effects on people and the environment. 

To remediate the potential impacts of the product use 
and ensure product safety, continuous monitoring is 
necessary to identify new or changing risks. In this 
sense, product re-evaluations are carried out 
spontaneously or reactively in the face of a legislative 
change and/or at the request of ECHA12 on products 
already made available to customers.

Repsol participates in voluntary programs of the sector 
itself and European organizations that delve into 
specific knowledge about certain products, such as the 
propylene oxide and glycols sector group of CEFIC13 or 
the consortium focused on improving scientific 
knowledge of short-chain NLP polyols14 led by the 
main manufacturers of this type of product, as well as 
in the Concawe consortiums for petroleum products 
and LOA15 for products derived from catalytic cracking. 

In compliance with internal regulations, Repsol uses 
safety data sheets and hazard labels to provide 
information on the hazards of each product it sells so 
that customers can appropriate measures to handle 
them safely.

The Company has a single repository that allows all 
employees and stakeholders to access all product 
safety documents. In addition, it allows analysis of 
different parameters of the products handled for the 
entire company, by company or by product range. 
These documents are available to Repsol customers in 
accordance with the laws of the country where the 
product is to be handled. In the database, commercial 
and product data converge, which allows Repsol to 
automatically send safety documents to customers. 

To respond to the possible negative impacts of the 
products sold by Repsol, there is a 24-hour emergency 
telephone number to manage safety incidents.

3.4.3 Metrics and targets
Targets related to managing material negative impacts, 
advancing positive impacts, and managing material risks 
and opportunities (S4-5)

Customer service-related metrics and targets

Customer and consumer satisfaction is key for Repsol. 
For this reason, the Company relies on various surveys 
to measure the degree of satisfaction in the interaction 
of users with customer service. This indicator is 
regularly monitored,  as it is a source of information for 
the improvement of customer service services and 
processes. 

To obtain this indicator, an annual survey is carried out 
at different times and points of contact where the 
customer is asked for a rating from 1 to 5 (with 5 being 
the highest level of satisfaction).

In 2025, customer satisfaction (CSAT) on the service 
received was 4.216 out of 5 (4.3 in 2024) from a total of 
20,969 customer surveys (20,878 in 2024) carried out. 
For on-call surveys, the total number of surveys 
conducted was 1,102,039 (701,401 in 2024).

In 2025, based on the information collected at the 
different points of contact with the customer, an 
experience improvement program has been 
implemented, with a focus on improving the process of 
contracting,  onboarding and billing for electricity and 
gas, brand experience in communication, interaction 
and service at physical points of sale, telephone and 
face-to-face, and in the optimization and simplification 
of digital communities.

In the case of customer service in Mexico, the level of 
service provided by the external provider is measured, 
with a requirement of 97%, as well as the service level, 
with a requirement of 90% of calls answered in under 
10 seconds. As for the average handling time (AHT), it 
varies depending on the service being managed: 170 
seconds for calls, 229 seconds for email, and 200 
seconds for social media.

For the evaluation of the service in Peru, this year’s 
indicators were as follows:

1. Cases handled within the service level: 85%, in line 

with the annual target.

2. Percentages of incoming calls answered: 98% with 

< = 30 seconds.

3. Average call time of 2.87 minutes vs. target of 

< = 3 minutes.

For the following year, weekly meetings with the 
external service are being implemented, with the aim of 
improving productivity ratios and guaranteeing a 
better service.

With regard to safety throughout the life cycle of

the base year, and the target for 2025–2026 is to have 
zero calls to the emergency hotline for high-severity 
incidents (serious injuries to people, major leaks or 
spills, significant damage to buildings or vehicles, etc., 
and/or significant reputational risk).

Businesses are informed of all high-level incidents as

3.5 Safety and security

Repsol carries out all its activities preserving the integrity 
of people and avoiding any damage to the environment, 
supporting a healthy and safe work environment from 
both a physical and emotional point of view, generating 
measures to address material IROs from Safety and 
Security.

In its double materiality assessment, Repsol has 
identified material IROs regarding safety and security. 

In relation to risks, the following have been identified as 
material:

•

Improper management of safety can lead to accidents 
at facilities (IRO-25). 

• Possibility of attacks, blockages in operational activity 

and accidents that could affect the Company’s facilities, 
resulting in operational interruptions and financial losses 
(IRO-27).

The following are material negative impacts:

•

Impacts on local communities due to industrial 
incidents that may occur at Repsol facilities (IRO-26).

• Accidents in higher-risk operations that could cause 

serious harm to workers (IRO-28).

3.5.1 Governance
As indicated in Governance, the Company has a 
corporate governance framework that establishes, 
develops, promotes and evaluates its focus on to 
transparency, integrity, respect, and excellence. 

Repsol’s Board of Directors and Executive Committee 
play a key role in promoting and overseeing the 
Company’s sustainability strategy, through the 
Sustainability Committee. 

The Sustainability Committee performs, in accordance 
with the provisions of the Regulations of the Board of 
Directors, supervisory functions related to personal and 
process safety. The Committee also regularly monitors 
strategic environmental and safety projects. 

In 2025, the Sustainability Committee reviewed, among 
others, the analysis and periodic monitoring of safety 
performance: scorecard of indicators, contractor safety 
management and change of safety metrics and 
objectives to improve the learning cycle. The incentive in 
terms of safety at the CEO level is related to safety 
rates, fatalities and incidents.

The Third-Party Due Diligence policy establishes the 
necessary measures for understanding the third parties 
with which the Company interacts or will interact, 
including safety and security risks. In safety and security, 
due diligence is addressed with the stakeholders: 
suppliers, contractors, and partners, as detailed in 
Business model and strategy.

Risk management and internal controls

Safety and security risks are assessed in conjunction 
with the businesses, and the management of these risks 
is incorporated into the Internal Control System over 
Sustainability Reporting (ICSR), the objective of which is 
to support the reasonable reliability of the sustainability 
information.

3.5.2 Strategy
Repsol is present throughout the energy value chain. 
Performance of the activities in the four business 
segments entails risks inherent to the integrity of 
workers. The Company conducts an annual review of its 
stakeholders, and creates a prioritization map, taking 
into account the nature of the stakeholders.

It also seeks to improve safety indicators in operations 
through constant and coordinated communication with 
contractors, one of the main stakeholders, addressing 
challenges and best practices, and promoting 
continuous improvement in work processes aligned with 
the principles of the Safety Excellence Program, as 
described in Business model and strategy.

3.5.3 Policies related to safety
Repsol has a Safety and Occupational Health Policy, 
which establishes that the Executive Committee leads 
the health and safety programs, with the chain of 
command being responsible for implementing them in 
their area of responsibility. The Safety Excellence 
Program Management and the business units are 
responsible for developing the specific procedures 
necessary to comply with Repsol’s regulations and the 
legal requirements in force in each location, so that the 
necessary protection measures are adopted in each of 
its operations.
