The Company will promote and require the principles 
set forth in this policy to its employees and, to the 
extent possible, will extend the policy to its contractors, 
partners, and collaborators. The scope of application of 
the policy and the different regulations inherent to 
occupational safety reaches all activities and operations 
carried out by Repsol in which it has management 
control.

In order to comply with the commitments established in 
the Safety and Occupational Health Policy, Repsol 
develops the necessary regulations, which are adapted 
and updated periodically in accordance with best 
practices and analysis of their effectiveness. In 2025, 
among others, the following regulations have been 
revised:

• Safety and Environmental Risk Management: The 

mission of this standard is to ensure that hazards that 
may affect the safety of people, the environment, 
assets, and the Company’s stakeholders are identified, 
and risks are analyzed, evaluated, and minimized to 

tolerable levels. In addition to integrating risk 
management into operational processes, the standard 
includes the requirements to be met in terms of 
analysis methodologies at each stage of the life cycle, 
the acceptability matrix and the actions, deadlines 
and authorizations for each of the risk levels. 

• Life-Saving Rules: Repsol has decided to adhere to 

the IOGP Life-Saving Rules, evolving from its previous 
10 Basic Safety Rules to an international, robust and 
recognizable standard for our contractors and 
partners. The official rollout, training, and awareness 
campaign for all employees and contractors began in 
January 2026.

3.5.4 Actions and resources related to  
safety
The Company has made available the financial resources 
required for carrying out actions that are considered 
minor or immaterial and are therefore not detailed.

The main safety actions seek to respond to IRO-25, 
IRO-26, IRO-28 and IR0-37 are summarized in the 
following table:

Safety-related actions

Description of the action

Scope

Implementation status

Expected result

1.- Strategy focused on reducing 
major accidents

Own 
operations

In progress

Prevent serious accidents and reduce fatalities and 
permanent injuries

2.- Safety culture diagnoses

Own 
operations

In progress

Identify areas for improvement and strengthen the 
safety culture

1.- Strategy focused on reducing major accidents

Repsol continues to deepen its strategy of preventing 
serious accidents and reducing fatalities and permanent 
injuries. This approach, the founding objective of the 
Safety Excellence Program, is based on 
recommendations from insurers and best practices 
established by industry associations, such as IOGP, 
which are already being adopted by different companies 
in the sector. The evolution is justified by the exhaustion 
of some of the more traditional processes and messages 
linked to safety, which, although they have been useful 
to reach current levels of performance, have lost part of 
their effectiveness or are generating unintended 
negative consequences. 

Under no circumstances should it be interpreted as 
renouncing the reduction of minor accidents, but rather 
so that higher quality and high-impact actions are taken 
to avoid major accidents. The aim of managing all events 
in the same way, regardless of their potential, has 
proven not only inefficient in terms of the use of 
resources, but even counterproductive in terms of 
results. 

In order for this change to represent an effective 
transformation and not just a declaration of intent, 
changes have been made to the key observation processes 
and metrics by which safety performance is evaluated, 
and the messages that are conveyed to the 
Organization.

a) Progressive abandonment of "zero accidents" 

messages. Although it makes sense as an ambition or 
within the framework of a project, a shutdown or any 
other time-limited activity in the medium term, it can 
lead to a disincentive to report, a reduction in only the 
most trivial accidents and a feeling of stagnation in 
improvement.

b)Elimination of internal targets associated with the 

total recordable injury rate, and its replacement with 
an indicator linked to fatalities, permanent injuries, 
and serious accidents. Similarly, the performance 
information requested from our contractors is 
reviewed on an ongoing basis and references to the 
frequency rate are eliminated.

c) Changes to the safety risk acceptability matrix 
(included in the Safety and Environmental Risk 
Management Standard) to accommodate scenarios of 
major consequences and very low probability of 
occurrence, such as loss of well control or air 
accidents.

d)Development of a specific program to better identify 

events that, without having caused significant 
damage, could have done so if an additional 
protective barrier had failed or the conditions had 
been slightly different, thus aiming to improve the 
assessment of potential incidents.

e) Investigation of incidents with potential major 

consequences, with the investigation requirements 
being limited to aspects related to the investigation 
methodology, composition and qualification of the 
investigation team and management of the resulting 
actions. The rest of the incidents are analyzed and 
managed by the corresponding operational units, but 
without the above requirements.

f) Promote greater identification of systemic causes and 
human and organizational factors in investigations, 
working both on the technical competence of the 
teams responsible for the analysis and on the 
Organization’s capacity to manage the information 
that emerges from these investigations. 

g)Organization learning, with the lessons learned from 

the incidents are transferred to other units and 
people where they can be applied, while new 
information is kept current and up to date in the 
learning portal. This action was initiated in 2024 and is 
ongoing.

h)Deployment of the Life-Saving Rules, which replace 
the current 10 Basic Safety Rules. The associated 
campaign will reinforce prevention measures in 
activities that are most likely to cause fatalities and 
permanent injuries.

In short, the strategy focused on reducing major 
accidents requires important changes in processes and 
the conviction that it has a positive impact on all safety 
management. It is possible that initially there will be an 
upturn in the personal accident rate indicator, including 
the lost-time accident indicator, but Repsol 
acknowledges this for the sake of transparency in 
reporting, the psychological certainty that is generated 
in the Organization and confidence in safety processes. 

There is no specific time horizon associated with this 
action, given its structural nature within the safety 
strategy.

2.- Safety culture diagnoses

Repsol continues to carry out specific safety culture 
diagnoses in all its assets and businesses, so that each of 
them has a diagnosis in cycles of approximately four or 
five years. The corporate area of the Safety Excellence 
Program conducts these assessments on a regular, 
independent basis and following a model of proven 
robustness covering the most decisive aspects of a solid 
safety culture.

Fair recognition

Trust
in the report

Shared 
information

LEADERSHIP

Adaptability 

Feeling of
vulnerability

Learning 
Organization

These assessments allow us to identify weaknesses and 
areas for improvement that are not apparent in other 
verification and audit exercises, as they are related more 
to perceptions and interpersonal realities than to formal 
and documented issues. One of the main lessons learned 
from major industrial accidents is the need to pay 
attention to the weak signals that occur in 
organizations, so these exercises are a crucial lever to 
prevent major accidents.

This initiative has been gradually carried out in Repsol’s 
various operations, and in 2025 it was carried out in the 
Venezuela business unit, in the Mobility Department of 
Spain and Portugal, and in the Cartagena and Pampilla 
industrial complexes in Spain and Peru, respectively. The 
results obtained allow business managers to develop 
action plans that are more in line with their cultural 
reality.

There is no specific time horizon associated with this 
action, given its structural nature within the safety 
strategy.

The actions reported in 2024 remain in force (except for 
those whose time horizon was 2024) and are aligned 
with the two general initiatives that are reported in 
2025 and their result is translated into the safety 
metrics, which are shown in detail in Safety-related 
metrics and targets.

Fair culture. Safety keys

3.5.5 Security-related policies
Repsol’s Protection of Tangible and Intangible Assets 
Policy brings together the Company’s commitments to 
comply with the legal requirements in force regarding 
asset protection in each location and country in which it 
operates, seeking to guarantee the integrity of people, 
prevent the commission of crimes, support the 
continuity of operations and prevent the loss of 
Company information, therefore minimizing the risk of 
attacks (IRO-27).

The Company provides the resources for the protection 
and safeguarding of Repsol’s assets, including persons, 
interests, assets, operations, knowledge, industrial and 
intellectual property rights, trade secrets, know-how, 
information and data, networks and information 
systems, as well as the Company’s operations and 
interests, that underpin Repsol’s activity. This protection 
is carried out through management based on risk 
analysis and a process of continuous improvement, 
promoting a culture of asset protection, under the 
protection of corporate values. 

The policy determines internal and external information 
channels that provide insight into the situation of asset 
protection and cybersecurity in the countries in which the 
Company is present. The policy also promotes awareness 
of asset protection through training and dissemination 
activities among employees so that the latter will, within 
their own areas of responsibility,  safeguard their own 
safety and that of Repsol’s other assets.

Repsol has established standards of conduct for the 
entire Company, disseminating asset protection criteria 
and a common framework for action, through the 
development of the internal security management 
standard. 

The security regulations include the different security 
and risk mitigation measures implemented, which are 
part of the various security plans and cover all 
employees, contractors, and anyone present within 
Repsol’s facilities, allowing for protection of the 
Company’s assets.

The application of security regulations is the 
responsibility of the Intelligence and Security 
Department and the units in each country, which are 
responsible for developing the specific procedures 
necessary for compliance with local regulations and for 
Repsol, adopting the equivalent protection measures in 
the territories.

Risks in facilities/assets that do not belong to the 
Company, but in which it has an interest and in which 
Repsol personnel provide services, must be analyzed in a 
manner equivalent to the Company’s own facilities/
assets of a similar category. Here,, Corporate Security is 
responsible for conducting the risk analysis or 
supervising the analysis carried out by the operator and 
determining the recommended mitigation measures to 
be taken to achieve the appropriate level of protection.

Through the intranet, Repsol makes available to 
employees the policy and regulations on the protection 
of tangible and intangible assets, as well as the security 
risk classification of countries.

The scope of application of the Policy and the metrics 
associated with security extend to all activities and 
operations carried out by the Company in which it has 
management control. It also covers all employees, 
regardless of the type of contract that determines their 
employment relationship, the position they occupy, and 
the place of work, as well as all those people who 
provide professional services in our facilities on a 
temporary basis, the secondees and the interns.

3.5.7 Safety-related metrics and 
targets
It is important to note that the detailed information on 
objectives is sensitive information for the Company 
since it can have an impact on it. 

Repsol monitors the effectiveness of its policies and 
actions with respect to incidents, risks and opportunities 
related to safety by monitoring safety metrics.

The Company’s safety performance is assessed monthly 
using a safety scorecard with several metrics, which is 
presented monthly to the Executive Committee and at 
each of the Sustainability Committee sessions that take 
place during the year. In addition, the information is 
available to all employees on the intranet. This 
continuous assessment allows for the different actions 
to be adjusted in order to optimize the management of 
risks and impacts, and to improve security metrics, in 
accordance with the established objectives.

based on personal and process accident result 
indicators.

Safety Management System (EMA)2 in place, thus 
establishing a common framework for risk and impact 
management that is applicable to all Repsol employees.

M

METHODOLOGY

Methodology for safety and security 
reporting and metrics 

The incident management process managed with the Synergi Life 
digital tool allows for the recording, reporting, classification, and 
tracking of incidents, including investigation, actions, and lessons 
learned. The automation of the tool is subject to an internal 
control for the correct functioning of the notification flows, as 
well as the different phases of the life cycle of the management 
of an incident.

Based on the information recorded in the tool, the accident rate 
indicators are obtained. These are reported in accordance with 
the internal incident management standard based on 
international standards (IOGP and OSHA).

The reported accident rate metrics are obtained from the 
different fields that have been completed in the tool and are 
defined as:

• Number of fatalities: number of deaths accumulated in the 

year, occurring within the Company’s control and due to work-
related causes.

• Number of recordable work-related injuries: total number of 
personal consequences (fatalities, permanent injuries, loss of 
days, medical treatment, and restricted work) accumulated in 
the year.

• Total Recordable Injury Rate (TRIR): total number of personal 

consequences (fatalities, permanent injuries, loss of days, 
medical treatment, and restricted work) accumulated in the 
year, for every million hours worked.

• Number of days lost: number of days not worked due to an 
accident at work with sick leave, accumulated in the year.

• Number of Tier 1 and Tier 2 incidents: number of process 

safety incidents recorded and classified according to severity 
as Tier 1 or Tier 2 according to API754 (American Petroleum 
Institute) in its third edition updated in August 2021. A process 
safety accident is one in which there is a loss of primary 
containment. The following criteria must be met 
simultaneously: i) There is a process or a chemical product 
involved. ii) It occurs at a specific location, that is, at a facility 
used for production, distribution, storage, auxiliary services 
(utilities) or pilot plants related to the process or chemical 
product involved. (iii) It results in an unplanned or uncontrolled 
release of material, including non-toxic and non-flammable 
materials (for example, steam, hot water, nitrogen, compressed 
CO2, compressed air), with certain levels of consequences. The 
process safety accident will be classified as Tier 1 or Tier 2, 
according to the defined thresholds.

2025  Consolidated Management Report

 4. Governance information

4.1 Business conduct

4.1.1 Governance
The role of administrative, management, and supervisory 
bodies (ESRS 2 GOV-1)

Repsol believes that ethical behavior and responsible 
practices are essential elements for the sustainable 
performance of its activity and for the generation of 
long-term value for its shareholders and stakeholders. 
Therefore, as indicated in Governance, the Company has 
a corporate governance framework where its 
commitment to transparency, integrity, respect and 
excellence in all its actions is established, developed, 
promoted and evaluated. In this regard, Repsol’s Board of 
Directors and Executive Committee play a key role in 
promoting and overseeing the Company’s sustainability 
strategy, as well as in fostering a corporate culture 
aligned with the values and ethical principles that inspire 
its Code of Ethics and Business Conduct.

4.1.2 Impact, risk and opportunity 
management
Description of the process for determining and assessing
material impacts, risks, and opportunities (ESRS 2 IRO-1)

The complete description of the process that has been 
followed, as well as the result of said analysis, is 
reported in Materiality assessment. 

In relation to business conduct, positive impacts have 
been identified that refer to:

• Promoting good practices in relation to corruption, 

bribery, and money laundering along the value chain 
through the introduction of specific contractual 
clauses (IRO-47).

• Repsol’s leadership and good corporate governance, 
which encourages the rest of the sector to embrace 
ESG standards, promoting more responsible practices 
throughout the industry (IRO-50).

• Fostering an ethical culture within Repsol’s workforce 
by promoting greater alignment with the Company’s 
values, greater trust and collaboration, and a stronger 
reputation for integrity (IRO-51).

• Promoting a safe environment for employees and 

third parties, providing a channel to report incidents 
or complaints without fear of retaliation (IRO-52).

Likewise, a negative impact has been identified in 
relation to: 

• Poor management and control of integrity in Repsol, 
which can lead to corruption, affecting investor and 
shareholder confidence, which entails financial and 
reputational risks for the Company (IRO-48).

Finally, a risk has been identified relating to:

• Regulatory non-compliance: reserve reporting, 

financial markets regulations, accounting standards, 
tax and sustainability reporting regulations, artificial 
intelligence, HSE legislation, intellectual property and 
other specific regulations of the markets in which we  
operate (IRO-49).

The assessment of these impacts encompasses all the 
activities and geographies where Repsol is present, 
including its value chain.

Business conduct and corporate culture policies (G1-1)

Code of Ethics and Business Conduct 

The Code of Ethics and Business Conduct1 applies to all 
directors, executive personnel, partners2 and other 
employees, regardless of the type of contract 
determined by their professional or employment 
relationship. It establishes the framework of reference for 
understanding and implementing the conduct and 
standards that the Company expects from each of them, 
thus helping to promote a culture of ethics and corporate 
compliance. 

Ethics and Compliance Committee

The Ethics and Compliance Committee is a collegiate, 
high-level, multidisciplinary body, with autonomous 
powers of initiative and control appropriate for the 
performance of its duties. It comprises the General 
Counsel and General Secretary of the Board of Directors 
of Repsol, S. A., the Chief People and Organization 
Officer, the Chief Compliance Officer, the Head of Audit, 
Control and Risks, and the Head of Labor Relations, 
Legal-Labor Management and Safety at Work. The 
committee is tasked with managing the oversight 
system and supporting compliance with the Company’s 
Code of Ethics and Business Conduct, among other 
duties. 

Ethics and Compliance Channel

The Ethics and Compliance Committee makes available 
to employees and any third party the Ethics and 
Compliance Channel, which is a robust, easily accessible, 
confidential and, if desired and permitted by applicable 
legislation, anonymous channel through which 
suspicions of unlawful, unethical behavior, or behavior 
involving a possible breach of the Code of Ethics and 
Business Conduct, internal regulations or applicable 
legislation, can be reported.

Communications received through the Ethics and 
Compliance Channel are handled by an independent 
company. This channel is available 24 hours a day, seven 
days a week, by post, through the website and by 
telephone, serving in the local language from which the 
communication is made.  

1 The Code of Ethics and Conduct is available on Repsol’s corporate website, in Spanish, English, and Portuguese, by following this path: Home > 

Sustainability > Sustainability Strategy > Policies > Code of Ethics and Business Conduct.

2 Partners (including non-operated joint ventures, contractors, suppliers, and other collaborating companies) are an extension of Repsol and, for 

this reason, must act in accordance with the code and any other applicable contractual provisions when intervening on behalf of or in 
collaboration with the Company. Repsol also encourages business partners to develop and implement ethical programs that are consistent with 
its standards.

The Company complies with Law 2/2023, regulating the 
protection of persons who report regulatory breaches 
and the fight against corruption, which transposes 
Directive (EU) 2019/1937. The Board of Directors of 
Repsol, S. A. has approved a single internal reporting 
system for the Repsol Group, and appointed the Ethics 
and Compliance Committee as its head, including the 
general principles applicable to the internal information 
system in its own Code of Ethics and Business Conduct. 
The system, which includes an instruction manual, is 
designed to guarantee confidentiality throughout the 
process, and the people who carry out the investigation 
will be obliged to respect the confidentiality of all the 
personal data and other information to which they are 
privy. Repsol has the obligation to preserve the identity 
of the person who submits the communication and the 
person about whom the report is made. Investigators, 
interviewees or participants in the process sign 
confidentiality agreements whenever they take part in 
an investigation.

The communications standard of the Ethics and 
Compliance Channel regulates the management and 
processing of any communication received through this 
channel in relation to circumstances or events that 
could constitute breaches of the Code of Ethics and 
Business Conduct, the Crime Prevention Model or the 
possible commission of a crime or infringement of 
internal regulations and applicable legislation, as well as 
Repsol’s Internal Reporting System.

Process of managing communications 

Process of managing communications

Ethics and Compliance Channel

Communication

The whistleblower receives a key 
and a password

 Acknowledgment of receipt is issued within 
7 days of receiving the communication

A preliminary analysis will be carried out 
by the Chief Compliance Officer

Admitted

Not admitted

Investigation over a period of 3 months, 
extendable 
by 3 further months for particularly complex 
matters

Report of conclusions

Filing

Communication to the whistleblower

〈

• Communication process: Communications about 
possible breaches are reported through Repsol’s 
Ethics and Compliance Channel, guaranteeing 
confidentiality and allowing for anonymity. Verbal 
communications may be made by telephone or by 
requesting a face-to-face meeting with the Chief 
Compliance Officer (CCO). 

• Analysis and processing: By delegation of the Ethics 
and Compliance Committee, the CCO or the person 
designated by the CCO carries out an initial analysis of 
the communication to evaluate its nature and content. 
As a result, the communication may be admitted for 
investigation, or classified as inadmissible when the 
facts lack any plausibility or are unintelligible, among 
other reasons. In those cases, the CCO informs the 
sender about the agreed course and may request 
additional information. 

• Conducting the investigation: The CCO appoints one 
or more investigators who act under its coordination 
and maintain confidentiality throughout the process. 
Investigators check the veracity of the information 
and can propose preventive measures. Legal 
regulations and legal guarantees are observed, paying 
special attention to the rights of defense and 
protection of personal data. 

• Resolution of the investigation: Once the 

investigation is complete, the investigator draws up a 
report of conclusions describing the investigation 
carried out and the conclusions reached and, where 
appropriate, makes recommendations for 
improvement and proposes an action plan. The Ethics 
and Compliance Committee decides on the report and 
the closure of the investigation. The CCO monitors the 
implementation of the action plans and keeps the 
Ethics and Compliance Committee informed of 
progress and completion. It also informs the 
whistleblower and, as the case may be, any other 
concerned parties of the closure of the investigation.

Anti-retaliation measures and controls 

Repsol has controls in place to prevent and manage any 
retaliation against whistleblowers who report violations 
through the Ethics and Compliance Channel, including:

1. Fostering a retaliation-free work environment 

through training and communication initiatives.

2. Establishing protection measures for the 

whistleblower and those affected, such as 
confidentiality clauses in investigations, preventing 
reprisals and promoting anonymity.

3. Implementing corrective action if retaliation is 

confirmed, and applying disciplinary sanctions to 
those responsible.

In addition, Repsol has a Whistleblower Protection 
Protocol as part of its Internal Reporting System, which 
aims to provide additional protection by prohibiting 
retaliation against those who make a communication in 
good faith through the Ethics and Compliance Channel.

Integrated Compliance Model  (ICM)

Supplier Relationship Management (G1-2)

Repsol, when managing relations with its suppliers, 
carries out activities to avoid or minimize the impact of 
disruptions in the value chain, while also interacting with 
vulnerable suppliers, as detailed in S2-4. Repsol 
conducts screenings and evaluations of the social and 
environmental performance of suppliers and evaluates 
the inclusion of local suppliers in the purchasing 
process, as described in S2-5.

For more information, see Workers in the value chain.

Prevention and detection of corruption and bribery (G1-3)

The Company has a global framework of action and a 
dedicated and specialized team in place, aimed at 
supporting the adequacy and observance of its 
obligations, both internal and external. The Compliance 
function helps to strengthen the global culture of 
compliance and improve the identification and 
monitoring of ethics and compliance risks, particularly in 
the areas of integrity, money laundering and terrorist 
financing, crime prevention, international sanctions and 
embargoes, competition and privacy and protection of 
personal data.  

The operation of the Compliance function is formalized 
in a Compliance Statute and a comprehensive 
Compliance Model approved by the Audit and Control 
Committee, as the delegated body of the Board of 
Directors of Repsol, S. A.: 
• The Compliance Statute sets out the mission, 

objectives and principles of the Compliance function 
and the obligation of the Chief Compliance Officer to 
support and report to the management and 
governing bodies so that they can exercise their 
responsibility to promote due control and good 
corporate governance.  

• The Integrated Compliance Model (ICM) defines the 
structure and general operation with an integrative 
vision of the Company’s Compliance Model and 
establishes the approval levels for compliance risks.  

In 2025, a total of 22,126 employees completed the new 
annual mandatory online training for all employees 
called the Repsol Code: Stories that teach. In a digital, 
modern and interactive format, the Chief Compliance 
Officer and a leading journalist in Spain reinforce the key 
ethical concepts among all employees through fictitious 
cases that force them to make decisions in different 
situations and contexts.  

For the first time in 2025, an innovative online training 
was developed targeting third parties in our value chain 
(suppliers, contractors and customers), in which the 
Company’s values and principles are reinforced through 
a journey that links Repsol’s businesses with its Codes of 
Ethics and Business Conduct (global and supplier). 

Meanwhile, the dissemination plan for courses related to 
Repsol’s Ethics and Compliance Channel, the internal 
reporting system, and the management of 
investigations for stakeholders continued throughout 
the period. 

Compliance Days at Repsol

In October, 2025 Compliance Day was held at our 
headquarters. On this occasion, the central focus of the 
event was the impact of the current geopolitical context 
on the strategic role of the Compliance function. 
Repsol’s General Counsel, Pablo Blanco, interviewed 
José María de Areilza, a renowned expert in geopolitics, 
university professor, and Secretary General of the Aspen 
Institute Spain Foundation, who offered us his current 
view of the complex environment in which we currently 
operate and its impact on the Compliance function.  

Subsequently, the 5th Compliance Awards were 
presented by members of the Company’s top 
management, in recognition of the best internal 
practices in ethics and compliance in the following 
categories inspired by Repsol’s new values: 
achievement, curiosity, courage and care. Special 
recognition was also given to the most outstanding 
member of People Compliance, a volunteer network of 
more than 360 employees who act as compliance 
ambassadors and promote the culture of compliance 
across different businesses and countries. 

In addition, in line with previous years, other similar 
events were also held at the regional or local level in 
various countries, including Compliance Day in Latin 
America, with the involvement of the regional front line, 
the Chief Compliance Officer and external experts; 
Compliance Day in Mexico, focused on corruption and 
anti-money laundering; and three meetings with the 
People Compliance volunteer network on topics such as 
international sanctions and their impact on operations, 
how our values have changed over time, and conflicts of 
interest – real cases. 

3 Training is available to any employee regardless of their contract type.

It is the integrated management and organization model that encompasses all the 
procedures and best practices adopted by Repsol and reflects the commitment 
and the Company’s culture of compliance.

There are also management system guides for the 
different work areas which describe the general standards 
and requirements, controls and associated risks. These in 
turn serve as a basis for carrying out specific risk 
assessments, and the implementation of measures that 
help prevent or mitigate compliance risks, as well as a 
guide to the compliance risk assessment methodology, 
which defines the necessary guidelines for the proper 
identification, analysis and assessment uniformly in all 
countries, business units, and corporate areas.  

Due diligence 

Repsol has a third-party due diligence standard, which 
sets out the necessary measures that the Company has 
in place to promote adequate knowledge of the third 
parties with which it relates or will interact in relation to 
compliance risks. All this with the purpose of allowing 
Repsol to have adequate information in the decision-
making process and in the evaluation and mitigation of 
possible compliance risks related to the third party.  

In addition, an in-house technological solution, known as 
RDD2, has been developed to manage due diligence 
with third parties, incorporating advanced data-driven 
and generative AI solutions, which allows Repsol to 
benchmark and match public and regulatory 
information, establish risk levels and propose mitigation 
measures.

Integrity

The Company reiterates its focus on strict compliance 
with the regulations for the prevention and fight against 
corruption and fraud, in all its forms, and has developed 
an Integrity Policy, which sets out its express position 
against unlawful conduct of corruption, fraud and 
conflicts of interest in accordance with the Company’s 
ethical values. In addition, it has implemented other 
specific regulations, including those relating to gifts and 
hospitality and conflicts of interest, and the guide to the 
keys to managing our relationship with public officials, 
which have undergone a planned review and update.

In addition, the N-1 Calculator has been designed, which 
identifies the Compliance Model to be included in the 
shareholders’ agreement with an anticipatory view and 
which must be required of investee companies where 
Repsol does not have control of the management, 
depending on the level of theoretical risk of the 
transaction, the degree of participation or the influence 
we have in the company, and the specific features or 
circumstances of the transaction.

Repsol annually updates its compliance risk map, which 
includes a theoretical and qualitative assessment of the 
inherent risks at the country, business, and area level. 
This process aims to identify and assess, among others,

risks related to corruption and bribery, thereby helping 
the Company achieve its global objectives.

This analysis allows Repsol to identify employees in the 
countries, businesses and areas most exposed to risk, 
thus providing an essential tool for their management 
and mitigation. In particular, risk-exposed employees 
have been identified at the Upstream businesses in 
Algeria, Bolivia, Brazil, Libya, Peru, Mexico, and 
Venezuela; at the Trading business in the United States; 
at the Lubricants, Asphalts and Specialized Products 
businesses in Colombia and Peru; and at the Mobility, 
Aviation and Refining businesses in Peru.

In 2025, the coverage of training in the prevention of 
corruption and bribery for these risk-exposed 
employees stood at 94%.

Crime prevention 

The Ethics and Compliance Committee is Repsol’s crime 
prevention body in Spain, for the purposes of the 
provisions of Article 31 bis, section 2.2 of the Spanish 
Criminal Code. Repsol has a standard that regulates the 
framework of action and responsibilities of the various 
areas involved in the Crime Prevention Model and the 
Crime Prevention Manual, which serve as guides to help 
employees understand criminal risks and the actions and 
conduct expected of them. In addition, in terms of 
criminal prevention, Repsol has formal models in Peru, 
Chile, and Colombia.

Training, communication and awareness on matters of 
compliance

Ethical culture, communication, and awareness are 
fundamental levers to strengthen the Integrated 
Compliance Model at Repsol. To this end, the 
Organization makes great efforts in communication and 
training of internal regulations related to ethics and 
compliance, thus improving levels of knowledge and 
understanding among all employees. It designs and 
deploys a global plan (countries and businesses) that 
annually takes into account: (i) the level of risk to which 
each group is exposed; (ii) the results of employee 
surveys (culture survey) and other sources in the People 
and Organization area; (iii) the requests of the areas, 
(iv) regulatory changes and legal updates, 
(v) compliance and audit recommendations, and (vi) the 
evaluation of the earnings of the actions carried out in 
previous years. Specifically, Repsol has a training 
program for the Board of Directors of Repsol, S. A. on 
the Crime Prevention Model that is carried out every 
two years, and that is carried out annually for the 
Executive Committee on various compliance matters. 

In 2025, face-to-face training has been given to both 
the Board of Directors and the Executive Committee 
focused on crime prevention. The biannual training 
aimed at executive personnel and administrators of 
Spanish companies has continued, which has consisted 
of a face-to-face training program focused on Repsol’s 

Crime Prevention Model (CPM), with the aim of 
reaffirming executive management’s focus on the 
prevention of irregularities and deepening the 
understanding and importance of the model. The 
interactive methodology has included practical 
exercises with simulated cases to identify compliance 
risks and apply CPM procedures, highlighting key legal 
responsibilities.  

Company rules and regulations

Internal regulations are a management tool, the main 
objective of which is to strengthen control systems and 
promote compliance with external and internal 
requirements; provide criteria for action that make it 
easier for employees to understand their 
responsibilities; to assist in the management and control 
of risks and to contribute to more agile and orderly 
execution of the Company’s activities. 

The General Directorate of People and Organization is 
responsible for keeping the Company’s rules and 
regulations up to date, through a process that allows for 
the involvement of the affected areas in drawing up the 
different regulatory documents, their coherence and 
consistency with the hierarchy and structure of the 
regulatory body, and their approval at the appropriate 
level. It also allows the internal dissemination of the 
regulations and their availability to all employees.

In 2025, 311 reports were received through the Ethics 
and Compliance Channel. Of these, 118 were closed, as 
shown in the table above, and 75 required an 
investigation. As a result of these investigations, 
11 cases of non-compliance with the Code of Ethics and 
Business Conduct were confirmed. Furthermore, Repsol 
identified three situations involving non-compliance 
which, while they did not constitute harassment, 
required corrective action to be taken. Likewise, there 
were no convictions or fines for violating laws related to 
corruption or bribery.

4.2 Responsible taxation

The Repsol Group is aware that the taxes it contributes 
boost the economic development of the countries and 
communities in which it operates and contribute to the 
well-being of its citizens. 

As a result of this conviction, Repsol undertakes to 
comply with best practices in responsible taxation and 
good governance, in line with the most widely 
recognized and prestigious international principles and 
recommendations (including the Spanish Code of Good 
Tax Practices, GRI 207, the OECD tax risk control model, 
and the B Team’s principles of responsible taxation, 
among others). 

In 2025, the double materiality assessment carried out 
at the Group level once again revealed that taxation is a 
material ESG topic for the Company, with a positive 
impact that is particularly evident in:

• Repsol’s contribution to the development of the 

countries in which it operates by complying with its 
tax obligations, applying criteria of transparency, and 
avoiding abusive practices or practices that are not 
aligned with industry standards (IRO-54).

4.2.1 Governance
As indicated in Governance, Repsol’s Board of Directors 
is the governing body tasked with approving and 
supervising the Company’s Tax Policy, which includes 
the tax strategy. This policy is mandatory for all 
employees and companies of the Group, and its 
execution and compliance is reviewed by the Board of 
Directors at least once a year. 

The Board of Directors examines the most significant tax 
aspects in terms of management, risks and practical 
application of the Group’s Tax Policy and strategy. 
Among other issues, it verifies the content of the 
Voluntary Tax Transparency Report, the taxes paid by 
Repsol, the impact of tax risks, the mechanisms for their 
management and control, the Group’s presence in tax 
havens and the initiatives carried out to promote tax 
transparency.

No member of the Company’s administrative, 
management and supervisory bodies, nor any employee 
in the scope of Repsol’s tax function, has their 
remuneration or receipt of incentives linked to specific 
tax savings objectives.

Risk management and internal controls

The Tax area is present in the Group’s business decision-
making, so that all decisions are in line with the 
principles enshrined in its Tax Policy and with the reality 
and economic motivation of its businesses. To this end, 
internal rules and procedures are in place so that Repsol 
adopts its tax positions based on robust business 
fundamentals, does not use opaque or artificial 
corporate structures that could compromise the 
transparency of its activities, and applies the arm’s 
length principle in its intra-group operations. Please see 
the Index of essential aspects of the due diligence 
process for further details.

The orderly management of Repsol’s 
tax affairs is carried out within a scope of activity, 
governance and Tax Control Framework (TCF) that rests 
on four basic pillars: (i) principles of action, (ii) expert 
team, (iii) processes and systems for tax compliance, and 
(iv) control and management of tax risks. Repsol 
periodically evaluates and updates its TCF, 
benchmarking it with other internationally recognized 
standards.

Repsol’s tax department is made up of experts in a 
range of tax-related disciplines; these professionals are 
responsible for managing all the tax affairs of the 
businesses and areas of the Group. Under a single 
dependency of the Tax Affairs Department (TAD), the 
management of tax matters is decentralized in the fiscal 
units of each country or business, in order to adequately 
address the particularities of each business and tax 
system. The continuity of strategy implementation and 
tax management in the face of unforeseen events is 
underpinned by a contingency plan for suitable 
succession in key tax-management positions.

Repsol incorporates tax risk management into its 
Enterprise Risk Management System (ERMS) through 
processes, systems, and internal controls. The TAD, as 
the body responsible for tax compliance, carries out tax 
risk control activities, among other initiatives, through 
the preparation and updating of a risk map. It also 
reports to the Audit and Control Committee, as a 
support body to the Board of Directors, on the impact 
of tax risks on the Company’s activities and on the 
mechanisms for their prevention, management, and 
control within the ERMS framework. Its powers include 
submitting transactions involving a particularly high tax 
risk to the Board for approval.

To mitigate tax risks, Repsol has documented processes 
that regulate tax compliance, assigning responsibilities 
and defining the tasks necessary to prepare and file tax 
returns and payments, supporting the reliability and 
traceability of the information. In addition, it has 
information management systems that preserve 
integrity and minimize human error, and digitization 
initiatives are being carried out to better manage the 
risks of tax operations. 

In terms of transparency, Repsol has an Ethics and 
Compliance Channel so that any employee or third 
party can report, confidentially and anonymously, 
possible breaches of the Code of Ethics and Business 
Conduct or the Crime Prevention Model, including 
potentially unethical or illegal conduct that affects the 
integrity of the Company in relation to taxation. This 
channel is permanently managed by an independent 
third party. See Business conduct for more information.

4.2.2 Strategy

Repsol’s Tax Policy is based on the responsible and 
efficient payment of its taxes, the application of good 
tax practices, the transparency of its actions and the 
promotion of cooperative relations with tax authorities.

In accordance with these principles, Repsol gives priority 
attention to the payment of taxes due in the different 
countries in which it operates, with the aim to comply 
with the law, respecting both its letter and its spirit. Tax 
decisions are made responsibly, in accordance with a 
reasonable interpretation of prevailing tax law and 
regulations and are aligned with the economic activity 
of the various businesses. 

Repsol reconciles strict compliance with the regulatory 
and competence framework in force in each jurisdiction 
with the defense of its legitimate interests, making use 
at all times of the remedies and actions provided for by 
law when it considers that certain actions may give rise 
to doubts as to their legal compliance.

Repsol is focused on being transparent and sharing 
significant information with its shareholders and 
stakeholders regarding the Business model and 
strategy.

To this end, it collaborates with tax authorities in the 
detection and search for solutions to fraudulent tax 
practices, facilitates access to relevant tax information, 
and prioritizes non-litigious ways of resolving disputes. 
Repsol also promotes institutional relations in order to 
align the Company’s tax policies with social reality, 
contribute responsibly to the creation of a more 
balanced and fair international tax framework, and 
anticipate the management of tax matters in the face of 
possible regulatory changes, minimizing risks and 
impacts.

Taxes paid in 2025

It also maintains an open dialogue with various NGOs 
and social action platforms in the search for a more 
equitable and effective tax system.

Tax contribution

Repsol’s tax contribution in 2025 was once again 
considerable: €13,265 million paid in taxes and similar 
public charges.

By country, the Company’s tax contribution in Spain of 
€9,899 million was particularly notable, representing 
75% of the Group’s total tax payments.

This significant tax contribution, generated by our 
activities and paid by the Group companies, has been 
borne both by the Company (own taxes) and by its 
customers, employees and investors (taxes collected1). 

The Company’s own taxes paid in 2025 amounted to 
€1,983 million (€746 million in Spain). Corporate income 
tax (CIT) paid amounted to €361 million (-€513 million in 
Spain). 

Lastly, the Group also paid a significant amount of 
environmental taxes in 2025, amounting to €7,308 
million (€6,088 million in Spain).

For further information, please refer to the table of 
taxes paid included in the section detailing the 
information required under Law 11/2018 on the 
Consolidated Statement of Non-Financial Information 
and in the Tax Contribution Report.2

(2) Tax on Hydrocarbons.

ultimately responsible for payment (even when it is paid through logistics operators), regardless of whether the Company itself bears it and not 
the consumers.

2 The Tax Contribution Report is available on Repsol’s corporate website, following this path: Home > Sustainability > Responsible taxation > Tax 

contribution.

Our tax contribution: Own taxes 
and taxes collected from third parties (M€) 

4.2.3 Policies
The Board of Directors of Repsol, in the exercise of its 
non-delegable powers, has adopted the Tax Policy, 
which is based on five principles: (i) responsible 
compliance, (ii) efficient management of tax costs, 
(iii) prevention of significant tax risks, (iv) promotion of 
cooperative relationships, and (v) transparency. 

Repsol not only discloses its Tax Policy, but also 
provides information to its stakeholders through regular 
reports available on the corporate website, which 
notably include:

1.- Tax Contribution Report, in which, among other 
issues, the taxes and public charges paid by item and 
country are detailed. 

2.- Country-by-Country Report for corporate income 
tax (CIT), which is submitted to the tax authorities and 
which Repsol voluntarily makes public, accompanied by 
a set of additional explanations relating to the CIT paid 
in each jurisdiction in which it operates. 

3.- Report on presence in tax havens, detailing and 
explaining the activities carried out and the taxes borne 
in non-cooperative jurisdictions and in other territories 
classified as controversial for tax purposes by civil 
organizations outside the official lists.

4.- Cooperative relations report, which sets out Repsol’s 
position on tax matters deemed significant (for example, 
in the face of the challenges of the energy transition), as 
well as our participation in different international 
forums and organizations.

5.- Report on payments made to public administrations 
in relation to Upstream activities, complying with 
Spanish and European regulations that require large 
companies in the extractive industries sector to prepare 
this report as a result of their extraction operations.

4.2.4 Action plans
In 2025, Repsol submitted its Voluntary Group Tax 
Transparency Report to the State Tax Administration 
Agency (AEAT), a practice it has maintained since 2015 
and plans to continue in the future. It includes relevant 
information related to the Group’s economic activity, as 
well as actions and decisions on tax matters adopted by 
the Group during the year. In addition, in order to 
deepen this relationship, in January 2025 and, for the 
second year running, Repsol voluntarily provided 
documentation on the related party transactions of the 
Repsol Group companies, in accordance with the 
proposals approved within the framework of the Code 
of Good Tax Practices.

Likewise, the Company maintains its track record of 
active participation in cooperative relationship 
programs and initiatives. Following its participation in 
the first International Compliance Assurance Program 
(ICAP) and in the European Trust and Cooperation 
Approach (ETACA), this line of work was further 
strengthened in 2025. Accordingly, Repsol participated 
in Brazil’s CONFIA pilot program and in the Large 
Companies Forum in Portugal. Repsol was also invited 
to join the collaborative compliance program promoted 
by the Peruvian tax authorities, taking part in the first 
thematic working group dedicated to the drafting of a 
Good Governance Code. Along the same lines, the 
Company has offered to participate in the Latin 
American tax risk assessment program promoted by the 
IDB (ICAP-like).
These actions, which will continue on an annual basis, 
are a further show Repsol’s firm focus on tax 
transparency and the improvement of the tax system 
within the framework of the promotion of cooperative 
relations. Their purpose is to increase the confidence of 
its stakeholders by providing greater clarity and 
traceability in its tax conduct.

4.2.5 Metrics and targets

Through its Tax Policy, in line with the Company'’ mission 
and values and with the tax objectives included in its 
Global Sustainability Plan, Repsol intends to be publicly 
recognized as a company with integrity and 
responsibility in tax matters.

Imbued with this spirit, Repsol has included measurable 
and traceable tax objectives in its Global Sustainability 
Plan, which is monitored annually.

Below is a summary of the progress made in 2025 
toward the initiatives being pursued within the Group to 
achieve the aforementioned objectives:

1. Zero presence in OECD/EU non-cooperative jurisdictions 

and streamlining of the corporate structure. In 2025, 
more than 15 redundant entities were removed from 
Repsol’s corporate perimeter, located in 11 jurisdictions. 
Repsol’s presence in Trinidad and Tobago (a non-
cooperative jurisdiction according to the Spanish list) was 
reduced, as a Group subsidiary (dormant) located in that 
country was liquidated.

2. Maintaining leadership in fiscal governance best 
practices. Once again, the GRI 207 standard was 
validated by the Group’s external auditors (in their 
exhaustive option) and Repsol earned public recognition 
for its good tax practices and transparency from various 
representative stakeholders. Examples here include the 
award of the Three-Star “T Transparent” seal, the highest 
distinction granted by Fundación Haz; the renewal of the 
Fair Tax Mark, the gold standard awarded by the Fair Tax 
Foundation for responsible tax conduct; and the receipt 
of the award for the most tax-transparent company in 
the EU, granted by the Dutch association of responsible 
investors (VBDO).

3. Fostering cooperative relations with tax administrations. 

Repsol submitted the Voluntary Tax Transparency Report 
to the Spanish tax authorities for the tenth year running, 
while also disclosing information on to its related-party 
transactions on a strictly voluntary basis, in accordance 
with the recommendations of the Code of Good Tax 
Practices in Spain. 

For more information, see Action plans and Taxation 
(GRI 207).

4.3 Cybersecurity

The Company makes extensive use of the benefits of 
digitalization. This makes it more exposed to cyber risks, 
which may lead to operational disruptions, theft of 
intellectual property or sensitive information, fraud, and 
similar incidents.

As a result of the double materiality assessment carried 
out, a risk has been identified relating to:

• The financial impact arising from events that affect 

the Company’s operations (IRO-53).

Cybersecurity risks are identified by assessing the 
impact that a compromise in the dimensions of 
availability, confidentiality, and integrity—both of 
information and of the technological assets that 
manage it—would have on the Company’s operations, 
its reputation, and the people affected.

4.3.1 Governance
As indicated in Governance, Repsol’s governing bodies, 
in exercising their responsibilities in terms of 
sustainability, play an active role in the supervision of 
cybersecurity. The Executive Committee is responsible 
for approving the Cybersecurity Charter and Policies, 
participating in cyber incident response preparedness 
exercises, and receiving direct reports from the Chief 
Information Security Officer (CISO).

The Executive Committee receives regular updates and 
information on developments in the field of 
cybersecurity, such as:

• Code of Good Governance of Cybersecurity.

• Cybersecurity Charter and Policies.

• Threat situation and significant events related to 

cybersecurity at the Company.

• Cybersecurity risk assessment.

• Risk mitigation plans (cybersecurity strategy).

• Resources needed to implement and execute the 

plans.

Accordingly, matters of interest are submitted to the 
Executive Committee on an annual basis. In addition, it 
requests information whenever it considers it necessary.

The Group also has the Audit and Control Committee 
attached to the Board of Directors, which also oversees 
cybersecurity matters. This Committee receives direct 
information at least once a year from the CISO, who is 
responsible for defining and supervising the security of 
the networks and information systems within the Repsol 
Group, while also acting as a point of contact and 
technical coordinator with the competent authorities 
and the competent Computer Security Incident 
Response Team (CSIRT).

Aside from establishing the CISO’s roles, the Charter 
establishes the Cybersecurity Committee as an internal 
and advisory collegiate body to support the CISO in 
these tasks.

Risk management and internal controls

The Company has a system of Internal Control over 
Sustainability Reporting (ICSR) to reasonably ensure the 
reliability of the reported sustainability information. The 
details of the ICSR can be found in Risk management 
and internal controls over sustainability reporting.

Cybersecurity risks are evaluated together with 
businesses and mitigation objectives are established at 
the global level, using the Cybersecurity Operating 
Model aimed at enhancing the resilience of the systems 
and the operations they support, as well as periodic 
strategic plans. 

This leads to state-of-the-art processes and 
technologies for protection, detection, response and 
recovery against cyberattacks, which are kept up to date 
through the Strategic Cybersecurity Plan. In particular, 
during the last year, the deployment of the 24–26 Plan 
has continued, addressing more than 50 initiatives. 

Cybersecurity risk management also takes account of the 
supply chain, since, depending on the purpose of the award, 
it is required to complete cybersecurity questionnaires for 
suppliers, requiring, depending on the risk, certifications and 
additional legal clauses in contracts. Likewise, human risk is 
addressed via an annual mandatory cybersecurity course for 
employees and ongoing additional awareness activities, 
such as phishing simulations .

The Company also has audit procedures, both internal 
and external, for examining and supervising the control 
framework for cybersecurity risks.

In this context, the Company did not suffer any 
cybersecurity incidents with a significant impact in 2025.

4.3.2 Strategy
As determined by the Cybersecurity Charter, the 
Company has appointed a CISO who leads a 
cybersecurity governance model with the following 
characteristics:

• The CISO has the hierarchical level and independence 

necessary to perform the role and make decisions.

• The model is implemented through a policy, 

regulations and procedures for the protection of 
information and management of cybersecurity to 
support compliance.

•

•

It is based on the risks arising from the business, which 
are determined and monitored periodically, together 
with their controls and mitigation measures, in the 
corresponding areas and with top management.

Internal cybersecurity analyses and third-party audits 
are carried out on a regular basis. International 
standards are taken as reference points and 
independent ratings and benchmarks are followed 
continuously to measure performance. In addition, the 
CISO regularly takes part in national and international 
forums on the subject.

• Specific training and awareness campaigns are given 

to all workers. 

• Business continuity and incident response plans are 

continuously trained and improved.

• There is a security operations center and a cyber 
intelligence service that continuously detects, 
analyzes, reports and corrects information on alerts 
and potential threats, identifies cyberattack patterns 
and manages security incidents.

In cybersecurity, we monitor that there is security with 
respect to  information and infrastructure protection, so 
as to instill trust in all the Company’s stakeholders 
referred to in Business model and strategy.  

4.3.3 Policies
In response to the European NIS2 Directive and 
following the Code of Good Governance for 
Cybersecurity and Cybersecurity Risk Control, we have 
adopted the following measures:

• Repsol has a Protection of Tangible and Intangible 

Assets Policy, in which it expresses its commitment to 
cybersecurity. This commitment is formalized through a 
Cybersecurity Charter that provides structure and 
continuity to the role of the Chief Information Security 
Officer (CISO), establishing the criteria for appointment, 
the role’s functions and responsibilities, and its position 
within the Organization. The Charter also establishes a 
Cybersecurity Committee as an advisory body to 
support the CISO in coordinating the management of 
cybersecurity risks.

• An asset protection and cybersecurity standard that 

establishes responsibilities for the protection of 
information and the devices, networks and systems 
that manage or store it.

4.3.4 Action plans
In 2025, the rollout of the Cybersecurity Strategic Plan 
continued, with a focus on maintaining the high level of 
cyber resilience and information protection achieved under 
the previous plan, thereby offsetting the ongoing increase 
and growing sophistication of threats. The plan also sets 
the objective of enhancing the capabilities of the 
Organization and its people, while at the same time making 
interaction with protection measures easier.

The plan consists of four workstreams:

1. Strengthen the cybersecurity of critical 

infrastructures and maintain a high level of cyber 
resilience in order to avoid operational incidents. 

2. Fortify the protection of digital information, identity 
control and the cloud for the protection of personal 
information. 

3. Improve the internal cybersecurity capabilities, both 

of our Organization and our staff, through the 
optimization of processes, training and awareness.

4. Comply with new regulations and standards such as 

NIS2 and DORA.

With the Cybersecurity Strategic Plan, the aim is to 
improve cybersecurity maturity in all defined aspects. 
The maturity review is carried out biannually in global 
terms and quarterly with the monitoring of the 
initiatives established in the Strategic Plan. 

4.3.5 Metrics and targets
It is important to note that the detailed information of 
these metrics and targets is sensitive information for 
the Company, since such information can have an 
impact on it. 

Monitoring the effectiveness of the actions is carried out 
through oversight of the four lines of work of the 
Strategic Plan. The plan is based on more than 
60 capabilities, which are assessed in terms of their 
maturity, effectiveness, and coverage across the 
Organization, and are linked to the risks they help 
mitigate, with validation by an external firm.

To this end, each line of work is monitored on a quarterly 
operational basis to check its progress in line with the plan, 
and every 18 months the status of the capabilities is 
reassessed to confirm that the plan is having the expected 
effect on them. The aim is the full rollout of all lines of work 
over the plan’s three-year duration.

5. Sustainability notes

5.1. Repsol’s policies1 related to sustainability

Below is a description of the key content of the various 
policies, including their overall objectives and the date 
of approval/revision. 

Code of Ethics and Business Conduct

Goal

To establish the framework of reference to understand and put into practice the conduct and 
standards that the Company expects from each of its employees in their daily work.

Date of approval/revision

Revision 4.1 approved on 28 June 2023 by the Board of Directors.

Description

At Repsol, ethical conduct is conduct that goes beyond strict compliance with the law.
Given the impossibility of anticipating all the issues and situations that may arise in the performance of professional activity, the Code 
provides minimum guidelines of conduct that guide professional and personal behavior. 
To contribute to the understanding of responsibilities and correct decision-making, this Code of Ethics and Business Conduct (the "Code") 
has been developed to define Repsol’s standards and expectations related to issues of ethics and professional and personal conduct.
With the approval of the Code, Repsol commits to respect internationally recognized human rights, which include the rights set forth in 
the International Bill of Human Rights and the principles relating to rights established by the International Labour Organization (ILO) on 
Fundamental Principles and Rights at Work (1) and the eight Fundamental Conventions that elaborate on them. 

(1) These act as a cross-cutting framework applicable to the rest of the policies related to the Company’s own workforce.

People Management Policy

Goal

To attract, develop and retain the talent necessary to meet the Company’s objectives in a sustainable 
way, through a value proposition to employees with whom we maintain a relationship based on 
mutual respect and trust.

Date of approval/revision

Revision 2.0 approved by the Board of Directors of Repsol, S. A. on November 22, 2022.

Description

Repsol’s People Management Policy focuses on attracting, developing, and retaining talent, promoting a work environment based on 
respect, trust, equal opportunities and diversity(1). It includes commitments to foster innovation, collaboration and continuous 
professional development, ensuring fair and competitive compensation. In addition, the prioritization of the health, well-being and safety 
of employees is highlighted, as well as support for the reconciliation of personal and professional life. This policy also includes the 
commitment to maintain an open dialogue with workers’ representatives at all times in an atmosphere of trust and respect, promoting 
freedom of association and collective bargaining. In short, Repsol seeks to create an inclusive, efficient environment aimed at the 
comprehensive well-being of its employees.

(1) Repsol has a Global Diversity and Inclusion Framework, endorsed by the Diversity and Inclusion Committee chaired by the People and 

Organization Department. This committee promotes and supervises the commitments contained in the Sustainability Plan and the Strategic 
Plan, aimed at guaranteeing a work environment that reflects the diversity of the society in which the Company operates. Under this approach, 
Repsol promotes the recognition of merit and individual contribution as pillars of talent management, actively works to identify and eliminate 
barriers that limit the full development of each person, and understands diversity in all its dimensions—gender, generational, functional, 
cultural, gender identity, and sexual orientation—fostering an inclusive environment that allows each professional to grow, contribute and 
evolve.

1 It should be noted that Repsol’s set of policies is subject to the Company’s duties of diligence and responsibility, as well as to the laws and 

regulations applicable to each policy and to the related matters in every jurisdiction in which Repsol operates.

Integrity Policy

Goal

To reiterate Repsol’s commitment to strict compliance with the regulations for the prevention and 
fight against corruption and fraud in all its forms, developing the principles set out in the Code of 
Ethics and Conduct and extending compliance with them, not only to all employees of the companies 
in which the Repsol Group exercises direct or indirect control of management, but also our business 
partners.

Date of approval/revision

Revision 2.0 approved by the Board of Directors of Repsol, S. A. on December 21, 2022.

Description

Repsol rejects all forms of corruption and fraud, public or private, and applies a zero-tolerance standard with respect to any breach of 
this policy. In order to prevent any of these situations, Repsol will carry out all its activities in accordance with the legislation in force in 
all areas of action and in all the countries in which it operates. 
If employees are aware, have doubts or suspicions regarding any form of corruption or fraud or situations provided for in this Policy, they 
must immediately notify their hierarchical superior or Repsol’s Chief Compliance Officer, who will act in accordance with the applicable 
regulations. 
Both employees and any third party may also transmit their information, doubts or concerns in this matter through Repsol’s Ethics and 
Compliance Channel.
Repsol will not tolerate any retaliation against anyone who, in good faith, communicates facts that could constitute a breach of this 
policy and will apply to the communicators the guarantees and protections established by the applicable regulations and legislation.

Risk Management Policy

Goal

To provide greater certainty and security in the achievement of the Company’s objectives to 
shareholders, customers, employees and other interested parties, through the anticipation, 
management and control, as far as possible, of the risks to which the Group is exposed, with a 
comprehensive view.

Date of approval/revision

Revision 3.0 approved by the Board of Directors of Repsol, S. A. on December 21, 2022.

Description

With this policy, Repsol undertakes to implement, under the supervision of the Audit and Control Committee of the Board of Directors, 
an Integrated Risk Management System aligned with international reference standards. Among these, the ISO 31000 standard stands 
out, under whose principles and guidelines the risk management system has been implemented. 
The system includes internal control and information systems based on the principles of the COSO Internal Control – Integrated 
Framework, 2013.

Safety and Occupational Health Policy

Goal

To carry out all the Company’s activities preserving the integrity of people and avoiding any 
damage to the surroundings and the environment, as well as to ensure a healthy and safe work 
environment from both a physical and emotional point of view.

Date of approval/revision

Revision 4.0 approved on 22 November 2022 by the Executive Committee. 

Description

In order to achieve the occupational health and safety goal, this policy includes the commitment to proactive risk management, 
considering risk in decision-making. Risk management will also focus on avoiding any damage to people and the environment, in 
particular the prevention of major accidents.
Through this policy, Repsol undertakes to comply with the legal requirements in force in each place, as well as the established internal 
regulations, which are prepared considering legislative trends and international standards, as well as other commitments that the 
Organization underwrites with its stakeholders.
The principles and commitments set out in this policy should be adopted by employees and, to the extent possible, by contractors, 
partners and collaborators.

Human Rights and Community Relations Policy

Goal

To respect human rights internationally recognized in international treaties and standards on 
business and human rights in all of Repsol’s activities and business relationships.

Date of approval/revision

Revision 3.0 approved by the Repsol Executive Committee on May 9, 2023.

Description

This policy reflects Repsol’s respect for internationally recognized human rights in international treaties(1) and standards(2) on business 
and human rights. 
The term "respect" implies that reasonable efforts will be made to prevent the Company’s activities from causing negative impacts on 
human rights and that, if they occur, efforts will be made to mitigate or repair the harm. To achieve this goal, a number of commitments 
are set out in the following high-level areas: due diligence, impact remediation, stakeholders and business relationships. 

(1) International human rights treaties: International Bill of Human Rights, Convention on the Elimination of All Forms of Discrimination against 
Women, International Convention on the Elimination of All Forms of Racial Discrimination, Convention against Torture and Other Cruel, Inhuman or 
Degrading Treatment or Punishment, Convention on the Rights of the Child, Principles concerning the rights set forth in the International Labour 
Organization (ILO) Declaration, as well as the eight Fundamental Conventions that develop them: Freedom of Association and Collective 
Bargaining (Conventions 87 and 98), Elimination of Forced Labor (Conventions 29 and 105), Effective Abolition of Child Labor (Conventions 138 
and 182) and Elimination of Discrimination (Conventions 100 and 111). ILO Convention No. 169. Humanitarian law in conflict zones. In addition, 
Repsol will also respect all international treaties of regional human rights protection systems in whose countries it operates.

(2) International standards on business and human rights: the United Nations Guiding Principles on Business and Human Rights, the OECD 
Guidelines for Multinational Enterprises, the International Finance Corporation (IFC) Performance Standards on Environmental and Social 
Sustainability and the 10 Principles of the Global Compact. These treaties and standards act as a cross-cutting framework applicable to the rest of 
the policies related to the Company’s own workforce.

Environmental Policy

Goal

To provide energy and products to society to meet its needs efficiently, guaranteeing the adequate 
protection of the environment and the sustainable use of resources without compromising the well-
being of future generations. The commitments contained in this policy are articulated as a 
fundamental lever to achieve the Company’s goal of being net zero emissions by 2050, in line with 
the objectives of the Paris Agreement to combat climate change, as well as with the objective of 
promoting environmental protection and the conservation of natural capital.

Date of approval/revision

Revision 1.0 approved by Repsol’s Executive Committee on December 10, 2024.

Description

Repsol is committed to protecting the environment, the sustainable use of resources, and the prevention of pollution. Therefore, priority 
is given to the actions necessary to identify, evaluate and manage, following the hierarchy of mitigation 1, our impacts, dependencies, 
risks and opportunities on the environment, in our activities and the places where we operate, along the value chain, as well as in the 
products and services we offer to society. To this end, measures related to mitigation and adaptation to climate change, the integration 
of the principles of the circular economy, the prevention of waste generation, the protection and conservation of biodiversity, 
ecosystems and ecosystem services, water management throughout the life cycle of operations, and products and services, and the 
prevention of impacts associated with emissions to air, water, and soil of substances polluting the air, water, and soil, derived from 
Repsol’s operations. 

Sustainability Policy

Goal

Date of approval/revision

Description

To meet the growing demand for energy and products by maximizing Repsol’s contribution to 
sustainable development, to meet present needs without compromising those of future generations, 
with the commitment to become a net-zero emissions company by 2050 in line with the objectives of 
the Paris Agreement.

Revision 2.0 approved by the Board of Directors of Repsol, S. A. on December 21, 2022.

This policy includes the commitment to support the United Nations 2030 Agenda for Sustainable Development and its Sustainable 
Development Goals (SDGs) as well as the principles of the Global Compact in terms of human rights, labor standards, the environment 
and the fight against corruption. It also promotes a fair energy transition that takes into account its impact on workers, local communities 
and society in general.
To this end, Repsol is committed to integrating sustainability into all the Company’s businesses and organizational levels and to promote 
and require sustainability commitments in accordance with/similar to those defined internally throughout its value chain: customers, 
partners, suppliers of products and services and other stakeholders.
Finally, Repsol is committed to knowing, analyzing, and managing the expectations of the Company’s various stakeholders, such as 
shareholders and the financial community, employees, customers, partners, suppliers and contractors, public bodies and institutions, the 
media, local communities, and society in general, and to respond transparently to its stakeholders.

Commercial Relations with Third Parties Policy

Goal

To establish relationships based on the law, ethical principles, integrity and values of the Company, 
with partners, suppliers, contractors and customers who collaborate in the conduct of its business 
activities and with whom commercial or business relationships are established.

Date of approval/revision

Description

Revision 1.0 approved by Repsol’s Executive Committee on November 22, 2022.

This policy includes the commitment to guide activities, processes and decision-making in accordance with Repsol’s ethical principles and 
values and to demand the highest levels of ethics and transparency in the processes of selection, negotiation and formalization of any 
relationship with a third party.
In addition, Repsol will establish and maintain relationships with third parties based on the principles of respect and will promote and 
require its counterparts the commitment to observe in their relationship with third parties. To this end, the behavior and performance of 
the counterparties will be systematically evaluated.

Corporate Tax Policy

Goal

To manage our tax affairs by applying good tax practices and acting with transparency, to pay our 
taxes responsibly and efficiently and to promote cooperative relations with governments, seeking to 
avoid significant risks and unnecessary conflicts.

Date of approval/revision

Revision 0.2 approved by the Board of Directors of Repsol, S. A. on November 25, 2015 and ratified 
by the Executive Committee of Repsol on November 22, 2022.

Description

This policy is aligned with the Company’s mission and values, as well as with the long-term business strategy.
Being aware of Repsol’s responsibility for the sustainable economic development of the societies in which it operates and that the taxes 
paid represent a significant part of the economic contribution to them, Repsol commits in this policy to the responsible payment of taxes 
in the countries in which it operates. Likewise, Repsol will guide its actions in the exercise of the Fiscal function with the maxim of public 
transparency.

Protection of Tangible and Intangible Assets Policy

Goal

To guarantee the fundamental right to the protection of the personal data of all individuals who are 
related to the companies of the Repsol Group; ensuring respect for the right to honor and privacy in 
the processing of different types of personal data.

Date of approval/revision

Revision 1.0 approved by Repsol’s Executive Committee on November 22, 2022.

Description

The commitments contained in this policy focus on complying with current legislation, internal regulations and dissemination of asset 
protection criteria, both its own and those of third parties.
To this end, Repsol will provide the appropriate means for the protection and safeguarding of the Company’s people, interests, assets, 
operations, knowledge, industrial and intellectual property rights, trade secrets and information, as well as the assets that support them, 
including the internet.
This protection is carried out through management based on risk analysis and a process of continuous improvement.

Privacy and Personal Data Protection Policy 

Goal

To ensure the protection of people, property, industrial and intellectual property rights, internet, trade 
secrets, knowledge (know how) and information, as well as the Company’s operations and interests, 
against internal and external aggressions and malpractices of any origin, physical or digital, paying 
special attention to strategic assets and promoting a culture of security, in line with our values.

Date of approval/revision

Revision 1.0 approved by Repsol’s Executive Committee on November 22, 2022.

Description

In this policy, the Company undertakes to protect the privacy of customers, employees and business partners, and the processing of their 
personal data. To this end, all activities will be carried out in accordance with the legislation of the countries in which it operates, 
attending to its spirit and purpose, and according to general principles of personal data processing such as legitimacy, lawfulness and 
fairness of the processing of personal data, minimization, accuracy, limitation of the storage period, transparency and information, 
legitimacy of the source, integrity and confidentiality, proactive accountability, application of due diligence measures before hiring data 
processors, prior evaluation of international data transfers and respecting the rights of data subjects.
