5.2 Taxonomy Regulation 

On June 18, 2020, the European Parliament adopted 
Regulation (EU) 2020/852 on the establishment of a 
framework to facilitate sustainable investments (also 
known as the Sustainable Finance Taxonomy and 
hereinafter referred to as “SFT”).

This taxonomy, which entered into force in 2021, defines 
a number of economic activities ("eligible" activities), 
which fall under 16 macro-sectors, and sets out the 
technical selection criteria for determining whether they 
make a substantial contribution to climate change 
mitigation and adaptation objectives. 

In 2023, the Commission adopted Delegated Regulation 
2023/2486 of 27 June 2023, which includes a new set of 
economic activities that contribute substantially to one 
or more environmental objectives (sustainable use and 
protection of water and marine resources, transition to a 
circular economy, pollution prevention and control, and 
protection and restoration of biodiversity and 
ecosystems), expanding the list of potentially 
sustainable activities that, in the case of Repsol, after an 
evaluation of the defined technical criteria, and 
following the same procedures used for the mitigation 
and adaptation objectives, no additional significant 
activities have been identified to be incorporated in 
this report.

In 2025, Repsol applied the new simplified structure for 
the presentation of economic indicators established in 
the Commission Delegated Regulation (EU) 2026/73 of 
4 July 2025 amending Delegated Regulation (EU) 
2021/2178  and Delegated Regulations (EU) 2021/2139 
and (EU) 2023/2486, as regards simplification of the 
content and information to be disclosed within the 
framework of Regulation (EU) 2020/852 on Taxonomy.

For an "eligible" activity to be considered 
environmentally sustainable, and therefore "aligned" 
with the taxonomy, it must make a substantial 
contribution to at least one of the six environmental 
objectives defined by the EU, not cause significant 
damage to the remaining objectives, and also comply 
with a minimum number of social safeguards. 

The economic indicators (or KPIs) of revenue, capex and 
opex defined by the Delegated Regulation are 
calculated through a process that supports the integrity 
and single imputation of the economic indicators 
reported in accordance with the defined disaggregation 
criteria.

This information is covered by the Internal Control 
Systems over Financial and Non-Financial Information, 
as well as the verification of the external auditor, as 
indicated in Appendix III Risks in this report.  

Alignment determination process
The process of determining the alignment of the 
activities identified as "eligible" by the SFT begins by 
verifying compliance with the criteria for making a 
substantial contribution to one of the six defined 
environmental objectives. 

Once the activities that meet the requirements are 
identified, compliance with the criteria of do no 
significant harm ("DNSH") is validated with respect to 
the different environmental objectives (adaptation to 
climate change, water resources, pollution, circular 
economy, and biodiversity). 

Finally, the appropriate checks are carried out to 
determine that Repsol complies with the so-called 
minimum social safeguards (the OECD Guidelines for 
Multinational Enterprises, the United Nations Guiding 
Principles on Business and Human Rights and the 
International Bill of Human Rights).

Compliance with the criterion of 
making a substantial contribution to 
climate change mitigation
The taxonomy establishes different types of substantial 
contribution criteria. The activities identified by Repsol 
are therefore classified according to the following 
criteria (which are specific to each activity):

• Activities that meet the substantial contribution 

criteria by nature.

• Activities that must exceed an established threshold 
of GHG emissions savings throughout their life cycle 
when compared to the conventional process or 
generate GHG emissions below a certain threshold.

• Activities that must exceed a certain energy density 

threshold.

• Activities that must comply with the applicable 

regulations.

Repsol’s activities are framed within the energy sector; 
therefore, after analyzing the Company’s contribution to 
the six environmental objectives established in the 
Delegated Regulation, the conclusion was reached that, 
at present, the only material objective to report is the 
mitigation of climate change. This objective incorporates 
technical criteria that include various activities carried 
out by the Company, which represent the main focus of 
the eligibility and alignment analysis.

While none of the Company’s activities are directly 
linked to the other five environmental objectives, certain 
complementary activities such as water treatment or 
waste management could be considered eligible. 
However, their compatibility with these objectives has 
not been assessed because they are not material to the 
Company. Furthermore, since these are not business 
activities, they do not generate specific revenue, and the 
opex and capex are included in general line items for 
accounting purposes without the level of traceability 
necessary for their individual assessment.

9.1 Research, development and innovation close to market (17)

(1) They include projects for the development, construction and operation of electrolytic hydrogen manufacturing plants in industrial complexes (CC.II.).
(2) It includes the production of monomers such as ethylene, propylene, benzene or butadiene in the steam cracking process, as well as propylene oxide, 
styrene and glycols in the Intermediates business. Due to the Company’s high level of integration, the vast majority of these raw materials are used to 
manufacture Repsol’s petroleum products and the rest are sold. The glycols produced by the Company, although not explicitly included in the 
description of activity 3.14. established by the taxonomy, also constitute an intermediate product used to obtain other compounds that are eligible 
according to the taxonomy.
(3) This activity includes new recycled polyol and polyolefin products from entirely mechanical recycling, as well as chemical recycling projects that also 
provide a reduction in the carbon footprint. 
(4) Repsol’s chemistry is geared towards the manufacture of plastics and other chemicals, and includes polyols and polyolefins. Regarding polyolefins, 
there are a wide variety of products: low-density polyethylene, high-density polyethylene, metallocene linear low density polyethylene, polypropylene, 
and EVA/EVA copolymers. With respect to polyols, they include polyether polyols for flexible, rigid, and CASE applications, as well as polymeric polyols. 
(5 )It includes projects for the development, construction and operation of solar parks for the generation of electricity from photovoltaic solar 
technology. 
(6) It includes projects for the development, construction and operation of wind farms for electricity generation from wind energy. 
(7) It includes projects for the operation of hydroelectric power plants for the generation of electricity.
(8) It includes the development, construction and operation of pumped hydroelectric plants.
(9)  It includes projects for the development and construction of renewable fuels (hydrobiodiesel, biojet, biogasoline and biopropane) for aircraft, ships, 
trucks or cars.
(10) It includes the operation of combined cycle plants for the generation of electricity from gaseous fossil fuels.
(11) It includes the operation of the cogeneration systems located in the combined cycle plants for the generation of electricity and heat from gaseous 
fossil fuels.
(12) Projects in the exploratory phase or in the initial stages of development for the permanent geological storage of CO2, including transport from the 
point of capture. These include both the dimensioning and characterization of possible geological repositories, as well as the associated initial 
engineering activities.
(13) It includes the installation of electric charging points at service stations
(14) It includes the installation of electric charging points in building parking lots.
(15) It includes the installation of solar panels in the different installations: LPG factories, refineries, chemical complexes, etc., as well as with third parties 
through initiatives such as Solmatch.
(16) It includes digital projects for monitoring and reducing GHG emissions.
(17) It includes research, development and innovation projects of technologies for the manufacture of aggregates through the capture of CO2.

Types of activities
Depending on the contribution that an activity makes to 
the different objectives, it can be classified as follows:

• Activities that, by their own performance, contribute 
to stabilizing greenhouse gas concentrations in the 
atmosphere. In the case of Repsol, this would 
correspond to the activities of renewable electricity 
generation or biofuel manufacturing, among others.

• Enabling: When the activity allows the performance of 
other sustainable activities, such as, for example, the 
installation, maintenance and repair of renewable 
energy technologies.

• Transitional: When the activity supports the transition 

to a climate-neutral economy, such as the 
manufacture of plastics in primary forms.

Compliance with DNSH criteria
The Company has evaluated compliance with each of the 
DNSH criteria for all the activities that meet the substantial 
contribution criteria.

• Climate change adaptation. Repsol has developed a 

semi-quantitative methodology to analyze in detail the 
physical risks of climate change at existing facilities, and 
especially at new facilities that have been added to the 
Company’s portfolio. To carry out this long-term 
analysis, the global warming scenarios described by the 
Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) have 
been used, with a wide range of emissions (SSP5-8.5, 
SSP2-4.5 and SSP1-2.6 scenarios) selected with time 
horizons of 2030, 2040, and 2050. At the moment, the 
physical risk assessment work shows a low risk in 
general, due to the engineering design bases of these 
facilities and the adaptation measures implemented. 
See Climate change for more details.

• Sustainable use and protection of water and marine 
resources. The Company has environmental impact 
studies, which include assessments of impacts on the 
hydric environment, and reports on the ecological,  
chemical and physical status of the waters, so that the 
ecological quality of water flows is at levels aligned 
with the Water Framework Directive to which the 
DNSH criterion refers.

• Transition to a circular economy. In 2024, Repsol 

approved its Environmental Policy, which establishes 
specific commitments in the field of circular economy: 
promoting the application of the principles of the 
circular economy and optimizing the use of natural 
resources and raw materials, including energy and 
water resources. In addition, since 2019 the Group has 
had a framework contract with the company Surus for 
the application of circular economy principles to all the 
assets that are no longer used by the Company. Repsol 
participates in international consortia that promote 
circular economy principles. What’s more, the circular 
economy commitments are applied in each project 
through the contracting of suppliers that have express 
commitments to withdraw and reuse equipment and 
components. In the specific case of activity 4.10. 
Energy storage, Repsol has implemented a waste 
management plan aimed at ensuring the highest 
possible level of recycling of the waste generated by 
this activity.

• Pollution control and prevention. Repsol has specific 
reports on the implementation of the Best Available 
Techniques (BAT) and on compliance with the 
emissions/discharge limits associated therewith. 
Likewise, the Company complies with the applicable 
European regulations regarding the presence of 
dangerous substances in equipment and products 
(REACH and ROHS, among others).

• Protection and recovery of biodiversity and 

ecosystems. The Company has environmental impact 
studies and reports on mitigation and compensation 
measures adopted in the affected terrestrial 
ecosystems, which guarantees compliance with DNSH 
principles in this area.

Compliance with minimum social 
safeguards
Repsol complies with the highest international standards 
in this area1: the OECD Guidelines for Multinational 
Enterprises, the United Nations Guiding Principles on 
Business and Human Rights (including the principles and 
rights set out in the eight Fundamental Conventions 
identified in the International Labour Organization’s 
Declaration on Fundamental Principles and Rights at 
Work) and the International Bill of Human Rights.

• Human rights (includes labor rights). Repsol has had 

a Human Rights and Community Relations Policy 
since 2008 that complies with the aforementioned 
international standards. In addition, it has a human 
rights due diligence model for the management of 
internal processes. The due diligence model is applied 
throughout all stages of the life cycle of the assets, 
from analysis to abandonment, and is based on the 
main international standards. The actions are 
accompanied by strategies for participation with local 
communities and other stakeholders in all the projects 
operated. See Affected communities for more detail.

• Corruption and bribery. The Company’s Integrity 

Policy is the reference framework for action in aspects 
related to both corruption and fraud. In addition, 
there is an integrated compliance model that 
contributes to reinforcing a global culture of 
compliance and improving the identification, 
monitoring and support in the management of 
compliance risks, including corruption. Repsol has 
training programs and response mechanisms on

Financial year 2025

events that could constitute breaches of the Code of 
Ethics and Business Conduct or indications of 
possible commission of criminal offenses covered by 
Repsol’s Crime Prevention Model or suspicions of non-
compliance in this area. See Business conduct for 
more detail.

• Tax policy. Repsol undertakes to comply with best 

practices in responsible taxation and tax governance, 
by voluntarily following the most prestigious 
international principles and recommendations 
(B Team, GRI 207, OECD standard for tax risk control). 
See Responsible taxation and Taxation (GRI 207) for 
more details.

• Fair competition. Repsol is focused on complying with 
anti-trust regulations in every area of activity and in 
all countries in which it operates. This goal is a core 
element of Repsol’s Code of Ethics and Business 
Conduct. In addition, specific risk assessments are 
carried out with respect to competition, and the 
Company has measures in place to prevent or mitigate 
the risks. On the other hand, specific training and 
awareness actions have been developed in this area. 
See Business conduct for more detail.

Economic indicators
The information is presented in accordance with the new 
simplified structure for the presentation of economic 
indicators set out in the European Commission 
Delegated Regulation of 4 July 2025 amending 
Delegated Regulation (EU) 2021/2178, Delegated 
Regulation (EU) 2021/2139 and Commission Delegated 
Regulation (EU) 2023/2486.

Determination and calculation of 
indicators
The turnover indicator corresponds to revenue from 
ordinary activities, specifically from sales and provision 
of services in accordance with IFRS-EU.

The following consolidated income statement headings 
are used for calculation of the denominator (see Note 
5.1. of the Consolidated Financial Statements):

but also the cost of the installed modules.

year due to operating investments and additions to the

The opex ratio includes the expenses necessary to 
support the continued and effective operation of the 
assets (provided that they are directly assignable to 
them) for maintenance and conservation (which includes 
expenses incurred in building renovation measures), 
lease expenses and research and development expenses 
under the heading Other operating expenses in the 
consolidated income statement.

Main changes in the period
In view of the above templates, in 2025:

• Revenue from aligned activities amounted to 

€437 million (0.8% of the Group’s total revenue) and 
correspond mainly to those from the manufacture of 
biofuels thanks to the new production plant located in 
Cartagena and the operation of electricity generation 
assets from renewable sources (wind, solar and hydro) 
as a result of the commissioning of new wind farms in 
Spain and the USA. The level of revenue is similar to 
that of 2024 (0.8%). For more detail, see Low-Carbon 
Generation and Industrial businesses in this report.

• The capex of the aligned activities amounts to 

€1,091 million (28% of the Group’s total) and relates 
mainly to the development and construction of 
electricity generation projects from renewable 
sources (wind and solar) in the USA, but also for the 
development of biofuel manufacturing projects 
mainly in Puertollano and Tarragona, hydrogen 
manufacturing and installation of solar panels and 
vehicle recharging points, among others. This is a 
reduction compared to 2024 (40%) due to the organic 
development of Repsol’s project portfolio in Spain 
and the USA and the inorganic acquisition of 
ConnectGen in 2024.

• Aligned opex amounted to €34 million (5%), at levels 

similar to those of the previous year (4%).

5.3 Taxation (GRI 207)

The proper management of the tax obligations to which a 
business group is subject has a direct effect on the social 
and environmental spheres, since the payment of taxes in 
accordance with the legal framework in force has a 
significant impact on countries’ development and growth.

Moreover, transparency on tax strategy and policy has 
recently gained immense significance among 
stakeholders. Aware of this challenge, Repsol has 
adopted the highest international standards 

in terms of the breakdown of tax information, most 
notably including those derived from GRI 207.

In order to graphically illustrate the Group’s degree of 
compliance with the commitments set out above, the 
following table provides a summarized reconciliation 
between the principles adopted by Repsol1 and the 
requirements of the GRI 207 standard, as well as 
evidence on the practical implementation of each 
requirement organized around the seven principles of 
responsible taxation of the B Team. 

RESPONSIBLE TAX PRINCIPLES

APPLICATION AND EVIDENCE OF COMPLIANCE

1 Responsibility and governance

Taxation is a crucial part of corporate 
responsibility and is supervised by the 
Board of Directors.

2 Compliance

Compliance with the letter and spirit of 
tax legislation and payment of taxes in 
the countries where value is created.

3 Corporate structure

On commercial grounds and with 
genuine substance. We do not seek 
abusive tax advantages.

4 Cooperative relations (1)
Development of corporate relations 
with tax authorities, grounded in 
mutual respect, transparency, and 
trust.

• The Board of Directors approves the Tax Policy.
• The Board of Directors monitors enforcement of the strategy and tax risk management at least once 

a year.

• The tax strategy is published on the corporate website.
• Compliance with the letter and spirit of the law.
• The GSP includes tax objectives.

• Regulations, internal control processes and whistleblowing channel to ensure compliance with tax 

obligations.

• Tax control framework compliant with best standards and validated by an independent expert.
• Tax risks integrated into Enterprise Risk Management System (ERMS). For more information, see 

Appendix III Risks in this report.

Internal procedure for transfer pricing aligned with value creation and the arm’s length principle.

• Monthly review of compliance with tax obligations. 
•
• Appropriate organizational structure and resources.
• Professional team undergoing continuous training, subject to a common compensation policy and 

with a contingency plan for key positions. Commitment to digitalization, as a tool to strengthen the 
Tax Control Framework (TCF).

• Corporate structure aligned with the business and adapted to legal requirements and corporate 

governance standards.

• Removing dormant companies from the corporate structure.
• Non-use of special purpose entities in tax havens.

• Application of the Spanish Code of Best Tax Practices.
• Voluntary submission of the Tax Transparency Report to the Spanish Tax Agency.
• Voluntary provision of documentation on related-party transactions (Code of Good Tax Practices).
• Voluntary participation in the ETACA(2) of the European Union, continuing that of the ICAP(3) of the 

OECD.

Peru (5)  and in other countries. 

• Participation in the CONFIA cooperative program(4) in Brazil, the collaborative compliance program in 

• Offered participation in the IDB’s Latin American fiscal risk assessment program (similar to the ICAP).
• Qualified as an authorized economic operator in the EU and Peru.

GRI 207

GRI 207-1

GRI 207-2

GRI 207-1

GRI 207-3

5 Seeking and accepting tax incentives

Promotion of safeguards to ensure 
transparency and consistency with the 
legislative and regulatory framework.

• Claiming and using tax benefits in compliance with the letter and spirit of the regulations.
• Verifying that the incentives applied are widely available to all economic operators.
• Supporting the publication of oil contracts and their tax incentives.
• Description of the main impacts that the application of incentives have on the effective tax rate in the 

countries where Repsol operates (Country by Country Report).

GRI 207-2

6 Support for an effective tax system(6)
Dialogue with governments, business 
groups and civil society to support the 
development of an effective tax 
system.

7 Transparency
Disclosing information on tax strategy 
and taxes paid.

• First non-mining EITI  energy company(7) to sign the beneficial ownership transparency agreement. 
• Collaboration with international organizations (OECD, UN or EU), governments and NGOs. Taking part 

in debates and public consultation processes.

• Participation in international initiatives on responsible taxation and tax governance (B Team).

• Benchmarks in Spain and the EU for tax transparency according to third-party reports and public 

accolades.

• Publication of tax payments by country.
• Publication of the Country by Country Report following the OECD criterion.
• Detailed tax information available on the corporate website and in the annual reports.

GRI 207-3

GRI 207-1
GRI 207-4

(1) Repsol maintains cooperative relations with the main tax authorities of which it is a taxpayer (Brazil, Spain, the USA, the Netherlands, Peru, Portugal, the United 

Kingdom, Singapore, etc.) and participates in forums in which transparent collaboration is promoted with the mutual objective of facilitating and improving the 
application of the tax system, increasing tax certainty and reducing litigation. More information on Repsol’s corporate website, following this path: Home > 
Sustainability > Responsible taxation > Responsible tax policy.

(2) European Trust and Cooperation Approach: A program sponsored by the European Commission aimed at facilitating tax compliance for companies, based on 

increasing cooperation, transparency and trust between taxpayers and authorities through the supervision of tax risks in terms of transfer pricing of 
multinational groups. The tax administrations of Spain, the Netherlands, Luxembourg, Portugal and Germany participated in Repsol’s review carried out in 2023. 
The process covered 2019 and 2020 and concluded by classifying the vast majority of the operations reviewed as "low tax risk".

(3) International Compliance Assurance Program: an OECD initiative aimed at achieving greater cooperation between tax authorities in monitoring tax risks at 

multinational groups, mainly in relation to transfer pricing and permanent establishments. The first ICAP (pilot) program covered 2016 and ran until 2018. The 
administrations of Canada, Spain, the USA, the Netherlands and the United Kingdom participated in the supervision of Repsol.

(4) CONFIA: Cooperative relationship pilot program developed by the Brazilian tax authorities. The aim is to foster a culture of responsible tax compliance.
(5) Collaborative Compliance Program: Through this program, Peru’s tax administration seeks to facilitate compliance with obligations, give taxpayers early certainty 

about the tax treatment of their operations and reduce litigation rates, based on transparency.

(6) Repsol comments on the work carried out by the subcommittees created by the UN Committee of Experts on International Cooperation in Tax Matters. It is also a 

member of the Tax Committee of the OECD’s Business and Industry Advisory Committee (BIAC).

(7) Extractive Industries Transparency Initiative (EITI): Initiative for the transparency of extractive industries. EITI is the global standard for the good governance of oil, 

gas and mining resources. It ensures transparency regarding the way in which a country’s natural resources are governed.

1 Repsol assumes the principles of the B Team, a group of companies, including Repsol, that aim to promote sustainable development and, in 

particular, responsible taxation and good governance in tax matters.

GRI 207 Compliance summary table 

GRI 207/CSRD Requirements

Evidence of compliance by the Repsol Group

GRI 207-1 Approach to tax

a.i) Tax strategy
GOV-1 and MDR-P

Balance in tax 
compliance, 
business activities, 
ethical, social and 
sustainable 
development 
expectations

a.ii) Review body of 
the tax strategy
GOV-1 and GOV-2

a.iii) Approach to 
regulatory 
compliance
SBM-1

a.iv) Relationship 
between the 
approach to tax, 
corporate strategy 
and sustainable 
development
GOV-4, SBM-1 and 
MDR-P

Repsol has a tax strategy, approved by the Board of Directors, which is mandatory for all employees and Group 
companies. 
In accordance with the principles contained in the Tax Policy, Repsol is committed to managing its tax affairs by 
applying good tax practices and acting with transparency, to paying taxes responsibly and efficiently, and to 
promoting cooperative relationships with governments, trying to avoid significant risks and unnecessary 
conflicts. The Tax Policy is available on Repsol's corporate website, following this path: Home > Sustainability > 
Sustainability strategy > Policies > Tax policy.

Repsol’s tax strategy, as well as its tax contribution, is reviewed at least once a year by the Board of Directors. 
The Board also oversees compliance and the key aspects of tax matters and risks.

In the awareness of its responsibility in the social and economic development of the countries where it is 
present, the Repsol Group accords priority to responsible compliance with the payment of taxes in host 
countries. Its commitment to comply respects both the letter and the spirit of the law. Repsol reconciles strict 
compliance with the regulatory and jurisdictional framework in force in each jurisdiction with the defense of its 
legitimate interests, using at all times the remedies and actions provided for by law when it considers that 
certain actions may raise doubts about their legal compliance.

The Group’s Tax Policy is aligned with the Company’s mission and values and with its Sustainable Development 
Goals. Repsol seeks to be publicly recognized as a company of integrity and fiscal responsibility. Tax decisions are 
made responsibly, in accordance with a reasonable interpretation of prevailing tax law and regulations and are 
aligned with the economic activity of the various businesses.
The tax function is involved in the Group’s business decisions, ensuring that they are aligned with the principles 
enshrined in its Tax Policy and reflect the economic reality and motivations of its various businesses. Thus, there 
are internal regulations and procedures (among others, rules on investments, on related-party transactions, etc.) 
to ensure that the tax approach is based on sound economic or business reasons (avoiding abusive tax planning 
schemes or practices), the non-use of opaque or artificial corporate structures with the aim of concealing or 
reducing the transparency of their activities and the application of the arm's length principle in their intra-group 
transactions. See also Presence in tax havens and "other Controversial Territories", a report available on Repsol’s 
corporate website, following this path: Home > Sustainability > Responsible taxation. For more information on 
the tax-related objectives incorporated into the PGS, see the Sustainability section of the corporate website.

GRI 207-2 Tax governance, control and risk management

Description of tax 
governance and 
control framework

a.i) Governing body 
responsible for 
compliance with tax 
strategy
GOV-1 and GOV-2

a.ii) How the 
approach to tax is 
integrated within the 
organization
GOV-3, GOV-4 and 
MDR-A

The Board of Directors is the governing body in charge of adopting the Repsol Group'’ Tax Policy, which 
contains the tax strategy. The execution and monitoring of the tax strategy is subject to supervision through 
meetings held at least once a year where they review, among other issues, the tax policies applied during the 
year, the content of the Voluntary Tax Transparency Report, the taxes paid by the Group, the incidence of tax 
risks,  the mechanisms for their management and control, the Group’s presence in tax havens and initiatives in 
the field of cooperative relations and tax transparency. For more information, see the Audit and Control 
Committee’s Annual Report, which is made available to shareholders on the occasion of the General 
Shareholders’ Meeting.

The orderly management of Repsol’s tax affairs is carried out within a scope of action, Governance and Tax 
Control Framework (TCF), which rests on four basic pillars: (i) principles of action, (ii) expert team, 
(iii) processes and systems for tax compliance and (iv) control and management of tax risks. For more 
information on how the approach to tax is integrated into Repsol’s organization, see the following path on the 
corporate website: Home > Sustainability > Responsible taxation > Tax control framework.
The integration of Repsol’s tax approach is governed by an orderly regulatory management model comprising 
policies, standards (global and specific), internal procedures and controls, and standardized processes, all of 
which are subject to the guidelines contained in the Code of Ethics and Business Conduct, aimed at mitigating 
the most relevant tax risks. 
Repsol’s tax department is made up of experts in a range of tax-related disciplines; these professionals are 
responsible for managing all the tax affairs of the businesses and areas of the Group. Under the broad 
oversight of the Directorate of Fiscal Affairs (DAF), the management of tax matters is decentralized to the tax 
units operating in each country or business, so that they are able to adequately address the specific needs 
and circumstances of each business and tax system. The continuity of strategy implementation and tax 
management in the face of unforeseen events is underpinned by a contingency plan that ensures suitable 
succession in key tax-management positions.
The Group’s tax experts are subject to the same remuneration and incentive policy as the rest of the 
Company’s employees, and there is also a comprehensive continuous training plan, updated annually, which 
allows them to strengthen and complete their professional skills and renew their commitments to comply 
with the obligations arising from the Code of Ethics and Business Conduct.

GRI 207-2 Tax governance, control and risk management

Description of the 
tax governance 
and control 
framework

a.iii) Tax risks, 
identification, 
management and 
monitoring
GOV-5

Repsol’s tax risk management is integrated into the overall policy of the Enterprise Risk Management System 
(ERMS) and is reflected in the existence of processes, systems, and internal controls (ICFR, Compliance Plan, 
key controls, etc.). The DAF, as the tax compliance body, is responsible for managing the Group's tax risks. In 
the context of the ERMS, the DAF monitors tax risks by drawing up and updating a risk map that comprises 
identification, analysis, assessment, verification and reporting stages.
In addition, in order to mitigate tax compliance risks, Repsol has implemented standardized and documented 
processes that regulate essential aspects of tax compliance. These processes identify the persons and areas 
responsible for each phase of the tax management process and define all the activities to be carried out for 
the preparation of tax returns and tax assessments. Tax management processes must, therefore, ensure the 
reliability and traceability of the information and establish a suitable level of prior reviews.
In addition, Repsol has robust information management systems that guarantee the integrity of information 
and tax compliance processes and minimize the possibility of human error in this type of action. This 
management is being reinforced with initiatives aimed at greater digitalization in line with our commitment to 
make processes more robust and reliable.

GRI 207/CSRD Requirements

Evidence of compliance by the Repsol Group

Description of the 
tax governance 
and control 
framework 

a.iv) Evaluation of 
compliance with the 
tax governance and 
control framework
GOV-5

The control over tax risks and the reporting of tax issues are complemented by procedures and controls that 
guarantee the integrity and reliability of the accounting information used in tax processes. One of thee main 
functions of the Audit and Control Committee is to support the Board of Directors in its oversight duties. Its 
powers include the periodic review of the effectiveness of internal control systems, internal audit and risk 
management systems, including tax risks, the process of regularly monitoring and reviewing the Internal 
Control over Financial Reporting (ICFR) system and the effective implementation of the tax risk strategy and 
risk management processes, submitting transactions involving special risks to the Board for approval. 
The standards and procedures are also reviewed by the corporate Department for People and Organization, 
with the aim of ensuring the integrity, uniformity, validity, availability and accessibility of the Company’s 
internal regulatory documents, while also facilitating their sound management through the channels in place 
and having them approved at the appropriate level. See Appendix III Risks in this report for further details.

b) Description of the 
mechanisms for 
reporting concerns 
related to taxation
GOV-4

Any employee or third party may report possible breaches of the Code of Ethics and Business Conduct or 
the Crime Prevention Model, including potentially unethical or illegal conduct affecting the integrity of the 
organization in relation to taxation. These disclosures may be made in absolute confidentiality, and indeed 
anonymously, through the whistleblower channel permanently managed by an independent third party 
authorized for this purpose.

c) Process of 
verifying tax 
information
GOV-4

Repsol has an expert team that analyzes the good tax governance initiatives of international organizations to 
align its tax strategy with the principles that inform global best practices.
Thus, Repsol carries out a self-assessment and periodic updating of its Tax Control Framework (TCF), 
comparing it with the highest standards in tax governance, including the B Team’s principles of responsible 
taxation, which have been contrasted by other B Team companies in a mutual review process (peer review), 
the requirements of GRI 207 or the OECD model for tax risk control, among others. Likewise, the adequacy of 
its TCF to the best international practices in tax compliance has been subject to verification and review by 
independent experts. According to the assessment carried out by these experts, Repsol’s TCF is considered to 
achieve a high level of convergence and compliance with the criteria set out in international standards and is 
in line with the requirements of the UNE 19602 tax compliance standard. Lastly, and as mentioned earlier, the 
Board of Directors is informed of and oversees the effective implementation of the Group’s tax policy and 
strategy.
For more information, see the Good Tax Practices Self-Assessment Report published on Repsol’s corporate 
website, by following this path: Home > Sustainability > Responsible taxation > Responsible tax policy.

GRI 207-3 Stakeholder engagement and management of concerns related to tax

Stakeholder 
participation and 
management of 
tax concerns(1)

a.i) Commitment to 
tax authorities
SBM-2

In accordance with the principles that guide our Tax Policy, Repsol is committed to supporting an effective 
tax system and maintaining cooperative relations with the tax authorities of the countries where it operates, 
based on mutual respect, transparency and trust. To this end, it collaborates with the tax authorities in 
identifying and searching for solutions to fraudulent tax practices, while facilitating access to information and 
prioritizing non-litigious channels for resolving disputes. This approach encompasses adherence to 
cooperation agreements and active audits in real time.
Key examples of Repsol’s initiatives in the field of cooperative relations include: (i) the voluntary adherence in 
Spain to the Code of Good Tax Practices and the presentation, since 2015, of the Voluntary Report on Tax 
Transparency, as well as the proactive submission of documentation relating to related-party transactions; 
(ii) the qualification of Repsol as an authorized economic operator in the European Union and in Peru, in 
recognition of its status as a reliable operator in the field of customs procedures; (iii) participation in the EU’s 
ETACA pilot program aimed at facilitating corporate tax compliance on the basis of a relationship of enhanced 
cooperation, transparency and mutual trust in line with the OECD’s ICAP initiative (coordinated verification by 
the tax authorities of different countries assessing tax risks, among others, in terms of transfer pricing) and, as a 
result, the qualification of Repsol as an entity with a low risk of non-compliance by the tax authorities 
participating in the initiative; and (iv) the strengthening of cooperative relationships through, among others, the 
CONFIA program in Brazil, the Forum of Large Taxpayers in Portugal, participation in the collaborative 
compliance program in Peru, the TGF in Singapore, and through approaches similar to the Compliance Assurance 
Process (CAP) program in the USA.

GRI 207-3 Stakeholder engagement and management of concerns related to tax

Stakeholder 
participation and 
management of 
tax concerns(1)

a.ii) Advocacy of 
public policy on 
taxation
SBM-2

A significant portion of the existing laws and regulations on tax transparency and responsibility originate 
from the debates and forums that go on at various international organizations (UN, OECD, EU, etc.). Repsol 
therefore promotes institutional relations with these authorities and other stakeholders to align the 
Company’s tax policies with social reality, contribute responsibly to the creation of a fairer and more balanced 
international tax framework, and enable anticipation in tax management in the face of any regulatory changes 
to minimize their risks and impacts. An example of this is Repsol’s participation, sometimes on its own behalf, 
in the public consultation  processes regularly requested by international organizations such as the UN, the 
OECD, the EU or the Platform for Collaboration on Tax Matters (UN, OECD, IMF and World Bank).
Through its participation in these debates (in most cases at the invitation of the corresponding body), Repsol has 
had the opportunity to present its views on issues of great importance in the current environment, such as global 
minimum taxation (OECD and EU), European regulatory proposals on corporate taxation (transfer pricing, BE-FIT, 
etc.), energy taxation or the carbon border adjustment, among others. It should therefore be noted that Repsol 
participates in the work carried out by the subcommittees set up by the UN Committee of Experts on International 
Cooperation on Tax Matters, which discuss and prepare tax guides for the authorities of developing countries. 
Repsol is also a member of the Tax Committee of the Business at OECD (formerly known as BIAC) and holds the 
position of vice-chair of the Tax Commission of the International Chamber of Commerce (ICC).

(1) For more information, see the Report on Environmental Management and Cooperative Relations published on Repsol’s corporate website, 
following this path: Home > Sustainability > Responsible taxation > Cooperative relationships.

GRI 207/CSRD Requirements

Evidence of compliance by the Repsol Group

GRI 207-3 Stakeholder engagement and management of concerns related to tax

a.iii) Processes for 
eliciting and 
considering 
stakeholder opinions 
and concerns
SBM-2

Repsol maintains an open and honest dialogue with NGOs and social action platforms (Intermon OXFAM, Haz 
Fundación) in pursuit of a fairer and more effective tax system. This interrelationship has enabled first-hand 
knowledge of the main concerns among stakeholders regarding Repsol’s social accountability process and 
has facilitated a closer understanding of the true magnitude and scale of the Company’s tax contribution in 
the countries in which it operates. Many of the concerns expressed by stakeholders have been answered in 
the enhanced tax transparency initiatives referred to in this Appendix IV.
Stakeholder response to these initiatives has been positive. Thus, internationally renowned organizations, 
such as the Fair Tax Foundation, the EU Tax Observatory, Norges Bank, the Corporate Responsibility 
Observatory, Transparency International Spain, OXFAM Intermón, Fundación Haz and the Dutch investment 
association VBDO have recognized the Company’s high level of tax transparency at the Spanish and European 
level, confirming the excellent degree of compliance by Repsol with its commitments in the area of 
accountability assumed in its Tax Policy. 

GRI 207-4 Country-by-country reporting

SBM-1 and SBM-2

Presentation of 
financial, 
economic, and tax 
information on 
each jurisdiction in 
which Repsol 
operates

Repsol maintains its commitment to be transparent and share relevant information with its shareholders and 
stakeholders, and since 2018 it has made public the data of its Country-by-Country Tax Report, with respect to 
those in which it has a fiscal presence. The table included in this section of the report and in the Country-by-
Country Tax Report published on Repsol’s corporate website corresponds to the 2024 data, as submitted to the 
Spanish tax authorities in 2025 for automatic exchange with other tax authorities. With the publication of these 
reports since 2018, Repsol has been ahead of the curve in complying with European regulations (EU Directive 
2021/2101), adapted to Spanish regulations by Law 28/2022, of December 21. The report publishes further 
disclosures to help readers truly understand the Group’s presence, performance and tax contribution in each 
country, which goes beyond the scope of the Directive.
Although the data included follow OECD standards to comply the requirements of GRI 207-4, the income 
received in each tax jurisdiction is also broken down with related entities from other tax jurisdictions. The 2024 
Report includes a significant new feature, which is a self-assessment on the application of the Pillar Two rules 
on transitional safe harbors.

Presence in non-cooperative 
jurisdictions 

Repsol’s Tax Policy prohibits the use of opaque or 
artificial structures that conceal or reduce the 
transparency of its activities. For this reason, the Group 
undertakes not to have a presence in tax havens, except 
for business reasons, and, if it does, to be transparent in 
its activities.

The definition of a tax haven or non-cooperative 
jurisdiction is unsettled. Repsol considers tax havens to 
be territories classified as such by the regulations of 
Spain2 and the European Union3, as well as

included by the OECD in its list of non-cooperative 
jurisdictions in terms of transparency and exchange of 
information.

Following the liquidation of an inactive subsidiary in 
Trinidad and Tobago at the end of 2025, only one 
Repsol Group entity has a presence in non-cooperative 
jurisdictions, and its income is negligible. This entity 
engages in oil and gas exploration and production 
activities in that country.

Meanwhile, certain non-governmental organizations 
concerned with responsible business practices also draw 
up their own lists of tax havens based on different 
criteria and objectives. Repsol has selected some of

these lists given their public visibility or 
representativeness and the countries included there are 
referred to as "controversial territories". In an exercise of 
enhanced transparency, Repsol identifies its companies 
and activities in these territories and publishes detailed 
information on its corporate website.

b) Organization of work

[GRI 3-3] Management of material topics

In 2025, 2,251,932.47 working hours of absenteeism 
were counted as a result of common illness and non-
work-related accidents. The total figure includes 
56,335.50 working hours of absenteeism due to 
incidents of common illness, and non-work-related 
accidents have been counted at Repsol Resources UK 
for the period from January 1, 2025 to July 29, 2025. A 
total of 2,195,597 working hours were lost due to 
absenteeism as a result of common illness and non-
work-related accidents, excluding hours corresponding 
to the UK.

In 2024, 2,142,068 working hours were lost due to 
absenteeism as a result of common illness and non-
work-related accidents.

e) Training2

In 2025, there was an increase in training hours 
compared with 2024, from 776,757 to 839,616 hours, 
which represents an increase of 8.1%. This growth is 
concentrated in the professional/technical and 
administrative/worker categories, with increases of 9.1% 
and 8.1%, respectively. Power skills (communication, 
collaboration, critical thinking and adaptability) were 
promoted as key competencies for organizational 
performance. Training in artificial intelligence was also a 
priority to prepare teams in the strategic use of 
emerging technologies. In addition, the deployment of 
the Safety and Environmental Management System 
(SEMS) continued at the operating units, guaranteeing 
excellence and safety in the processes.

5. Information on society

a) Commitment to sustainable 
development: partnership 
and sponsorship actions

Repsol actively participates in various global initiatives 
and associations related to sustainability, as well as in 
other thematic initiatives and associations related to 
different ESG aspects. These collaborations allow 
Repsol and other companies to share best practices, and 
learn from other organizations, fostering continuous 
improvement and progress in sustainability. In addition, 
these collaborations are established as one of the 
17 Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) of the United 
Nations. These are some of these associations and 
initiatives in which Repsol participates:

• Spanish Network of the United Nations Global 

Compact

•

IPIECA

• OGCI

• Zero Routine Flaring by 2030 (World Bank)

• Climate and Clean Air Coalition (CCAP)

• Natural Capital Coalition

• CEO Water Mandate

•

International Process Safety Group (IPSG)

• Concawe

• Extractive Industries Transparency Initiative (EITI)

• Voluntary Principles on Security and Human Rights

b) Subcontracting and suppliers

[GRI 308-1] New suppliers that were screened using 
environmental criteria. [414-1] New suppliers that 
were screened using social criteria

In 2025, as in 2024, 100% of new suppliers were 
screened based on criteria related to human rights, 
social, labor, and environmental criteria.

[GRI 308-2] Negative environmental impacts in the 
supply chain and actions taken

In 2025, a total of 1,949 environmental assessments 
(2,381 in 2024) were carried out on 1,204 suppliers 
(1,569 in 2024). In 25 evaluations, corresponding to 
21 suppliers, the environmental performance rating was 
less than 5 out of 10 (24 evaluations corresponding to 
20 suppliers in 2024). Negative assessments are 
associated with, among other factors, contracts for 
installation, cleaning, business services and equipment 
maintenance.

[GRI 414-2] Negative social impacts in the supply chain 
and actions taken

In 2025, as in 2024, there was no negative social impact 
on the Company’s suppliers in the supply chain, so it was 
not necessary to define any action.

Public grants received 

[GRI 201-4] Financial assistance received from 
government 
In 2025, operating and capital grants were recognized in 
the Group’s Consolidated Financial Statements in the 
amount of €87 million (€43 million in 2024). In addition,

 in 2025, funds were received from public authorities to 
finance the development of two electrolyzers at the 
Cartagena and Bilbao refineries to produce renewable 
hydrogen in the amount of €315 million (see Note 19 of 
the Consolidated Financial Statements).

M

Methodology for Consolidated 
Statement of Non-Financial Information 
reporting and metrics 

Average remuneration of directors by gender

The methodology used to calculate the average remuneration 
of directors by gender follows a structured process. First, 
information is gathered on the amounts paid to each director 
for their participation in the various committees during the 
2025 fiscal year. In 2025, these values are identical to those in 
2024, as there were no changes to the point value or to  the 
composition of the Board or its Committees. Secondly, each 
director is classified according to their gender. The period 
during which each person served as a member of the Board is 
then identified and, based on this information and according to 
their time of membership, the corresponding FTE is calculated. 
With this data, the simple average remuneration by gender is 
obtained by dividing the sum of remunerations for each group 
by the number of directors within that group. Finally, this 
calculation is performed twice: once including the Chair and 
once excluding them, in order to provide an alternative 
comparative perspective.

METHODOLOGY

Number of recordable occupational injuries

The number of recordable occupational injuries corresponds to 
the total number of personal consequences (fatalities, 
permanent injuries, lost‑day injuries, medical treatment, and 
restricted work) accumulated during the year.

Total Recordable Injury Rate (TRIR)

The Total Recordable Injury Rate (TRIR) is the total number of 
personal consequences (fatalities, permanent injuries, lost‑day 
injuries, medical treatment, and restricted work) accumulated 
during the year per one million hours worked.

Severity Index

This is the total number of workdays lost due to occupational 
injuries with sick leave accumulated during the year per one 
thousand hours worked.

Absenteeism hours

The number of absenteeism hours included in section b) Work 
organization has been calculated considering only common 
contingencies and non‑occupational accidents, explicitly 
excluding occupational accidents and occupational diseases.

5.5 TNFD - Taskforce on Nature-related 
Financial Disclosures

In 2025, Repsol has carried out a comparative analysis of 
its practices with the TNFD’s disclosure 
recommendations. This information is not part of the 
Consolidated Statement of Non-Financial Information 
and Sustainability Information and has not been verified.  

Repsol’s commitment to nature

Pollution

Repsol will focus on 
pollution prevention, 
management and 
remediation in all its 
operations.

Water

Repsol will focus on 
reducing freshwater 
withdrawal from the 
environment, 
especially in areas at 
water risk.

Biodiversity 
and ecosystems

Repsol will focus on 
protecting and 
preserving biodiversity, 
ecosystems and 
associated ecosystem 
services.

Ongoing objective: Define action plans for the management and 
remediation of soil or groundwater in the event of contamination in 
less than 12 months .

Reduce NOx emissions by 30% by 2035 compared to 2018 in the 
Refining and Chemical complexes in the Iberian Peninsula. In 
alignment with GBF Target 7. 

Achieve 10% water reuse in Marcellus E&P asset in the United States 
compared to the 2022 baseline and planning. In alignment with GBF 
Target 11.

Achieve net zero freshwater withdrawal in Marcellus E&P asset in the 
United States compared to the 2022 baseline and planning. In 
alignment with GBF Target 11.

Achieve a 30% reduction in freshwater withdrawal at the industrial 
facilities of the Iberian Peninsula compared to the 2022 baseline. In 
alignment with GBF Target 11.

Achieve net zero freshwater withdrawal at industrial facilities in the 
Iberian Peninsula compared to the baseline of 2022. In alignment 
with GBF Target 11.

Ongoing objective: Not undertake any activity within the 
boundaries of sites included in the UNESCO World Heritage List 
classified as natural, cultural or mixed.

Ongoing objective: Have biodiversity action plans or equivalent 
plans in place for the operated assets material in biodiversity and 
ecosystems that are located within the boundaries of sensitive areas 
or protected areas and for those operated assets whose area of 
direct influence is within the boundaries of a site included in the 
UNESCO World Heritage List.(1).In alignment with GBF Targets 1, 10 
and 12.

Improve the conservation status of at least 5 threatened species 
through the financing, implementation and monitoring of specific 
projects. In alignment with GBF Targets 1-4 and 11.

Resource use 
and circular 
economy

Repsol will focus on 
integrating circular 
economy principles into 
its processes, products 
and services.

Reach a renewable fuel production capacity in the Iberian Peninsula 
of 1.6-1.8 million tons. In alignment with GBF Target 16.

Reach a production volume of between 125,000 and 146,000 tons by 
2030 of circular or biological origin products. In alignment with GBF 
Target 16.

Reach a biomethane production capacity from organic waste of 
between 0.7 and 0.8 TWh by 2030. In alignment with GBF Target 16.

(1) The following sensitive areas have been considered: IUCN management categories I to IV, Ramsar sites, OSPAR Network of marine protected 
areas and Key Biodiversity Areas (KBA).

5.1 Repsol's policies related to sustainability

C. Describe the Organization’s human 
rights policies and engagement activities, 
and oversight by the Board and 
management, with respect to indigenous 
peoples, local communities, affected and 
other stakeholders, in the Organization’s 
assessment of, and response to, nature-
related dependencies, impacts, risks and

and 2014/17/EU and Regulation (EU) No 596/2014 (OJ L 171, 29.6.2016,  p. 1).

Repsol, S.A. and investees

Independent verification report 
Consolidated Statement of Non-Financial Information  
for the year ended 31 December 2025

--- PAGE 257 ---
This version of our report is a free translation of the original, which was prepared in Spanish. All possible care has 
been taken to ensure that the translation is an accurate representation of the original. However, in all matters of 
interpretation of information, views or opinions, the original language version of our report takes precedence over this 
translation. 

Independent verification report 

To the shareholders of Repsol, S.A. 

Pursuant to article 49 of the Code of Commerce, we have verified, with the scope of a limited assurance 
engagement, the Consolidated Statement of Non-Financial Information (hereinafter, SSNFI) for the year 
ended 31 December 2025 of Repsol, S.A. (Parent company) and investees comprising the Repsol Group 
(hereinafter Repsol or the Group) which forms part of the accompanying Group consolidated 
management report. 

The SNFI contains information in addition to that required by applicable commercial regulations 
concerning non-financial information, which has not been covered by the scope of our verification work. 
In this respect, our work has been restricted solely to the verification of the information identified in table 
5.4.1 “Non-financial information and diversity required by Law 11/2018”, which is part of Annex IV 
“Consolidated Statement of Non-Financial Information and Sustainability Information” of the 
accompanying consolidated management report. 

Responsibility of the directors of the Parent company 

The preparation of the SNFI included in Repsol's consolidated management report and the content 
thereof, are the responsibility of the directors of Repsol, S.A. The SNFI has been drawn up in 
accordance with the provisions of current mercantile legislation and in accordance with the selected 
criteria contained in Commission Delegated Regulation (EU) 2023/2772 of 31 July 2023, regarding 
sustainability reporting standards (i.e., the ESRS), as well as other criteria described for each topic as 
specified in table 5.4.1 'Non-financial information and diversity required by Law 11/2018', which forms 
part of Annex IV 'Consolidated Statement of Non-Financial Information and Sustainability Information' of 
the attached Consolidated Management Report. 

This responsibility also includes the design, implementation and maintenance of the internal control 
considered necessary to allow the SNFI to be free of material misstatement due to fraud or error. 

The directors of Repsol, S.A. are also responsible for defining, implementing, adapting and maintaining 
the management systems from which the information required to prepare the SNFI is obtained. 

Inherent limitations in preparing the information 

In accordance with certain ESRS selected by Repsol to prepare its SNFI, the Parent company's directors 
are required to prepare prospective information based on assumptions and hypotheses, which should be 
included in the sustainability information, regarding events that could occur in the future, as well as 
possible future actions, where appropriate, that the Group could take. Actual results may differ 
significantly from estimated results since they refer to the future and future events often do not occur as 
expected. 

www.pwc.es 

1 

PricewaterhouseCoopers Auditores, S.L.  
Torre PwC, Pº de la Castellana 259 B, 28046 
Madrid, España

--- PAGE 258 ---
In determining disclosures relating to sustainability information, the Parent company's directors interpret 
legal and other terms that are not clearly defined and could be interpreted differently by others, including 
the legality of such interpretations and, consequently, they are subject to uncertainty. 

Our independence and quality management 

We have complied with the independence requirements and other ethical requirements of the 
International Code of Ethics for Professional Accountants (including International Independence 
Standards) of the International Ethics Standards Board for Accountants (IESBA Code of Ethics) which is 
founded on fundamental principles of integrity, objectivity, professional competence and due care, 
confidentiality and professional behaviour. 

Our firm applies International Standard on Quality Management (ISQM) 1, which requires the firm to 
design, implement and operate a system of quality management including policies or procedures 
regarding compliance with ethical requirements, professional standards and applicable legal and 
regulatory requirements. 

The engagement team consisted of professionals specialising in Non-financial Information reviews, 
specifically in information on economic, social and environmental performance. 

Our responsibility 

Our responsibility is to express our conclusions in a limited assurance independent report based on the 
work we have performed. We carried out our work in accordance with the requirements laid down in the 
current International Standard on Assurance Engagements (ISAE) 3000 Revised, Assurance 
Engagements other than Audits or Reviews of Historical Financial Information (ISAE 3000 Revised) 
issued by the International Auditing and Assurance Standards Board (IAASB) of the International 
Federation of Accountants (IFAC) and in the Guidelines for verification engagements of the Non-
Financial Information Statement issued by the Spanish Institute of Auditors (“Instituto de Censores 
Jurados de Cuentas de España”). 

In a limited assurance engagement, the procedures performed vary in nature and timing of execution, 
and are less extensive, than those carried out in a reasonable assurance engagement and accordingly, 
the assurance provided is also lower. 

Our work consisted of posing questions to management as well as to the various units of the Group that 
were involved in the preparation of the SNFI, of the review of the processes for compiling and validating 
the information presented in the SNFI, and in the application of certain analytical procedures and review 
procedures on a sample basis, as described below: 

• 

• 

• 

• 

2 

Meetings with the Repsol, S.A. personnel to understand the business model, policies and 
management approaches applied, principal risks relating to these matters and to obtain the 
information required for the external review. 

Analysis of the scope, relevance and integrity of the content of the SNFI for the year 2025, based 
on the materiality analysis carried out by the Group and described in section 1.4 "Materiality 
assessment", taking into account the content required by current mercantile legislation. 

Analysis of the procedures used to compile and validate the information presented in the SNFI for 
the year 2025. 

Review of information relating to risks, policies and management approaches applied in relation to 
material matters presented in the SNFI for the year 2025. 

Repsol, S.A. and investees compromising the Repsol Group

--- PAGE 259 ---
• 

• 

Verification, by means of sample testing, of the information relating to the content of the SNFI for 
the year 2025 and that it was adequately compiled using data provided by the sources of the 
information. 

Obtaining a management representation letter from the directors and management of the Parent 
company. 

Conclusion 

Based on the procedures performed in our verification and the evidence we have obtained, nothing has 
come to our attention that causes us to believe that the SNFI of Repsol, S.A. and its subsidiaries, for the 
year ended 31 December 2025 has not been prepared, in all material respects, in accordance with the 
provisions of current mercantile legislation and in accordance with the selected criteria contained in 
Commission Delegated Regulation (EU) 2023/2772 of 31 July 2023, regarding sustainability reporting 
standards (i.e., the ESRS), as well as other criteria described for each topic as specified in table 5.4.1 
'Non-financial information and diversity required by Law 11/2018', which forms part of Annex IV 
'Consolidated Statement of Non-Financial Information and Sustainability Information' of the attached 
Consolidated Management Report. 

Use and distribution 

This report has been drawn up in response to the requirement established in current Spanish mercantile 
legislation and therefore may not be suitable for other purposes and jurisdictions. 

PricewaterhouseCoopers Auditores, S.L. 

Juan Manuel Anguita Amate
